<a id="t-270174a48a"></a>
## Thread: Multiple definitions in code — 10 msgs

### Multiple definitions in code

- **From:** Michael Mehlich <michael@mehlich.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389719A4.2C507948@mehlich.com>`

```
I recently have seen code containing multiple definitions of the same name as in

01 FILLER
   02 item-a PIC X.
   02 item-a PIC X.

I wonder whether Cobol really allows this.
My original understanding of the ANSI Cobol 85 standard was no.
However, after seeing this fragment in a real piece of code I' not
sure any more (even after revisiting the standard).

The same code also contained the fragment

77 item-b PIC X.
   88 cond-b VALUE 'A'.
   88 cond-b VALUE 'B'.

Does the condition cond-b now actually mean 
  item-b == 'A' OR item-b == 'B'
?
```

#### ↳ Re: Multiple definitions in code

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_yKl4.8463$qU.181227@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<389719A4.2C507948@mehlich.com>`

```
As long as you DON'T REFERENCE the item-a or cond-b,
it will compile and execute

Michael Mehlich <michael@mehlich.com> wrote in message
news:389719A4.2C507948@mehlich.com...
> I recently have seen code containing multiple definitions of the same name
as in
>
> 01 FILLER
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Austin, Texas 78758
```

#### ↳ Re: Multiple definitions in code

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8778t8$51p$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<389719A4.2C507948@mehlich.com>`

```
The '85 Standard (unlike earlier Standards) explicitly allows you to have
non-unique and impossible to make unique via qualification data-names.
HOWEVER, this is only in the case where the items are never used (referenced)
in the procedure division.  This is "useful" <G> in the case of COPY
statements that bring in data-names that are never used.  Other than that,
they serve no real "purpose".
```

#### ↳ Re: Multiple definitions in code

- **From:** "Charles W. Cribbs II" <CHARLESCRIBBS@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<877a4q$oq4$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<389719A4.2C507948@mehlich.com>`

```

what you are showing is not a multiple definition of the same thing.  by
grouping items under an 01 you can derive benefits by the use of the 01
level.

say if i did:
01  ssn.
      05  ssn-1-3        pic x(03).
      05                      pic x(01)    value '-'.
      05  ssn-4-5        pic x(02).
      05                      pic x(01)     value '-'.
      05  ssn-6-9        pic x(04).
i save steps latter on by move the group level instead of the individual
parts.
this can also be done with your dates because you can accept system date
into a non-formatted field and send the component parts to the individual
formatted fields and have you date in the format that is usually displayed.

with respect to you use of the 77 level.  the books talk about it but i have
sure not seen it in many programs.  how you usually see this is:

01  grades                          pic x(01).
      88  valid-entries         value 'A' 'B' 'C' 'D' 'F'

01  prices                           pic 9(4).
      88  valid-prices             value 1 thru 9999.
you can list your 88 level in the same value clause or you can list them
separately but you would have to use another condition name.

"Michael Mehlich" <michael@mehlich.com> wrote in message
news:389719A4.2C507948@mehlich.com...

as in

> 01 FILLER
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Austin, Texas 78758
```

- **From:** Tonia <tshainberg@home.com>

```
Michael,
	Was this clean compiled code. To the best of my knowledge you can not
have more than 1 name with the same name unless it is under a separate
group level. The code for an 88 level with multiple values would be
using the "OR" statement. For example:
77 ITEM-B PIC X.
   88  COND-B VALUE 'A' OR 'B'.
					Tonia

>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple definitions in code

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<877k77$lfa$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<389719A4.2C507948@mehlich.com> <38974651.8F18234D@home.com> <38974FF5.F18520D0@mehlich.com>`

```
I don't think that the "OR" in the 88 level is needed (allowed?)

77 ITEM-B PIC X.
  88 cond-b Value "A" "B".

*means* that cond-b is true when  ITEM-B is equal to "A" or "B"

FYI (because it might be missed in this thread)

77 ITEM-A PIC X.
  88 cond-b Value "B".
77 ITEM-B PIC X.
  88 cond-b Value "b".

Evaluate True
    When cond-b of Item-B
          Display "ITEM-B = b"
     when cond-b of item-A
          Display "ITEM-A = B"
end-evaluate

shows how you use "qualification" with 88-level names (that are not unique)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple definitions in code

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ShqSDAyxEm4EwPw@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<389719A4.2C507948@mehlich.com> <38974651.8F18234D@home.com>`

```
In article <38974651.8F18234D@home.com>, Tonia <tshainberg@home.com>
writes
>Michael,
>       Was this clean compiled code. To the best of my knowledge you can not
>have more than 1 name with the same name unless it is under a separate
>group level. 

May I take this opportunity to remind people of the data-item FILLER?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple definitions in code

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87a0io$27n$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<389719A4.2C507948@mehlich.com> <38974651.8F18234D@home.com> <5ShqSDAyxEm4EwPw@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Sorry for being "pedantic" - but "filler" (whether specified or implied) is
NOT a data-name - but an alternative to specifying a data-name in a
data-definition.

HOWEVER, the rules (for multiple uses) are the same, i.e. it is legal to have
multiple FILLER items, but you cannot reference them anywhere.  (similarly,
as stated elsewhere in this thread) you can have multiple data-items with the
same name (including all levels of qualification) - as long as you never
"use" them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Multiple definitions in code

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87a17m$k8o$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<389719A4.2C507948@mehlich.com> <38974651.8F18234D@home.com> <5ShqSDAyxEm4EwPw@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
:>       Was this clean compiled code. To the best of my knowledge you can not
:>have more than 1 name with the same name unless it is under a separate
:>group level. 

: May I take this opportunity to remind people of the data-item FILLER?

FILLER is not a _name_ it is a reserved word.
```

On a slightly lighter, but true note, I worked for an organisation which
was asked to perform some work for the client. After many discussions
with the client the requirements were agreed with the user and signed
off. Development progressed and the deliverable was delivered on time.

However, we did receive a letter of complaint from the client which said
(roughly):
     I know that I asked for A, B, C... and that you delivered A, B,
     C... on budget and to schedule and that it works as originally
     agreed. However, you should have known that what I really wanted 
     was D, E, F...
The user was serious!

Didn't Bart Simpson say that: you are damned if you do and damned if you
don't?
```

WDS wrote:

Reminds me of what I always used to tell users.

1. We can do it quickly
2. We can do it cheaply
3. We can do it correctly

PICK TWO OUT OF THE THREE!

<a id="t-98d56a06e2"></a>
## Thread: STD - Please test this for me — 2 msgs

### STD - Please test this for me

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<877rh4$akr$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The following is a "test program" that I would appreciate getting actual
results from - from as many compilers as possible.  I have stuck with all
upper-case and IF (not EVALUATE) so I *think* this will compile with any '74
Standard compiler - as well as any '85 Standard compiler.

I don't think the whole NG will be interested in all the replies, so please
send me the replies via private email (and I will post a "summary" when I get
enough results)


000011 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000020  PROGRAM-ID. ALPHEDIT.
000030 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000040 DATA DIVISION.
000050  WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000060 01  GROUP1.
000070     05  ALPHA-EDIT1      PIC  X/X.
000080 01  MISC.
000090     05  ALPHANUM         PIC  XX.
000100     05  ALPHA-EDIT2      PIC  X/X.
000110     05  ALPHA-EDIT3      PIC  XBX.
000120 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000150     PERFORM INIT
000160     PERFORM TEST1
000170     PERFORM TEST2
000180     PERFORM TEST3
000190     STOP RUN
000200      .
000210 INIT.
000211     MOVE "ABC"  TO GROUP1
000220    MOVE SPACES  TO MISC
000230     .
000231 TEST1.
000232     MOVE ALPHA-EDIT1 TO ALPHANUM
000233     DISPLAY "TEST1 - PIC XX:" ALPHANUM
000234     IF ALPHANUM = "AB"
000235         DISPLAY " BELIEVE THE DATA"
000236     ELSE
000237         IF ALPHANUM = "A/"
000238             DISPLAY " BELIEVE THE PICTURE"
000239         ELSE
000240             DISPLAY " OTHER !!!"
000241     .
000242 TEST2.
000243     MOVE ALPHA-EDIT1 TO ALPHA-EDIT2
000244     DISPLAY "TEST2 - PIC X/X:" ALPHA-EDIT2
000245     IF ALPHA-EDIT2 = "ABC"
000246         DISPLAY " BELIEVE THE DATA"
000247     ELSE
000248         IF ALPHA-EDIT2 = "A/C"
000249             DISPLAY " UNEDITED MOVE"
000250         ELSE
000251             IF ALPHA-EDIT2 = "A/B"
000252                 DISPLAY " RE-EDIT THE DATA"
000253             ELSE
000254                 IF ALPHA-EDIT2 = "A//"
000255                     DISPLAY " RE-EDIT BUT BELIEVE SENDING PIC"
000257              ELSE
000258                  DISPLAY " OTHER !!!"
000259     .
000260 TEST3.
000261     MOVE ALPHA-EDIT1 TO ALPHA-EDIT3
000262     DISPLAY "TEST3 - PIC XBX:" ALPHA-EDIT3
000263     IF ALPHA-EDIT3 = "A C"
000264         DISPLAY " BELIEVE THE DATA"
000265     ELSE
000266         IF ALPHA-EDIT3 = "ABC"
000267             DISPLAY " UNEDITED MOVE"
000268         ELSE
000269             IF ALPHA-EDIT3 = "A B"
000270                 DISPLAY " RE-EDIT THE DATA"
000271             ELSE
000272                 IF ALPHA-EDIT3 = "A/ "
000273                     DISPLAY " RE-EDIT BUT BELIEVE SENDING PIC"
000274              ELSE
000275                  DISPLAY " OTHER !!!"
000276     .
```

#### ↳ Re: STD - Please test this for me

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87a0v8$pr$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<877rh4$akr$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI - I think I have enough test results from this sample.  UNLESS YOUR
TESTING GIVES SOME OTHER RESULTS - than the following:

It looks like every compiler is treating "PIC X/X" *exactly* as if it were
coded as "PIC XXX" - for a sending item (not receiving item).  This means
that even if you move a PIC X/X item to another PIC X/X item, it still
"re-edits" whatever data was in the original item.

Results (reported by all compilers so far):

TEST1 - PIC XX:AB
 BELIEVE THE DATA
TEST2 - PIC X/X:A/B
 RE-EDIT THE DATA
TEST3 - PIC XBX:A B
 RE-EDIT THE DATA


P.S. Sorry for the missing paragraph name in the sample - for those working
with compilers that required one to be inserted.
```

Dave Chilvers wrote:

> I need to be able to intgrate with a system written in RM Cobol 85.  I
> know nothing about this system, have no source, only raw data files.
>
> Does anybody know if it is possible to determine what the record
> layout is?

That might be propriety information.  You might need to contact the the
people who wrote the compiler and/or run unit for more information.  I
assume these are indexed or relative files and as such the format for the
same information could change on different platforms.

I am curious as to why you wouldn't have source on your system.  Do you
have a compier?  Would your interface be written in COBOL or some other
language?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting data from RM Cobol 85

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2SuXOCgqGhg9BWiZCWWDy0WtXUBs@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<m2bd9sckbs78nbnhpi5vs1napbbe80jlve@4ax.com> <3896FC02.FB875DB@bellsouth12.net> <i11e9sk4siqslp8jgck192timssvcj6v2f@4ax.com>`

```
>>That might be propriety information.  You might need to contact the the
The layout of a file (not the file system internals) should always be
supplied to the customer. so he can access the information in the
files in case something drastic happens to the manufacturer of the 
software. (like closing the company...).

>
>We don't have the source because we are interfacing to a 3rd party
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The fact that it is no longer supported or maintained does not mean
you can not ask (and GET) the file layout. You do not need (normally)
to have any source program, but only the FD. If you can get this
information then it will be easy for a COBOL programmer to create
a program to extract the information.
If you need to have files updated on line then you will need the
also Relational Databridge ( and a very good programmer).

FF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: STD - What do you think *should* happen

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3896EDEA.90BB5B81@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<871vmu$2ns$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <20000131225539.24953.00000881@ng-bj1.aol.com> <3896D546.DBDC8936@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:

> Steve Newton wrote:
> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> can be done though :-})

Such debugging is extremely rare in COBOL.  The use of meaningful name
variables which are explicitly defined and the fact that most programs are
stand-alone (this is changing but sub-programs HAVE local variables by
default) create an environment where it is very unlikely that you will
start using a variable which is being used elsewhere in the program.  This
is especially true now that we don't need to save every byte of memory.
Everything's out in the open in COBOL.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: STD - What do you think *should* happen

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87cvl5$mau$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<86v2ur$77b$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <kjtj9s82i6lqeu933vikn66gt0lmc6fj97@4ax.com>`

```
FYI,
  all reporting compilers (and there have been a LOT) currently "totally
ignore" the "/" in the sending item, so the receiving item ends up with "AB"
(the same as if the PIC X/X sending field had been defined as PIC XXX).  I am
not certain that I think that this is a "good" way to work, but it does seem
to be the only way that would be compatible with existing compilers and
applications.
```

> the silliest thing I've ever heard. Apparently, the Micro Focus
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Oh, it's true.  And silly in the extreme.  In fact, let's say you
wanted to be a one man ASP using a Linux server.  Cool.  You cough up
the nearly 3000 bucks for the compiler plus the $800+ for the 10
DEVELOPER runtime license, then code away.  Then you find to run behind
the web server, you need the "Application Server" runtime.  The cost, a
whopping $10,000.00 - yes the decimal place is in the right place.
That includes a year of maintenance - some $1780 bucks, that must be
renewed every year.  Oh, and that's for a single server.

Sure makes Fujitsu on NT look attractive.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Bogus Micro Focus COBOL for Linux

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QBum4.23876$Fb1.514937@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<389242AA.BA498A19@gethip.com> <877db3$v35$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote:
> Oh, it's true.  And silly in the extreme.  In fact, let's say you
> wanted to be a one man ASP using a Linux server.  Cool.  You cough up
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> renewed every year.  Oh, and that's for a single server.

Thane, i've been travelling a while; haven't got to your email, but i think
this is on the same topic. seems like there's a lot of confusion here, which
is a shame.

Micro Focus COBOL (always on UNIX & sometimes on NT depending on functions
used) charges a run-time fee for deployed applications. you do NOT have to
buy additional run-times for development (i don't know who told you that,
but they are wrong).

there are two available models for the run-time (also called the
"Application Server"). a 10-user license (i think that's something like the
$800 you mention; so $80 each) and an unlimited-user license (this is the
$10K item) for transaction based server applications (such as web apps).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Zellers Congruence slow (long)

- **From:** walterm@rose.hp.com (Walter Murray)
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<877dko$s36$2@web1.cup.hp.com>`
- **References:** `<38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca> <20000117114103.28428.00000487@ng-fl1.aol.com> <86v1me$4kh$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein (wmklein@nospam.netcom.com) wrote:
: Interestingly enough, there was an explicit ANSI Interpretation Request on
: this.  (I know, because I asked it <G>).  The answer was that although we are
: an "ANSI Standard" - so you *might* think that it would be based on the dates
: in what was eventually to become the US - the Standard "assumes" (requires)
: that you treat all dates "as if" the Gregorian correction(s) were already
: applied in the local area.  

This is sometimes referred to as using a "proleptic" Gregorian
calendar.  Thus we can say that A.D. 300 was not a leap year in
the Gregorian calendar, even though the Gregorian calendar was
not invented until centuries later.

Here's a bit of trivia that would further complicate things if
you tried to program for the calendar change.  Prior to the
adoption of the Gregorian calenar, New Year's Day did not fall
on January 1, but on March 25.  In Britain and the American
colonies, for example, the day following March 24, 1700, was
March 25, 1701.  George Washington was born on February 11, 1731,
according to the calendar that was in effect at that time.  After
the adoption of the Gregorian calendar in September, 1752, his
birthday became February 22, 1732.

Walter
```

<a id="t-40df4eaeb5"></a>
## Thread: NetExpress OpenSQL SCROLLOPTION — 1 this month / 2 total

_Thread started [2000-01](2000-01.md#t-40df4eaeb5)._

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress OpenSQL SCROLLOPTION

- **From:** "Randy & Chris Zimmerman" <rwzcez91@execpc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389749c2$0$1400@news.execpc.com>`
- **References:** `<164c000c.d6e042df@usw-ex0102-014.remarq.com>`

```
Positioned updates are possible if you use the directive SQL(USECURLIB=YES).
This will cause OESQL to use the ODBC Cursor Library.  You should make sure
that you have applied the latest OESQL web-sync fixpack to have this
functionality.

Because all SQL statement functionality doesn't translate to ODBC calls, you
can also use the DB2 ESQL preprocessor which is invoked via the DB2
directive.  See the Data Access on-line docs for more details.



fabiochi <fabiochi@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:164c000c.d6e042df@usw-ex0102-014.remarq.com...
> Hi,
> I have some cobol programs that use cursor to fetch and update tables
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

*

<a id="t-4d9cc10b9c"></a>
## Thread: Y2K — 2 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

_Thread started [2000-01](2000-01.md#t-4d9cc10b9c)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38974a41.3872858@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<387DFF69.58F0CF68@gecm.com> <85r2fe$fmf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Sun, 16 Jan 2000 00:13:42 GMT, Arthur wrote:

>Hello, I think not all bugs were corrected. I also think the Feb 29 2000
>problem will cause lot of troubles (because nobody know about it). Just
>to say not all computers know leap-year formula is 1/4 - 1/100 + 1/400.

Actually, I think that the Year 2000 Feb 29 issue will probably little
different from any other leap year.  That is, it will probably be
nearly a non-event.

It is true that some computers/programs/people do not know the correct
way to determine leap year in a year ending in 00.  In fact, most of
said computers/programs/people do not know that the determination is
any different than on any other leap year.  That being the case, it is
likely that 02/29/2000 will work by default; it would be different had
it been 1900, 2100, or such.

There could possibly be problems that are related to faulty
programming, anticipating the special case for a year divisible by
400, but introducing errors in the process.  Otherwise, it is not
likely that 02/29/2000 will provide any different experiences than
would 02/29/AnyOtherLeapYear.
```

<a id="t-e902d3e411"></a>
## Thread: Variable length records (output) — 4 this month / 5 total

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

_Thread started [2000-01](2000-01.md#t-e902d3e411)._

#### ↳ Re: Variable length records (output)

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZHKl4.8479$qU.187019@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<5Rtl4.1135$yj3.65270@news.uswest.net>`

```
FD  MY-FILE
      RECORD CONTAINS 1 TO 100 CHARACTERS.
01  ONE-BYTE-REC     PIC X.
01  FIFTY-BYTE-REC   PIC X(50).
01  ONE-HUNDRED-BYTE-REC PIC X(100).

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  WORK-AREAS.
     05  WS-WORK                  PIC X(50) VALUE '1........10.........20........30........40........5'.
     05  WS-100-BYTE-WORK  PIC X(100) VALUE SPACES.
PROCEDURE  DIVISION.
...
MOVE SPACES TO ONE-HUNDRED-BYTE-REC.
MOVE '1' TO ONE-BYTE-REC.
WRITE ONE-BYTE-REC.
MOVE WS-WORK  TO FIFTY-BYTE-REC.
WRITE FIFTY-BYTE-REC.
WRITE ONE-BYTE-REC.
STRING FIFTY-BYTE-REC DELIMITED BY SIZE,
             FIFTY-BYTE-REC DELIMITED BY SIZE
   INTO WS-100-BYTE-WORK.
WRITE ONE-HUNDRED-BYTE-REC FROM WS-100-BYTE-WORK.
...
STOP RUN.
Your output will be 
1
1........10.........20........30........40........5
1
1........10.........20........30........40........51........10.........20........30........40........5






  Garry Dell <gdell2@uswest.net> wrote in message news:5Rtl4.1135$yj3.65270@news.uswest.net...
  How do I create a variable length output record without depending upon a length index ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length records (output)

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38968512.A859293E@home.nl>`
- **References:** `<5Rtl4.1135$yj3.65270@news.uswest.net>`

```
I don't think that is possible. In your record area you have to reserve
space for the maximum record size, and somehow you have to tell your
filesystem that only a part of that space is to be written to the file.
For that purpose you have to use the "depending on" phrase.

The only other way I can think of (don't know if it works !!) is to make
several record entries with different sizes, like this:

FD	my_file.
01	record_1
	05 field_a	pic x(20).
01	record_2
	05 filed_b	pic x(100).

You could give it a try.

Regards,

Dirk

Garry Dell wrote:
> 
>    Part 1.1    Type: Plain Text (text/plain)
>            Encoding: quoted-printable
```

##### ↳ ↳ SV: Variable length records (output)

- **From:** "Bjorn Alvik" <bjorn.alvik@asp.no>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Olwl4.19102$in5.335249@news1.online.no>`
- **References:** `<5Rtl4.1135$yj3.65270@news.uswest.net> <38968512.A859293E@home.nl>`

```
I joined this discussin a little late, so I do not know anything about what
Dirk think is not possible.
But what I know for sure, is that Dirk's way of defining variable length
records is right. Some compilers, though, might demand a filler of  4 (or
was it 8?) bytes in the start of each record to contain record /block
length. I do not think this is the case now.
Anyway, Dirk is right, and when writing records, you must take care and
specify the right name of the record to be written.
(When handling different fixed-length records, the record-name you specify
does not matter, since 01-levels within a FD always redefines the same area)
Bjorn

Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl> skrev i
meldingsnyheter:38968512.A859293E@home.nl...
> I don't think that is possible. In your record area you have to reserve
> space for the maximum record size, and somehow you have to tell your
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> >            Encoding: quoted-printable
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length records (output)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<876i66$u79$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<5Rtl4.1135$yj3.65270@news.uswest.net> <38968512.A859293E@home.nl> <Olwl4.19102$in5.335249@news1.online.no>`

```
I also joined this conversation "late" - but in ANY (every) ANSI'85
conforming compiler, you can specify

FD file-name
      Record varying in size from n to m
           depending on ws-data-item
    .
(where "n" and "m" are the smallest and largest record sizes - excluding any
"internal" RDW or similar control word).

If you then move the "correct" size to ws-data-item (defined in
working-storage) - then the record that is written will be the "desired"
size.

NOTE WELL: To ensure that this works, you MUST use "RECORD VARYING IN SIZE"
and *not* "RECORD CONTAINS".  The latter may (is permitted) to produce
variable size records - but is not guaranteed to.
```

```
Here is the (3rd hand) reply that I received from one of my "usually reliable
sources",

"The customer is getting the DB2 not found message most likely because he
does not have db2lib installed in usr/lib.   The user does not have to
specify BEGIN DECLARE and END DECLARE around his host variables
declarations.  But we know that Workstation DB2 and S/390 DB2 have some
incompatibilities.  So this migration may or may not be easy depending on
how many S/390 DB2 extensions they have in their code.  We eliminated a lot
of incompatibilities with the co-processors, but we did not eliminate all
of them."
```

## Thread: STD - from the San Diego J4 meeting — 2 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

_Thread started [2000-01](2000-01.md#t-04f9b01993)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO: Compiler vendors please note...

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3896D9E6.5F0C2046@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<871t1r$s91$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <389586E3.ACED0486@zip.com.au> <874h36$jd5$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Steve Biggs wrote:
> 
> An explicit "delete"/"finalize" can be used against an explit "new"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the garbage collector prevents!

I do not like garbage collectors.  They are unpredictable.  Would you
fly on a plane that decided to do a garbage collection on final decent
and took a few seconds to clean up?

Garbage collection is about recovering memory.  Destruction is about
that final stage of programming.  The actual memory involved might be
minuscule (a window handle) but the timing of destruction crucial
(close that window please).

Other examples are open a file with a lock and closing a file.  With
creation and destruction controlled by the compiler and a known method
name called when the object is destroyed by the compiler we can
guarantee to locks are removed.

Look up the GNU concurrent versioning system (cvs) locking mechanism,
locking is done be creating a file and deleting a file, no memory
would be required for this object at all.
 
> Existing Net Express "Finalize" methods would be unlikely to
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> what syntax COBOL could use?

The same concept as PIC  VALUE ...any arguments the new uses...

> Always declaring an object on the stack like this is also awfully
> wasteful if, say, the routine ends up using only one of the fifty
> objects declared...
> (This isn't to say that it's a bad idea, just that we do need
> dynamically created objects, too.)

This is definitely not what I meant, just that other options should be
considered. Objects work better at dynamic allocation than procedural
code.

Dynamic object are required they would typically live in collections
so that they are easy to find and clean up.

Thanks for the feedback.  At least I got a bite from one compiler
vendor :-}

Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO: Compiler vendors please note...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<876vbd$tt0$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<871t1r$s91$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <389586E3.ACED0486@zip.com.au> <874h36$jd5$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <3896D9E6.5F0C2046@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
>I do not like garbage collectors.  They are unpredictable.  Would you
>fly on a plane that decided to do a garbage collection on final decent
>and took a few seconds to clean up?

Well... It's a bit of a myth that garbage collectors are slow. Written and 
used properly, they can be almost unnoticable (and can, in some 
circumstances, save time). And with the right interface at the 
compiler/programmer level, they *can* be more predictable.

That's not to say that they couldn't cause problems - where Java is a 
tightly-OO language and can build in garbage collection from the start, 
languages like C++ and COBOL, of course, have to contend with a certain 
amount of history. :-) But, with the appropriate rules and good 
programming, they should just be another tool in the box, used or not.

>Garbage collection is about recovering memory.  Destruction is about
>that final stage of programming.  The actual memory involved might be
>minuscule (a window handle) but the timing of destruction crucial
>(close that window please).

Most systems with garbage collection have a facility whereby the collector 
and finalizers can be deliberately run. So you might destroy the window, 
but not the object that represents it until the garbage collector runs. Of 
course, you could run the garbage collector when the window receives its 
destuction notice...

[Re Declaring objects in working-storage with arguments]
>The same concept as PIC  VALUE ...any arguments the new uses...

And if the constuctor takes two or more arguments, each being another 
object that may be in linkage? I guess there's always ways around, but 
they look more ugly (or less COBOL-like) to me!

>Thanks for the feedback.  At least I got a bite from one compiler
>vendor :-}

Just my *personal* opinion, based on a certain amount of experience with 
garbage collectors. :-)

Steve.
```

    DISPLAY 'YIKES! I *HAVE* LOST IT!'

re:  Basic
Michael Mattias wrote:

> IF 'IF' EQUAL "ELSEIF'
>     DISPLAY 'YIKES! I *HAVE* LOST IT!'
> END(" IF" or "-IF", depending on language)
>
> (In BASIC, ELSEIF is not an IF, and may only appear subordinate to an IF).

I don't care for it then.  If an ELSEIF cannot have its own ELSE, then avoid
confusion by having a CASE verb such as COBOL's EVALUATE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IF... END-IF (in BASIC)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bFDl4.7700$qU.172140@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<86vnoj$nlr$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <20000129175206.15820.00000099@nso-cf.aol.com> <n7Pk4.902$aZ4.34284@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38959F7E.C3B600F6@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <32ol4.5769$rC3.132515@news3.mia> <38961640.18A1D2ED@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <BWpl4.512$qH.32509@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3896EC69.EC67CE26@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
>Michael Mattias wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>confusion by having a CASE verb such as COBOL's EVALUATE.

It doesn't mean you can't embed IF/ELSE statements in the normal way.
BASIC does have a CASE statement (SELECT CASE).  Remember, BASIC is
older than structured programming and still has some baggage left
over from before standard control structures were added, just like
COBOL does.  Actually, you can write very well structured code, even
in QBasic.  Modern BASIC has SELECT CASE, DO WHILE ..., DO UNTIL ...,
DO ... WHILE, DO ... UNTIL, functions and subroutines with formal
declarations and parameters, data typing, user defined data types,
local and global variables, and lots of other goodies.  Like COBOL,
BASIC is a good language if you use it properly, and stay away from
old non-structured features, left in for compatibility reasons.  I'm
not sure you could call VB or VBA 'BASIC' in the traditional sense,
because they have been altered so drastically.  After all, BASIC is
an acronym for Beginner's All-purpose Symbolic Instruction Code. :-)
```

_(thread depth: 10)_

```
>Both 01's &
>> 77's are double word boundary aligned, but the fields in an 01 are
>> contiguous 

Bill, are you sure about this?? I no longer have my OS VS COBOL book, but I
clearly remember that 77 being defined at byte aligned, that was the difference
from the 01, and thus was recommended for all those small variables that were
not switches and not part of something else.

The alternative, to save the same storage, was to define a 01 MISC-FIELDS. and
then define them all as subordinate to it.

I'm not saying you are not right, just that is opposite of what was explained
to me.

Thanks,


Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SV: Cobol-problem CICS -> TransActionServer Rel. 1.3

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<877g2d$ukb$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<875fra$82ls$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com> <20000131232749.24953.00000889@ng-bj1.aol.com> <876lu2$4992$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
: Steve Newton <stevenln@aol.comnospam> wrote in message
:>
:> The alternative, to save the same storage, was to define a 01 MISC-FIELDS.
: and
:> then define them all as subordinate to it.

Grouping miscellaneous flags and data under an 01 does not necessarily
'save memory'.  While it uses less working-storage it may use
more code if the machine requires additional instructions to 
extract non-aligned bytes and/or mask off the rest of the word.

A few decades ago I recall helping a client to get his program to
fit into his 12Kword memory by changing all his character flags
to words ( specifically PIC 9 to PIC S1(23) COMP SYNC - it was a
24bit word).
```

---

#### ↳ Re: SV: SV: Redefines

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)

"OUT" area.  Not sure if the same thing exists for Email or not.

Zeus for Windows Version 3.20 Now Released
------------------------------------------
This is an announcement to indicate that the Zeus for Windows 
Version 3.20 has now been released. Zeus is a programmer's is 
designed to run on the Windows 95, Windows 98 or NT platforms.

What is new in Version 3.20:
    - FTP file editing
    - Full DDE support added
    - Supports for the wheel mouse added
    - Minor bug fixes

Other features include:
    - Builtin scripting support for the Python, LUA or 
        Small C languages 
    - Fully configurable syntax highlighting comes 
        pre-configured for: 
         C/C++, Java, Perl, HTML, Python, Pascal, etc
    - Fully configurable compiler, linker and tool support
        will run almost any compiler, linker or tool.

<a id="t-07baee1538"></a>
## Thread: Centralized systems — 13 msgs

### Centralized systems

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38983C3A.17105521@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
One reason I prefer standard COBOL to the OO work and distributed
processing I have done is that everything is visible in one place.
Stand alone systems not dependent upon anything else means there's no
finger pointing.

A co-worker is working on a distributed system where he was on the
carpet when one customer couldn't get his data FTP'd to our mainframe.
Only when the bigwigs became involved did the customer admit that his
changing their firewall may have had something to do with the problem
(that WAS the problem).  Finger pointing occurs when different people
own different pieces of a system.

One reason OO's use of reusable objects is much smaller than predicted
is because people don't want to be dependent upon code/behavior owned by
others and/or hidden from us.   We don't want finger pointing.  We don't
want to search layers within layers to find out why some system is
behaving the way it does.  We want OUR code to be predictable.

A powerful, reliable centralized system provides the background for
stand-alone jobs and programs with few dependencies on other people's
code.
```

#### ↳ Re: Centralized systems

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pEXl4.78350$Dv1.2238723@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38983C3A.17105521@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Howard offers a lot of valid points. It's funny how as adults we need
complex rules for sharing, something we were supposed to have already
learned as kids.

While centralization has merits, there are legitimate reasons to
decentralize. There are reasons why businesses are divided into distinct
units. Rather than ignoring the divisions, software developers need to
respect the divisions. We have to find ways to make software development
effective in the 'real world' of the corporation rather than in an ideal
world where we have all the answers to all the problems.

For example, I worked on a project where a business unit was responsible for
keeping track of customer information. Clearly the information was useful to
many other departments. However, the rules concerning the presentation and
validation of the data were the responsibility of my department.
Centralization would be inappropriate since it would transfer the
responsibility for the problem domain out of the hands of the experts and
into the hands of some other group that could not (and should not)
understand all the details of customer registration.

The solution to the problem was to create a 'stock exchange' for objects.
The stock exchange is an appropriate model for sharing objects. Exchanges
establish rules for trading and provide enforcement for those rules. Groups
that want to trade on an exchange are required to abide by the rules.

Getting back to my earlier example: any group can 'list' on the exchange,
provided they adhere to the rules of the exchange which means standards for
code,  versioning, documentation and, most important, a service level
agreement which details the mechanism by which defects and design changes
will be addressed. Any group can use a listed object as long as they agree
to follow the rules for reconciling problems. The central group is
responsible for the process rather than the objects themselves.

A side-benefit of the exchange is each object is documented and indexed.
This addresses a big O-O dilemma, finding the appropriate object for the
job. If you can't find it you won't use it.

There are techniques for using somebody else's objects which provide
stability and isolation from change. I suspect that Howard even uses these
techniques. Every time he uses a compiler, system subroutine, or database he
is depending on some other company's code so I'm sure there are procedures
for getting around problems and upgrading.

I agree with Howard when he says that code should be 'predictable'. I submit
that code which is based on shared components is more likely to be stable
than code which is developed from scratch.

-- Karl Wagner

"Howard Brazee" <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
news:38983C3A.17105521@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...
> One reason I prefer standard COBOL to the OO work and distributed
> processing I have done is that everything is visible in one place.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Howard Brazee wrote:

As pointed out distribution is nothing to do with OO.  It can be
implemented many ways.  OO is better though :-}

> A co-worker is working on a distributed system where he was on the
> carpet when one customer couldn't get his data FTP'd to our
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> different people own different pieces of a system.

We no longer live in ivory towers and churn code.  We must deal with
the real world.

I have had to explain FTP to a group named the 'file transfer unit'.

> One reason OO's use of reusable objects is much smaller than
> predicted is because people don't want to be dependent upon
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> code to be predictable.

The inheritance concept is incredibly powerful and very difficult to
come to terms with.

I can (and have) written a reusable library in 'standard' cobol.  I
could also do this in OO cobol.  Some of the design decisions that you
made in your library become very different with OO and more flexible.

> A powerful, reliable centralized system provides the background for
> stand-alone jobs and programs with few dependencies on other people's
> code.

This is the easy life.  However self dependence leads to longer
development time.  Customer Off The Shelf (COTS) development will be
an increasing trend due to cost trade off.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

errg. there is a difference between oo, different languages, and

can re-use code from structured languages, so that isn't oo. i know
that the main difference i see between c and c++ (classically called
oo) is the use of private classes, and code can become a subset of a
new *class*. there is also linking in structured, so the hiding of
code into object files cannot be called oo.

>A co-worker is working on a distributed system where he was on the
>carpet when one customer couldn't get his data FTP'd to our mainframe.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>own different pieces of a system.

>One reason OO's use of reusable objects is much smaller than predicted
>is because people don't want to be dependent upon code/behavior owned by
>others and/or hidden from us.   We don't want finger pointing.  We don't

hidden code isn't exclusively an oo problem. there is much code
written in structured languages ( c windows hint hint) which is hidden
from customers. also, when a new piece of hardware becomes available,
do you lease a piece of code from a company, or do you start from
scratch?

(for example, when they made the improvement from modem to fax/modem,
did all the pre-existing terminal software work well? i am sure the
device drivers could be re-used, but the screens couldn't, and you
*would* have to port from the fax format of the hardware to a format
which was viewable to a graphics screen... in which case you had to
build a graphics routine as well.)

>want to search layers within layers to find out why some system is

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Centralized systems

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389ED8C3.C979477A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<38983C3A.17105521@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <n5um9sgbnb867di97m58bohhg2eu7sc5bo@4ax.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:

> >One reason OO's use of reusable objects is much smaller than predicted
> >is because people don't want to be dependent upon code/behavior owned by
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> scratch?

Certainly, but hidden code is a necessary and basic part of OO.

My PC's tape backup program hasn't worked since I upgraded it a year ago.
Today I got an e-mail from tech support to upgrade the bios on my tape drive
and also to upgrade the bios on my motherboard.  I had used their program to
find the bios on the tape drive, so they knew what to require there, but I
suppose I'll have to find the bios chip on the motherboard.  Last time I
needed to upgrade the bios on a PC was when I got a large hard drive - at
that time, I couldn't find a new bios and bought a new motherboard instead.
See my problem?  These hidden dependencies make assumptions.  An upgrade of
an object may assume that everything else is up-to-date.   This isn't too big
of a problem where we can see everything which is dependent upon that hidden
object - where it's use isn't too widely distributed.

Complex, interactive dependencies mean that bugs are complex and widespread.
Where this is needed in the physical world (say, a bridge), overbuilding is
necessary.  The internet allows multiple paths so that we're not dependent
upon a single path.  I have to admit that I have seen "belts and suspenders
code":
IF A=B GO TO A1
ELSE  GOTO A2.
GOTO A3...
But this isn't the same thing.  I do put in some "never happen code", though:

IF  FUNCTION LENGTH (IABRCBC)
  = FUNCTION LENGTH (BEFORE-RECORD)
     CONTINUE
ELSE PERFORM ABORT-ROUTINE.
Or
LAST-PARAGRAPH.
    Display "Code should never reach this, contact whomever last did
maintenance".
    PERFORM ABORT-ROUTINE.

I wish all COBOL functions provided failure codes.  But still, most serious
bugs are design bugs, not coding bugs.  If the code/object/subroutine doesn't
quite do what it did before, when we used it for our program, it might not
give an error message.  It may simply work partially wrong.  But it isn't
YOUR object, you didn't test the change in that object, since you didn't know
it was changed.

When a compiler change makes a program different, we have to compile it to
make that change.  Then we test it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Centralized systems

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87qpql025i7@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<38983C3A.17105521@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <n5um9sgbnb867di97m58bohhg2eu7sc5bo@4ax.com> <389ED8C3.C979477A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
I don't think oo has much to do with re-usability as it does with 
encapsulation.  I reuse lots of code, everyday, and that is the result of
writing it right the first time (design).  It saves me lots of time.  We
have modules we call that perform very well defined functions,everybody uses
them and they work great.  That is encapsulation...not having to understand
HOW the code works to be able to use it.

I think the finger-pointing analogy is very true in the business world.
There are certainly more of these 'dependencies' you describe on a
distributed system.

People all think differently.  That's the inherent problem with developing
complex systems.  It's like cars.  Ever try to work on a new car?  It's
almost impractical for a home-mechanic to do anything outside routine
maintenance.  Ever popped the hood of one of them old cars like a late 60s
Chevy?  There's like only 20 parts under the hood, 8 visible wires, and
ground visible in the engine bay.  Certainly, cars are much much better
these days, but they also cost alot more and it took 30 years so I don't
think getter more complicated is really that great of a solution.  Why does
GM continue to build almost all it's vehicles with Overhead Valve pushrod
engines with 2 valves per cylinder and chain drive while every other
manufacturers goes the high tech route?  How can an engine like the 3800 or
350 small block be so good if it's so 'low-tech'?  Apparently there is
something to be said for simplicity.

Anyways I now feel bad for pointing the finger at production control all
those times they screwed up.

Jeff

----------
In article <389ED8C3.C979477A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote:


> G Moore wrote:
>
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Centralized systems

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87s1d4$mjb$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<38983C3A.17105521@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <n5um9sgbnb867di97m58bohhg2eu7sc5bo@4ax.com> <389ED8C3.C979477A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <87qpql025i7@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
I agree with you regarding OO.  However, one of my pet peeves regarding the
new OO specification is oriented to source code only.  A central point to OO
methodology is that code should be re-usable at the OBJECT level. If there
is a point to it, that is central.  Yet I cannot blindly substitute an MF
object for a (theoretically) identical Fujitsu object.

I cannot see OO Cobol fulfilling it's mandate without some sort of object
compatibility at the DLL level. That, in turn, mandates a similarity at the
linkage level for type checking.

With object compatibility, we could get into standardized browsers, and true
re-use of all Cobol code, regardless of Compiler.  As it sits, I only have
re-usability between each individual Cobol and the "other" languages that
exist.  That is quite ridiculous, in my opinion ... that I can more easily
use an outside object written in Visual Basic than an outside object written
in another Cobol.  I might as well stick with subroutines.

>> ELSE  GOTO A2.
>> GOTO A3...
>> But this isn't the same thing.  I do put in some "never happen code",
though:
>>
>> IF  FUNCTION LENGTH (IABRCBC)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I wish all COBOL functions provided failure codes.  But still, most
serious
>> bugs are design bugs, not coding bugs.  If the code/object/subroutine
doesn't
>> quite do what it did before, when we used it for our program, it might
not
>> give an error message.  It may simply work partially wrong.  But it isn't
>> YOUR object, you didn't test the change in that object, since you didn't
know
>> it was changed.
>>
>> When a compiler change makes a program different, we have to compile it
to
>> make that change.  Then we test it.
>>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Centralized systems

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87s2k5$48j$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38983C3A.17105521@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <n5um9sgbnb867di97m58bohhg2eu7sc5bo@4ax.com> <389ED8C3.C979477A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <87qpql025i7@enews2.newsguy.com> <87s1d4$mjb$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald,
  Although I agree with you (at least somewhat) on this, there is nothing NEW
in this problem.  You can't "blindly" substitute CALLed sub-programs from
COBOL vendor to vendor either.  I don't see anything new in the "problem" in
the Standard. (FYI, there was an "interpretation" which said this about the
'85 Standard.  In the  revision it is explicit that CALLs between
two-different vendors' COBOL systems *may* be treated as if they were
inter-language calls.)

ON THE OTHER HAND,  I think (but won't swear to it) that if two
implementations did stick to a CORBA (or similar de facto or de jure
Standard) for their interfaces, then I think that you probably COULD "plug
and play" with objects (whether they were COBOL, C++, or whatever).
```

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> ON THE OTHER HAND,  I think (but won't swear to it) that if two
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Klein

CORBA (common object request Broker ??something) is a IP method of
communicating with object and a text language that is interpreted
before use on a specific platform (precompiler of cobol and one for
C++ for example).

This helps the situation immensely but there is nothing like a
standard library that you can rely on.  Could you use a cobol compiler
without a string verb?  Yes but you would have to change code.

Common objects is one thread of OO but certainly not the reason to go
to OO.  Well designed 'standard' libraries are excellent for this
without the learning curve.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Centralized systems

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87tfju$3ge$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38983C3A.17105521@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <n5um9sgbnb867di97m58bohhg2eu7sc5bo@4ax.com> <389ED8C3.C979477A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <87qpql025i7@enews2.newsguy.com> <87s1d4$mjb$1@news.igs.net> <87s2k5$48j$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <38A2198A.618E3DC1@zip.com.au>`

```
A common library is an identified "need" for COBOL's OO support.  It is
currently identified as the "highest priority" (for a Technical Report) after
this revision "gets out".

PERSONALLY, I would guess that getting the "base language support" out first
will allow for a BETTER class library to be developed (based on "real world"
experiences with the Standard language features).  However, I also understand
that some people think that adding OO syntax without a class library is like
buying a car in an environment where gas doesn't exist.

One thing that I would "urge" readers of this NG to think about (especially
those interested in OO - and those outside the US) it to insure (via your WG4
"national body representative") that the ISO work item for a Class Library
ACTUALLY gets started.  I have heard people talking about it for several
years - and so far haven't seen any real evidence that it is progressing.
(This may be my own ignorance of ISO processing, but is an issue that I think
non-US countries can escalate.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Centralized systems

```
Jeff Baynard wrote:
>
>People all think differently.  That's the inherent problem with developing
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>something to be said for simplicity.

There is a common three stage pattern for how a given new technology
develops:

    1. First form: Simple, crude, inefficient design.

    2. Second form: Complex, more efficient design.

    3. Final form: Simple, efficient, correct design.

If the technology is still (relatively) highly complex, it isn't done
yet.  Judging by this, OO would seem to be about step 1.5: Complex,
crude, inefficient. :-)
```

<a id="t-77a1f29fdc"></a>
## Thread: Unstructured Programming in COBOL — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Unstructured Programming in COBOL

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<879g6q$fhe$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Hi:

I read the post by RMP Consulting Partners LLC about structured
programming and thought I would say something.

Surely, the objective of application development is to produce
a totally dependable, self-documented, easy-to-learn, easy-to-use,
efficient and economical-to-operate software system to accomplish
a particular task.  The programming style should be subordinate to
the objective.

I have been an active programmer for almost 40 years and can still
turn out a couple of hundred lines of debugged code in a day. I have
never
embraced the structured style although I experimented with it in
the sixties. I have programmed in many languages on many different
platforms. I have used PL/I, ALGOL, FORTRAN, various assembler
languages, RPG and FARGO(!), etc. but my overall favorite which I
use to this day is MicroFocus COBOL with Multi-Key ISAM (Using
the truly marvelous FLEXUS GUI) (Who needs CICS?). My programs use
GO TO, PERFORMS and all those other things which are despised by
the structured devotees (although ALTER is never used).  Furthermore,
I use a subset of COBOL consisting of only the barest essentials.
I never use EVALUATE or any of the modern stuff. (There is one
very important benefit to this approach and that is that the COBOL
code is highly portable and can be converted to another compiler not
only in a relatively short time but with a high degree of confidence
that the converted code will not contain mysterious bugs caused
by the use of 'obscure' coding methods. SORT and REPORT-WRITER
are NEVER used.)

The problem which structured programming attempts to address is
poor programming when, in actuality, poor programming arises from
poor design. Furthermore, sloppy, horrible code can be, and is,
written in any language.

In the olden days, there were programmers and coders. The programmer
DESIGNED the program and the coder CODED FROM THE FLOWCHART.

Unfortunately, programming is not taught anywhere that I am aware of.
People go to school to learn a programming language, and, having taken
the course, it is assumed that since they 'know' the language they
know how to program. Knowing COBOL or any other language does not
mean that one knows how to program. Getting a 'Computer Science'
degree, in my opinion, is totally useless for a COBOL application
programmer.

If a program is PROPERLY designed by someone who knows what to do,
there is no reason why the program can not be written in a non-
structured style. Although efficiency in software is a largely
forgotten and irrelevant consideration, the purist should derive
satisfaction from designing a program which considers every
logical eventuality, and is a model of efficiency and clarity.
This can be done, but not by many and not by anyone without a
LOT of experience.

The reason most software is so horrible is that it is written
by people with no or little experience. They start to write something
without really knowing how to do it, encounter problems, devise
crude solutions which complicate it further and end up with a shaky
liability. Windows is a perfect example of software written
by amateurs.

Perhaps, every program should be written twice: the first
time to find out what the problems are and the second to write it
properly.

As a commercial-application programmer, I rarely write programs from
scratch. In the world of commercial data-processing there are two
primary types of programs: File-Maintenance programs and 'Report'
programs. The former let users add, change, delete, records from
files and may provide other basic functions pertaining to the
data. 'Report' programs read files sequentially and produce some
output; normally a report or display.  I have hired and supervised
trainee programmers who could 'write' programs of this type using
skeleton-program generators which produce the efficient type of
code mentioned above. Virtually all systems can be developed using
only programs of these two types.  I wonder if someone could come
up with a structured File-Maintenance skeleton-generator?

The biggest single factor in the design of ANY system is the
design of the DATA FILES to be used. Poor file-design will lead
to amateurish solutions to problems arising because of the poor
design. (And, no, I don't leave 'gaps' in my level numbers!).

My objective during my programming career has been to design
and program applications which require no external documentation,
are completely self-explanatory, are totally dependable and do
NOT require telephone support. I am happy to say that I have
succeeded and I did (and still do it) in my unstructured way.

Anthony Dilworth

p.s. there are many varieties of spaghetti.






Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

Where have you been? There are hundreds of companies doing just this. I work
for one such company and would be delighted to tell you all about it, but
not in the newgroup.

<<SNIP>>


> p.s. there are many varieties of spaghetti.

A long time ago we had a discussion in R&D about being able to produce
programs in a variety of coding styles. We developed the concept of 'pasta
programming' and decided the optimum was ravioli.

spaghetti - a mess
spaghetti and meatballs - a mess with a few meaty bits worth keeping
lasagna - way too structured with performs of performs of performs of
paragraphs
fetuccini - kind of like spaghetti but a little more manageable
fetuccini alfredo - you think it's manageable until your arteries clog
ravioli - manageable bite-size pieces with substance in the middle

-- Karl Wagner

>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Unstructured Programming in COBOL

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38985021.D856C72A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<879g6q$fhe$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:

> In the olden days, there were programmers and coders. The programmer
> DESIGNED the program and the coder CODED FROM THE FLOWCHART.

Or from pseudo code.  The coder was just a clerk, and nobody wanted to be
a coder.  Now we're all programmers who can type, so the flow chart is
pretty much obsolete.

> The reason most software is so horrible is that it is written
> by people with no or little experience. They start to write something
> without really knowing how to do it, encounter problems, devise
> crude solutions which complicate it further and end up with a shaky
> liability.

I think the reason most software is so horrible is that it was designed,
written, and then changed a zillion times with changing business needs.
Unstructured programs have unstructured modifications all over the place,
and the concepts which flowed out of the original programmer's head have
been obscured by more unstructured code.  Neither the trees nor the forest
are really visible anymore.

So the goal in making better software is to write in such a way that the
changing requirements can be integrated in a readable, accurate
technique.  That is why structured programming is useful.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Unstructured Programming in COBOL

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87f3ah$g1f$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<879g6q$fhe$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38985021.D856C72A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

> Foodman wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Or from pseudo code.  The coder was just a clerk, and nobody wanted
to be
> a coder.  Now we're all programmers who can type, so the flow chart is
> pretty much obsolete.

We may all be able to type but that does not mean that we know how
to program. You suggest that one just sits down and starts merrily
typing and it all comes out alright in the end? I hope
that newcomers to programming are not led to believe that the
'flow chart is dead'. Certainly, a flow chart or its logical
equivalent should be the first thing prepared before typing.

One can learn programming without knowing a programming language by
drawing flow charts so long as one knows the fundamental capabilities
of the machine and the requirements of the application.

Tony

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unstructured Programming in COBOL

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389B1BAE.347225C4@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<879g6q$fhe$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38985021.D856C72A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <87f3ah$g1f$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
> We may all be able to type but that does not mean that we know how
> to program. You suggest that one just sits down and starts merrily
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> equivalent should be the first thing prepared before typing.

My statement you quoted was more of an observation than a recommendation,
but...

It depends on the complexity.  It has been 20 years since I have seen
anybody create a flow chart before writing a simple extract, match, and
report program.  (in those days people flow charted each if, etc.).  I still
see (and write and use) data flow diagrams, batch dependency diagrams, etc.
And of course, flow charts can be a wonderful debugging tool.

I have seen similar overly detailed pseudo code as near as 10 years ago,
only because I have been primarily a consultant who has worked in many
shops.

Where there is a standard stand-alone program, the structured programming
does the same thing as the old style flow chart.

> One can learn programming without knowing a programming language by
> drawing flow charts so long as one knows the fundamental capabilities
> of the machine and the requirements of the application.

No argument there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unstructured Programming in COBOL

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87fg3s$1mk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<879g6q$fhe$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38985021.D856C72A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <87f3ah$g1f$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:

: We may all be able to type but that does not mean that we know how
: to program. You suggest that one just sits down and starts merrily
: typing and it all comes out alright in the end? I hope
: that newcomers to programming are not led to believe that the
: 'flow chart is dead'. Certainly, a flow chart or its logical
: equivalent should be the first thing prepared before typing.

I used to do flowcharts a few decades ago, then structure
diagrams then I moved to pseudo-code.

However, I soon realised that when I used interactive text editors,
such as WordStar and such in the late 70s, that I was keying the
pseudo-code into the editor (rather than scribbling it down on paper)
and then revising it until it would work correctly and then going back
over it converting the pseudo-code into Cobol.

I found that by using Cobol _as_ the pseudo-code language I could
save a step and arrive at correctly working programs a good deal
faster.

I would agree, however, that each new programmer should go through
the process of learning logic through flowcharts, structure
diagrams and pseudo-code (or other equivalents) until they are
proficients in these and the language(s) they are using.  It is
a matter of abstraction.  I can abstract in Cobol because I no
longer need to think about the language (or those parts that I
actually use).  If a new programmer tried to use this technique
they would get distracted by the detailed usage of the language.
Pseudo-code is supposed to be simple and flexible so that one
can make it do whatever one wishes and then worry about 
implementing each item one at a time.
```

>Surely, the objective of application development is to produce
>a totally dependable, self-documented, easy-to-learn, easy-to-use,

It is good if the code can be understood by another if errors do arise
and/or enhancements are required. I assume that your style would
be consistent within a program or system.

>I have been an active programmer for almost 40 years and can still
>turn out a couple of hundred lines of debugged code in a day. I have

>platforms. [...]

in-line SORT. Otherwise, I agree with the approach of using only a
subset of the language.

[...]

program design, that you have realized that you have been doing
that for years (without the almost religious fanaticism).

It's good to hear from another programmer from the 60's or
earlier.

Kindrick       (IBM 650, '57)

- **Message-ID:** `<A4169DF2C6DB0F08.F056A108D67A8EBD.282B35A7547017D5@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<879g6q$fhe$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Anthony,

Along with some other interesting observations, you wrote:

> 
> In the world of commercial data-processing there are two
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> output; normally a report or display. 

I would add a third archetype:  the 'Posting' program (e.g., interface
Accounts Payable to General Ledger).  Posts are often structured like a
Report program, but the main function is to update one or more files. 
Typically a report is also produced (the posting journal), but it is
secondary to the main update function. 
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Unstructured Programming in COBOL

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87f27q$fbh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<879g6q$fhe$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <A4169DF2C6DB0F08.F056A108D67A8EBD.282B35A7547017D5@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Hi David:

The example you give is really another 'Report' program. Read the
A/P file sequentially, post directly via ISAM to the other files
and print a report. The sequential reading of the A/P file is
the 'logical process' which is the heart of the program and which makes
it fit into the 'Report' category. Maybe 'Report Program' is not a good
name for these; perhaps 'Sequential-Process Program' might
be a better name instead. (Besides, we need more things to make
acronyms from).

tony

In article
<A4169DF2C6DB0F08.F056A108D67A8EBD.282B35A7547017D5@lp.airnews.net>,
  "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com> wrote:
> Anthony,
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Accounts Payable to General Ledger).  Posts are often structured like
a
> Report program, but the main function is to update one or more files.
> Typically a report is also produced (the posting journal), but it is
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unstructured Programming in COBOL

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<07E77CA3EADEAFC0.4C868DD19C4CF535.B71432381F2FEB38@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<879g6q$fhe$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <A4169DF2C6DB0F08.F056A108D67A8EBD.282B35A7547017D5@lp.airnews.net> <87f27q$fbh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi Tony:

You're right--the essential logic driving a report and 'post' is the
same, but I always mentally separate the two.  I can run a 'report' over
and over without harm, but I can only run a 'post' once.  If we're going
to invent acronyms we could keep things simple:  FM (file maintenance)
and SP (sequential process)   :-)



Foodman wrote:
>

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

site, http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html

This tutorial will explain to you the format of  "COBOL produced" data; it

something called the "COBOL File Description," which should be available
from the publisher of the software which produced the data.

#### ↳ newbie seeks help

- **From:** "joseph archibald" <joe88@waitrose.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87a0nd$68n$1@gxsn.com>`
- **References:** `<IRXl4.1091$0Z5.13768@news.swbell.net>`

```
Hi
I'm studying COBOL programming and so far in my course have not been too
troubled, however I now have some problems. I think the best way to make
clear my situation is to list the question and then to stick my own
questions at the bottom. So here goes:

A program is required to validate batches of transactions that are held on a
disk file. Each batch should consist of up to 100 transaction records
followed by a batch control record. Each field of the transaction record
needs to be validated and hash totals of all the numeric fields calculated.

If the accumulated hash totals match those of the batch control record and
there are no invalid records and the number of transaction records does not
exceed 100, then the batch is to be classed as valid. All valid records are
to be written to a valid file. An erroneous batch is to be printed out on an
error report which contains the appropriate headings, with each batch
starting on a new page. It is also required that the accumulated hash totals
for that batch are printed after each invalid batch. A separate disk file is
to be used as temporary storage of a batch until the validity of the batch
is ascertained.

Now I think that the main part of the question which I find confusing is the
last sentence - the separate disk file. It says that all valid records are
to be written to a valid file. Would this mean the records have to be
validated before sending them to the separate disk file or is this valid
file another file altogether? And do the erroneous records have to be
written to this separate disk file before being printed or are they printed
straight from the validation procedures?
If both the erroneous records (batches) and the valid records (batches) are
to be written to this separate disk file temporarily, how (and why, for that
matter) are they re-identifiied as erroneous or as valid so they can then be
either printed or sent on their way to the valid file?

I guess because I'm a novice at programming that I just don't understand the
uses of the various file types in this question.

Any help would be truly appreciated! I've been pouring over this for weeks
now.

Very best regards
Joseph Archibald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: newbie seeks help

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87a3tg$ekg$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<IRXl4.1091$0Z5.13768@news.swbell.net> <87a0nd$68n$1@gxsn.com>`

```
I think you have analyzed the problem pretty well and am happy to give you
some clues of how I would approach the problem.  I must start by saying that
this is MUCH easier in a "homework" problem (like yours) then it would be in
the "real world".  In your case, you are told that you can never have more
than 100 records in a "batch".  Keeping 100 records in storage (in your
Working-Storage Section) is fairly "reasonable" to do.  On the other hand, if
this were a "real world" problem where you could have an "undefined" number
of records per batch, another technique would probably be better.

The following "suggestion" will ONLY work if you have already gotten to the
part of your course dealing with "tables" in COBOL.  If you don't know how to
process those (yet), then you will need to use some other technique.

Pseudo-code:
Do while any more records

  Initialize table and hash accumulator

  Do while more records in batch
     read record into table
     add this records value to accumulated hash counter
  End-Do

  Read batch-record
  Compare batch total with accumulated total

  If totals are equal
     write out all records from table to "good" file
  Else
    write out all records from table to "bad" file
  end-if

End-do
```

#### ↳ Re: What format does Cobol write its data files in?

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e7hDRWmb$GA.274@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<IRXl4.1091$0Z5.13768@news.swbell.net>`

```
Whatever format the OS provides for files to be written in.

OS/390 uses QSAM, VSAM, etc.
Other OSes use their own file formats.

Please provide information on your platform, OS, and compiler (with release
numbers).


John <etips@swbell.net> wrote in message
news:IRXl4.1091$0Z5.13768@news.swbell.net...

it?
> Any ideas?
> Thanks
>
>
```

<a id="t-7557558304"></a>
## Thread: Passing tables in Linkage Section — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Passing tables in Linkage Section

- **From:** "Steven Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@flash.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9p0m4.3567$wR.206750@news.flash.net>`

```
When passing a non-fixed-size table to a CALLed program how should it be
declared in the LINKAGE SECTION?

Lets say  you want to pass this to a CALLed program

01  STAT-DATA.
    05  STAT-DATA-ITEMS    PIC  9(3).
    05  STAT-DATA-TABLE     OCCURS 1 TO 999 TIMES
                                                    DEPENDING ON
STAT-DATA-ITEMS.
          10  DATA-VALUE        PIC S9(3)V99.
          10  VALUE-SUB-MEAN    PIC  S9(4)V99.
          10  VAL-SUB-MEAN-SQUARED   PIC  9(7)V99.

Would you declare it the same way in LINKAGE or would you use a REDEFINES?

Steve Kalemkiewicz
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing tables in Linkage Section

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87a44k$g3n$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9p0m4.3567$wR.206750@news.flash.net>`

```
I would use the same definition in both calling and called program.  There
are a couple of questions/issues however,

1) Is STAT-DATA-ITEMS   "filled in" by the calling or called program?  If it
is the calling program, then everything should be "fine" every time.  If it
is the called program, then SOME compilers may have problems with it.

2) IBM in their "early" '85 Standard compilers did NOT handle cases where an
ODO was filled in by the called program.  Instead, they tended to "abend".
ANSI "clarified" that this wasn't Standard conforming, but I can imagine some
compilers still having problems with this.
```

A very basic question:  what is the significance of Friday, 14 October
1582 that it should be "day 1" of the Lillian day count?  It sounds about
right for the introduction of the Gregorian calendar - presumably in Rome
since the English speaking countries didn't adopt it until 17-something
and the Orthodox world has never completely adopted it.  Going further
east, of course, Buddhist countries didn't have any "Year 2000" excitement
either.  Whichever - it's a pretty parochial scheme. ;-)
```
