- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38b5f6f3_2@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<ep7m8s0j268sehc15hoi9ejvnvtv484tha@4ax.com> <20000125035025.07131.00000179@ng-ck1.aol.com> <h6hr8s0nrib4o56qm0ctiuhh45csmbiso4@4ax.com> <388F0520.45B58AC1@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <1r4v8s49aqv044ooq0sreclpoahf7sapvm@4ax.com> <B8kk4.16789$ln.1163231@news4.mia> <j6qh9s077l1uau1covtv8hdi27r90dgu39@4ax.com> <38B5F0E6.A737CF55@home.com>`

```

David A. Cobb <superbiskit@home.com> wrote in message
news:38B5F0E6.A737CF55@home.com...
> A very basic question:  what is the significance of Friday, 14 October
> 1582 that it should be "day 1" of the Lillian day count?  It sounds about
> right for the introduction of the Gregorian calendar

 History of the Calendar
The history of the calendar is fascinating.  The year of a planet is the
time it takes to orbit the Sun.  There are, however, different types of
year.  The time it takes the Earth to make one complete revolution with
respect to the distant stars is called the sidereal year (deriving from the
Latin word sidus, which meant constella-tion).  The sidereal year  (for
1950) is equal to 365.25636566 days or 31,558,149.993 seconds.
The time it takes for the Earth to return to the same point in its seasonal
cycle (which is what counts as far as the calendar is concerned) is called
the tropical year (after the Greek verb trepein, which means to turn).  The
tropical year is equal to 365.24219342 days or 31,556,925.511 seconds being
about 20 m 25 s shorter than the sidereal year.  The modern definition of a
second is the duration of 9,192,631,770 cycles of the radiation emitted by
the transition between the two hyperfine levels of the ground state of the
133Cs atom.
The tropical year is shorter than the sidereal year because of the
precession of the Earth's axis.  Precession is the motion of the axis of a
spinning top.  The Earth, like a top, has an equatorial bulge caused by the
centrifugal force of its rotation - the equatorial radius is 21.360 km
longer than the polar radius.  This bulge experiences a torque applied by
the gravitational influence of the Moon, the Sun, and the planets.  The
result is a precessional motion of the Earth's axis.  This was discovered by
Hipparchos about 150 BC.
The astronomical motions of the Earth and the Moon provide the basis for the
calendar - the subdivision of time into years, months, weeks, and days.  The
problem with these subdivisions is that they are based on phenomena that
have different periods - the revolution of the Earth around the Sun, the
revolution of the Moon around the Earth, and the rotation of the Earth.  The
periods are not commensurate: there are about 29.53 days in a lunar month,
4.22 weeks in a lunar month, and 365.24 days in a tropical year.  The
development of an accurate calendar has been a vexing problem since
antiquity (the name calendar derives from the Latin name kalendae for the
day of the new Moon, marking the beginning of the lunar month).
The Egyptians started their year in the latter part of July, when the Nile
began flooding the delta.  This was a big event, because the flooding
fertilized the delta.  The Egyptians divided the year into 12 months of 30
days each, giving a total of 360 days, to which they added an extra 5 days
at the end.  In the years around 4241 BC, the Egyptians noticed that the
rising of the Nile coincided with the day when the star Sirius (known as
Sothis) became again visible in the southern sky as seen from the capital
city of Memphis.
The Roman author Censorinus reported in his work De die natali (written in
238 AD and dedicated to his patron on his birthday) that in 139 AD the
rising of Sothis had occurred on July 20.  Now, the Egyptian year of 365
days was shorter by 0.242 day than the tropical year.  As a result, the
Egyptian calendar fell behind by 1 day in 4 years, making one full circle
(called the Sothis cycle) in 4x365 = 1,460 years.  Because the rising of
Sirius at Memphis and the beginning of flooding of the delta coincide only
once in 1,460 years, the Egyptians must have developed their calendar at one
of the following times: 1460 - 139 = 1321 BC, or 1321 + 1460 = 2781 BC, or
2781 + 1460 = 4241 BC...  This last date is the most probable, because the
calendar was already in use about 3000 BC.
The early Greeks adopted the Egyptian calendar, but in the fifth century BC,
the oktaeteris was introduced, a period of 8 years in which each year
consisted of 6 lunar months of 30 days and 6 lunar months of 29 days, plus 1
lunar month of 30 days in 3 out of the 8 years.  The total number of days
was thus 2,922, giving an average of exactly 365.25 days per year.  The
Greeks set the beginning of the year at either the summer or winter solstice
(depending on which Greek city).
According to legend, Rome was founded by Romulus, its first king, on April
21, 753 BC.  At that time, the Romans had a calendar of 10 months (Martius
after Mars, the god of war, Aprilis, after Apru, the Etruscian goddess of
love, Maius, after Maia, eldest daughter of At-las, Junius, after Juno, wife
of Jupiter, Quin-tilis, Sextilis, September, October, November, and
December, the fifth month through the tenth month, of which four had 31 days
and the other six had 30 days.  In addition, there were two unnamed months
each in the winter.
The second king of Rome, Numa Pompilius (715-672 BC), is said to have named
the two winter months Ianuarius, after Janus, the two-faced god of gates,
and Febriarius, after Februa, the day of purification (the 15th) and to have
moved the beginning of the year to January 1.  In the reform recommended by
the Alexandrine astronomer Sosigenes and promulgated by Julius Caesar (100 -
44 BC), the year remained equal to 365.25 days, by having 365 days per year,
but adding to the month of February one day every fourth year (the leap
year).  In addition, the names Quintilis and Sextilis were changed to July
and August to honour Julius Caesar and his adopted son Augustus, and both
given a length of 31 days, together with December which also got 31 days.
Poor February was used as a source for extra days.  The first Julian year
began on January 1, 709 AUC (Ab Urbe Condita, i.e. since the founding of the
City), which translates into January 1, 45 BC.
In 526 AD, the Byzantine emperor Justinian asked a monk named Dionysius
Exiguus to change the calendar, reckoning time no longer from the founding
of Rome, but from the birth of Jesus Christ.  Dionysius figured that Jesus
had been born 753 years after the founding of Rome, apparently not knowing
that Herod, under whom Jesus was born, had died 749 years AUC.  Accordingly,
Jesus had to be born at least four years earlier than the good monk thought.
This error was discovered long after the Justinian Calendar had been adopted
by all the Christian nations of Europe.
To complicate things, some recent research indicates that Jesus Christ was
crucified on April 3, 33 AD.  Because He was 33 years old when He died, His
birth could be fixed at the end of the year 1 BC or at the beginning of the
year 1 AD (there is no year zero), four years after Herod had died.  At this
point we notice that there are discrepancies among the four gospels.  Most
important, the earliest gospel, Mark, says nothing about the birth of Christ
or about Herod, and neither does John.  It is thus possible that Dionysius
Exiguus made no mistake after all and that the birth of Christ took place at
the beginning of year 1, after the death of Herod.  In any case, we still
reckon the years from the fix provided by Dionysius Exiguus.  May his soul
rest in peace.
The number of days in a tropical year, as fixed by Julius Caesar's reform is
not quite exact - it is too large by 0.0078 days = 11.23 minutes.  By the
middle of the sixteenth century, the calendar was off by 11.7 days with
respect to the seasons.  Pope Gregory XIII ordered that the day after
Thursday, October 4, 1582, should be henceforth Friday, October 15, 1582; in
addition, from that year on, centennial years should not be leap years, even
though divisible by 4, unless divisible by 400.  In a 400-year cycle,
therefore, there would be (365x400) + 97 = 146097 days, equal to 365.242
days per year.
The Gregorian calendar was not the work of Gregory XIII (just as the Julian
calendar was not the work of Julius Caesar), but of the Jesuit priest
Christopher Clavius (after whom a prominent crater on the Moon is named) and
the Neapolitan astronomer and physician Luigi Lillio Ghiraldi.  The new
calendar was soon adopted in all Catholic countries, but only in 1752 in
England and the British Empire (by which time they were 12 days behind), and
only in 1918 in Russia, when that country was 13 days behind - which is why
the October Revolution was celebrated in November.  Lillian days, named
after Luigi Lillio, are simply days counted from the start of the Gregorian
reform.
In the same year, 1582, that Pope Gregory XIII promulgated his reform of the
calendar, the scholar Joseph Scaliger (1540-1609) devised the Julian period,
which he named after his father, Julius Caesar Scaliger.  The Julian period
is a period of 7,980 years that is obtained by multiplying three cycles, the
solar (28y), Metonic (19y), and indictio (15y) cycles: 28x19x15 = 7,980.
The solar cycle is a period of 28 years that derives from the fact that in
365 days there are 52 weeks + 1/7 of a week.  In successive years,
therefore, the same day of the year will fall on the following day of the
week.  Were it not for leap years, in which the day of the week advances by
two, the cycle would be completed in 7 years.  It is instead completed in
7x4 = 28 years.  The Metonic cycle (discovered by the Greek astronomer Meton
in the fifth century BC) is a period of time that is divisible into both a
whole number of years and a whole number of lunar months; it is equal to 19
years and includes 235 lunar months.  The indictio cycle is an ancient
Egyptian cycle, that beginning with January 1, 313 AD, was adopted by the
emperor Constantine as the Roman taxation cycle.
Scaliger adopted the three cycles not for astronomical reasons, but because
they were already used in Hellenistic, Roman, and Byzantine calendars.
Scaliger set the begin-ning of the current Julian period at January 1 at
12:00 noon Greenwich time, 4713 BC.  Reck-oning from noon is advantageous in
astronomy because most astronomical observations are made at night, meaning
that the observations from one night are not divided into separate days (at
least in Europe).  Days are counted in continuity from that date.  The
Julian period is important, because astronomers, even today, use it to
record the dates of celestial phenom-ena.  Thus January 1, 2000, at 00:00h,
will be Julian date (JD) 2,451,544.50.  Often the term Julian day is used to
mean the day count within a given year.  This is a confusing prac-tice, that
unfortunately has taken root, and is here to stay.
The seven day week dates from antiquity - it appears in Genesis, for
instance.  It may have originated from the observation that the lunar cycle,
29.5 days long, exhibits four phases - new Moon, half Moon, full Moon, half
Moon.  Each phase, therefore, lasting about 7 days.  In fact, in many
languages the word for week derives from the word for seven (e.g. ebdomaz in
Greek, septimana in Latin).  Noticing, in addition, that there were seven
heavenly bodies, the ancients related each body to a day of the week: Sun
for Sunday, Moon for Monday, Mars for Tuesday (dies Marties in Latin) where
the Norse god Tir takes the place of Mars in the Germanic languages, Mercury
for Wednesday (dies Mercurii), and so on.
A lot of the history of the calendar can be found back in the various
constants and algorithms used by CALRPK.

http://www.etk.com/download/etkpak/etkpak.htm#CALRPK
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Lillian Date Question (Was: Leap Seconds and COBOL)

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<894uof$1o8s$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<ep7m8s0j268sehc15hoi9ejvnvtv484tha@4ax.com> <20000125035025.07131.00000179@ng-ck1.aol.com> <h6hr8s0nrib4o56qm0ctiuhh45csmbiso4@4ax.com> <388F0520.45B58AC1@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <1r4v8s49aqv044ooq0sreclpoahf7sapvm@4ax.com> <B8kk4.16789$ln.1163231@news4.mia> <j6qh9s077l1uau1covtv8hdi27r90dgu39@4ax.com> <38B5F0E6.A737CF55@home.com>`

```

David A. Cobb <superbiskit@home.com> wrote in message
news:38B5F0E6.A737CF55@home.com...
> A very basic question:  what is the significance of Friday, 14 October
> 1582 that it should be "day 1" of the Lillian day count?  It sounds about
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> either.  Whichever - it's a pretty parochial scheme. ;-)


By 1582, the Spring Equinox fell on Mar. 11 (10 days slow), so Pope Gregory
XIII established  "NS" calendar.  Luigi Lilio proposed the new calendar.
Thu., Oct. 4 was followed by Fri., Oct. 15.  That makes Oct. 15, 1582 day 1
of the Lilian day count.  England & the colonies made up for the 11 days
difference in 1752 and Wed., Sep. 2 followed by Thu., Sep 14 that year.  By
the year 3200, the calendar is estimated to be 1 full day "slow" again.  I
don't plan on being around to see if they decide to "skip" a leap day or
what. :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Lillian Date Question (Was: Leap Seconds and COBOL)

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2000-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B615A0.4E5605B9@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<ep7m8s0j268sehc15hoi9ejvnvtv484tha@4ax.com> <20000125035025.07131.00000179@ng-ck1.aol.com> <h6hr8s0nrib4o56qm0ctiuhh45csmbiso4@4ax.com> <388F0520.45B58AC1@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <1r4v8s49aqv044ooq0sreclpoahf7sapvm@4ax.com> <B8kk4.16789$ln.1163231@news4.mia> <j6qh9s077l1uau1covtv8hdi27r90dgu39@4ax.com> <38B5F0E6.A737CF55@home.com> <894uof$1o8s$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```


Terry Heinze wrote:
...
> By 1582, the Spring Equinox fell on Mar. 11 (10 days slow), so Pope Gregory
> XIII established  "NS" calendar.  Luigi Lilio proposed the new calendar.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> what. :)

Actually 3200 is approximately the point (the closest multiple of 400
years) where the error will exceed 0.5 days and hence be time for a one
day jump to keep the calendar rounded to the nearest day.  The error is
approximately 1 day in 3200 years or 0.5 day in 1600 years and things
were in sync after the correction in 1582 (approximately 1600).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Lillian Date Question (Was: Leap Seconds and COBOL)

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8957le$iup$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ep7m8s0j268sehc15hoi9ejvnvtv484tha@4ax.com> <20000125035025.07131.00000179@ng-ck1.aol.com> <h6hr8s0nrib4o56qm0ctiuhh45csmbiso4@4ax.com> <388F0520.45B58AC1@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <1r4v8s49aqv044ooq0sreclpoahf7sapvm@4ax.com> <B8kk4.16789$ln.1163231@news4.mia> <j6qh9s077l1uau1covtv8hdi27r90dgu39@4ax.com> <38B5F0E6.A737CF55@home.com>`

```
Although not asked in this thread, I thought I would repeat (?) why COBOL
uses Jan 1, 1601 for INTEGER-OF-DAY/DATE - instead of the "correct" Lillian
date.  This is because that was a SUNDAY - and therefore, the remainder when
dividing INTEGER-OF-DATE/DAY by 7 gives you the same number as ACCEPT FROM
DAY-OF-WEEK (with the minor problem of 7 versus 0)

Also, for the IBM mainframers among you, LE does use the "true" Lillian date,
but there is an option to "switch" to a COBOL-compatible number.
```

<a id="t-484c97bb4d"></a>
## Thread: Flushing the Perform Buffer — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Flushing the Perform Buffer

- **From:** steven_jones@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87cnoa$r0r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I am several Perform levels deep in a Cobol program.
I encounter conditional logic such that I do not wish to
return back up the Perform tree ... rather I just want to
do a "Go To" some paragraph or section and forget that the Performs
ever happened.

We are using Micro Focus Cobol ... a variety of versions and platforms.

Is there a way to just 'flush' the Perform buffer and convince the
program that these Performs never happened ?

Thanks !

sj


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Flushing the Perform Buffer

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87cpgr$sbi$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<87cnoa$r0r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <87cnoa$r0r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  steven_jones@my-deja.com wrote:
> I am several Perform levels deep in a Cobol program.
> I encounter conditional logic such that I do not wish to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> We are using Micro Focus Cobol ... a variety of versions and
platforms.
>
> Is there a way to just 'flush' the Perform buffer and convince the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Issuing a "GO TO" out of a PERFORMED paragraph to a label outside of
the scope of the PERFORM, regardless of the level, IMHO is a big NO-
NO ! Can you say "Spaghetti Code" ? You should always "Bubble" up/back
to the original logic and proceed from there.

Just my .02 cents....

Chaio,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Flushing the Perform Buffer

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87coup$q58$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<87cnoa$r0r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Simple answer is "no" there is no way to GUARANTEE that what you want to
happen will happen.

Practical answer -
  If you set a switch to indicate that you want to "get out quick" - Micro
Focus supports the "EXIT PARAGRAPH" statement and you could test that switch
and when it is set, exit each level of the PERFORM structure.

Possible answer -
  If you never use another "go to" to enter one of those paragraphs again
(but instead only enter them thru EXPLICIT PERFORM statements - and never
"fall-thru") then you probably will NOT "notice" that you haven't reset the
PERFORM buffers.  ON THE OTHER HAND, this is exactly what is meant by
"spaghetti code" and will be a nightmare to maintain.
```

#### ↳ Re: Flushing the Perform Buffer

- **From:** Joseph Kohler <joe_kohler@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3899E717.6327BE92@yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<87cnoa$r0r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


steven_jones@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> I am several Perform levels deep in a Cobol program.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You can just use NEXT SENTENCE and the program logic will continue after
the next period (.) it encounters.  Sloppy but it works.

Joe
```

#### ↳ Re: Flushing the Perform Buffer

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GcFm4.3493$Sx.62521@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<87cnoa$r0r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
steven_jones@my-deja.com wrote:
>I am several Perform levels deep in a Cobol program.
>I encounter conditional logic such that I do not wish to
>return back up the Perform tree ... rather I just want to
>do a "Go To" some paragraph or section and forget that the Performs
>ever happened.

Perhaps not exactly what you are asking for, but when I want to exit
multiple layers of PERFORM, I set a switch (ech!) which is tested after
each PERFORM, and immediately exits that paragraph when the switch is
'set'.  Messy, but it's structured, it works, and I haven't seen a way
I like better.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Flushing the Perform Buffer

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qqvo9sgot4fofs69tul4qo91rq96o3j8qq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<87cnoa$r0r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <GcFm4.3493$Sx.62521@news4.mia>`

```
On Fri, 4 Feb 2000 12:51:11 -0600 "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
wrote:

:>steven_jones@my-deja.com wrote:

:>>I am several Perform levels deep in a Cobol program.
:>>I encounter conditional logic such that I do not wish to
:>>return back up the Perform tree ... rather I just want to
:>>do a "Go To" some paragraph or section and forget that the Performs
:>>ever happened.

:>Perhaps not exactly what you are asking for, but when I want to exit
:>multiple layers of PERFORM, I set a switch (ech!) which is tested after
:>each PERFORM, and immediately exits that paragraph when the switch is
:>'set'.  Messy, but it's structured, it works, and I haven't seen a way
:>I like better.

Once one exits a PERFORMed range, or perhaps, if one GOTOs to code that also
may be invoked via a PERFORM or a PERFORM range, isn't COBOL required to cause
the EXITs to do nothing?

I would think that COBOL would require that the (return from) PERFORMs be
nullified by definition. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flushing the Perform Buffer

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87ielj$c0p$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<87cnoa$r0r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <GcFm4.3493$Sx.62521@news4.mia> <qqvo9sgot4fofs69tul4qo91rq96o3j8qq@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
  <much snippage>
> :>Perhaps not exactly what you are asking for, but when I want to exit
> :>multiple layers of PERFORM, I set a switch (ech!) which is tested after
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Once one exits a PERFORMed range, or perhaps, if one GOTOs to code that
also
> may be invoked via a PERFORM or a PERFORM range, isn't COBOL required to
cause
> the EXITs to do nothing?
>
> I would think that COBOL would require that the (return from) PERFORMs be
> nullified by definition.
>

Binyamin,
  UNFORTUNATELY, COBOL (or at least the ANSI/ISO Standard) has no such
requirement.  There is a requirement that when you EXIT PROGRAM from a called
subprogram, that PERFORM ranges are "re-set" - but there is no such rule for
when you "GO TO" out of a PERFORM RANGE.  Worse yet, there is a significant
amount of (truly UGLY spaghetti) code that actually "relies" on this.  EVEN
WORSE YET, this isn't either required OR prohibited, so the actual run-time
behavior of different compilers MAY be different in such cases (i.e. same
soruce code - different run-time results).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flushing the Perform Buffer

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87ih51$91h$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<87cnoa$r0r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <GcFm4.3493$Sx.62521@news4.mia> <qqvo9sgot4fofs69tul4qo91rq96o3j8qq@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

: Once one exits a PERFORMed range, or perhaps, if one GOTOs to code that also
: may be invoked via a PERFORM or a PERFORM range, isn't COBOL required to cause
: the EXITs to do nothing?

The EXIT statement _always_ does nothing.

The end of a PERFORM is not controlled by an EXIT, but by the
presence of the next label of the same type (ie section or
paragraph).

A GO TO out of a PERFORM range may, or may not, lead to
complete loss of control of the program or of the next
maintenance programmer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flushing the Perform Buffer

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** jraben19@mail.idt.net (Jeff Raben)
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ac243e.2612863@news.idt.net>`
- **References:** `<87ih51$91h$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
depending on the cobol...	
1)some performs actually have an implied exit posted in a cell 
so a "go to" leaves the cell ready to 'return' if you ever fall thru
the code.
2) some performs at entries to a stack 
so a "go to" leaves stuff on the stack until it overflows.
(also a nested perform may not return to the perform that is "obvious"
from the code)

both leave booby traps.

both can be fun and usefull ... but only during a full moon 
i've actually used number 1 for tracing. 

jr 
and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

#### ↳ Re: Flushing the Perform Buffer

- **From:** William Reed <adamreed@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A49C5D.19571F7E@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<87cnoa$r0r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
If there is an instance for "bailing out" of the perform several levels
deep, I would say that it's time to rethink the logic for doing those
nested performs.  Setting switches to do this is messy and inefficient and
leads to maintnance headaches(especially at 2 a.m.)

1.  Changing the nested PERFORMs to serial PERFORMs.
2.  Change the logic to add a special routine for this new instance.
3.  The program was not designed for this new instance.  Maybe it is time
for a redesign.

I have seen very few programs that have required nesting past 2 or 3 levels
deep.  More time in the design phase can lead to less time in debugging and
maintaining.  Modularity of design also makes things better.  I have
encountered few programmers that understand structured COBOL.  Most of them
were enamored with GOTOs.  To them, Top-down meant you started at the top
and ended at the bottom.

I have been programming in COBOL for over 20 years.

…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
```

<a id="t-82e04ba5d9"></a>
## Thread: Centralized systems — 2 msgs

### Re: Centralized systems

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38998B66.83AF3606@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<38983C3A.17105521@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <87af5f$1jf$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:

> What has that got to do with OO?  All the objects are mine as well. I can
> have an Isam object, for example, that not only includes all the file
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> person's code.  I can re-use my own code as well.

Sure.  The advantage of having reusable code is much smaller than advertised if
you only use your own objects though.

Still, it can be a pain with any layered technology, to drill down to where a
problem exists and fix it.  I discovered this with Paradox for Windows and C
before I ever tried OO.  Sure, COBOL doesn't eliminate this problem, but
generally, one can do a search in a COBOL source code module for a variable
string and have a high degree of confidentiality that he finds what he's looking
for.

On the other hand, the days of simple solutions are gone.  Even with a mainframe
computer with tight controls over the data of record, we have people at remote
sites entering data on PCs, cash registers, sensors, web servers, remote
databases, etc..   Monday a COBOL programmer lost a day's work here because the
remote site personnel claimed that there was no way that their new firewall made
their cash machines stop being able to communicate with the mainframe.  They
were wrong.  We don't have our simple integrated world anymore, so we have to
encapsulate procedures and data as they go back and forth.

OO is becoming more and more necessary even though it is a lot more work than
what we had before our world was so complex.  So we have to learn it.  But that
doesn't mean we should implement it for simple solutions.
```

#### ↳ Re: Centralized systems

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38AA4A34.3AF74CE@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38983C3A.17105521@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <87af5f$1jf$1@news.igs.net> <38998B66.83AF3606@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> 
> OO is becoming more and more necessary even though it is a lot more work than
> what we had before our world was so complex.  So we have to learn it.  But that
> doesn't mean we should implement it for simple solutions.

At first blush, yes it does seem like a lot of hard work. Judson touched
on development saying OO is currently at 1.5. I would suggest I started
at -2.0 and worked my way up from there !  As I use it more and more I
become daily more comfortable.

The real issue is in the planning - and that's not particularly
different from structured :-

- (a) what to store, where - DB system ( in my case flat files )
- (b) what to do with it - editing/processing programs
- (c) how to display/get data - screen-section/windowing etc. (And must 
I say it again - OO is NOT about Windows !)

The planning is of course (a) and (c) - with (b) giving the reason for
the other two to exist.

DATABASE :-

As I've indicated previously, I have a common template for flat file
handling :-

- 1. the Calling program(class) - Edit, calculate, print etc.

- 2. A DB interface, (primarily has methods to create
collections/dictionaries used for listboxes/droplists),  and,

- 3. The specific file-handler, (all the file VERBS). 

I am now able to generate a new file and all its handling in some 30
minutes (and that's assuming that the file structure is right and
holding the data I need to access).

PROCESSING :

Editing/Processing - each is unique to a specific process in an
application, but can also call (invoke) reusable code like date
checking/formatting, imperial/metric conversion etc. from pre-canned
classes.

WINDOWING :

Screening/Windowing - and this is where I started at -2.0 but I'm
getting very quickly to Judson's +1.9. Having struggled with GUI classes
and invariably doing a 'window' in a different fashion as each one came
along, I came to realize that there was much in common. (At the current
time I don't use all the gizmos but restrict myself primarily to -
entryfields, the odd text label, pushbuttons, radiobuttons, listboxes
and droplists). Thinking on it, (Ken Mullins is doing a similar thing
with Windows APIs, and some prodding from Ken Foskey), I now have a
common template (Mydialog) which generates dialogs by passing a series
of parameters from the invoking class. One routine, like file-handling ,
to cover all dialogs. The next stage is to subclass MyDialog, (which
does the bread-and-butter stuff), to handle two-dimensional entryfield
tables.

The real gain in OO COBOL will come from common classes, (and as Donald
stresses), with the ability to pass objects between say, Fujitsu and
NetExpress, or any other OO compiler. As Bill has indicated J4 has
'common classes' as a priority after the WG4/J4 get-together in Newbury
in May. This common class format is PARAMOUNT to success of OO within
COBOL. Simple example - if I can write the following in NetExpress :-

	*>--------------------------------------------------
	Method-id. "display-hello".
	*>--------------------------------------------------
	  Display "Hello World"
	End Method "display-hello".
	*>-------------------------------------------------- 

	OR :-

	*>--------------------------------------------------
	Method-id. "get-date21".
	*>--------------------------------------------------
	Linkage section.
	01 lnk-date21			pic x(21).
	Procedure Division returning lnk-date21.
           move function current-date to lnk-Date21 
	End Method "get-date21".
	*>-------------------------------------------------- 
	
then the above limited syntax, ( no 'red tape'), for portability, MUST
work in other COBOL compilers using OO.

We are very much at the beginning with OO COBOL - but as more folks get
involved, common distributed classes, (with the source), will allow us
to make speedy progression to Judson's Stage 3.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

PS: Note for Ken - you'll get source when I've figured sub-classing
tables :)
```

## Thread: Packed Data in an Ascii File — 4 msgs

### Packed Data in an Ascii File

- **From:** "Carl Lindner" <carl@bdos.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s9khr5jmdh51@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Today I received my first exposure to Cobol data files.  I received an ascii
file - via binary transfer - which contains packed fields.

The owner provided a File Description with record descriptions.  They also
provided 2 pages from Appendix D: Data types from VOS Cobol Language Manual
describing comp-3 variable length packed decimal data.

Much of the data is character and that part of it is easy.  But I have to
get those packed fields converted (is that the correct word?) to decimal
equivalents.

Is there an relatively easy way to do the conversion?

I went to the flexus site.....If I have to read Thane Hubble's book about
Teaching Myself in 24 hours I will, but will it provide the tools and info I
need?  I looked at cobodata.zip by Mattias but that would mean some brute
force method to read and rewrite the packed fields.... if I have to I
will....

Thanks for any insight.

Carl Lindner
BD Operating Systems
carl@bdos.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Packed Data in an Ascii File

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EjAm4.128$tZ6.3733@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<s9khr5jmdh51@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Carl Lindner wrote in message ...
>Today I received my first exposure to Cobol data files.  I received an
ascii
>file - via binary transfer - which contains packed fields.
>
>The owner provided a File Description with record descriptions....
> I looked at cobodata.zip by Mattias but that would mean some brute
>force method to read and rewrite the packed fields.

If you are Working under Windows/32, Mattias has a free 32-bit DLL which,
given the FD, will convert COBOL datatypes to IEEE datatypes (the types used
by all the popular non-COBOL languages and tools).

If you drop him an e-mail, he will send you a complete kit, with
documentation, test data files and two BASIC (PB/CC) programs which
demonstrate the use of the DLL. He will ask you to comment on the tool in
exchange for the use of it.

Mattias
michael.mattias@gte.net






>
```

```
No, my book will not help you much.  I speak to the topic
superficially, because the actual format of COMP-3 (and any of the non
display USAGEs) varys from compiler ot compiler.  Mattias' document is
the best, most complete source.

However, there is another option that my book should help you with.

You could use the compiler in the book to convert the records to all
text format.  That would save you the brute force format.

Any one of us here could accomplish such a conversion  (given the
record layout and all!) in a few minutes.  In fact if you want a
program to convert it, to say a comma delimited file, e-mail me.  I
should be able to do it for under $100.00, and you would have a
program that you can use in the future for other conversions.
>
```

## Thread: homework help -- PERFORM syntax — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### homework help -- PERFORM syntax

- **From:** "krgudim" <krgudim@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dxrm4.34898$up4.627619@news1.rdc1.ab.home.com>`

```
i am looking for some information and assistance to piece together the right
structure for the cobol verb PERFORM.  any help, comments, or information on
where i could go would be apreciated

i am wanting to write a simple perform statement to check against an 88
level and force the user to input a value until the right value is received.
i am using NetExpress on a w'98 box.  school books include Cobol by Stern
and Stern, as well as Cobol for Dummies (and the dummies quick reference).
after looking at the syntax and doing a bita reading and not seeing my idea
as an example, i am wondering if it is possible, or if i have to restructure
it.  am i on the right path, or way out  in left field?


in the data division, in the working storage section:

* 88 levels for flag checking
  01 flag-loop-input      pic x.
    88 iloop-continue   VALUES ARE "C", "c".
    88 iloop-cancel     VALUES ARE "C", "c".
    88 iloop-quit       VALUES ARE "Q", "q".
    88 iloop-save       VALUES ARE "S", "s".

in the procedure division,

VERIFY-DATA-AQUISITION SECTION.
      *Options for ending the data aquisition:
      * -- 1) save or canel current data input
      * -- 2) input new record or quit input

DISPLAY "(S)ave or (C)ancel current input? " AT LINE line-no COL col-no

PERFORM ACCEPT flag-loop-input
  AT LINE 0 COL 0 WITH REVERSE VIDEO
  UNTIL flag-loop-input EQUALS iloop-save OR UNTIL flag-loop-input EQUALS
iloop-cancel

IF ...
.
.


COMPUTE line-no = line-no + 1

DISPLAY "(C)ontiue  or (Q)uitl adding records? " AT LINE line-no COL col-no
...
.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

krgudim <krgudim@home.com> wrote in message
news:dxrm4.34898$up4.627619@news1.rdc1.ab.home.com...
> i am looking for some information and assistance to piece together the
right
> structure for the cobol verb PERFORM.  any help, comments, or information
on
> where i could go would be apreciated
>
> i am wanting to write a simple perform statement to check against an 88
> level and force the user to input a value until the right value is
received.
> i am using NetExpress on a w'98 box.  school books include Cobol by Stern
> and Stern, as well as Cobol for Dummies (and the dummies quick reference).
> after looking at the syntax and doing a bita reading and not seeing my
idea
> as an example, i am wondering if it is possible, or if i have to
restructure
> it.  am i on the right path, or way out  in left field?
>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> DISPLAY "(C)ontiue  or (Q)uitl adding records? " AT LINE line-no COL
col-no

Try this another way.

01 VALID-INPUT     PIC X VALUE 'N'.

PERFORM 200-GET-INPUT  UNTIL VALID-INPUT = 'Y'.
    DISPLAY "(S)ave or (C)ancel current input? " AT LINE line-no COL col-no

200-GET-INPUT.
ACCEPT FLAG-LOOP-INPUT
EVALUATE FLAG-LOOP-INPUT
    WHEN FLAG-INPUT = 'S' OR 's'
            PERFORM 300-SAVE-SEQUENCE
            MOVE 'Y' TO VALID-INPUT
    WHEN FLAG-LOOP-INPUT = 'C' OR 'c'
            PERFORM 400-CANCEL-SEQUENCE
            MOVE 'Y' TO  TO VALID-INPUT
    WHEN OTHER
            DISPLAY 'YOUR INPUT IS INCORECT PLEASE REENTER'
END-EVALUATE.

somethimg like this may just work, anyway you need to look at pages, 149 for
evaluate and pages 106 - 110 in stern & stern
its a good book, keep pluging away, and GOOD LUCK
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Sorting

- **From:** john741@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87f7cf$jcp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<87dal1$mpt$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
In article <87dal1$mpt$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>,
  Bill Tredinnick <73043.162@CompuServe.COM> wrote:
> Ignoring the COBOL Sort, is there an intrinsic function
> or a Callable Service which sorts ( elements of an
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Try this. Nested program with REPLACE. For multiple sorts I set it up
as a COPY and use COPY REPLACING ==:NAME:== BY ==progrname==.
      * :NAME:   = NAME OF PROGRAM
      * :LENGTH: = LENGTH OF EACH ENTRY
      * :KO:     = KEY OFFSET RELATIVE TO 1
      * :KL:     = KEY LENGTH
      * :COMP:   = 4 (FEWER THAN 32768 ITEMS), 8 (UP TO 2 GIG ENTRIES)
      * :A-OR-D: = < FOR ASCENDING, > FOR DESCENDING SEQUENCE
           REPLACE ==:NAME:==      BY ==RPSORT==
                   ==:LENGTH:==    BY ==47==
                   ==:KO:==        BY ==1==
                   ==:KL:==        BY ==25==
                   ==:COMP:==      BY ==4==
                   ==:A-OR-D:==    BY ==<==.
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. :NAME:.
      * COPYRIGHT 1979 BY JOHN YINGLING.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      * TREESORT3 WORKS
       01  WORKS SYNC.
           05 I                                  PIC S9(:COMP:) COMP.
           05 LS                                 PIC S9(:COMP:) COMP.
           05 MS                                 PIC S9(:COMP:) COMP.
           05 AS                                 PIC S9(:COMP:) COMP.
           05 FS                                 PIC S9(:COMP:) COMP.
           05                                    PIC X.
              88 0-W-INLOOP-DONE                 VALUE LOW-VALUES.
              88 W-INLOOP-DONE                   VALUE 'Y'.
       01  HOLD                                  PIC X(:LENGTH:).
      *---------------------------------------------------------------
       LINKAGE SECTION.
      * # OF ENTRIES IN THE TABLE
       01  MAX-SUB                       PIC S9(:COMP:) COMP.
       01  SORT-T.
      *         OCCURS IN LINKAGE NOT NECESSARY WHEN NOSSRANGE
      *         IT JUST WASTES TIME AND STORAGE FOR A BLL EVERY 4096.
           03 S OCCURS 1 TIMES
                INDEXED BY A F L M       PIC X(:LENGTH:).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING MAX-SUB SORT-T.
       MAIN SECTION.
           PERFORM SORT-IT
           GOBACK
           CONTINUE.
       SORT-IT SECTION.
      * FROM SORTING AND SORT SYSTEMS - APPENDIX B A-65
      * WITH A CORRECTION TO INITIALIZE L
           COMPUTE MS    = MAX-SUB
           COMPUTE LS    = MS
           SET M        TO MS
           SET L        TO M
           COMPUTE I   = MS / 2
           PERFORM VARYING I FROM I BY -1
                     UNTIL I < 2
               COMPUTE AS = I
               SET  A     TO AS
               MOVE S (A) TO HOLD
               PERFORM UPTREE
               IF I NOT = AS
                   MOVE HOLD TO S (A)
               END-IF
           END-PERFORM
           MOVE 1 TO I
           SET LS       TO M
           PERFORM VARYING L FROM M BY -1
                     UNTIL L < 2
               COMPUTE AS    = 1
               SET  A       TO 1
               MOVE S (1)   TO HOLD
               PERFORM UPTREE
               IF 1 NOT = AS
                   MOVE HOLD TO S (A)
               END-IF
               MOVE S (1)   TO HOLD
               MOVE S (L)   TO S (1)
               MOVE HOLD    TO S (L)
               SUBTRACT 1 FROM LS
           END-PERFORM
           CONTINUE.
      ****************************************************************
       UPTREE SECTION.
           SET 0-W-INLOOP-DONE TO TRUE
           PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER
                 UNTIL W-INLOOP-DONE
              COMPUTE FS = 2 * AS
              SET F TO FS
              IF FS < LS AND
                 S (F) (:KO:::KL:) :A-OR-D: S (F + 1) (:KO:::KL:)
                  SET F UP BY 1
                  ADD 1 TO FS
              END-IF
              IF FS <= LS AND
                 HOLD (:KO:::KL:) :A-OR-D: S (F) (:KO:::KL:)
                  MOVE S (F)          TO S (A)
                  COMPUTE AS           = FS
                  SET  A              TO F
              ELSE
                  SET W-INLOOP-DONE TO TRUE
              END-IF
          END-PERFORM
          CONTINUE.
       END PROGRAM :NAME:.
          REPLACE OFF.






Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Sorting

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I7Am4.120$tZ6.3387@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<87dal1$mpt$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Bill Tredinnick <73043.162@CompuServe.COM> wrote in message
<87dal1$mpt$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>...
>Ignoring the COBOL Sort, is there an intrinsic function
>or a Callable Service which sorts ( elements of an
>array for example )?


Huh?  Depending on your O/S and/or compiler, SORT *is* a callable service.

But...

I think Microfocus COBOL supports a COBOL SORT of a table (array) in
addition to standard COBOL sorts of files defined with SD's (i.e.,
RELEASE..RETURN or USING...GIVING).

In the mainframe environment, it's quite common to sort files outside the
COBOL program before the COBOL program ever sees the data, using DFSORT or
SYNCSORT. You can then sequentially load a table in sorted order (presumably
to facilitate binary searches).

Depending on your OS, there are expensive third party sort libraries
 SyncSort, Opt-Tech Sort) and free sorting tools written by talented and
generous developers (e.g., moi and the soon-to-be-mentioned Mr Maclendon).

You can write your own table sort using standard COBOL verbs. I think Judson
Maclendon has some examples on his web page (check one of his posts in this
forum for the URL).

Last but not least, if that's not enough to stimulate your grey matter, surf
on over to http://www.mvshelp.com and browse through the messages in the
"sort" and "programming languages" sections of the help board. That's all
mainframe, but you might get some ideas which port to other OS's.

Oh, yeah: what the hell is wrong with the COBOL sort anyway?

MCM
```

---

## Thread: Microsoft XML, Version 2 (msxml.dll) using NetExpress. — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Microsoft XML, Version 2 (msxml.dll) using NetExpress.

- **From:** "Neil Hayes" <NeilH@syspro.co.za>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38997be8$0$35291@helios.is.co.za>`

```
Hi,

I want to be able to use this dll in order to create and xml documents and
to parse xml documents via various cobol and VB COM components, but
somewhere the light bulb went out.  I struggle to find any good examples or
any examples of using Object cobol(MF) and automataion with other languages
and looking at converting vb to cobol is sometimes not fully explained.

In theory this should be just 5 or 6 lines of code:-

$set ooctrl(+p)

class-control.
myserver is class  "$OLE$Microsoft.FreeThreadedXM LDOM".

01 theServer object reference.

invoke myserver "new" returning theServer.

......etc:

Somewhere I'm going wrong or I'm using the wrong concept.

Has anyone done this ?.....

Neil
NeilH@syspro.co.za
```

#### ↳ Re: Microsoft XML, Version 2 (msxml.dll) using NetExpress.

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87c6rq$h0b$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38997be8$0$35291@helios.is.co.za>`

```
"Neil Hayes" <NeilH@syspro.co.za> wrote:
>$set ooctrl(+p)
>
>class-control.
>myserver is class  "$OLE$Microsoft.FreeThreadedXMLDOM".

The above should work (I just removed a space)...
You *do* have to have version 2.0 installed (I first tried this on one 
that didn't, and did get an error!). :)

>01 theServer object reference.
>
>invoke myserver "new" returning theServer.

This, from looking at the type library in the Type Library Wizard, should 
return an object reference (theServer) representing an instance of 
the automation interface IXMLDOMDocument. 
From there, you should be able to call the methods from that interface 
(remembering that things like BSTRs translate to PIC X(N) and other 
automation interfaces/objects as object references).

>Somewhere I'm going wrong or I'm using the wrong concept.

It seems to be a reasonable approach. What was the problem you got?

Steve.
```

#### ↳ Re: Microsoft XML, Version 2 (msxml.dll) using NetExpress.

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87cht8$i89$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38997be8$0$35291@helios.is.co.za>`

```
Hi Neil,

I have had some success using the Microsoft XML support using code along the
lines of:-

       CLASS-CONTROL.

           XMLDoc              is class "$OLE$MSXML.DOMDocument"
           OLEVariant          is class "olevar"
           XMLErr              is class "$OLE$MSXML.IXMLDOMParseError"
           olesup              is class "olesup"
           CharArray           is class "chararry"
           mfoleapi            is class "mfoleapi"


and in the procedure division:-

      ***** Create the XML DOM Object

           invoke XMLDoc "New" returning  XMLDocument

      ***** Load the document syncronously

           move OLEFALSE to async

           invoke XMLDocument "setasync" using by value async
                                         returning ws-result

           invoke OLEVariant "New" returning ws-OLEVariant

           move length of thestring to theStringLength
           invoke ws-OLEVariant "setStringAsBytes" using
                               by value     theStringLength
                               by reference theString
                   returning ws-result

      ***** Now load the document

           invoke XMLDocument "load" using by reference thestring
                                              returning ws-result

           if ws-result = OLEFALSE
               perform XML-Load-Error
           end-if

You can then access the information thats loaded.

You can use the Type Library Assistant in Net Express to generate a CPY that
shows the info you need to call the various methods.

Hope this helps.
David.

           .


Neil Hayes <NeilH@syspro.co.za> wrote in message
news:38997be8$0$35291@helios.is.co.za...

>

or
> any examples of using Object cobol(MF) and automataion with other
languages
> and looking at converting vb to cobol is sometimes not fully explained.
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
is something that I had a problem with sometime ago and discovered that it
was all based around security and the setting up of security.  When users
use the internet they basically come in anonymously and have authorization
to use data on the internet server, when you start wanting to gain access to
other servers you end up with a problem that IIS does not want to pass the
credentials of the user to the other machine.

of how it was resolved.
```

<a id="t-291dbed97b"></a>
## Thread: RANDOM function? — 2 msgs

### RANDOM function?

- **From:** Ray Guerette <rguerett@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3899C538.876066E3@earthlink.net>`

```
Has anyone used the RANDOM function?  If so, how exactly does it work?
What is meant by "sequence" of pseudo-random numbers? How is/are the
value(s) returned?  If you have a coding example, PLEASE send it my way.

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: RANDOM function?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87ci99$c7r$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3899C538.876066E3@earthlink.net>`

```
A few things to understand about the COBOL Random Intrinsic Function:

1) It returns a value between 0 and 1 (with lots of decimal points, actually
zero is possible but it is always less than 1)

2) It can be invoked (referenced) with or without a "seed-value".  The
statement about a "series" of numbers replies to the fact that if you
reference it in a loop WITHOUT a seed-value, it will return a "random" (see
below) series of numbers.

3) The actual series of numbers is called "pseudo-random".  This is because
*if* you start with the same seed value followed by the same number of
references without seed-values, then you will get the identical series of
numbers.  (Lots of people "hate" this - but it is done intentionally - to
allow for "testing" and "audit-trails".)  However, see below ...

4) It is "customary" to use a relatively random seed-value - if what you
really want is an "un-reproducible" random series of numbers.  Often using a
portion of the CURRENT-DATE function works well for this (either seconds - or
hundredths of a second - depending on what your computer supports).

5) If what you really want is a random integer between 0 and n, then multiply
the results of the intrinsic function random by "n" - and you will get what
you want.  If you want an integer between 1 and n, then multiply the results
by "n - 1" - and then add 1 to the results.

6) Like all numeric intrinsic functions, this can only be used (in ANSI
conforming source code) where an arithmetic expression can be used (i.e.
COMPUTE works, but MOVE doesn't).  (This will be "fixed" in the next
revision - and some compilers don't enforce it today).

  ***

If this isn't enough information to get you started, let us (the NG) know
what your remaining problems are (and show us what you have tried).
```

---

<a id="t-c59136e6e5"></a>
## Thread: RANDOM Intrinsic Function - Sample program — 1 msgs

### RANDOM Intrinsic Function - Sample program

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87coce$pov$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I have created the "following" sample program to show how to use the COBOL
"RANDOM" Intrinsic Function (as this topic comes up from time to time).  I
plan on adding it to the FAQ (or at least a portion of it).  This sample
shows both pseudo-random and "real" random series; how to get integers from 1
thru n; and how to make certain that you don't return "duplicated" values.
Are there any "enhancements" that people suggest that I add to help readers
with this?

NOTE:
  The use of the "multi-digit" variable rather than just sticking with
"two-digit" is due to a "bug" in earlier versions of the compiler that I am
using (name withheld - as I think they have fixed the problem in more recent
versions).  It should be possible to just use two-digit (without the
intermediate MOVE statements) - but this does seem to work.  ALSO, I think I
have some lines past column 72 - if your compiler doesn't accept this, just
change the appropriate variables.

   ****

 Identification Division.
  Program-ID. SampRand.
 Data Division.
  Working-Storage Section.
 01  Misc.
     05  Pseudo      Pic X.
     05  Multi-Digit  Pic 99.9(15).
     05  Two-Digit   Pic 99.
     05  Vary        Pic 99.
 01  Tabl.
     05  Lvl Occurs 52 times.
         10  Elem   Pic X   Value "N".
 Procedure Division.
  Mainline.
     Initialize Misc
     Display "This program will return 10 unique 'random' values"
        with no advancing
     Display " between 1 and 52"
     Display " If you use the ""pseudo-random"" option," with no advancing
     Display " the series will always be the same -"
     Display "     i.e. reproducible"
     Display "Do you want a pseudo or real random series?"
     Display "   Enter 'P' or 'R':" with no advancing
     Accept Pseudo from Console
     Evaluate Function Upper-Case (pseudo)
       When "P"
         Perform Return-Pseudo
       When "R"
         Perform Return-Real
       When Other
         Display " "
         Display "Incorrect reply, program terminated"
     End-Evaluate
    GoBack
      .
 Return-Pseudo.
     Compute Multi-Digit = (Function Random (1) * 51) + 1
     Move Multi-Digit  To Two-Digit
     Move "Y"    To Elem (Two-Digit)
     Display Two-Digit
     Perform
       Varying Vary from 1 by 1
       Until Vary > 9
        Compute Multi-Digit = (Function Random * 51) + 1
        Move Multi-Digit to Two-Digit
        If Elem (Two-Digit) = "N"
           Move "Y" to Elem (Two-Digit)
            Display Two-Digit
        Else
            Subtract 1 from Vary
        End-If
     End-Perform
      .
 Return-Real.
     Compute Multi-Digit = (Function Random
         (Function Integer
           (Function NumVal (Function Current-Date (13:4))))
              * 51) + 1
     Move Multi-Digit  to Two-Digit
     Move "Y"     To Elem (Two-Digit)
     Display Two-Digit
     Perform
       Varying VAry from 1 by 1
       Until Vary > 9
        Compute Multi-Digit = (Function Random * 51) + 1
        Move Multi-Digit to Two-Digit
        If Elem (Two-Digit) = "N"
            Move "Y" to Elem (Two-Digit)
            Display Two-Digit
        Else
            Subtract 1 from Vary
        End-If
     End-Perform
      .
```

## Thread: Newbie: ?'s on DISPLAY and a status code — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Newbie: ?'s on DISPLAY and a status code

- **From:** "Robin Webber" <r.webber@cmich.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3899f343@news.cmich.edu>`

```
Hi,   I'm very new to COBOL and having a hard time with my first program. I
get a status code of 48 when trying to run it. I have successfully, I think,
linked the correct files, etc. Could anyone tell me what that code means?

Also,  I have tried using DISPLAY statements so I can see what's going on
before it crashes, but I cannot get the compiler to accept them. It
complains about being in area A, so I move them to area B, then it says
"Display was invalid."

I will really appreciate anyone's help here.

Thanks a lot!
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie: ?'s on DISPLAY and a status code

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87cu4p$3bp2$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3899f343@news.cmich.edu>`

```

Robin Webber <r.webber@cmich.edu> wrote in message
news:3899f343@news.cmich.edu...
> Hi,   I'm very new to COBOL and having a hard time with my first program.
I
> get a status code of 48 when trying to run it. I have successfully, I
think,
> linked the correct files, etc. Could anyone tell me what that code means?
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks a lot!

Could you include the platform you're using for your COBOL program?  Is the
48 a FILE-STATUS return code?  What does the exact code look like for your
DISPLAY statement?  What field name or literal are you trying to display?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Newbie: ?'s on DISPLAY and a status code

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87dg0m$5jg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3899f343@news.cmich.edu> <87cu4p$3bp2$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```

: Robin Webber <r.webber@cmich.edu> wrote in message
:> Hi,   I'm very new to COBOL and having a hard time with my first program.
: I
:> get a status code of 48 when trying to run it. I have successfully, I
: think,
:> linked the correct files, etc. Could anyone tell me what that code means?

A WRITE operation has been tried on a file not opened OUTPUT
or I-O or EXTEND mode or on a file open I-O in the sequential
access mode.

You have probably got a file where the SELECT .. ASSIGN has
ACCESS SEQUENTIAL (or no ACCESS which then defaults to SEQUENTIAL),
you have opened it I-O and then tried to WRITE.

Make the SELECT .. ACCESS DYNAMIC, or OPEN OUTPUT the file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie: ?'s on DISPLAY and a status code

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3899FB91.1628AE8F@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<3899f343@news.cmich.edu>`

```


Robin Webber wrote:

> Hi,   I'm very new to COBOL and having a hard time with my first program. I
> get a status code of 48 when trying to run it. I have successfully, I think,
> linked the correct files, etc. Could anyone tell me what that code means?

Status codes are in most COBOL manuals.  In MVS a status code of 48 is the same
for flat files as for VSAM files, so you can check the following URL:
http://mvshelp.com/vsamretn.htm .  I recently got that error when I converted a
program from an old ANSI COBOL to COBOL for MVS where the old program opened the
file I-O, did a READ, and then tried to do a WRITE.  The solution was to change
the WRITE to a REWRITE.


> Also,  I have tried using DISPLAY statements so I can see what's going on
> before it crashes, but I cannot get the compiler to accept them. It
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I will really appreciate anyone's help here.

You didn't give us enough information to find your error.  Typically, you need
to tell us your compiler, your OS, and your code.  In this case, we may need to
look at the DISPLAY command as well as the definition of the field (s) being
displayed.
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie: ?'s on DISPLAY and a status code

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oKom4.228$rc4.2747@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<3899f343@news.cmich.edu>`

```
*Probably* a file status of 48 means:

4 = Logical file error
8 = WRITE statement attempted against an unopened file or a file
opened for input.
```

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87eg5k$kna$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I suspect that the problem with your displays is that you are attempting to
display a file record, which Fujitsu (I am assuming Fujitsu as well) does
not allow.  Move the data to working storage, and display that instead.  It
is much easier to debug something when you have some idea of the code, the
compiler, and the computer it is running on.
```

- **From:** Jim Ferguson <"Jim Ferguson">
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3899F2F3.1265@notes.ntrs.com>`

```
I'm running mainframe COBOL/MVS.   I need to find the name of the
mainline (first cobol program in the run unit).  I wrote an assembler
routine to extract it from the RUNCOM control block, but I would prefer
and IBM supported method.  Any  suggestions?

Jim Ferguson jwf1@notes.ntrs.com
Northern Trust
```

#### ↳ Re: Find Mainline name

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87d9jg$9j5$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3899F2F3.1265@notes.ntrs.com>`

```
Assuming that by the name "COBOL/MVS" that you mean "IBM COBOL for MVS & VM"
(or even "IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM"), then you might look at the

  CEE3GRN--Get Name of Routine that Incurred Condition

LE callable service.  I am not certain exactly how this will/could work for
your specific problem, but it is documented (including a COBOL example) at:

http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/CEEA306/3%2e5%2e6
```

#### ↳ Re: Find Mainline name

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Saqm4.1762$Sx.30936@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<3899F2F3.1265@notes.ntrs.com>`

```
No such creature exists for COBOL.
The IBM supported method would be to use the EXTRACT macros and xTCBx macros
to retrieve it from the system.
```

<a id="t-7df5c0c96b"></a>
## Thread: Implementing concurrent system — 9 msgs

### Implementing concurrent system

- **From:** Ritchie Menzies <ritchie.menzies@gecm.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389A940E.55E5E973@gecm.com>`

```
I have to code an application which can be run over a network, which
will be run on two machines, and which will both run concurrently
exchanging data every 5 minutes, the data has to be a numerical value...

Would you all say that COBOL is the best language to use for this?
```

#### ↳ Re: Implementing concurrent system

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2UAm4.4294$45.187518@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<389A940E.55E5E973@gecm.com>`

```
If it's the language you know and the language you are prepared to maintain
for the life of the application then it's the best language for the job.

Of course, you could do this by copying a file across the network every 5
minutes. In that case it could be written in your favorite shell scripting
language using the system task scheduler (cron, at, etc...) with no fancy
code at all.

-- Karl Wagner

"Ritchie Menzies" <ritchie.menzies@gecm.com> wrote in message
news:389A940E.55E5E973@gecm.com...
> I have to code an application which can be run over a network, which
> will be run on two machines, and which will both run concurrently
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Hi:

I wonder whether something written in a shell scripting language
could be described as 'no fancy code at all'? Personally, I am
happy to say that I do not know any shell scripting language and
have no plans to learn one.

If people stopped messing about with shell scripting languages and
other stuff and kept it in COBOL, life would be a lot simpler, no?

Tony Dilworth
```

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00

```
Simpler for who?

Do you  honestly mean to tell me that you would prefer write and maintain a
COBOL program that does FTP, rather than write a one line script that makes
use of the FTP program that already exists on your system? Have you never
used REXX, CLIST, or JCL on the mainframe. What about scripts to handle
precompile, compile, link, bind for a COBOL program? Or do you recommend
writing a COBOL program to handle building a COBOL program?

Use the right tool for the job.

-Karl Wagner


"Foodman" <foodman123@aol.com> wrote in message
news:87f433$gqm$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi:
>
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Implementing concurrent system

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87jlet$jfh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<389A940E.55E5E973@gecm.com> <2UAm4.4294$45.187518@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <87f433$gqm$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <xZEm4.4556$45.209492@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Hi:

No, I have never used REXX or CLIST. I used JCL in the olden days
on IBM mainframes but I am happy to say that I have worked exclusively
on the PC since 1983.  Of course, I use DOS commands in a batch file
to compile and link. Otherwise I never seem to find the need for
using this other stuff. No, I would not write a COBOL program for
FTP (and wouldn't know how) since I use WS_FTP which works fine for
updating my websites. I also think that people wind up using things
like REXX (whatever that is) to solve problems which might not have
arisen if the system and its data files had been designed properly
in the first place, no?

People usually invent software systems to make money. Programmers
usually like to use as many different 'things' as they can so that
their resume will be more appealing. In other words, if someone
invents a tool, whether it is necessary or not, people will find
an excuse to use it.

Tony Dilworth
```

#### ↳ Re: Implementing concurrent system

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87fa2r$9ut$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<389A940E.55E5E973@gecm.com>`

```
The language choice has little to do with the fact that it is on a network,
though if you have two different platforms involved, Cobol has the advantage
that your data will be the same on both. I currently have several systems
that span multiple machines, written in Cobol. One thing that I would advise
you to do, if you can, and that is to make the files ASCII with no Comp
fields, and make them line sequential.  Communications becomes trivial to
debug when you can look at the data flowing between systems with a text
editor.

The easiest way to do this on PC's is to write the programs as if they were
batch, and then use the task scheduler to run them every five minutes.

Ritchie Menzies wrote in message <389A940E.55E5E973@gecm.com>...
>I have to code an application which can be run over a network, which
>will be run on two machines, and which will both run concurrently
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-6cdc86a151"></a>
## Thread: Verification of the scope of the GO TO — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Verification of the scope of the GO TO

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u3xpl92b$GA.269@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```

Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
news:87ff54$14o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...

> Finding where things are referrenced is only a small part of
> the problems associated with GO TOs and their labels.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> -------------------------------------------------------------- */
In my travels as a Migrant Programmer a.k.a. consultant, I have found an
almost universal standard for the naming of Paragraphs, and restrictions of
GO TOs in a program.

1) The paragraph has a high level qualifier which must be used for all
Paragraph names from the entry point
to the EXIT.
.i.e   0300-BEGIN-PARAGRAPH.
        0300-something-in-the-middle.
        0300-exit.             exit.

2)  Any GO TO within the Paragraph is confined to the scope of the
Paragraph, unless the GO TO is to an ABEND routine. (My fanaticism, sorry).

There are a variety of solutions to the enforcement of rule 2.

1) A COBOL program can be written to report any violations of the rule.

2) Edit macros can be used to either:
  a) report violations of the rule
  b) present the source program in a manner which simplifies visual
inspection of the code.

The Source Editors I am familiar with (ISPF Edit, XEDIT, KEDIT, and SPF/SE)
all have Edit Macro languages.
ISPF Edit, XEDIT, and KEDIT use REXX, and SPF/SE has a C Interpreter.

They all support the suppression (hiding) of source lines. (EXCLUDE).

You may be able to use the following method to determine the scope of the GO
TOs within your program without the need of 1 or 2a.

1) EXCLUDE ALL of the source.
2) Find ALL occurrences of the word GO.
3) Exclude COMMENTS ("*" in col 7)
4) You should now have only the lines with "GO TO" showing. (provided the GO
TO is on one line)
5) Find ALL non-blanks in col 8 thru 11. (this will show you the Paragraphs,
and "01" "FD" etc.)
6) Locate the Start of the PROCEDURE DIVISION.
7) Page through the program. It should be easy to spot GO TO Violations.

The Text Editor is the most powerful tool we have. The Macro Languages
extend the basic instruction set of the editor.
```

#### ↳ Re: Verification of the scope of the GO TO

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389EE7AD.CD6AF09E@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<u3xpl92b$GA.269@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```
DennisHarley wrote:

> 1) EXCLUDE ALL of the source.
> 2) Find ALL occurrences of the word GO.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> and "01" "FD" etc.)

I almost always have comments with more than one '*', to make their listings
look different from pre-compiler comments.


> 6) Locate the Start of the PROCEDURE DIVISION.
> 7) Page through the program. It should be easy to spot GO TO Violations.
>
> The Text Editor is the most powerful tool we have. The Macro Languages
> extend the basic instruction set of the editor.

It is much easier to check my programs.  Every GO TO points to an abort
routine.  I don't need to go through any of this because they weren't written in
a way in which GO TO added any functionality.

Your checker should also verify that each PERFORM THRU was exactly as intended,
and that there weren't any inadvertent paragraphs added under your SECTIONS.
Again, that check is easy for me as I don't perform sections nor THRU.
```

- **References:** `<389B2C38.AD46722@netzone.com>`

```
John,
I migrated Wang Cobol onto a hp-9000 with acucobol gt ver 4.0
Our apps continued to run in wang extended cobol thru an emulator runtime
from a 3rd party vendor.
As far as native acucobol code running under hp's operating system, i found
it to be a really robust product...  we also combined their other
products; acuodbc and acu4gl to touch our access and oracle databases thru
odbc and embedded sql.  they also have acusql as a new product.
If your question is one of compatibilty between hp cobol and acucobol, you
might want to talk to acucorp personally, but my guess is if the code
was written with ansi-74 or 85 standards in mind, you won't have any
problems.  if you have anything specific to ask about this project, let me
know.
-doug
```

<a id="t-740a70b0cd"></a>
## Thread: Sorting with cobol — 1 this month / 27 total

_Thread started [2000-01](2000-01.md#t-740a70b0cd)._

#### ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

- **From:** Carl Hardeman <cwhardeman@fedex.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389AD8A1.E2D902E6@fedex.com>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
There are always tradeoffs. One argument in the past is that
SOMETIMES you have a large long running srt and if your
program fails after the sort completes, then you have to rerun
the sort, whereas you need not if working from a file sorted
in a previous step. But that's that one case.
My personal preference is based on simplicity argument even
at the cost of some computer resources. An internal sort
does not require the output file from a previous sort step to
be written then read back in the subsequent step since the
output from the internal sort is directly fed to the rest of
the program without that intervening file write/read.

Mark Framness wrote:
> 
> Greetings All
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-72d3432473"></a>
## Thread: Help on Error 105 — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help on Error 105

- **From:** "Roberto Bongini" <futura.softw@mclink.it>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87e437$odu$1@news.mclink.it>`

```
Hi,
I use NetExpress 2.0 and I have a routine that open/close/delete a temp file
like this:

< ....>
OPEN OUTPUT TEMPFILE.
CLOSE TEMPFILE.
OPEN I-O TEMPFILE.
< .....>
CLOSE TEMPFILE.
CALL "CBL_DELETE_FILE" USING "TEMPFILE  ".
CALL "CBL_DELETE_FILE" USING "TEMPFILE.idx ".
< ....>
EXIT METHOD.


The routine run correctly but after 10 minutes the Prog fall in error 105
that means: "File allocation error".

Anybody help me?

Thanks in advance.
Roberto.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help on Error 105

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87eb7g$7ah$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<87e437$odu$1@news.mclink.it>`

```
Roberto,

Roberto Bongini wrote in message <87e437$odu$1@news.mclink.it>...
>Hi,
>I use NetExpress 2.0 and I have a routine that open/close/delete a temp
file
>The routine run correctly but after 10 minutes the Prog fall in error 105
>that means: "File allocation error".
>

Error 105 is a memory allocation error, not a file allocation error. It
means that the run-time system cannot allocate memory for an operation being
executed. You will normally only get that if a CBL_ALLOC_MEM call specified
a huge size, or if a memory leak or corruption had occurred.

Have you tried animating the program to find out on exactly which COBOL
statement the error occurs, and have you also tried the same
open/close/delete without any other code in between to see if the error
still happens? Doing both should help you track down the problem.
```

##### ↳ ↳ R: Help on Error 105

- **From:** "Roberto Bongini" <futura.softw@mclink.it>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87f6hk$b2j$1@news.mclink.it>`
- **References:** `<87e437$odu$1@news.mclink.it> <87eb7g$7ah$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Thank you for your suggest.

After your reply I find my error.

Roberto.

Gael Wilson <gael.wilson@merant.com> wrote in message
87eb7g$7ah$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk...
> Roberto,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> means that the run-time system cannot allocate memory for an operation
being
> executed. You will normally only get that if a CBL_ALLOC_MEM call
specified
> a huge size, or if a memory leak or corruption had occurred.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help on Error 105

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87f9fq$9ta$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<87e437$odu$1@news.mclink.it> <87eb7g$7ah$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Gael Wilson wrote in message <87eb7g$7ah$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>...
>Roberto,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>means that the run-time system cannot allocate memory for an operation
being
>executed. You will normally only get that if a CBL_ALLOC_MEM call specified
>a huge size, or if a memory leak or corruption had occurred.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I notice as well that you are using OOP.  If you are creating a new object
each time, and not destroying after use, that may be the problem.  With
Fujitsu, you have to set the object pointer to null after you are finished
with the object, or it will stay in existence until the run completes.  I do
not know if MF has a similar function.
```

```
the program locks up any time I try to send output to the printer i.e.

                Select Report-Output

problems with printing but since the installation of the windows version all 
it does is lock up when I get to the output everything else seems to run fine. 
I checked with Merant tech assistance and they sent me this

 SELECT TRANSCRIPT-FILE                
     ASSIGN TO "OUTFILE"               
     ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.  


WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                     
01  DISK-INPUT-FILE        PIC X(12)  VALUE "DATAFILE.DAT".  
01  ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS PIC X(3)   VALUE "YES".           
01  MFPRNT16PTR PROCEDURE-POINTER.                           

PROCEDURE DIVISION.                          
    SET MFPRNT16PTR TO ENTRY 'MFPRNT16'.     
300-PRINT-FILE.                                              
     call "PC_WIN_PRINT_FILE_EXT"                            
               using by reference "OUTFILE" &x"00"           
                     by reference "Output Record" &x"00"     
               BY VALUE 4 SIZE 2.                            
    .

Thanks in advance
Stevekh1
```

```
I visited your web page.  The fact that Linux is Free is not the only
reason for it to be interesting.

Information week of January 24, 2000 has an interesting article:
http://www.informationweek.com/770/linuxOS.htm
"Linux comes Alive"  and
http://www.informationweek.com/bizint/biz770/70bzlnx.htm
"Behind The Numbers:  What's Driving Increased Interest In Linux?"

The upsurge in interest in Linux means Jobs for Cobol Programmers for a
long time.  Think of all the Legacy installs that can be migrated to
Linux
hosts.

http://www.excelco.com/ven_lnx_2.htm for a list of accounting systems
that run
on Linux.

Cobol and its portability is going to give Cobol-based solutions new life

on the Linux platform.
```

```
On Sat, 05 Feb 2000 11:16:48 -0700, John Mosier <jmosier@netzone.com>
wrote:

>Cobol and its portability is going to give Cobol-based solutions new life
>
>on the Linux platform.
>

Not until there is a viable (read FREE) COBOL compiler.  There isn't
one.  Heck, I'd settle for an inexpensive one.  PS - I know about
LegacyJ's offering.  I know about the way over priced MicroFocus
offering, and I know about AcuCorp's high price per runtime offering.
None are "viable" IMHO.
```
