###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Compilers for Linux

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<amin4.25429$Fb1.564488@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38924327.9A54DF1@gethip.com> <38942C6D.D93E350E@zip.com.au> <389C6910.24663636@netzone.com> <389da5c1.217195918@news.freewwweb.com>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com>
> Not until there is a viable (read FREE) COBOL compiler.  There isn't
> one.  Heck, I'd settle for an inexpensive one.  PS - I know about
> LegacyJ's offering.  I know about the way over priced MicroFocus
> offering, and I know about AcuCorp's high price per runtime offering.
> None are "viable" IMHO.

since i work for MERANT (Micro Focus), this may seem unobjective, but cut me
some slack if you're thinking that way...

neither Micro Focus or Acu (or any other COBOL vendor so far as i'm aware)
have changed their pricing for Linux. why not? because Linux is already
commercially viable (just look at RHAT's mkt cap). so if you think some of
the tools are overpriced, it has nothing to do with Linux (apart from some
residual & illogical thought process that equates Linux wth FREE).

yes, some companies will offer FREE tools (for Linux or other platforms),
but it's all part of the same game (just using different hooks).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Compilers for Linux

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87kkrh$s3i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<38924327.9A54DF1@gethip.com> <38942C6D.D93E350E@zip.com.au> <389C6910.24663636@netzone.com> <389da5c1.217195918@news.freewwweb.com> <amin4.25429$Fb1.564488@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`

```
Gazaloo <gaz@home.com> wrote:

: since i work for MERANT (Micro Focus), this may seem unobjective, but cut me
: some slack if you're thinking that way...

: neither Micro Focus or Acu (or any other COBOL vendor so far as i'm aware)
: have changed their pricing for Linux. why not? because Linux is already
: commercially viable (just look at RHAT's mkt cap). so if you think some of
: the tools are overpriced, it has nothing to do with Linux (apart from some
: residual & illogical thought process that equates Linux wth FREE).

MF have kept their _Unix_ prices for Linux, presumably because they
think of Linux as a form of Unix.

However, most Linux users see this OS as a desktop system that can
run server facilities rather than as a multiuser box.

Typically a Unix system has a central server with client machines
that run from dumb 80x25 terminals through to Windows machines.
The Unix box is seen as if it were a small 'mainframe'.  The cost
kept it in that position while DOS and Windows were seen as
cheap.

Now that MS is pushing Windows 2000 as servers and clients (the
Edsel of Operating Systems) this will become the expensive 
option as MS try to hang onto their 'brand loyalty' pricing
model (when brand loyalty has now been broken) and pushes up
the costs through draconian licencing and extra licencing
requirements.

The Unix/Linux market will become the lower cost option as 
lightweight single-function servers on cheap hardware reduce
the TCO by almost completely eliminate administration costs.
eg see Cobalt.

With hardware costs falling the cost of MS software exceeds the
cost of everything else.  Thus hardware vendors see their
profits eliminated while MS rakes in most of their revenue.
The solution for these vendors to compete is to build their
own solution based on Linux for everything from PDAs to
mainframes.

Ths will bring Linux to the corporate desktop either through
server based terminals or as desktops.

On the current desktop systems: DOS, Windows, OS/2 I can distribute
licence fee free using MS Cobol (old), MF Cobol or Fujitsu.
Accu provide added value by catering for multiple distribution
targets from a single compilation system, thus though there
are licence fees, they provide advantages to the developer
by widening his target market to hundreds of platforms that
are somewhat captive.  ie users that cannot or will not buy
shrink wrapped software.

With MF on Linux it seems that I would need to buy an expensive
10 user runtime for each machine.  Where I would sell software
for a small single user site, or SOHO, MF now wants _more_
for the apps server than I would charge for a simple system.

Of course I could put the DOS system under DOSEMU, or even a
Windows system under Wine, but MF does not yet 'get' Linux.

With your pricing structure MF will simply miss out on the
Linux market.  Your sales figures will show that 'Linux is
not selling' even as they take significant market share.

If Fujitsu (or some other) comes out with a pricing model
that matches their Windows pricing model then they will
take the complete market.  (unless one of the free ones
comes through with a usable system).  Alternately the
Cobol programmers will move to Java, or Python, or ..
and the Cobol market will die in the next iteration of
desktop and user machines (where this has progressed from
CP/M to DOS to Windows 3.x to Win95/98 and has staggered
around OS/2 then NT and will next move to Linux rather
than the Edsel of 2000).

If you recall the Edsel, it was built on the basis that it
incorporated _everything_ that anyone had said they wanted
in a car (just like Windows 2000).  Where it failed was that
noone wanted it all in _one_ car (as is W2000), they wanted
some features in a car for commuting and different features
in a car for the wife to take kids to school/shopping, and
different features in a sports car.

One thing that I want to _not_ have is a Windows 2000 PDA
that has multi-processor support and data centre features.
Another thing that I want to _not_ have is a departmental
server that can runs games.

With Linux I can have the same system that is cut down or
built up to suit the application and the users.  This makes
the scope of Linux from PDAs to server farms with simple
desktop NCs and workstations in between.  MF can't see this
yet.  Linux is the new DOS and Windows as well as the new
Unix.
```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote:
> MF have kept their _Unix_ prices for Linux, presumably because they
> think of Linux as a form of Unix.

i'd say that was reasonable.

> However, most Linux users see this OS as a desktop system that can
> run server facilities rather than as a multiuser box.

i'd say that wasn't :-) Linux is (and will continue to grow as) a server
o/s. it's already heavily used by lots of web sites (both external and
internal to an organization). i saw some industry data recently that showed
Linux as the fastest growing server o/s (i can't recall the source or the
numbers).

<snip>

> On the current desktop systems: DOS, Windows, OS/2 I can distribute
> licence fee free using MS Cobol (old), MF Cobol or Fujitsu.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> shrink wrapped software.

er, multi-distribution is a standard feature of Micro Focus as well. the
compiler produces an INTermediate code that is platform-independent and
portable. it also produces an array of different formats for fully-compiled
(machine specific) code when performance is key.

> With MF on Linux it seems that I would need to buy an expensive
> 10 user runtime for each machine.  Where I would sell software
> for a small single user site, or SOHO, MF now wants _more_
> for the apps server than I would charge for a simple system.

it's your opinion as to whether or not the run-time fees are too expensive.
all i can say is that the pricing hasn't changed for Linux. the "run-time"
and "application server" are the same thing (so no one is asking you to pay
twice).

> Of course I could put the DOS system under DOSEMU, or even a
> Windows system under Wine, but MF does not yet 'get' Linux.

i think we do. it's a UNIX variant; it's also very cool, has lots of hype
and a very interesting development & distribution model. what are we
missing?

> With your pricing structure MF will simply miss out on the
> Linux market.  Your sales figures will show that 'Linux is
> not selling' even as they take significant market share.

my, your crystal ball must really be glowing now :-) i'm obviously not going
disclose sales figures, suffice to say the release of Micro Focus Object
COBOL Developers' Suite for Linux is one of the fastest ramping releases
we've ever made.

> If Fujitsu (or some other) comes out with a pricing model
> that matches their Windows pricing model then they will
> take the complete market.  (unless one of the free ones
> comes through with a usable system).

then why hasn't that already happened on the UNIX platforms where Fujitsu
have compilers (such as HPUX)? my point is that, as you seem to know (wrt
Acu anyway), price (though important) is not the only issue when making a
choice. value is.

> Alternately the
> Cobol programmers will move to Java, or Python, or ..
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> than the Edsel of 2000).

well, seems like we've got a year or so left then. it's been nice knowing
everyone here. all the best.

<snip>

> With Linux I can have the same system that is cut down or
> built up to suit the application and the users.  This makes
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Unix.

well, thanks for letting us know. it's much clearer now.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Compilers for Linux

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aLOn4.27115$Fb1.607264@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38924327.9A54DF1@gethip.com> <38942C6D.D93E350E@zip.com.au> <389C6910.24663636@netzone.com> <389da5c1.217195918@news.freewwweb.com> <amin4.25429$Fb1.564488@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com> <87kkrh$s3i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <lwpn4.25710$Fb1.576610@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com> <389f98c3.344961288@news.freewwweb.com>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote
> But if I take this system I built and I want to see it, to a user as
> the back end of a CGI application, where MANY or few users will be
> concurrently connected, I MUST by Application Server -- for $10,000
> per server.

IMO: i don't see why you can't deploy your CGI on a run-time that is limited
to 'n' users. so buy a 10-user license and go with that (or match the
10-packs to what you think the limit will be). i know you've said that
you've been asked to name each user; i can't explain that. it makes some
sense if we're talking about development seats, but not run-time.

the $10K option is a compromise of sorts. perhaps not a very good one, but
on one end of the scale you have a few users and potentially thousands+ on
the other end.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Compilers for Linux

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389e4d93.260164027@news.freewwweb.com>`
- **References:** `<38924327.9A54DF1@gethip.com> <38942C6D.D93E350E@zip.com.au> <389C6910.24663636@netzone.com> <389da5c1.217195918@news.freewwweb.com> <amin4.25429$Fb1.564488@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`

```
On Sun, 06 Feb 2000 17:40:22 GMT, "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com> wrote:

>since i work for MERANT (Micro Focus), this may seem unobjective, but cut me
>some slack if you're thinking that way...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>but it's all part of the same game (just using different hooks).

I come at this from a slightly different Point of view.

I *posit* that there are a lot of COBOL programmers out there who
would LOVE to use their skills to create programs and systems outside
their present positions.  In fact, some have and have become rather
successful.  These self/same programmers have sometimes started their
own small businesses and/or offered their services to companies and
individuals who traditionally cannot afford such a highly qualified
professional, and all the system bagage that comes with such an
individual.  Having engaged these services, they are left with a
highly reliable and stable system that meets their exact business
needs - all because the programmer was able to leverage his/her
considerable business system design and coding skill.

Now, in the past, there have been reasonably priced compilers, or
COBOL development systems offered in "chunks".  Personally I bought
the $900.00 Realia compiler to start my work.  Why Realia?  I had
experience with both it, MicorFocus and RM at the time.  Realia would
let me sell my applications sans runtime fees.  At the time I was
selling a $35.00 piece of software to end users.  ANY runtime fee
would have killed me. 

So it is today.  I have a Windows based compiler that I can afford
(coincidently $900.00) that does not charge a runtime fee.  Fujitsu
COBOL.  I can continue to sell my systems.  

Interestingly, my mix of development tools leads me to be able to do
something I never thought possible, but is very attractive.  I can
create applications that are Windows based, Internet Enabled (with
Thin Client) or even browser based (behold the power of COBOL!).  Now
as an individual, servicing companies and individuals, I would like to
get into the ASP market.  I would like to provide at least a demo of
my wares and skills on the internet.  One nice way to do this would be
a browser based demo.  I can do this with my present tools - provided
I use an NT server.  Alas, the companies I have contacted to host my
application all either refuse to run NT or have higher prices based on
the lack of stability and scalability of NT.  If I were to run on Unix
or Linux however, the doors or open!  So, I say, I'll just use this
highly cross platform COBOL I have and move to Linux.  Oh, wait.
Runtime fees. GAG!  I can't sell my application or act as an ASP
because I frankly can't aford it.  Too bad for me, I guess I have no
place in the market.  BAH, I say.  I surely DO have a place.  I can do
things better, faster and more reliably than the average Joe.  I just
can't afford it.

So here I sit, waiting for Linux to take off.  It could have a flying
start if it had a COBOL *I* could use.  Right now there isn't one.
Maybe someday - but then maybe someday NT will become stable and
scalable.......

End Tirade.
```

also an interesting aside, is that you mention the multi-platform

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Compilers for Linux

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389f9954.345106017@news.freewwweb.com>`
- **References:** `<38924327.9A54DF1@gethip.com> <38942C6D.D93E350E@zip.com.au> <389C6910.24663636@netzone.com> <389da5c1.217195918@news.freewwweb.com> <amin4.25429$Fb1.564488@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com> <389e4d93.260164027@news.freewwweb.com> <0yun4.25799$Fb1.586161@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`

```
On Mon, 07 Feb 2000 07:32:12 GMT, "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com> wrote:


>interesting.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, I have a few problems with that idea, but let me think out loud
here.

Why should MicroFocus profit from my success?  Should I no tbe the one
to profit?  Are they going to support my users runtime problems if
they collect this fee?  What value are they providing from this fee?

I was a long time Borland user.  There are a ton of Delphi and Borland
VIsual C applications out there.  Visual Basic is used for desktop
consumer and shareware applications.  Why not COBOL?  None of these
development tools charge a runtime license fee, although all have
runtimes.

So the short answer is - NO runtime fee should be charged, unles MF is
going ot support that runtime with my customer.  If they pay for your
runtime, then they should get something for it.

Additionally there is the required record keeping.  I have to track
unique runtimes.  That kind of kills the "Try before you buy" plan.
Or an I allowed to give away the runtime if I give away my software?
Can I please have a free copy of Application Server to go along with
my free software?  I think not.  

I would be happy with a bare bones $1000.00 Linux COBOL compiler with
no runtime fees.  When someone makes one, I'll buy it.

Richard's observations have merit.  If I want to go to Linux, I'll
have to go with something other than COBOL, or so it seems.  I surely
don't want to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Compilers for Linux

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87plrp$soe$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<38924327.9A54DF1@gethip.com> <38942C6D.D93E350E@zip.com.au> <389C6910.24663636@netzone.com> <389da5c1.217195918@news.freewwweb.com> <amin4.25429$Fb1.564488@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com> <389e4d93.260164027@news.freewwweb.com> <0yun4.25799$Fb1.586161@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com> <389f9954.345106017@news.freewwweb.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote:

: Well, I have a few problems with that idea, but let me think out loud
: here.

: Why should MicroFocus profit from my success?  Should I no tbe the one
: to profit?  Are they going to support my users runtime problems if
: they collect this fee?  What value are they providing from this fee?

: I was a long time Borland user.  There are a ton of Delphi and Borland
: VIsual C applications out there.  Visual Basic is used for desktop
: consumer and shareware applications.  Why not COBOL?  None of these
: development tools charge a runtime license fee, although all have
: runtimes.

: So the short answer is - NO runtime fee should be charged, unles MF is
: going ot support that runtime with my customer.  If they pay for your
: runtime, then they should get something for it.

One of the problems that I see is that MF may consider the run-time
to compete against, say, database systems.  One cannot distribute
MySQL or DB2 or Oracle for free.  The Cobol run-time includes
FileShare as well as other packages such as Dialog and all manner
of such tools, none of which I want.

On DOS, OS/2 and Windows, if you don't use the advanced run-time
items (OSX) then you can distribute free, if you use them you
should pay.

However, on Unix/Linux, the much more expensive run-time must
be used (as far as I can see) even if the OSX bits are not
used.

From talking to salesmen at MF it seems this is simply to
increase their revenue.  

MF also used to sell the compiler products as 3 packages:
Compiler, Toolbox, Workbench.  You can see that the WB 3.4
manuals come split up in this way.  I never use the Workbench
it is far too clunky and has no tools that would do anything I
need.  I have built up my own development system using
better tools that do it the way I like.  I don't want the
toolbox.

Yet if I buy the MF product it has to be everything, I can
no longer buy just the Compiler.

Same with the run-time.  I don't use FileShare, will never use
Dialog, nor any of the other bits that MF think 'add value'.
I see them as adding cost and overhead.

I want a system that is the equivalent of just the Compiler
and a free simple run-time.

If I wanted the added value items then I would want to buy
them not have them forced down my throat - this is why
people are going to Linux in the first place, MS pissed
people off by forcing their stuff down everyone's throat
with compulsory Win95 with every box and compulsory IE.
```

## Thread: Print problems under NT — 5 msgs

### Print problems under NT

- **From:** "Neil Watson" <neilw@peregrine.ie>
- **Date:** 2000-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87h47v$m1n$1@kermit.esat.net>`

```
Hello,


I've developed an application using MicroFocus Cobol/2 (c 1990 - old I
know!).

The same application (without any modifications) has run successfully under
DOS, Windows 3.1, Windows 95 and Windows 98 without any printing problems.

However, it's been installed on an NT Workstation box where everything runs
fine except printing. When I print a report, nothing prints on the printer
UNTIL I exit the application when the report prints OK. It appears as though
all printer output is buffered until the application exits.

The code looks like this:


Under the Environment Division:

      Select PrintFile Assign "LST:"
      Organisation Line Sequential.


In the Data Division:

FD PrintFile.
   01 PrintLine.
      03 PrintRecord PIC X(132).


When printing:

Move "alskdfsadh" to PrintLine.
Write PrintLine.



Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated. Hopefully there is an NT
configuration/printer configuration option which can be modified (printer is
a dot-matrix NEC).


Thanks,



Neil Watson
neilw@peregrine.ie
```

- **References:** `<87h47v$m1n$1@kermit.esat.net>`

```

You might try assigning to "LPT1" or
"\\computername\printername".

Tony Dilworth

#### ↳ Re: Print problems under NT

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<chZm4.7547$45.386583@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<87h47v$m1n$1@kermit.esat.net>`

```
It can think of a few things...

1) If it's a 16-bit application these notes from MSDN apply

--- start of MSDN excerpt ---

MS-DOS Applications Running Locally
If you print from an MS-DOS application that is running locally, or from a
Windows NT command line utility that sends data to a printer port, NTVDM
receives the print job. NTVDM queries the redirector to determine if the
redirector is managing the target port (you run NET USE LPT1:
\\SERVER\SHARE) or if the spooler is managing the port.

If the redirector is managing the target port, the redirector takes control
of the print job, and none of the spooler options are used. If the
redirector is not managing the port, but a printer defined in Print Manager
is printing to that port, the print job goes to the printer, and the
printer's default datatype and spooling options are used. If neither the
redirector nor any printers are managing the port, then the job goes to the
port device driver, unaltered.

For example, one printer in Print Manager prints to COM1, none of the
printers in Print Manager print to LPT1, and you issue a NET USE command to
redirect output from LPT2 to a network print share. If an MS-DOS-based
application prints to LPT2, the print job goes to the network print share.
If the application prints to COM1, NTVDM submits the print job to the
printer that prints to COM1, and used that printer's default datatype. If
the application prints to LPT1, NTVDM submits the job directly to the
parallel port device driver; no alteration occurs.

--- End of MSDN excerpt

2) If you have not closed the print file then it may not be handed to the
spooler, so it waits until the program exit closes the file before printing.

3) You may need to disable the spooler for that printer if it used primarily
by your 16-bit application.

4) Visit www.microsoft.com and check the KnowledgeBase and troubleshooters

-- Karl Wagner

news:87h47v$m1n$1@kermit.esat.net...

>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

under

runs

> UNTIL I exit the application when the report prints OK. It appears as
though
> all printer output is buffered until the application exits.
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…

is
> a dot-matrix NEC).
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

the 'Scheduling' tab for the print driver.   It may be set on 'Print
after
last page is spooled' and will not print until the program is exited.

Steve

Subscripts (and indexes) are a method of referring to an array element as an
occurrence of a field-name.  Your textbook, if you're a student, should
explain this for you.  Could you be more specific about what it is about
subscripts you don't understand?

#### ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zeBn4.79$st4.4678@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I don't know what they use for textbooks these days, but this should
certanly be covered under the keyword "OCCURS" in the text on the DATA
DIVISION.

If you are familiar with BASIC langauge, subscripts are used the same for
arrays in BASIC as for tables in COBOL.

01  MY-TABLE
     05  MY-ITEM       PIC X(01) OCCURS 10 TIMES.
01 SUB1                  PIC 9(02).


    You reference which one of the ten 'MY-ITEM's  you mean with a
subscript.

  MOVE "A"  to MY-ITEM (1)
  MOVE "B"  TO MY-ITEM (2)
....
 MOVE "J"  TO MY-ITEM (10)

 MOVE 1 TO SUB1
 PERFORM 10 TIMES
    DISPLAY SUB1 SPACE MY-ITEM (SUB1)
    ADD 1  TO SUB1
 END-PERFORM.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87moj9$bbh$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com>`

```
A subscript is just a method of referencing something in a list.  For
example, instead of having a single name referenced by a variable name, I
declare the name as occurring a certain number of times. I then refer to it
by a  number in a subscript.  So NAME-VALUE(1) would refer to the first
value in the list, NAME-VALUE(2) would refer to the second value in the
list, and so on.

annasteinunn@hotmail.com wrote in message ...
>I need  help on subscripts!  Can anyone explain that for me?
>thanks
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.help.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com>`

```
annasteinunn@hotmail.com wrote:
>I need  help on subscripts!  Can anyone explain that for me?
>thanks

Data items are referenced by using their data name, such as STREET-ADDRESS,
CUSTOMER-NAME, etc.  You can think of a table as a mechanism for having a
number of data items, all with the same name.  This collection of data items,
all with the same name, is called a 'table'.  You can create a table in COBOL
using the OCCURS clause of a data declaration.

    03  COUNT    PIC  9(06)  OCCURS 20 TIMES.

This creates a table of 20 data items, all named COUNT.  In COBOL 85 the first
element in a table is always 1, and the last element is defined in the OCCURS.
Sometimes that maximum number is determined at runtime by the contents of
another numeric data item specified in the OCCURS.  For example:

    03  MAX      PIC  9(02)  VALUE 15.
    03  COUNT    PIC  9(06)  OCCURS 1 TO 20 TIMES DEPENDING ON MAX.

In this example, as the value of MAX is changed in the program, it will
be as if the OCCURS clause was simultaneously changed to the same value.

Because each data item has the same data name, each time you reference the
table, you must somehow indicate which of the several data items you are
referring to.  To do this we use what is called a 'subscript', as in
mathematical notation.  Since most programming languages use straight text,
and do not support the tiny fonts used in printed subscripts, the subscript
is typically enclosed in parenthesis () following the data name.

    COUNT(1)     Data name COUNT, #1
    COUNT(2)     Data name COUNT, #2
    COUNT(12)    Data name COUNT, #12

In some programming languages (e.g. C/C++), subscripts are enclosed in square
brackets [] instead of parenthesis (), but they do the same thing.

Tables and subscripting, along with loops, are some of the most powerful
features of programming languages.  They allow you to write one set of
instructions which can be applied over and over to different data.  This is
accomplished by using a numeric data item instead of a numeric literal as a
subscript into the table, like this:

    COUNT(SUB)   Data name COUNT, # contained in SUB

Obviously, SUB must be a numeric data item, and the value must be an integer
within the valid subscript range.  To add 5 to each data item COUNT in the
table, you could code a simple loop like this:

    PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB > 5
        ADD 1 TO COUNT(SUB).

The data item in the table may be any normal COBOL data item, even a group
item.  For example:

    03  FRIEND-TABLE        OCCURS 100 TIMES.
        05  FRIEND-NAME         PIC  X(20).
        05  FRIEND-STREET       PIC  X(20).
        05  FRIEND-CITY-ST      PIC  X(20).
        05  FRIEND-ZIP          PIC  9(05).

The OCCURS clause may not be used on a level 01, 66, 77 or 88 item, but only
on levels 02 through 49.  You may not REDEFINE an item containing an OCCURS
clause, but you may REDEFINE an item subordinate to an item containing an
OCCURS clause.

Tables with one subscript are very useful, but sometimes there are situations
when we need more.  For example, say you have divided your city into a 10x20
grid, and you want to store the population of each grid square into a table.
You could do this:

    03  CITY-GRID-X      OCCURS 10 TIMES.
        05  CITY-GRID-Y      OCCURS 20 TIMES.
            07  CITY-POP         PIC  9(06).

This creates a table with 200 data items named CITY-POP, but now when you
reference it, you must specify two subscripts, one for each dimension:

    CITY-POP(1, 1)              CITY-POP grid at X=1, Y=1
    CITY-POP(3, 5)              CITY-POP grid at X=3, Y=5
    CITY-POP(10, 20)            CITY-POP grid at X=10, Y=20
    CITY-POP(SUBX, SUBY)        CITY-POP grid at SUBX, SUBY

If you wanted to store the population of each grid square for ten cities
you could do this:

    03  CITY-CITY        OCCURS 10 TIMES.
        05  CITY-GRID-X      OCCURS 10 TIMES.
            07  CITY-GRID-Y      OCCURS 20 TIMES.
                09  CITY-POP         PIC  9(06).

This creates a table with 2000 data items named CITY-POP, but when you
reference it, you must specify three subscripts, one for the city and one
for each dimension.  To sum the population of all the cities, you could
code this:

    03  TOTAL-POP        PIC  9(08).
    03  CITY             PIC  9(02).
    03  SUBX             PIC  9(02).
    03  SUBY             PIC  9(02).

    MOVE ZERO TO TOTAL-POP.
    PERFORM VARYING CITY FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL CITY > 10
        AFTER VARYING SUBX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUBX > 10
            AFTER VARYING SUBY FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUBY > 20
                ADD CITY-POP(CITY, SUBX, SUBY) TO TOTAL-POP.

As a matter of form, I prefer to indent my PIC and other clauses four more
spaces with each level of OCCURS.  This provides a clear visual cue when
you are scanning a record definition.

In addition to subscripting, COBOL provides a feature called 'indexing'.
Indexing works very similarly to subscripting, but there are differences.
First, an 'index' is defined as part of the table with which it is
associated, by using the INDEXED BY phrase of the OCCURS clause.  The city
table above could have been defined like this:

    03  TEMP             PIC  9(02).
    03  CITY-CITY        OCCURS 10 TIMES INDEXED BY CITY.
        05  CITY-GRID-X      OCCURS 10 TIMES INDEXED BY SUBX.
            07  CITY-GRID-Y      OCCURS 20 TIMES INDEXED BY SUBY.
                09  CITY-POP         PIC  9(06).

The above PERFORM would work on this table in the same way.  But if you want
to change the value of an index you normally must use the SET verb.  For
example:

    SET SUBX TO 4.
    SET SUBX UP BY 1.
    SET SUBX DOWN BY 2.
    SET TEMP TO SUBX.
    SET SUBY TO TEMP.
    SET SUBX TO SUBY.

Using indexes instead of subscripts can have advantages.  One is that you can
often write faster code using indexes, and the other is that you can use the
COBOL SEARCH verb on tables which have indexes declared.  But those are beyond
the scope of this brief introduction.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389EF66F.2BFC7BF4@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia>`

```
My guess is that you're confused between subscripts and indices.  They are
slightly different ways of doing the same thing, and look the same in many cases.

An index is set up when you create the occurs, and used to be slightly more
efficient than subscripts.  It is still required for SEARCH statements, but I
don't see it used much anymore outside of this.  It requires the SET command to
change, and cannot contain a calculated value.

A subscript is not declared around the OCCURS clause and works just as you would
expect a subscript to work.

If you think this is the source of your confusion, please reply and we will give
you a more detailed explanation, although it may be that what I said will enable
you to read the text with better understanding.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia>`

```
Judson,

A very lucid, well written tutorial, thanks. A related question: Can a COBOL
subscript be negative? If not, what is the reason grandeur? And a related
observation (cannot contain myself on this one): As much as I like COBOL, its
wordiness, where it is  totally unjustifed, makes me almost as sick as a sight of
Hillary Clinton. Why

PERFORM SUB=1 TO 10
    <do something...>
END-PERFORM

would be less clear than

PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB > 10,

or should one desire to  code SUB=10, even more monstrous

PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1  UNTIL SUB=10?

Saying that the latter is more self-explanatory than PERFORM SUB=1 TO 10  is the
same as to say that "find such a number that if the base of natural logarithms
were raised to the power which is equal to that number then the result would be
equal to X" is more self-explanatory than LN(X).  At certain level of abstraction,
being "self-explanatory" becomes superfluous.

There are things in "the other languages" which I definitely dislike seeing in
COBOL in fear that the language might become cryptic, but there are always the
balance and measure. Some excessive wordiness has already been eliminated from
COBOL progressively by COMPUTE and intrinsic functions (although the syntax of
their usage is completely moronic; especially ridiculous is the necessity of using
the very word FUNCTION, as if the compiler could not parse the name of a function
- reserved! - out of the context). There is still a long way to go.

Kind regards,
===================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
===================


Judson McClendon wrote:

> annasteinunn@hotmail.com wrote:
> >I need  help on subscripts!  Can anyone explain that for me?
…[140 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net>`

```

Paul M. Dorfman <sashole@mediaone.net

> A related question: Can a COBOL
> subscript be negative?

Sure. At least a variable used as a subscript can take on a negative
value and will be evaluated properly at run time. For example:

PERFORM VARYING INDX FROM 10 BY -1 UNTIL INDX = -20
```

Thanks, Jerry, of course. I should have expressed it more precisely.
What I meant was: Can a table be negatively subsripted? In almost any
language, an array can be declared as, for example,

ARRAY TABLE (-100 : 200) .....

and I do not see any reason why COBOL should be deprived of this
functionality. I meant to ask, therefore, since in COBOL it is just
OCCURS so many times, is there a syntactic option I might not be aware
of equivalent to, hypothetically,

TABLE OCCURS 301 TIMES FROM -100 TO 200...

so that the elements of the table could be subsequently referenced using
a correspondingly signed subscript?

Kind regards,
=================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
=================

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87sevt$jkv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A12679.76A04D67@earthlink.net>`

```
Paul M. Dorfman <sashole@earthlink.net> wrote:

: Thanks, Jerry, of course. I should have expressed it more precisely.
: What I meant was: Can a table be negatively subsripted? In almost any
: language, an array can be declared as, for example,

: ARRAY TABLE (-100 : 200) .....

This may be so in 'almost any' Pascal or Pascal derivitives, but
not in 'almost any' Basic, C, Fortran, Cobol, or their derivitives.

: and I do not see any reason why COBOL should be deprived of this
: functionality. 

Probably because no one saw any reason why it would be useful.

: I meant to ask, therefore, since in COBOL it is just
: OCCURS so many times, is there a syntactic option I might not be aware
: of equivalent to, hypothetically,

: TABLE OCCURS 301 TIMES FROM -100 TO 200...

No such option exists.

: so that the elements of the table could be subsequently referenced using
: a correspondingly signed subscript?

But why would this be useful ?  There is the First item, then the 
2nd, then the 3rd, ...  Cobol also does not have the Zeroth item in
its arrays.  In business things are counted from 1.

What does the -57th item mean ?

In some cases the use of negative subscripts can be used where, for
example, sample data has a range.  This may be a temperature from
-40 to +79 and an array of tempeartures is created directly using 
this.  In Cobol just have an offset.  In other languages there
may be a 'associative array' (but not in Pascal).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A26193.D151D556@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A12679.76A04D67@earthlink.net> <87sevt$jkv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:

> Paul M. Dorfman <sashole@earthlink.net> wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> not in 'almost any' Basic, C, Fortran, Cobol, or their derivitives.

I assume, you meant to say "ALGOL derivatives" since Pascal has been derived
from ALGOL itself. Also, both FORTRAN-90 and FORTRAN-95 have no problem with
negative subscripting, to say nothing of their ability to allocate and
deallocate arrays at run-time.

> : and I do not see any reason why COBOL should be deprived of this
> : functionality.
>
> Probably because no one saw any reason why it would be useful.

The same can be said about telephone, radio, quantum mechanics, television,
air conditioning, space flights, automatic transmission, transistor, computer.
Nobody envisioned a fraction of their usefulness before they started being
used. In the case of the negative subsripts, there is at least an suspocion
that it could be useful because in a number of languages (OK, not 'almost
any') it has already been used. Besides, the functionality of a language must
go ahead of the user demand, not behind it. As an example, look at SAS. Any
time the life requires a new functionality, chances are it is already there.
If not, SAS users scream bloody murder, the item is put on SAS Ballot, and is
usually implemented by the SAS Institute in the very next release. And this is
despite of the fact that SAS's functionality compares to that of COBOL like a
Swiss knife to a toothpick.

> : I meant to ask, therefore, since in COBOL it is just
> : OCCURS so many times, is there a syntactic option I might not be aware
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It answers my question, and it is too bad.

>
> : so that the elements of the table could be subsequently referenced using
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> its arrays.  In business things are counted from 1.

Not necessarily. Look up the examples I gave in the reply to Howard.

> What does the -57th item mean ?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> this.  In Cobol just have an offset.

It is not a big deal to compute an offset, but it should be the business of a
compiler, not programmer, all the more that implementing negative subscripting
at the compiler level is as trivial as natural subscripting. Likewise, it is
no big deal to grab the address corresponding to the beginning of a table and
derive the pointer to the given element from the structural item length and
subscript number, but luckily, COBOL does it for us automatically.

> In other languages there may be a 'associative array' (but not in Pascal).

Right, but it has nothing to do with the integer negative subscripts.
Associative arrays are hash tables and therefore can be subscripted by an
arbitrary character string - a totally different concept and implementation. I
would love to see them implemented in COBOL (and also in PL/I and SAS, even
though the latter has at least something similar, i.e. the formats, which I
think is a kludge and needs to be complemented with the real thing).

Kind regards,
======================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
======================
```

```
Paul M. Dorfman <sashole@earthlink.net> wrote:
:> Probably because no one saw any reason why it would be useful.

: The same can be said about telephone, radio, quantum mechanics, television,
: air conditioning, space flights, automatic transmission, transistor, computer.
: Nobody envisioned a fraction of their usefulness before they started being
: used. In the case of the negative subsripts, there is at least an suspocion

Maybe so, but you seem to want to add several new features to Cobol just
because they exist in other languages.

That is to say, if negative subscripts are available in other languages
and are a 'tv' then you are asking for this to be added to Cobol is
like adding a tv to an automatic transmission.  Perhaps it may be
useful, perhaps it will work, we won't know unless we add it.

: that it could be useful because in a number of languages (OK, not 'almost
: any') it has already been used. Besides, the functionality of a language must
: go ahead of the user demand, not behind it.

Perhaps the requirement in Cobol is to _not_ have negative subscripts
or even zero.  Cobol programmers tend not to be whizz kids trying out
every toy in the store and demanding whatever the kid next door has,
they tend to make programs that work and have to be maintained
several years later.  

Negative subscripts may not fit this model.

The other issue is that Cobol has indexed files which do an excellent
job of carrying the functionality of associative arrays and/or
arbitrary codes or values as lookups (and can be shared).

: 
: As an example, look at SAS. Any
: time the life requires a new functionality, chances are it is already there.
: If not, SAS users scream bloody murder, the item is put on SAS Ballot, and is
: usually implemented by the SAS Institute in the very next release. And this is
: despite of the fact that SAS's functionality compares to that of COBOL like a
: Swiss knife to a toothpick.

Fine, use SAS then, I prefer Cobol to be stable rather than adding
every little gadget that someone thinks up.


: Not necessarily. Look up the examples I gave in the reply to Howard.

Then write those examples in Algol or Pascal and be happy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **Message-ID:** `<38A537EF.794A1617@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A12679.76A04D67@earthlink.net> <87sevt$jkv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A26193.D151D556@earthlink.net> <880g24$isk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

The one time I really want to modify the offsets of an array is when I
want them to start at zero.  If my index is pic 9(04) (like our
postcode) I think that it would be clearer to say the array starts at
zero than add 1 all the time.  If the array is accessed multiple times
in the code and the add 1 is forgotten once you have a bug.

I personally think that it is clearer to say an array exists from 50
to 100 but I have not missed it much (I started with Pascal).

Computing an index is a waste of programmer time.  To say you can do
it another way is not useful, I could code in assembler but I choose
not to.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

change my unit of measurement.  We don't talk displacement in the real

Volker Bandke wrote:

> Sure we do - look at the 0 degree line through Greenwich, UK....., and the equator.  They
> provide a perfect, zero based coordinate system in the real world, really<g>

Good example.  A table of longitude lines would be natural to start with zero.  Still COBOL
use of tables starting with the first occurrence instead of the zeroith occurrence is
dominant in the real world even with exceptions.

It's interesting though that a runway going straight north is #36 not #0.  24 hour digital
clocks never display 00:00, and 12 hour digital clocks usually lie.  There's no such thing as
12:00 PM nor 12:00 AM.  The correct (if never used) terms are 12:00 M and 12:00 MM.   It's
OK, if I have to set an alarm for noon, I say 11:59 AM, as I know what time that is.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1343edf1.957b7a52@usw-ex0106-047.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A12679.76A04D67@earthlink.net> <87sevt$jkv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A26193.D151D556@earthlink.net> <880g24$isk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A537EF.794A1617@zip.com.au> <38A8126D.F1BD6C75@cusys.edu> <gs9oas8gjdf3vpbcoe7ketijvuisjh8fip@4ax.com> <38AC3333.EE72D32D@cusys.edu> <l7mr4.730$04.18766@news.swbell.net>`

```
>> Volker Bandke wrote:
>
>>The correct (if never used) terms are 12:00 M and 12:00 MM.
It's
>> OK, if I have to set an alarm for noon, I say 11:59 AM, as I
know
>what time that is.
>
>You're correct. 12:00 is either midnight or noon, never AM or
PM.
>
>And it's 500 B.C, but it's A.D. 500 (not 500 A.D.)
>
"a.m." is the Latin abbreviation for ante meridiem = before noon.
"m." is the Latin abbreviation for meridies = noon.
"p.m." is the Latin abbreviation for post meridiem = after noon.
Thus 12:00 A.M. is midnight at the beginning of whatever day.
(Twelve o'clock before noon.)
12:00 M. is twelve o'clock noon.
12:00 P.M. is midnight at the end of whatever day.
(Twelve o'clock after noon.)
And, regarding years, one might remember the Jewish way of
writing B.C.E. (before Common Era) and C.E. (Common Era).



* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Language

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B53C6D.32EEF2F8@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A12679.76A04D67@earthlink.net> <87sevt$jkv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A26193.D151D556@earthlink.net> <880g24$isk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A537EF.794A1617@zip.com.au> <38A8126D.F1BD6C75@cusys.edu> <gs9oas8gjdf3vpbcoe7ketijvuisjh8fip@4ax.com> <38AC3333.EE72D32D@cusys.edu> <l7mr4.730$04.18766@news.swbell.net> <1343edf1.957b7a52@usw-ex0106-047.remarq.com> <38B53B31.FDD390C9@cusys.edu>`

```
> I read that midnight is 12:00 mm.  (mid meridian).  But since virtually knows
> it, (including VCRs), it doesn't really matter.   While these days people say
> 2000 A.D. more often than A.D. 2000, I still would like to know the correct
> syntax of C.E. dates.  (2000 C.E. or C.E. 2000)?

In the field of data processing, I am constantly irritated by redundant acronyms
such as "PIN number".  I can accept (with a grimace) "data is", at least while
people whose profession is communication refer to the television media.  But why
create a shortcut jargon such as PIN and then ignore what it means??  How about
going to a "Personal Identification PIN Number ID"?
```

When I noticed that the SAME proposal for the draft Standard used "DATA IS"
in one place and "DATA ARE" in another, I asked our "technical editor" if
"we" had a standard for this.  (So I could decide which needed fixing.  I
know which is "correct" but I also know that "usage" may rule in the draft
revision.)  The answer that I received was that "we" have given up on this,
and BOTH are used (totally inconsistently) within the Standard.

OTOH, I wish that were the WORST of the problems with the draft <G>.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0F9ZXAAw8zq4Ew$s@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A12679.76A04D67@earthlink.net> <87sevt$jkv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A26193.D151D556@earthlink.net> <880g24$isk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A537EF.794A1617@zip.com.au> <38A8126D.F1BD6C75@cusys.edu> <38AAA1D9.1913CA83@zip.com.au>`

In article <38AAA1D9.1913CA83@zip.com.au>, Ken Foskey
<waratah@zip.com.au> writes
>> But in English/Business, it doesn't make sense to tell someone to
>> fill in the zeroith box on the form or the zeroith position in the
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

All versions of reality must be at variance to all other versions
(Heisenberg's Uncertainty Priciple) and it has nothing to do with the
nature of your problem(s). Are you sure that your 'tools of choice' can 
meet your requirements? If you extend the Uncertainty Principle, then
all programs must model reality but perhaps some to lesser degrees of
accuracy (like my spelling). Complexity may lead us into the simplistic
realm of fractals. BTW, the simplest program that I have had to maintain
for a long time also caused me the most heart-ache.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A63562.7C85885D@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A12679.76A04D67@earthlink.net> <87sevt$jkv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A26193.D151D556@earthlink.net> <880g24$isk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:

> Paul M. Dorfman <sashole@earthlink.net> wrote:
> :> Probably because no one saw any reason why it would be useful.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> useful, perhaps it will work, we won't know unless we add it.

I'd think it would be rather like adding a reverse gear to the transmission, would
not you? In a motor vehicle, I occasionally find it useful. Range checking would be
like adding a pair of good brakes, so that crushing into a wall would not be the
only way to stop it.

> : that it could be useful because in a number of languages (OK, not 'almost
> : any') it has already been used. Besides, the functionality of a language must
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> arbitrary codes or values as lookups (and can be shared).

First, COBOL per se has _no_  indexed files, as well as has _no_ intrinsic file
structures.  It is just one language that can access and process various types of
files, including, but not limited to, indexed files. PL/I, C, SAS can access and
process indexed files just as well as COBOL. Second, the speed of a properly
organized in-memory lookup cannot be substituted by any kind of disk lookup, no
matter how smartly an indexed file is organized.

> : As an example, look at SAS. Any
> : time the life requires a new functionality, chances are it is already there.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> every little gadget that someone thinks up.

Define "stable". COBOL had been perfectly stable before the advent of COBOLII that
added a host of new features. Does that mean that these features, clearly emulated
after other languages, are to be considered harmful "gadgets" someone had thought
up, or welcome additions to the language expanding its functionality?

Kind regards,
======================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
======================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<889fru$sts$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A12679.76A04D67@earthlink.net> <87sevt$jkv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A26193.D151D556@earthlink.net> <880g24$isk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A63562.7C85885D@mediaone.net> <hxVp4.2490$Ez.59281@news2.mia>`

```
Judson,
  I had a similar reaction (at first) but ASSUMED that the person writing
this meant that there is no "standard" definition of the internals of an
indexed file system.  This means that the indexed file system used by Micro
Focus is different from that used by Fujitsu which is different from that
used by IBM, etc (and each of these is INCOMPATIBLE - if you try using it
from one of the others).

Clearly (???) the language and semantics are the same (mostly) - but the
actual file systems are different (unless you use a 3rd party product such as
C-ISAM or Btrieve)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B20911.DAA7930@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A12679.76A04D67@earthlink.net> <87sevt$jkv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A26193.D151D556@earthlink.net> <880g24$isk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A63562.7C85885D@mediaone.net> <hxVp4.2490$Ez.59281@news2.mia>`

```
Judson,

I had explained it already but you snipped it. I was responding to
Richard Plinston who had written:

> The other issue is that Cobol has indexed files which do an excellent
> job of carrying the functionality of associative arrays and/or
> arbitrary codes or values as lookups (and can be shared).

To which I replied:

>First, COBOL per se has _no_  indexed files, as well as has _no_
intrinsic file
>structures.  It is just one language that can access and process various
types of
>files, including, but not limited to, indexed files. PL/I, C, SAS can
access and
>process indexed files just as well as COBOL.

Note that I did not state that COBOL is incapable of processing indexed
files (quite on the contrary). I just stated that the ability is not
unique to COBOL and objected to the expression "COBOL has indexed files".
COBOL per se does not "have" files of _any_ type, it simply has the
ability to process a variety of external file structures. You don't say
"COBOL has DB2 tables" just because COBOL has no problem working with
DB2, do you? It is not just semantics: Other languages, notably, SAS, do
"have" their intrinsic file types allocated, manipulated, maintained, and
used for whatever purpose necessary solely by means of the language
itself. Just as COBOL, such packages do not "have" indexed files, DB2
tables, sequential files, and so on, but can read them and write into
them.

Kind regards,
=====================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
=====================

Judson McClendon wrote:

> Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88t68h$1ml$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A12679.76A04D67@earthlink.net> <87sevt$jkv$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A26193.D151D556@earthlink.net> <880g24$isk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38A63562.7C85885D@mediaone.net> <hxVp4.2490$Ez.59281@news2.mia> <38B20911.DAA7930@mediaone.net>`

```
Paul,
  I think mentioning DB2 shows exactly WHY we (most of us) say that COBOL
*does* have indexed files.  It has native (and portable) language for using
indexed files - this is a part of the Standard COBOL language and works the
same in all environments.  On the other hand, DB2 (or even SQL) is *not* a
part of the COBOL language.

No Standard conforming COBOL implementation doesn't support indexed files.
   LOTS of Standard conforming COBOL implementations do not support DB2 (or
SQL)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A175A1.6F6DB4CA@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote:

> Paul M. Dorfman <sashole@mediaone.net
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> PERFORM VARYING INDX FROM 10 BY -1 UNTIL INDX = -20

I have had perform statements performing until a negative value such as
that is reached, but I am still trying to understand what business
requirement leads to having a true negative subscript.  Since COBOL
doesn't do any bounds checking, out-of-range subscripts can allow one to
walk through other working storage values, not to mention code and
such.  But that's not what COBOL is designed for.

Or is this question simply wanting to know if we will get a nice abend
when such is tried?

What is the question behind the question?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87s3ql$uv1$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A175A1.6F6DB4CA@cusys.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38A175A1.6F6DB4CA@cusys.edu...
> Jerry P wrote:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

"Standard" COBOL explicitly states that results are "unpredictible" when a
subscript becomes negative.  In the next Standard "bounds checking" will
actually be a part of the Standard (where it may be OPTIONALLY selected or
ignored).

FYI - many vendors already have a method of checking subscript "out of range"
already.  The interesting difference between most existing implementations
and what the next Standard says is that the next Standard will require that
EACH subscript in a multi-level element be "within range" while existing
implementations often only check if the the total calculation puts the
element within the table.  This impacts a case where you have a 2 by 3
occurence table.  The next Standard will require subscript of (3 2) to get an
"out of bounds" error - while most current implementations would allow it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A25475.C7C1780E@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A175A1.6F6DB4CA@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> Jerry P wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> requirement leads to having a true negative subscript.

I have tried to give a number of real-life examples. Sorry if I have failed.

>  Since COBOL
> doesn't do any bounds checking, out-of-range subscripts can allow one to
> walk through other working storage values,

But what does the ability of a table to be subscipted by a signed integer
have to do with the bound checking? No matter whether one has declared a
table in the range (1:100000) or (-50000: 49999), the program will S0C4 if
the subscript is out of range. How does allowing the table to be indexed by
natural numbers only instead of signed integers make it less of a problem?
One must make sure that the subscript is in range in the either case.

> not to mention code and
> such.  But that's not what COBOL is designed for.

COBOL (and not only COBOL) is designed to carry out the meaning of business
logic programmatically. For example, card balances are subdivided in ranges
used in marketing as one of the targeting criteria; each range is
collectively designated by an integer value whose sign naturally reflects
whether the balance is negative or positive. Say there are 11 ranges like
that, from -5 to 0 to +5. If one desires to process such an entity as a
group placing other fields corresponding to the ranges in the appropriate
buckets, it is most straighforward to interpret the business meaning in a
program by declaring a table indexed from -5 to +5 including 0 as zero
balance; but it is unnatural and contrived to associate -5 with 1, 0 with 6,
and 5 with 11.

> Or is this question simply wanting to know if we will get a nice abend
> when such is tried?

That too. And why not?

Kind regards,
==================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
==================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A2C6A8.F22460DE@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <C%3o4.1913$nF5.55269@news.swbell.net> <38A175A1.6F6DB4CA@cusys.edu> <38A25475.C7C1780E@earthlink.net>`

```
"Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:

> > I have had perform statements performing until a negative value such as
> > that is reached, but I am still trying to understand what business
> > requirement leads to having a true negative subscript.
>
> I have tried to give a number of real-life examples. Sorry if I have failed.

I read your examples after I read and replied to the above message.  Such are
the vagaries of the internet.


> >  Since COBOL
> > doesn't do any bounds checking, out-of-range subscripts can allow one to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> One must make sure that the subscript is in range in the either case.

There is no guarantee that a SOC4 will occur.  I have seen SOC7, and I have even
seen the program "work" with data outside of the boundaries.  But as I read this
before your explanation, I thought you were wondering what happens if you put in
a negative subscript in a COBOL table. Since COBOL tables cannot be declared in
any way but zero to whatever, I figured you were trying to access data outside
of the bounds of the table.

I have had to do that - when my cobol wouldn't handle as large of a table as I
like, and while I dislike it intensely, it is possible (if not reliably
portable).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A03FA5.415C79B0@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net>`

```
"Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:

> Judson,
>
> A very lucid, well written tutorial, thanks. A related question: Can a COBOL
> subscript be negative? If not, what is the reason grandeur?

What business function would you use a negative subscript for?

> And a related
> observation (cannot contain myself on this one): As much as I like COBOL, its
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> being "self-explanatory" becomes superfluous.

So what's the big deal?  They both seem the same to me, neither more monstrous,
neither more clear.  Is it that hard to read or write an extra word?  Are you allergic
to the word "varying"?  Does that monster word give you the heebie jeebies?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A11E73.32BA0B37@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <38A03FA5.415C79B0@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> "Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In my org, e.g., transaction codes are given negative and positive integer values
depending on their business meaning. I may not approve of this and even consider it
obtuse, but this has been devised by business analysts, and I as a programmer, I am not in
the position of making them change it. I have to work with these business entities, and
they are processed in groups on a regular basis. Another example is a job stream where a
COBOL program accepts a file from a SAS program producing a file with SAS date values in
their native format. As you of course know, a SAS date value is just a number of days
elapsed since January 1, 1960, so the dates preceding the "beginning" are small negative
integers. The aforementioned COBOL program also has to process them in groups and must
return them in the output in the same format, since the next SAS program produces a report
based on those dates, and it is paramount that they do not get converted. A whole slew of
entities pertaining to APR computations are discrete signed integers, and they are also
frequently subject to group processing, and so on. I can give many more examples but hope
the above suffices to answer the question. Sure it is no big deal, and a negative value
can always be rescaled by adding a suitable constant; however, it means that the code
addresses deficiencies of the language rather than the business goal. But likewise, what
is wrong about allowing negative array subscripting? Other languages - PL/I, Fortran, SAS,
Basic, to name a few - have it, so what is the big deal? Yeah, I've heard: "It is not
there because there is no need for it" about COMPUTE, inline PERFORMS, intrinsic
functions, CONTINUE, EVALUATE - all in due time, and what? They have arrived, and nobody
seems to regret having them. Give me a functionality, and I will find how to apply
it.

> > And a related
> > observation (cannot contain myself on this one): As much as I like COBOL, its
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> to the word "varying"?  Does that monster word give you the heebie jeebies?

It is not hard to read or write an extra word, but it is harder and not necessary. While
it is OK in a natural language, in a  _programming_ language any token not bearing
absolutely unalienable functionality makes a program less readable, more difficult to
write and understand. If 100 per cent of COBOL programmers perfectly understand the
meaning of PERFORM SUB=1 TO 10 without a word of explanation or having to look it up in a
manual - _that_ is called self-explanatory -, writing,  reading, visually interpreting,
typing, or having anything to do with

PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1  UNTIL SUB=10?

serves absolutely no goal, and therefore it is simply a verbal garbage - including the
word VARYING. To say nothing of the fact that the very construct PERFORM UNTIL has been
counterintuitive from the very beginning, actually meaning WHILE (so much for the much
heralded "English-like syntax"), but instead of seeing it fixed _properly_ by admitting
the fault, adding PERFORM WHILE, and placing the meanings accordingly, we see the dirty
patch "WITH TEST AFTER" instead. Never once have I heard anyone asking what DO UNTIL or DO
WHILE mean in PL/I; but turn a couple of web pages back, and you will see the question
"What the heck does WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL mean if it is _already_ UNTIL?" in this very
group.

On a different note, I did not and do not intend to start any "language war" here; I have
simply brought a point for discussion. I work with COBOL among other languages, find it
adequate in most respects and insufficient in others, and actually like it better than a
number of tongues. However, by no means I see it as a religious ground and do not have to
also like a syntactic feature if I find it excessive, verbose, confusing, and serving no
purpose, and feel it is perfectly right to openly criticize it.

Kind regards,
==================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
==================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A177FA.E39A65A6@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <38A03FA5.415C79B0@cusys.edu> <38A11E73.32BA0B37@earthlink.net>`

```
"Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:

> Howard Brazee wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> they are processed in groups on a regular basis.

So you are given a negative value and don't choose to normalize it.  The code looks like this:

01   FILLER                PIC X(100000).
01   MY-TABLE.
       05   MY-RECORD     OCCURS 1000.
              10   MY-FIELD-1    PIC X(5)....

MOVE INPUT-DATA       to MY-RECORD (-8).

I infer that you're expecting your data to be somewhere in the FILLER.  I have done similar
tricks to handle large tables with compilers which won't handle them.  But an optimizing
compiler can move working storage fields around.  Your code is no longer portable and can
break with the next compiler upgrade or with someone compiling with different compiler
options.

Wouldn't it be better to offset all of your index values to make them positive so that they
fit within the occurs?   Or am I missing your point?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz
- **Date:** 2000-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<886vug$5ff$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <38A03FA5.415C79B0@cusys.edu> <38A11E73.32BA0B37@earthlink.net>`

```
In article <38A11E73.32BA0B37@earthlink.net>,
  "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net> wrote:
>
> In my org, e.g., transaction codes are given negative and positive
integer values
> depending on their business meaning. I may not approve of this and
even consider it
> obtuse, but this has been devised by business analysts, and I as a
programmer, I am not in
> the position of making them change it. I have to work with these
business entities, and
> they are processed in groups on a regular basis.

And in what way would having these transaction types as subscripts
be useful ?  It may be for keeping a count by type, but certainly
not for storing or processing the transactions themselves.

In fact if you are processing a subset of transactions (the ones
ypu recognise) and counting all then it can be dangerous and
wasteful to have the type as a sunscript - you must either have
an array large enough for all possible values ( -32768 .. 32768 )
or filter out those outside the size you had determined initially.

Business types may invent new transaction types which would not
affect your program (ie should just pass through) but may have
a type outside your subscript range giving an exception, or worse
a corruption of data elsewhere.

The _safe_ way to count the transactions is to use a table that
hold the type and count and create an entry each time a new type
is found.  This removes a constraint within the program and allows
it to count _any_ type regardless.

> Another example is a job stream where a
> COBOL program accepts a file from a SAS program producing a file with
SAS date values in
> their native format. As you of course know, a SAS date value is just
a number of days
> elapsed since January 1, 1960, so the dates preceding the "beginning"
are small negative
> integers.

Would you really process dates from pre 1960 to current using an array
?

Given that each array item is a single entry, how would you deal
with two transactions on one day ?

> the above suffices to answer the question.

No it doesn't.  It only shows examples of negative values, not
of how it would be useful to have negative subscripts.  One would
also have negative values in a credit invoice but no would would
ever suggest that it would be useful to use this as an array
index.



Sure it is no big deal,
and a negative value
> can always be rescaled by adding a suitable constant; however, it
means that the code
> addresses deficiencies of the language rather than the business goal.

"To a hammer all problems look like nails".

You seem to have dealt with all problem by using an array.  There are
_other_ ways of solving problems, when you eventually find these you
will realise that negative subscripts are a non-issue.

But likewise, what
> is wrong about allowing negative array subscripting? Other languages
- PL/I, Fortran, SAS,
> Basic, to name a few


> word VARYING. To say nothing of the fact that the very construct
PERFORM UNTIL has been
> counterintuitive from the very beginning, actually meaning WHILE (so
much for the much
> heralded "English-like syntax"), but instead of seeing it fixed
_properly_ by admitting
> the fault, adding PERFORM WHILE,

How could you be so wrong.

UNTIL and WHILE are opposite.

        UNTIL a > b
        WHILE a NOT > b

PERFORM UNTIL does not mean PERFORM WHILE


 and placing the meanings
accordingly, we see the dirty
> patch "WITH TEST AFTER" instead. Never once have I heard anyone
asking what DO UNTIL or DO
> WHILE mean in PL/I; but turn a couple of web pages back, and you will
see the question
> "What the heck does WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL mean if it is _already_
UNTIL?" in this very
> group.

Al that shows is you are not the one one unable to understand
the variations of PERFORM.



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ek92as8k2f6ulutb5jr1osuvq9vog010vq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <38A03FA5.415C79B0@cusys.edu>`

```
On Tue, 08 Feb 2000 09:09:09 -0700 Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>What business function would you use a negative subscript for?

There is a business need to be able to specify the range of the index and have
the compiler compute the absolute values.

Something like

                 05  year-statistics occurs from index 1977 to 2020.	 
                     10  eastern-sales pic ..........

and have the compiler convert

                     move eastern-sales (whatever-year) to  .....

to

                     move eastern-sales (whatever-year - 1976) to ......

so that the code is more readable.

   [ snipped ]
```
