```
> > And a related
> > observation (cannot contain myself on this one): As much as I like COBOL, its
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> to the word "varying"?  Does that monster word give you the heebie jeebies?

It is not hard to read or write an extra word, but it is harder and not
necessary. While it is OK in a natural language, in a  _programming_
language any token not bearing absolutely unalienable functionality
makes a program less readable, more difficult to write and understand.
If 100 per cent of COBOL programmers perfectly understand the meaning of
PERFORM SUB=1 TO 10 without a word of explanation or having to look it
up in a
manual - _that_ is called self-explanatory -, writing,  reading,
visually interpreting, typing all of

PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1  UNTIL SUB=10?

serves absolutely no goal, and therefore it is simply a verbal garbage -
including the word VARYING. To say nothing of the fact that the very
construct PERFORM UNTIL has been counterintuitive from the very
beginning, actually meaning WHILE (so much for the much heralded
"English-like syntax"), but instead of seeing it fixed _properly_ by
admitting the fault, adding PERFORM WHILE, and placing the meanings
accordingly, we see the dirty patch "WITH TEST AFTER" instead. Never
once have I heard anyone asking what DO UNTIL or DO WHILE mean in PL/I;
but turn a couple of web pages back, and you will see the question "What
the heck does WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL mean if it is _already_ UNTIL?" in
this very group.

On a different note, I did not and do not intend to start any "language
war" here; I have simply brought a point for discussion. I work with
COBOL among other languages, find it adequate in most respects and
insufficient in others, and actually like it better than a number of
tongues. However, by no means I see it as a religious ground and do not
have to also like a syntactic feature if I find it excessive, verbose,
confusing, and serving no purpose, and feel it is perfectly right to
openly criticize it.

Kind regards,
==================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
==================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A11FA5.976F0CCA@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <38A03FA5.415C79B0@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> "Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In my org, e.g., transaction codes are given negative and positive
integer values depending on their business meaning. I may not approve of
this and even consider it obtuse, but this has been devised by business
analysts, and I as a programmer, I am not in the position of making them
change it. I have to work with these business entities, and they are
processed in groups on a regular basis. Another example is a job stream
where a COBOL program accepts a file from a SAS program producing a file
with SAS date values in their native format. As you of course know, a
SAS date value is just a number of days elapsed since January 1, 1960,
so the dates preceding the "beginning" are small negative integers. The
aforementioned COBOL program also has to process them in groups and must
return them in the output in the same format, since the next SAS program
produces a report based on those dates, and it is paramount that they do
not get converted. A whole slew of
entities pertaining to APR computations are discrete signed integers,
and they are also frequently subject to group processing, and so on. I
can give many more examples but hope the above suffices to answer the
question. Sure it is no big deal, and a negative value can always be
rescaled by adding a suitable constant; however, it means that the code
addresses deficiencies of the language rather than the business goal.
But likewise, what
is wrong about allowing negative array subscripting? Other languages -
PL/I, Fortran, SAS, Basic, to name a few - have it, so what is the big
deal? Yeah, I've heard: "It is not there because there is no need for
it" about COMPUTE, inline PERFORMS, intrinsic functions, CONTINUE,
EVALUATE - all in due time, and what? They have arrived, and nobody
seems to regret having them. Give me a functionality, and I will find
how to apply it.

> > And a related
> > observation (cannot contain myself on this one): As much as I like COBOL, its
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> to the word "varying"?  Does that monster word give you the heebie jeebies?

It is not hard to read or write an extra word, but it is harder and not
necessary. While it is OK in a natural language, in a  _programming_
language any token not bearing absolutely unalienable functionality
makes a program less readable, more difficult to write and understand.
If 100 per cent of COBOL programmers perfectly understand the meaning of
PERFORM SUB=1 TO 10 without a word of explanation or having to look it
up in a
manual - _that_ is called self-explanatory -, writing,  reading,
visually interpreting, typing all of

PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1  UNTIL SUB=10?

serves absolutely no goal, and therefore it is simply a verbal garbage -
including the word VARYING. To say nothing of the fact that the very
construct PERFORM UNTIL has been counterintuitive from the very
beginning, actually meaning WHILE (so much for the much heralded
"English-like syntax"), but instead of seeing it fixed _properly_ by
admitting the fault, adding PERFORM WHILE, and placing the meanings
accordingly, we see the dirty patch "WITH TEST AFTER" instead. Never
once have I heard anyone asking what DO UNTIL or DO WHILE mean in PL/I;
but turn a couple of web pages back, and you will see the question "What
the heck does WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL mean if it is _already_ UNTIL?" in
this very group.

On a different note, I did not and do not intend to start any "language
war" here; I have simply brought a point for discussion. I work with
COBOL among other languages, find it adequate in most respects and
insufficient in others, and actually like it better than a number of
tongues. However, by no means I see it as a religious ground and do not

Kind regards,

Paul M. Dorfman
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A18B51.DA0EE6C7@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <38A03FA5.415C79B0@cusys.edu> <38A11FA5.976F0CCA@earthlink.net>`

```
One thing you may wish to look into is using pointers to act as relative subscripts in a
table.  In PL/I I have used relative subscripts.  You can point to the middle of a table and
then point to the side.  I haven't done this and have limited experience with COBOL
pointers, but I would be interested in seeing how well it works.

My natural inclination is to offset your keys to a positive integer and use it as a regular
subscript though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aQgo4.9578$RT4.173615@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net>`

```
Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
>
>And a related
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB > 10,

There are a few points to this that I haven't seen others mention.

One is that COBOL is supposed to be 'English like'.  "SUB=1 TO 10" is
not English like, therefore violates the original premise of COBOL.  I
can't give you specific, hard data here, but I will say that one of the
things that I have 'intuitively' learned over the years is that it is
very important to have a theme, paradigm, strategy, whatever you want
to call it, and to stay with it, unless and until it is proven to be
unsatisfactory.  It provides a coherence that is not present otherwise.
And creative, productive construction is, in essence, the production
of coherence and order out of chaos, or other, less desirable or useful
order.  There is also a feature of human nature that we are creatures
of habit.  Habits are at the core of our ability to deal with a complex
environment.  Once something becomes so familiar it is a habit, then it
can be 'internalized' by our brain, then assigned to some subconscious
part of our mind, where it can be carried out with little or no effort
at the conscious level.  We employed this process over and over as we
learned to crawl, walk, tie our shoes, etc.  Though created from the
bottom up, the result is a top down, stepwise refinement of common
tasks, to permit the enormous details to be managed at levels far below
the conscious level, where we deal with highly abstracted forms.  Because
we are constructed this way, we naturally tend to use habits in all our
activities, to a level most people hardly realize, and in things we
don't usually consider in this light.  Even when we are not aware of it,
our minds are searching and analyzing patterns, then using them.  We pick
up the body language and speech patterns of our friends, usually without
even realizing it.  We do the same thing with more abstract activities,
such as programming.  When we study a programming language, as we learn
the syntax an semantics of that language, our pattern recognition ability
automatically begins to internalize the 'theme' of the language, just as
it does in the other common tasks we do.  If the 'theme' is coherent, it
greatly facilitates this process, in the same way that learning a series
of interrelated facts is easier than learning a series of disjoint facts.
In other words, when a programming language has a clear and coherent
theme, and stays consistent to that theme, it makes mental processing of
that language quicker, easier and more reliable.

All of us have built an enormous body of patterns involving the syntax
and semantics of our native language(s).  COBOL is built upon English,
and when we learn COBOL, because the semantics are also built upon the
same base as English, we can use this body of pattern recognition that,
for native English speakers, is embedded and entrenched far deeper than
any later learned programming language can be.  The result is that we
have a built-in, habitual 'reflex' that automatically interprets and
checks syntax against semantics.  In other words, the 'style' of COBOL,
because it is based on our (English speakers) own natural language, is
reinforced by the body of English natural language pattern recognition
already learned.

I'm not sure it would be possible to quantify the above benefits, but
I am completely convinced of their validity.  I think they contribute
significantly to COBOL being one of the easier languages to learn and
master, at least by English speakers.  There are programmers who do
productive jobs as COBOL programmers, but who would never master C/C++.
I believe this to be an advantage of COBOL.

But a further benefit of COBOL being 'English like' is that it can be
largely read and understood by non programmers.  I have, on several
occasions, gone over code with accountants or other non programming
users, to make sure we were understanding each other.  I doubt if
anybody ever does this with C/C++.  (In APL you don't even do it with
another APL programmer! ;-)  Personally, I think this is a valuable
thing, and in this context,

    PERFORM SUB=1 TO 10

is certainly less readable to a non programmer than

    PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB > 10

which most English speakers would understand straight away. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A24D35.D3E90C99@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <aQgo4.9578$RT4.173615@news3.mia>`

```


Judson McClendon wrote:

> Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
> >
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> at the conscious level.

This is exactly my point. Once we have risen to a certain level of abstraction,
we can comfortably operate at this level without descending to more primitive
levels. Moreover, it is often almost impossible to operate at the lower levels
because they tend to fragment the whole into parts it takes a greater effort to
perceive in their entirety as a single entity. Also, you have not mentioned
that, besides English, we (or, at least, the vast majority of programmers,
business analysts, accountants, etc.) have learned basic arithmetic which has
also become part of our subconscious nature. Mathematics, as Willard Gibbs has
pointed out, is a language in its own right. I assert that even the marginal
"math" taught in schools nowadays is more than sufficient to comprehend the
meaning of the statement

PERFORM SUB=1 TO 10

way more readily than

PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB > 10

which, by the way, also requires an explanation of "SUB > 10" usually boiling
down to the notion that in COBOL, UNTIL actually means WHILE, and therefore the
conditional clause is evaluated _before_ each iteration of the loop. How is
that coherent with the natural structure of English? Being curious, I have
conducted a little experiment today by showing ten customer reps (who, as you
realize, are not 'technical' by any stretch of imagination) the two loops

PERFORM SUB=1 TO 10
   WRITE 'STUFF'
END-PERFORM

and

PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB > 10
   WRITE 'STUFF'
END-PERFORM

and asking what, in their opinion, they were supposed to accomplish. The
result: nine out of ten understood immediately and 'subconsciously' what "SUB=1
TO 10" meant and proceeded straight to "WRITE 'STUFF'", while stumbling upon
the meaning of the lengthy PERFORM VARYING.... Does the experiment resolve the
dispute?

We employed this process over and over as we

> learned to crawl, walk, tie our shoes, etc.  Though created from the
> bottom up, the result is a top down, stepwise refinement of common
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> which most English speakers would understand straight away. :-)

As follows from above, I beg to differ. Your assertion is certainly valid in
the theory. However, as you know, if the experiment says 'no' to the theory, it
means 'no'; if it says 'yes', it means 'maybe'.

Kind regards,
=====================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
=====================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PaAo4.6736$HX.131626@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<s9s0vt8gdvd116@corp.supernews.com> <aZBn4.4008$Xi6.50644@news2.mia> <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net> <aQgo4.9578$RT4.173615@news3.mia> <38A24D35.D3E90C99@earthlink.net>`

```
Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>meaning of the statement

Not quite.  While it is true that, when we are dealing with abstractions,
the details are not necessarily involved at that level, it is also true that
they are involved at the point where they are processed, even if not
consciously.  My point is that using a deeply ingrained, long established,
pattern structure consistently leads almost certainly to greater efficiency
at that level.  And if you take poll of people who last studied math ten
or more years ago, you would be surprised how few people think in
those terms. :-)  Anyone involved with child development will tell you
that young children have a far higher capacity to learn language than
they do later, even during childhood.  Language is a tool for the
expression of ideas and concepts, and a computer language is only
different in that it is far more narrowly focused, highly formalized and
(hopefully :-) completely unambiguous.  Also, nothing academically
learned after a person reaches the age of six (actually about age four),
will ever be so internalized as to become 'instinctual' to the degree of
those things learned at a very early age.  Severe physical or emotional
trauma might approach it, but certainly nothing academic.

One common expression of what I am trying to say is called the
"Rule of Least Astonishment."  In design, generally, the principle is to
so design the human interface such that, if the user does not know
how to do a certain thing using the interface, that the interface would
work so as to 'cause the least astonishment'.  In other words, work
most closely to what the user would expect.  The reason for this rule
is that it was learned long ago, long before computers, that human
interfaces that match what is as close as to what would be normally
expected by an untrained person are easier to learn, faster to use,
and produce lower error rates.  Such is what one would expect, is
it not? :-)  Natural English (or any other natural language) is quite
different from mathematical notation.  COBOL has the feature of
following natural English as closely as a programming language
might, and still be effective and non-ambiguous.  Mixing paradigms
midstream for no apparent reason would be, by definition, a violation
of the Rule of Least Astonishment.  Actually, these principles can be
easily verified by testing, and ergonomics labs live and die by them.
Being completely unaware of the effects does not invalidate them. :-)

We're not discussing "can work" or "can't work" here, we're discussing
whether mixing paradigms simply for the sake of some brevity would
enhance or detract from a programming language. I contend that the
body of evidence and established knowledge on human nature and
mental processing shows that you pay a penalty for every violation
of the Rule of Least Astonishment.  The more violations, the greater
the cumulative effect on the mental time and energy needed to
process the rules, each reducing efficiency to some extent.  Personally,
I don't think gaining a small amount of brevity makes a good trade. :-)
```

```
Paul M. Dorfman wrote in message <389FB628.B8824EB3@mediaone.net>...

>There are things in "the other languages" which I definitely dislike seeing
in
>COBOL in fear that the language might become cryptic, but there are always
the
>balance and measure. Some excessive wordiness has already been eliminated
from
>COBOL progressively by COMPUTE and intrinsic functions (although the syntax
of
>their usage is completely moronic; especially ridiculous is the necessity
of using
>the very word FUNCTION, as if the compiler could not parse the name of a
function
>- reserved! - out of the context). There is still a long way to go.


I thought you were describing the German language until I looked at the
message header.

But I digress.

Sure, COBOL can be wordy and sometimes cryptic, but you get used to it.

I can see you come from a BASIC language background. I've been know to write
a few lines of BASIC, too, and other than now and then forgetting that the
"THEN" in an IF statement is optional in COBOL but mandatory in BASIC, I
seem to get by in both languages.

You can master COBOL the same way you get to Carnegie Hall: Practice,
Practice, Practice!

MCM
```

<a id="t-71519fc750"></a>
## Thread: simple newbie question — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### simple newbie question

- **From:** Sorden <steve@sorden.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389D23C8.192DCF7E@sorden.com>`

```
I've been working on this for hours and I'm stumped. Obviously I'm new
to Cobol.

I'm loading a sequential file, formatting the data, and printing it to a
report file. I want to take some of the numeric data and turn them in to
decimal values.

This is the code from the working storage section:

           01  400-Work-Record.
               05  400-ID              Pic 9(5).
               05  400-Name-First      Pic X(10).
               05  400-Name-Last       Pic X(15).
               05  400-Dept            Pic X(12).
               05  400-Hours           Pic 99v99.
               05  400-Rate            Pic 99v99.

           01  500-Detail.
               05  500-ID              Pic 9(5).
               05  Filler              Pic X(2).
               05  500-Name-First      Pic X(10).
               05  Filler              Pic X(2).
               05  500-Name-Last       Pic X(15).
               05  Filler              Pic X(2).
               05  500-Dept            Pic X(12).
               05  Filler              Pic X(2).
               05  500-Hours           Pic 99v99.
               05  Filler              Pic X(2).
               05  500-Rate            Pic 99v99.

My problem is the Hours and Rate fields. The values are passed with
decimals to the 500-Hours and 500-Rate fields and these fields hold the
values with decimals, but the decimals are not included when I write
500-Detail to the report file.

How can I get 500-Detail to pass the numeric values with decimals?

Thanks,
Steve Sorden
```

#### ↳ Re: simple newbie question

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389da772.217628543@news.freewwweb.com>`
- **References:** `<389D23C8.192DCF7E@sorden.com>`

```
On Sun, 06 Feb 2000 00:33:29 -0700, Sorden <steve@sorden.com> wrote:

>I'm loading a sequential file, formatting the data, and printing it to a
>report file. I want to take some of the numeric data and turn them in to
>decimal values.
>

You want to use "Numeric Edited" fields in your report.  Check out
chapter 3 of my book, Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours. You can
view the chapter on the internet at http://www.barnesandnoble.com  -
after searching for the title.
```

#### ↳ Re: simple newbie question

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B235F9421C3D921.5478C42F3AD5D7AE.BE0E272EC8D15349@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<389D23C8.192DCF7E@sorden.com>`

```
On Sun, 06 Feb 2000 00:33:29 -0700, Sorden <steve@sorden.com>
enlightened us:

>I've been working on this for hours and I'm stumped. Obviously I'm new
>to Cobol.
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>               05  500-Hours           Pic 99v99.

This needs to be 99.99    The "v" says implied decimal position which
means it is not really in the data but if it were it would be here.
You have it defined correctly in your input data.

>               05  Filler              Pic X(2).
>               05  500-Rate            Pic 99v99.

As above, this should be 99.99

>
>My problem is the Hours and Rate fields. The values are passed with
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Do you need a silencer if you are going to shoot a mime? 


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: simple newbie question

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<erWXxWHc$GA.72@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<389D23C8.192DCF7E@sorden.com>`

```
The Pic clause of the report values need to be changed.

from >                05  500-Rate            Pic 99v99.
to >                05  500-Rate            Pic 99.99.



Sorden <steve@sorden.com> wrote in message
news:389D23C8.192DCF7E@sorden.com...
> I've been working on this for hours and I'm stumped. Obviously I'm new
> to Cobol.
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: simple newbie question

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l3oq9s0tdrj4030e7kqgdpjfdv10pphd4r@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<389D23C8.192DCF7E@sorden.com>`

```
On Sun, 06 Feb 2000 00:33:29 -0700 Sorden <steve@sorden.com> wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>               05  500-Hours           Pic 99v99.
                                             99.99         or
                                             z9.99 
:>               05  Filler              Pic X(2).
:>               05  500-Rate            Pic 99v99.
                                             99.99         or
                                             z9.99 

:>My problem is the Hours and Rate fields. The values are passed with
:>decimals to the 500-Hours and 500-Rate fields and these fields hold the
:>values with decimals, but the decimals are not included when I write
:>500-Detail to the report file.

:>How can I get 500-Detail to pass the numeric values with decimals?

V is an implied decimal point.

. (period) is a printed decimal point.
```

<a id="t-c340eb8559"></a>
## Thread: Whatever happened to UTAH COBOL? — 2 msgs

### Whatever happened to UTAH COBOL?

- **From:** "Gregory Brooks" <brooksg@phenixcable.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Exin4.321$25.47051@eagle.america.net>`

```
This compiler was written by Ellis Computing, Inc. in the late 80's.
I received it taking a home study course with NRI.  At one time is was
called Nevada COBOL.
It is a neat and simple compiler.  Ellis Computing also had UTAH Pascal and
UTAH Fortran.

Can't find any good links searching web.  Its like they fell off the face of
the earth.

Thanks for any assistance finding out information,
Gregory B. Brooks
```

#### ↳ Re: Whatever happened to UTAH COBOL?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87kl4i$s3i$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<Exin4.321$25.47051@eagle.america.net>`

```
Gregory  Brooks <brooksg@phenixcable.net> wrote:
: This compiler was written by Ellis Computing, Inc. in the late 80's.
: I received it taking a home study course with NRI.  At one time is was
: called Nevada COBOL.
: It is a neat and simple compiler.  Ellis Computing also had UTAH Pascal and
: UTAH Fortran.

: Can't find any good links searching web.  Its like they fell off the face of
: the earth.

They did 'fall off the face of the earth', for good reason, they
were awful.  They were not completely standard, were not complete,
were to a standard that is now over 25 years old, were slow and
buggy.

IIRC they did not even have an ISAM file system.
```

<a id="t-1d01e7e765"></a>
## Thread: perform with test after — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### perform with test after

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kWon4.5095$NS3.16208@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Hi,

I'm a COBOL student and need your help.  Our book and teacher aren't too
clear on this (not clear enough
for this tired brain, anyway).  Now, I do have this program working two
ways, so this particular problem is just for future reference and because it
bugs the heck out of me.

We're into tables and arrays.  We have a data file with high and low
temperature data for a year.  The user enters a beginning and ending date
and the program finds the high, low, etc.....  Now, I have the program
search for the beginning date, then do a PERFORM until it finds the ending
date.  I want it to find >= to then end date, yet still execute the
statements after the PERFORM, in other words, do the test at the bottom of
the loop (do until??).  I was told PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER will do this, but
it doesn't seem to be working,  I know how many temperature records they are
and this is one short.  Does the WITH TEST AFTER only work on the 1st run
thru the loop???

Here's the code:

260-LOOK-UP-REST.
      PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER
          UNTIL AR-DATE(AR-INDEX) >= AR-END-DATE OR
                ARRAY-FULL
          ADD 1 TO AR-NUMBER-OF-DAYS
          ADD AR-HIGH-TEMP(AR-INDEX) TO AR-HIGH-ACCUM
          ADD AR-LOW-TEMP(AR-INDEX) TO AR-LOW-ACCUM
          IF AR-HIGH-TEMP(AR-INDEX) > AR-NEW-HIGH
              COMPUTE AR-NEW-HIGH = AR-HIGH-TEMP(AR-INDEX)
          END-IF
          IF AR-LOW-TEMP(AR-INDEX) < AR-NEW-LOW
              COMPUTE AR-NEW-LOW = AR-LOW-TEMP(AR-INDEX)
          END-IF
          SET AR-INDEX UP BY 1
          ADD 1 TO WA-NUMBER-OF-INDEXES
   END-PERFORM
.

If I change the first line to PERFORM UNTIL AR-DATE(AR-INDEX) = AR-END-DATE
and then add
IF AR-HIGH-TEMP(AR-INDEX) IS NOT NUMERIC   (indicating end of array and a
blank entry, I think....)
   SET ARRAY-FULL TO TRUE
    SET AR-INDEX DOWN BY 1
END-IF

then I get a good result.  I just want to know how to properly use the TEST
AFTER function.  Or any way to do a test at the bottom of the loop, run the
statements then do the test.  Which I think is a do until, right??

I also have this program working with subscripts, so I'm not asking for you
to do my homework..!!  I know how much you hate that!!!!!

Thanks!
Pat in Duluth, MN
```

#### ↳ Re: perform with test after

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Douglas Miller)
- **Date:** 2000-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kJpn4.153$as3.2324039@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<kWon4.5095$NS3.16208@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
In article <kWon4.5095$NS3.16208@newsfeed.slurp.net>, "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com> wrote:
>Hi,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>bugs the heck out of me.

You get ten bonus points for posting your code here. :-)
My comments below, in the middle of the code.
>
>We're into tables and arrays.  We have a data file with high and low
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>                ARRAY-FULL
I believe this is the problem here. You're not filling the array at this point 
-- it's already been loaded, and now you're scanning it -- so there is no need 
to test for ARRAY-FULL. Since the array has already been populated, presumably 
ARRAY-FULL is already true, and the program is doing exactly as instructed: it 
PERFORMs the instructions inside the loop once, then tests for the end-of-loop 
condition, sees that ARRAY-FULL is true, and terminates the loop after one 
iteration.

Instead, what you need to test for is AR-INDEX > MAX-ARRAY-INDEX (or whatever 
name you have given the variable that holds the number of array elements). 
Alternatively, you could add one more element at the end of the array, and set 
the date in that entry to HIGH-VALUES; then you need only to test for 
AR-DATE(AR-INDEX) > AR-END-DATE.

>          ADD 1 TO AR-NUMBER-OF-DAYS
>          ADD AR-HIGH-TEMP(AR-INDEX) TO AR-HIGH-ACCUM
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>          ADD 1 TO WA-NUMBER-OF-INDEXES
Not sure what this statement does, but I'm guessing that ARRAY-FULL is a 
conditional (88-level item) under WA-NUMBER-OF-INDEXES. This statement appears 
unnecessary here. Again, remember that you're now merely scanning an 
already-populated array.The count of array elements won't change any more, so 
you should not alter that variable.
>   END-PERFORM
>..
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>   SET ARRAY-FULL TO TRUE
Again, your array is already filled. This serves no purpose.
>    SET AR-INDEX DOWN BY 1
>END-IF
>
>then I get a good result.  I just want to know how to properly use the TEST

You're using TEST AFTER properly. You just don't have the termination 
condition quite right yet. You don't need to check if the array is full, you 
need to check to see if you have scanned past the end of it.

>AFTER function.  Or any way to do a test at the bottom of the loop, run the
>statements then do the test.  Which I think is a do until, right??

PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER is the Cobol equivalent of DO UNTIL in other 
languages. Perhaps it would help if you imagined how you might code the same 
problem in Pascal or C.

Feel free to e-mail me if you have questions.
```

#### ↳ Re: perform with test after

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u2wJ5aXc$GA.255@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<kWon4.5095$NS3.16208@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
I thing your problem is that you are not resetting the index to 1 before
each run thru the loop.

Try this:
Change
> 260-LOOK-UP-REST.
>       PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER
to
> 260-LOOK-UP-REST.
          SET  AR-INDEX  TO  1
>       PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER

news:kWon4.5095$NS3.16208@newsfeed.slurp.net...

it
> bugs the heck out of me.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

but
> it doesn't seem to be working,  I know how many temperature records they
are
> and this is one short.  Does the WITH TEST AFTER only work on the 1st run
> thru the loop???
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> If I change the first line to PERFORM UNTIL AR-DATE(AR-INDEX) =
AR-END-DATE
> and then add
> IF AR-HIGH-TEMP(AR-INDEX) IS NOT NUMERIC   (indicating end of array and a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> then I get a good result.  I just want to know how to properly use the
TEST

the
```

```
change.  You need a runtime unit for each platform.
```

<a id="t-7f9ce2e10a"></a>
## Thread: Pseudo conversational future — 4 this month / 14 total

_Thread started [2000-01](2000-01.md#t-7f9ce2e10a)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudo conversational future

- **From:** "Michael D. Kersey" <mdkersey@hal-pc.org>
- **Date:** 2000-02-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389F9858.F6113DAE@hal-pc.org>`
- **References:** `<387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-I44eeWdndhrR@localhost>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
<snipped> 
> Guess what.  As PC applications move "to the web" they BECOME pseudo
> conversational.  Any CGI application is pseudoconversational, up to
> and including the point of using hidden fields on the screen to hold
> place keeping data!  So we have not come as far as we appear to have
> come.
<snipped>
> Now, going the OTHER way, from a field by field display/accept program
> or an event driven GUI program, where there is code behind each
> object, is much much harder - but it's being done every day as people
> move applications into the browser.

Yes! Many mainframe programmers may not know it, but their CICS screens
are almost exactly like the internet's HTML forms, except that HTML
forms don't have quite as much functionality. Once one sees how similar
these two environments are, it should be very easy to make the move. The
underlying communications protocol is different: SNA versus TCP/IP, and
the screen definition languages are different. But otherwise, they are
almost the same thing. The web is much, much more similar to then
mainframe environment than either is to client/server event-driven
processing. 

BTW, does IBM have a CICS-based web server yet? So those "legacy" COBOL
apps can be used to handle HTML forms?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudo conversational future

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87oci0$fim$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-I44eeWdndhrR@localhost> <389F9858.F6113DAE@hal-pc.org>`

```
Michael D. Kersey <mdkersey@hal-pc.org> wrote:
: Thane Hubbell wrote:
: <snipped> 
:> Guess what.  As PC applications move "to the web" they BECOME pseudo
:> conversational.  Any CGI application is pseudoconversational, up to
:> and including the point of using hidden fields on the screen to hold
:> place keeping data!  So we have not come as far as we appear to have
:> come.

While this is true of CGI, this is not the only way of moving
"to the web".  For example see Flexus Client-server or Java
Client.

: <snipped>
:> Now, going the OTHER way, from a field by field display/accept program
:> or an event driven GUI program, where there is code behind each
:> object, is much much harder - but it's being done every day as people
:> move applications into the browser.

In general it is not suitable to just move screen based or field by
field applications to the web, do you want anyone with a browser
to be able to key in orders or maintain your customer file ?

If it is required to export the system to give wider access for
your own staff then telnet will do the job and requires no
changes for text based terminal systems.

If you want a public access system then it requires a proper
design and is not just a conversion.

: Yes! Many mainframe programmers may not know it, but their CICS screens
: are almost exactly like the internet's HTML forms, except that HTML
: forms don't have quite as much functionality. Once one sees how similar

HTML forms have _different_ functionality.  It is only a superficial
view to say it has less.

: these two environments are, it should be very easy to make the move. The
: underlying communications protocol is different: SNA versus TCP/IP, and
: the screen definition languages are different. But otherwise, they are
: almost the same thing. The web is much, much more similar to then
: mainframe environment than either is to client/server event-driven
: processing. 

Or completely different, depending on how you implement your
web access.

I guess this is an example of 'to a hammer all problems look like
nails'.  You have noticed how you could implement a 'CICS'
like CGI/HTML environment and think that this is all there is.

: BTW, does IBM have a CICS-based web server yet? So those "legacy" COBOL
: apps can be used to handle HTML forms?

I think that they have had this for some time.  But who wants
to export in-house CICS screen to the world ?  Which web users
could handle using your CICS screens without the training your 
in-house users have had.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudo conversational future

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A00EAA.C146C6C@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-I44eeWdndhrR@localhost> <389F9858.F6113DAE@hal-pc.org> <87oci0$fim$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Actually since CICS TS V1.2, IBM has provided CICS with an INTEGRATED
WEB SERVER.
It works essentially like the BMS map interface.
There are API's to produce HTML code and to import HTML code from other
pre-defined sources.

It is all reasonably clever, except for the fact that the users
transaction does
not run against a CICS terminal and therefore cannot use the
SIGNON/SIGNOFF api's.
Also the code to determine what transaction to run is a kludge.

I think it's about time that the CICS developers forced the issue with
the selection
of transaction codes from the incoming HTML request.

I would prefer that it works similar to normal web servers in that you
use CGI-BIN to
indicate the transaction to be executed thusly:

HTTP://x.x.x.x/cgi-bin/tran?.....

Of course, since they give you the code that makes the transaction
determination,
in various languages I might add (COBOL {yea}, PL/1 {ok}, assembler
{yawn} and
C {yuckko}), you can always set it up to work that way.

I just found it annoying to have to change the default sample to do
that.


Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> : BTW, does IBM have a CICS-based web server yet? So those "legacy" COBOL
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> -------------------------------------------------------------- */
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudo conversational future

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000209093829.01543.00000003@ng-fp1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<38A00EAA.C146C6C@iinet.net.au>`

```
>BTW, does IBM have a CICS-based web server yet? So those "legacy" COBOL
>> : apps can be used to handle HTML forms?

Actually, for a situation where this connecntion made business sense, I think
the "approved" response would be to create a WEB application designed for that
environment, and then use MQSeries to establish dynamic links back to the CICS
environment ... or better yet, skip CICS and connect directly to the data store
- if possible.  Aside from the advantages of updating user interfaces, this
approach also sells more software for IBM <G>.


Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

```
This is coming, although I haven't heard a date. Don't know how "huge"
an effort it will be (non-trivial, certainly), but everyone will have to
be ready on the same date, chosen by the SEC or SIAC (not certain which
governing body applies here), the way we were when the NYSE changed
settlement date from trade+5 to trade+3. Probably a lot like Y2K
(remember that?), lots of detail analysis & regression testing to insure
we haven't broken anything; but no moon shots or the like.

Bill Lynch
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87pcg3$7vb$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <xksIPEA94Co4Ew9P@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
I'm not sure the Euro is big.  Certainly not in Europe.   The problem was in
reporting in two currencies (local or Euro) for the Euro countries,
maintaining parities between Euro local currencies, following the rules for
converting between two 'in' currencies etc.  This all had to be in place at
the start of last year for multi currency systems.  Ours was.  I.e it was
more important than Y2K 2 years ago (although we had already done our Y2K
changes).

If this hasn't been done yet, then I wouldn't think it would take the
importance of Y2K changes.  It would just be a back burner job unless
someone had a marketing opportunity.

Just like Y2K (but far more complex and expensive for us), this became a 'so
what have you done for us lately' issue with our clients when it worked. :-)

Rick

Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software

- **From:** "Topcoder" <none@all.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net>`

```

|Ritchie Menzies wrote:
|
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
|> curiousity.

It happens to be a very good question.
Y2K may be over with, but the century problem is definitely not.

It never will be.

Companies which use windowing will have to move those windows,
not once, but twice per century to avoid the same problems.

(How's that for Long Range Systems Planning?)

Yes, it is a long time, and you probably won't be around by then.
But that is exactly what was said back in the 60's, 70's, etc.

Anyone who "solved" the Y2K problem by using windowing
will face the century problem all over again, sooner or later.
Those who compared the input year to 20,
setting the century to 20 if the year was less than 20,
and setting the century to 19 if the year was not less than 20,
will face it much sooner than those who used 50 to compare to.

But face it they must.  Perhaps you've heard the old refrain?

"That won't be for 10 or 15 years yet.  We won't be using COBOL by then".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>

```
Topcoder wrote:
>
>Anyone who "solved" the Y2K problem by using windowing
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>will face it much sooner than those who used 50 to compare to.

Actually, this is only true for fixed windowing.  Sliding windows
using a full 4 digit computer date for reference will continue to
work indefinitely (well, until Y10K :).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net> <_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> Topcoder wrote:
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> work indefinitely (well, until Y10K :).

They will work indefinitely - sort of.  The "indefinite" part is fine,
the "works" part is dependent upon the assumptions still applying.
Assuming no 60 year mortgages, no 120 year old drivers, no students who
have been going to school for 50 years, etc..

Nobody will write new systems based upon sliding window dates because
those extra bytes are virtually free now, and there WILL be exceptions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net> <_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> Topcoder wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>have been going to school for 50 years, etc..

Yes, but that applies to all design limits, no? :-)

>Nobody will write new systems based upon sliding window dates because
>those extra bytes are virtually free now, and there WILL be exceptions.


What I believe is that people will start using 2 digit year again.
"After all," the users will say, "neither we, nor our children, nor
our grandchildren will ever need a four digit transaction year, so
why should we have to type the century for the next 9x years?"  I
will be really surprised if this doesn't happen. :-(  If it does,
then sliding windows will (should) become standard, when possible.

After WWI, the U.S. disarmed to the point that when Pearl Harbour
was bombed, we had less than 150,000 active military personnel,
even with all that was going on in Europe.  Six years after the
second of two world wars in 30 years, the U.S. had disarmed to the
point it was hard pressed to meet the demands of the Korean War,
a small skirmish compared to WWII.  Now, less than ten years after
the Gulf war (note which had nothing to do with the Cold War), the
U.S. is no longer capable of repeating the effort, when the need
could arise within a week.  So quickly we forget.  Human nature is
depressingly consistent. ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software - a contrarian view

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B321D6.59870FEE@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net> <_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu> <n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia>`

```
Noone has actually showm that the programmers who coded 2 digit
years did the wrong thing.

How can you say such a stupid thing, you ask?

- without the saved memory some programs might not have fitted
into memory, and so could not have run, thus losing benefits
- extra costs for more memory to run bigger programs
- wasted screen real estate meaning more interactions
- you had to scan and test anyway just prior to Y2K so actually
having y2k complint code did not save very much
- most of the non-y2k compliant code *was* actually discarded
befire 2000.
- the time value of money means it was better to spend $100 in
1999 than to spend $10 in 1972.
etc

All of these considerations apply now. I advocate writing non-y2k
commpliant code from now on, unless the particular app needs to
go back to the C19.

Anyway, according to Moore's law computers will be smarter than
people by 2020-2030 or so, so it will be their problem!

Tim Josling

Judson McClendon wrote:
> ... What I believe is that people will start using 2 digit year again.
> ... So quickly we forget.  Human nature is
> depressingly consistent. ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software - a contrarian view

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UsSs4.7634$zu3.118468@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net> <_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu> <n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia> <38B321D6.59870FEE@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling wrote:
>Noone has actually showm that the programmers who coded 2 digit
>years did the wrong thing.

I quite agree, using 2 digits for year *was* the right thing to do
back before the early 80's.  But I don't think we really need to save
disk space that drastically any more.  Personally, I advocate using
sliding windows for date ranges that are not ambiguous, so the user
can deal with 2 digits, and using 4 digit user year otherwise.  But
always store 4 digit year in the database.  Some of my users wanted
sliding windows, some wanted 4 digit year, so I did what they asked.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software - a contrarian view

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B3F91A.80289530@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net> <_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu> <n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia> <38B321D6.59870FEE@melbpc.org.au> <UsSs4.7634$zu3.118468@news3.mia>`

```


Judson McClendon wrote:

> Tim Josling wrote:
> >Noone has actually showm that the programmers who coded 2 digit
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> sliding windows, some wanted 4 digit year, so I did what they asked.

I agree.  New systems can choose how data entry is done (save screen
space), but they should NEVER store a two digit year.  Not anymore, they
shouldn't.

Why is the Y2K problem always mentioned with COBOL?  It's only
relationship with that language is that COBOL is old enough to have been
used when 2 character years made sense.  When people were afraid their
elevators and cars wouldn't work, were they thinking about embedded
COBOL?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software - a contrarian view

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B40BB5.2BE7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net> <_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu> <n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia> <38B321D6.59870FEE@melbpc.org.au> <UsSs4.7634$zu3.118468@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Tim Josling wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> disk space that drastically any more.  

It didn't save disk space even then. In the 1970s I was already using Julians for my 
dates internally.  A 32 bit binary julian (i.e. 4 bytes) can hold every date from the 
start of human history (well, 4713 BC anyway) to the next several millenia and takes 
only 2/3 the disk space of MMDDYY and half that of MMDDYYYY. At the shop where I was the 
programming supervisor we had a couple of standard functions (only a few lines of code) 
to put dates into or out of julian form.  Julians have the other advantage that date 
arithmetic works with them directly - that was as much my reason for using them as the 
disk space issue.

(A "julian" is the date expressed as the number of days since 1 Jan 4713 BC.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software - a contrarian view

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B42C8A.64A8CDEF@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net> <_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu> <n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia> <38B321D6.59870FEE@melbpc.org.au> <UsSs4.7634$zu3.118468@news3.mia> <38B40BB5.2BE7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`

```
Ed Guy wrote:

> Judson McClendon wrote:
> >
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> (A "julian" is the date expressed as the number of days since 1 Jan 4713 BC.)

Some arithmetic works with them directly.  But if I want look at a date, add a year to it,
and place this new date on a report,  Julian isn't the most efficient.

Back to COBOL, the COBOL standard has Julian dates available, but IBM's language environment
is about 11 years off (if I remember correctly) with a Lilian date.  Some spread sheets
start with days since 1900, and I believe Unix has built in date from some time recent.
Gregorian may be safer for portability (and it is easier for me to look at a file and
recognize last Sunday's date), it just isn't efficient at comparing the number of days
between two dates (and as you say, it takes up more space)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software - a contrarian view

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>I believe Unix has built in date from some time recent.Yes, it's seconds since Jan 1 1970.  You'll see it internally in things like the 
NetScape bookmark file for access dates.  It too will have a problem in a few years, 
since it will cycle.
(My ParseRat recognises that format too and can translate it too julian or other date 
formats.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software - a contrarian view

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.13260cd87c2dfbb989919@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net> <_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu> <n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia> <38B321D6.59870FEE@melbpc.org.au> <UsSs4.7634$zu3.118468@news3.mia> <38B40BB5.2BE7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38B42C8A.64A8CDEF@cusys.edu>`

```
I noticed that Howard Brazee as howard.brazee@cusys.edu said...
> Some arithmetic works with them directly.  But if I want look at a date, add a year to it,
> and place this new date on a report,  Julian isn't the most efficient.

The insurance system that I mentioned a short while ago here used 4-byte 
dates split into two 2-byte packed fields of +YYY+DDD and all 
manipulation was controlled by standard systems routines.  The reason was 
that storage was at a premium back then.  The base year chosen was 1800 
and so I expect them to have difficulties in the 2790s if the systems 
aren't changed before then.  ;-)
 
> Back to COBOL, the COBOL standard has Julian dates available, but IBM's language environment
> is about 11 years off (if I remember correctly) with a Lilian date.  Some spread sheets
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> between two dates (and as you say, it takes up more space)

Anyone who says that disk space is cheap is being disingenuous but it 
isn't so expensive that we need to shirk the idea of storing full dates 
with the full years.  If you want to future proof it, maybe using a 5-
byte field, like the 4-byte one above but with the year as +YYYYY might 
be a reasonable suggestion and it could use 0 as a base year. - I think 
that by the time it becomes defunct we will either have more technology 
than we can shake a stick at or the planet will have had other plans for 
us anyway, or someone will have altered the calendar once more.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software - a contrarian view

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tsbt4.9342$Wv2.110726@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net> <_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <38B2A832.809B0E2B@cusys.edu> <n8As4.5256$Wv2.63862@news2.mia> <38B321D6.59870FEE@melbpc.org.au> <UsSs4.7634$zu3.118468@news3.mia> <38B40BB5.2BE7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`

```
Ed Guy wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> Tim Josling wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>disk space issue.

Well, 32 bits = 4 bytes = 8 BCD digits (except on IBM mainframes, or
their clones), exactly the same as a CCYYMMDD date.  Or, a CCYYMMDD
year in signed binary (on IBM mainframes and clones) would also take
exactly 4 bytes.  But a 6 BCD digit date = 3 bytes = 24 bits, or a 6
signed binary digit date would take 3 bytes.  In other words, your
going through all those gyrations of working with Julian saved no
storage at all over using CCYYMMDD dates, yet it added significant
complexity and overhead of all those conversions.  Very clever. ;-)

You are also making some unwarranted assumptions: that binary was
available across all platforms, that the overhead of converting dates
to/from Julian was not important, that the memory consumed by the extra
code used for the conversions (and all the performs of those routines)
was not that important, and that the processor time consumed was not
that important.  These assumptions are not valid in many cases, and it
often depended more on the number of dates in the application than on
the platform.  If you think that the processing time for such things
is not significant, you must not have spent much time programming MICR
applications in those days. ;-)

Of course, we could assume that the vast majority of the the programming
community (excepting yourself, of course) were (and are) incompetent. ;-)
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> Well, 32 bits = 4 bytes = 8 BCD digits (except on IBM mainframes, or
> their clones), exactly the same as a CCYYMMDD date.  Or, a CCYYMMDD
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> complexity and overhead of all those conversions.  Very clever. ;-)

I saw lots of code that DIDN'T pack it that way and used 6 bytes (48 bits) for MMDDYY.
 
> You are also making some unwarranted assumptions: that binary was
> available across all platforms, that the overhead of converting dates
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> applications in those days. ;-)

Fair comment.  However the algorithm is fairly fast (unless you worked on one of the 
VERY old machines with software multiply/divide).
 
> Of course, we could assume that the vast majority of the the programming
> community (excepting yourself, of course) were (and are) incompetent. ;-)

That's a nasty crack.  Lots of us used julians back then (as well as now) - or so I see 
from posts in other newsgroups.

There were always competent programmers and incompetent programmers. I didn't make that 
broad a generalisation.
```

```
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

No doubt, but that is a separate issue from using CCYYMMDD, MMDDYY,
or Julian, no?

>> Of course, we could assume that the vast majority of the the programming
>> community (excepting yourself, of course) were (and are) incompetent. ;-)

statement to the effect that "I know all you others folks did such-
and-such, but it was pointless, because I did ..., which was much
better", especially when their premise is unfounded, as was yours.
Doing so really does imply that every one else must have been
incompetent, not to have seen their '...' 'solution'.  However, I
do apologize for being so blunt. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - legacy software

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HSSs4.7676$zu3.119266@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<389FE639.5EB3C3C3@gecm.com> <38AA9F11.F7952F3F@bellsouth.net> <_drs4.7679$tk7.675746@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <cpxs4.4897$Wv2.60970@news2.mia> <rPHs4.9762$tk7.776811@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Topcoder wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>|Topcoder wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>my post.

That depends on what you mean by "Anyone who 'solved' the Y2K problem
by using windowing."  If you mean anyone who continues to use 2 digit
years in the database, you are correct.  But most windowing solutions
are not (should not) be used in that way.  The idea is to permit the
user to continue to enter 2 digit year, but reliably expand them into
4 digit year to be stored in the database.  Sliding windowing permits
this do be done reliably for the next 8,000 years, assuming the user's
dates meet certain criteria.  Actually, if the database is purged in
such a way that all dates in the database will always meet the criteria,
then sliding windowing could be used for database dates too, though
ordering and comparison would be cumbersome.  Since you didn't specify,
my remarks were based on that assumption.  But I will explain how
sliding windows should work anyway, in case you meant what I thought
you did. :-)

The reason you gave to show that using windowing is bad, is that if
you compare the 2 digit year to some constant, 20 or 50 in your
examples, eventually you would reach a point when that number was
passed and no longer would work.  This is true, if your code compares
the year to a constant.  A 'sliding window' is based on 'reflecting'
the 2 digit year into a century defined around the 4 digit current
date obtained from the operating system.  For example, the target
century could be the current year - 79 through the current year + 20.
Or, for dates that are always in the future, current year through
current year + 99.  Consider the following code which takes a MMDDYY
date input from a user and converts it into CCYYMMDD form.  The
century defined here is current year - 79 through current year + 20.
As long as all the dates processed will be within that range at the
time they are entered, this routine will work correctly indefinitely
(until 9999).  If the input dates do not meet that criteria, sliding
windowing is not applicable.  Note that the century could be defined
as any 100 year period relative to the current date, even dates
entirely in the past or future, by making both offsets negative or
positive, respectively.  The difference between the two offsets must
always be exactly 99. :-)

           03  DW-TODAYS-CCYYMMDD      PIC  9(08).
           03  DW-TODAYS-CCYYMMDD-R    REDEFINES DW-TODAYS-CCYYMMDD.
               05  DW-TODAYS-CCYY          PIC  9(04).
               05  DW-TODAYS-CCYY-R        REDEFINES DW-TODAYS-CCYY.
                   07  DW-TODAYS-CC            PIC  9(02).
                   07  DW-TODAYS-YY            PIC  9(02).
               05  DW-TODAYS-MM            PIC  9(02).
               05  DW-TODAYS-DD            PIC  9(02).
      *
           03  DW-WORK-CCYYMMDD        PIC  9(08).
           03  DW-WORK-CCYYMMDD-R      REDEFINES DW-WORK-CCYYMMDD.
               05  DW-WORK-CCYY            PIC  9(04).
               05  DW-WORK-CCYY-R          REDEFINES DW-WORK-CCYY.
                   07  DW-WORK-CC              PIC  9(02).
                   07  DW-WORK-YY              PIC  9(02).
               05  DW-WORK-MM              PIC  9(02).
               05  DW-WORK-DD              PIC  9(02).
      *
           03  DW-SHORT-DATE           PIC  9(06).
           03  DW-SHORT-DATE-R         REDEFINES DW-SHORT-DATE.
               05  DW-SHORT-MONTH          PIC  9(02).
               05  DW-SHORT-DAY            PIC  9(02).
               05  DW-SHORT-YEAR           PIC  9(02).

      ******************************************************************
      *                                                                *
      *               M M D D Y Y   T O   C C Y Y M M D D              *
      *                                                                *
      *    CONVERTS A DATE IN FORM MMDDYY INTO FORM CCYYMMDD.          *
      *                                                                *
      *    USAGE:  MOVE <MMDDYY DATE> TO DW-SHORT-DATE.                *
      *            PERFORM MMDDYY-CCYYMMDD.                            *
      *                                                                *
      *    RESULT: DW-WORK-CCYYMMDD = DATE IN CCYYMMDD FORM            *
      *                                                                *
      ******************************************************************
      *
       MMDDYY-CCYYMMDD.
      *
           MOVE DW-SHORT-MONTH TO  DW-WORK-MM.
           MOVE DW-SHORT-DAY   TO  DW-WORK-DD.
           MOVE DW-SHORT-YEAR  TO  DW-WORK-YY.
      *
           MOVE DW-TODAYS-CC  TO  DW-WORK-CC.
           IF (DW-WORK-CCYY < DW-TODAYS-CCYY - 79)
               ADD 1 TO DW-WORK-CC
           ELSE
           IF (DW-WORK-CCYY > DW-TODAYS-CCYY + 20)
               SUBTRACT 1 FROM DW-WORK-CC.
```

### Sending Email From Within An Application

- **From:** Doug Blaze <Doug.Blaze@merchants-fla.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<96k0as4mgb2smocseesq6t05qeari745ma@4ax.com>`

```
Hi All,

What is the easiest way to get your application to send email or
invoke the program designated as the email app?  We are using MF
4.0.32 (Workbench) and Fujitsu 4.2.  I seem to remember seeing
something like a 'sendto:' command in VB which I believe invokes the
email program (Outlook, Eudora, etc) that is set as the default, but I
can't find the API calls.  Does this involve getting into MAPI?  I
know you can buy a library from MarshallSoft to do email but that
involves writing a whole app.

Any suggestions would be appreciated.
Thanks,
Doug
```

```
> Hi All,
>
> What is the easiest way to get your application to send email or


I have great success with a command line emailer called POSTIE.

All you need to so is spawn (WinEXEC) it and it sends your message,
supports attachments, the whole nine yards.  It's free for private use
and very reasonably priced for commercial use.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
Hi Tom,

Thanks for the info.  After much searching on the MSDN site, I found

'sendto:') but then had to try to figure out who to send it to and
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sending Email From Within An Application

- **From:** B W Spoor <bwspoor@fridaycs.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38AACFBD.9DE9A4B5@fridaycs.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<96k0as4mgb2smocseesq6t05qeari745ma@4ax.com> <%T%p4.19$ou2.344498@news1.i1.net>`

```
I am just playing with a similar thing (for future use) using the Novell
Groupwise API on a PC DOS/Windows platform on a Netware using M$-Cobol
v4.5.

Sending is easy, just create 2 text files in the API directory, one is a
control file, the other contains the body of the message.

Receiving was a bit trickier, I ended up writing a small assembler
sub-routine to check for the presence of a control file (of random name)
in the interface directory and passing it's name back so that it can be
opened.

The idea is then to process the content of an email (file name in
control file) generated by FormMail on a web page and entering the data
into system automatically. Certain of web pages will also be refreshed
by the interface, without needing a full CGI/online system.

If you need anymore info - please email.

Brian
```

<a id="t-ab0974381a"></a>
## Thread: AS400 info needed — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### AS400 info needed

- **From:** "abc" <srahi@together.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38a0d49f@news.together.net>`

```
I  have been hearing a lot about AS400 but don't know much about it. How is
it different from mainframes and is the much demand for As400?
```

#### ↳ Re: AS400 info needed

- **From:** "W. Thomas Hansen" <tom@vederso.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A394BC.50C866CF@vederso.com>`
- **References:** `<38a0d49f@news.together.net>`

```
The AS/400 is an IBM system which has been around for about a dozen years and
has a well-
deserved reputation for being a 'bullet-proof' system.  It runs a proprietary
Operating
System (OS/400) which includes a version of DB/2 built in.  The OpSys and DB
run in a 'separate' processor from problem programs.

It supports a lot of languages, including COBOL, and can be obtained in a
config
strong enough to support anythere from 1 - 4,000 users with terra-byte DBs.


Tom Hansen
Vederso Consulting Group
Longmont, CO


abc wrote:

> I  have been hearing a lot about AS400 but don't know much about it. How is
> it different from mainframes and is the much demand for As400?
```

```
DumpMaster from Macro4
```

#### ↳ Re: Debug tools to MVS COBOL

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<53546A2C920F636D.79CEDA550B3CBAAE.06C79639E68E9C49@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<87p2jl$ncc$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
On Tue, 8 Feb 2000 13:36:04 +0100, "John Kurt Nielsen"
<johnkurt@image.dk> enlightened us:

>I am looking for debugging tools to COBOL under MVS and CICS.
>I am using Xpediter, but my boss want information about:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The only one of those you listed that I've used is Intertest.  It is
very similar to Xpediter, which I've also used.  Personally, I like
Xpediter better but that's just me.  Intertest does a good job with
both CICS and batch programs written in either Cobol or Assembler
(mainframe speak here).  So far CA hasn't messed with it since they
bought the company a few years back.  But if you already have
Xpediter, there would be no need to switch to Intertest.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

If the shoe fits, get another one just like it.


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

```
John Kurt Nielsen <johnkurt@image.dk> wrote in message
news:87rsg2$sa4$1@news.inet.tele.dk...
> That right but the price is interessered and I have heard that CompuWare
> (and CA) has a very high price
>
Price is an issue when competing products have the same functionality.

The reason these products are purchased is that they allegedly increase
productivity. The productivity savings justify the purchase.

If I can get 1 product which supports Batch, and CICS, the learning curve is
reduced by eliminating the need for someone to learn 2 products which may
have different interfaces. This saves money. If 2 products together are
cheaper than a single product, the savings may be offset by the time it
takes someone to become proficient in both products.
```

```
Just an "observation" - your list doesn't seem to include either of the IBM
solutions:

   Debug Tool - for interactive debugging
   "Formatted Dump" - for static debugging

I am not saying that they would (necessarily) meet your business needs but
they do have some advantageous (inter-language support, cost) over some of
the other options listed.
```

#### ↳ Re: Debug tools to MVS COBOL

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<38A109AD.678073A3@worldnet.att.net>`

```
John Kurt Nielsen wrote:
> 
> I am looking for debugging tools to COBOL under MVS and CICS.
> I am using Xpediter, but my boss want information about:

> Intertest fraom CAI

I think Xpediter is a superior product. I worked with InterTest from the
early 80's on (knew the guy who developed it back when OSI was an
independent co.). I was never comfortable with InterTest's menus, they
seemed pasted on as an afterthought. Last I looked (3 or so years ago)
debugging a COBOL program called by a CICS COBOL program was a bear with
InterTest. It's a no brainer with Xpediter. Then there's the CAI factor.

Bill Lynch
```

<a id="t-9870ccaa0e"></a>
## Thread: MF file compression — 3 msgs

### MF file compression

- **From:** john_barrie@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87q5p9$7jd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Can someone help with the different levels of file compression
available on MF ISAM files, i.e what the real diffs are between each of
the levels 1-7?

Also, I'd be grateful if anyone's had any experience with 'mix and
match' compression levels on .DAT and .DAT.idx files, e.g. one level on
the DAT, and anoither on the idx ?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF file compression

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38a0ebf1.11281393@news.freewwweb.com>`
- **References:** `<87q5p9$7jd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Tue, 08 Feb 2000 22:36:26 GMT, john_barrie@my-deja.com wrote:

>Can someone help with the different levels of file compression
>available on MF ISAM files, i.e what the real diffs are between each of
>the levels 1-7?
>

I've mixed index and data compressions before with good results.  As I
recall 7 combines the most algorythms.  But I always had to look in
the manual for what the different levels mean.  It's in the books,
clearly laid out.  One is for zero suppression.  Another for blank
suppression, etc.  I usually had the best results with Index
compression 1 and data compression 7.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF file compression

- **From:** jgill_1@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87t59g$c7s$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<87q5p9$7jd$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38a0ebf1.11281393@news.freewwweb.com>`

```


On my system, I added the following to the COBOL.DIR file:
DATACOMPRESS"1"
KEYCOMPRESS"7"

This works good for me. If you have alternate keys that are
often blank, I add the line "SUPPRESS WHEN SPACES" when describing
the key in the select statement.




In article <38a0ebf1.11281393@news.freewwweb.com>,
  thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

of
```

<a id="t-2d2c1a2a3b"></a>
## Thread: Help creating a packed decimal file — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help creating a packed decimal file

- **From:** wax_man@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87pgok$o4r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Would someone please help me.  I need to create a packed decimal file
that I can ftp to a mainframe.  I'm working in a windows environment.
I currently have a file in ascii that I convert to ebcdic.  However, I
don't know how to get from ebcdic to packed decimal.  Would someone be
kind enough to explain how to perform this conversion?

Tia,

chris


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help creating a packed decimal file

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A0528A.9DA4655B@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<87pgok$o4r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
wax_man@my-deja.com wrote:

> Would someone please help me.  I need to create a packed decimal file
> that I can ftp to a mainframe.  I'm working in a windows environment.
> I currently have a file in ascii that I convert to ebcdic.  However, I
> don't know how to get from ebcdic to packed decimal.  Would someone be
> kind enough to explain how to perform this conversion?

You FTP numbers in ASCII, converting them to EBCDIC, and then you use a
mainframe program to read it as display and convert it to whatever format
you need (packed).  COBOL will work fine to read your file and write out a
new file.
```

<a id="t-f97af54f55"></a>
## Thread: STD - The J4 meeting in Newbury England is "set" — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### STD - The J4 meeting in Newbury England is "set"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87qhn9$q3p$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI,
  The J4 meeting for this upcoming May in Newbury, England is now "set".  If
you are interested in attending, visiting, or "otherwise,"  I suggest that
you view the meeting notice at:

     http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/00-0128.doc

This meeting (or these two halves of a single meeting - separated by a
break) are VERY DEFINITELY "open" and anyone who wants to, can attend as much
(or as little) as they wish.  There is, however, a request that you contact
the host or J4 chair in advance if you plan on attending some of the meeting.

The WG4 meeting (which comes in the middle of the J4 meeting) is *not* open
to the public.  Please contact your "national body" if you are interested in
attending that meeting.  It is my expectation that the WG4 meeting will make
"final" decisions on forwarding the draft Standard to a  public review
(following the ISO rules for such) - and *may* make any other "final fine
tuning" decisions (such as whether to "resurrect" FINALIZER - or change the
"processor dependent" definition or almost anything else) BUT these topics
will ONLY be discussed if some national body brings them up for discussion.
If there is some topic that you think should be discussed, then I suggest you
contact your national body to see what their position is on it (if any) and
what procedures (if any) they have for getting your topic submitted to WG4
for discussion.

Please feel free to email me privately if you have any questions on how to
contact your own national body. (use the email address below and do NOT reply
to this note - as I have a "spam protector" id)
```

---
