## Thread: I want to switch to IBM COBOL — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### I want to switch to IBM COBOL

- **From:** anonymous <federalreservebostonNOfeSPAM@juno.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<03e6683b.0d6857f0@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`

```
I was marginalized out of the Burroughs/Unisys COBOL market
locally several years ago by the failure of several banks which
used the economical brand computer.  With the job market really
humming now, I would like to get back into the swing of things.
Are there web sites which will educate me about the currently
popular operating systems (I guess that would be VM and MVS) and
COBOL dialects, so I can at least talk about today's iron if I
can get in the door for an interview?


* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

#### ↳ Re: I want to switch to IBM COBOL

- **From:** JohnGalt <JohnGalt@notreet.speedfactory.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<q7p2aso0kogb6fqgihnlro3jomtfar2p3e@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<03e6683b.0d6857f0@usw-ex0101-005.remarq.com>`

```
On Tue, 08 Feb 2000 09:15:26 -0800, anonymous
<federalreservebostonNOfeSPAM@juno.com.invalid> wrote:

>I was marginalized out of the Burroughs/Unisys COBOL market
>locally several years ago by the failure of several banks which
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I made the move from Burroughs to IBM about 15 years ago after working
on Medium and large (B4800, B6900) systems right out of college for
some local S&Ls. I was able to find an employer starting a class for
non-IBM trainees (yes, we were called NITs). After two or three weeks
of intensive study using DELTAK training materials, we were declared
IBM experts. 

If you can't find an employer willing to train, you might consider
taking a course or two at a local tech school or community college.
The investment in time and money should pay off shortly.

P.S. You're gonna love JCL after using WFL, not.
>
>* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
>The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!

JohnGalt
NRA Life
Libertarian
remove notreet to reply via e-mail

<a id="t-4f1280057b"></a>
## Thread: MicroFocus calling-conventions — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus calling-conventions

- **From:** kentf30461@aol.com (KentF30461)
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000207195232.01027.00000080@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
Hi,
I am trying to call a C DLL from a MicroFocus COBOL program using
calling-convention 2.  When compiled with Debug code the call works with no
problems.  When compiled without the Debug code the call works and the C DLL
performs its task but the COBOL paragraph does not return to the code that
called it.  It runs off the end of the program.  The C program is exporting the
entry points as CDECL.  Is there another calling-convention that I should be
using? I hope someone here can shed a small light on what is going on.

Thanks for any input,
Kentf@qstr.com
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus calling-conventions

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87oq4e$ogg$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<20000207195232.01027.00000080@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
CDECL and normal COBOL call-convention are the same, so just call the DLL
with no call convention (or call-convention 0) and it should work correctly.


KentF30461 wrote in message
<20000207195232.01027.00000080@ng-cm1.aol.com>...
>Hi,
>I am trying to call a C DLL from a MicroFocus COBOL program using
>calling-convention 2.  When compiled with Debug code the call works with no
>problems.  When compiled without the Debug code the call works and the C
DLL
>performs its task but the COBOL paragraph does not return to the code that
>called it.  It runs off the end of the program.  The C program is exporting
the
>entry points as CDECL.  Is there another calling-convention that I should
be
>using? I hope someone here can shed a small light on what is going on.
>
>Thanks for any input,
>Kentf@qstr.com
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus calling-conventions

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87pbh4$c2o$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<20000207195232.01027.00000080@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
"KentF30461" <kentf30461@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000207195232.01027.00000080@ng-cm1.aol.com...
> Hi,
> I am trying to call a C DLL from a MicroFocus COBOL program using
> calling-convention 2.  When compiled with Debug code the call works with
no
> problems.  When compiled without the Debug code the call works and the C
DLL
> performs its task but the COBOL paragraph does not return to the code that
> called it.  It runs off the end of the program.  The C program is
exporting the
> entry points as CDECL.  Is there another calling-convention that I should
be
> using? I hope someone here can shed a small light on what is going on.

Be aware that a DLL can contain several functions, and each of them have
their individual calling declaration. Thus, although one function may be
declared cdecl, another one may be declared stdcall.

Cheesle
```

## Thread: Browser enable COBOL apps. — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Browser enable COBOL apps.

- **From:** davej3809@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87o0kg$lfo$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We're new to COBOL, so I will start here. We are a Pick Software house
that has written software to connect a web server to a Pick application
(Pick is a well established DBMS environment running on NT/Unix). By
embedding HTML statements into the Pick programming language (Basic),
any browser can read a Pick application screen. Typically screen
input/output program statements are modified to make it work.

I believe web enabling existing applications is also an issue in the
COBOL world? If so, our web enabling software, which connects by
TCP/IP, may be of use for COBOL programmers.  If so, is there any brave
COBOL experimenter that would like a go at trying to use our software
with COBOL?  We wish to sort out the issues involved. We will provide
any help we can. Visit site www.pixieware.com for more info. in respect
of the Pick world. If it works we will give free software to all major
contributors.

Thanks,  Dave Johnstone, The TOTAL Computer Company.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: NetExpress API RS232 — 5 msgs

### NetExpress API RS232

- **From:** "Charles Goodman" <cgoodman@mbnet.mb.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KUbo4.3860$a27.122870@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com>`

```
I am porting a bunch of programs from CTOS environment where there are
system calls for I-O to RS-232 ports.  The programs set comm parameters,
raise lower and check RS232 leads, rely on OS to do buffering on both input
and output.

It seems that the Win32 API can be used with NetExpress.  I have downloaded
the sample program from Merant's support site.  It compiles, and executes
when I use CBLLINK, but does not seen to work.  I have tried the IDE but it
gives error saying called program not found.

1.  How do I use Win32 calls from within IDE?

2.  Can someone provide a working example of the serial I-O using API.

3.  Are there better alternatives? (yes I need to be in 32bit environment)
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress API RS232

- **From:** jgill_1@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87vu02$cgg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<KUbo4.3860$a27.122870@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com>`

```
I had a situation where I had to read the output of an electronic scale
using the RS-232 port. This was strictly an input operation using MF
Cobol 16bit. I finally had to write an assembler program is Microsoft
Assembler and then call it from Cobol. The Assembler program polled the
RS-232 port and read the output from the scale and passed it back to the
Cobol program.
John Gill    (jdgill@juno.com)

## Thread: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer — 9 this month / 26 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

_Thread started [2000-01](2000-01.md#t-968a9c8b13)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer

- **From:** joebonans@aol.com (JoeBonans)
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.comp.lang.learn.c-c++,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<20000208231415.29145.00000322@ng-ba1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<9Mle4.49434$xC4.657357@newshog.newsread.com>`

```
>> "C for COBOL programmers" by Jim Gearing
>> Addison-Wesley Publishing Co.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>using it.

I _am_ former COBOL programmer who has used Gearing, and it's not that bad. I
wouldn't reccomend it as the primary book to learn C from, but he does a pretty
good job of explaing the parralels between the 2 languages.
For example he shows a COBOL
01 STUFF.
 05 MORESTUFF PIC X(10).
 05 NUMSTUFF PIC 9(05).

next to a C struct
struct stuff
{
 char moreStuff[11];
 int numstuff;
}

Since COBOL was my first language, I found thses kinds of comparisons very
useful while learning C.

As I primary C book, I reccomend "C Programming a Modern Approach" by K.N.
King. I used this myself to learn, and still refer to it. It's damn good. Check
out my 2 5 star reviews on Amazon.

Peace,
Joe Bonansinga
Staten Island, NY
joeBonans@aol.com
```

I would be interested in knowing HOW MANY dialects this supported?
(Particularly - how many different meanings of COMP it supported and how many
COMP-n USAGEs it supported.)  Also of interest would be how it handled
different variations on SYNC and filler bytes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.comp.lang.learn.c-c++,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<65ho4.9608$RT4.173793@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<9Mle4.49434$xC4.657357@newshog.newsread.com> <20000208231415.29145.00000322@ng-ba1.aol.com> <38A17721.DFF61C4F@dignus.com> <87s29a$mk4$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>I would be interested in knowing HOW MANY dialects this supported?
>(Particularly - how many different meanings of COMP it supported and how many
>COMP-n USAGEs it supported.)  Also of interest would be how it handled
>different variations on SYNC and filler bytes.

Bill, I suspect you aren't going to get a 'binary equivalent', but a
'logical equivalent'.  Creating a true binary equivalent in C would
require substantial procedural code, beyond the data declarations.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "slick" <p_rocket@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.comp.lang.learn.c-c++,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<0b0p4.8$n72.85210@gazette.loc1.tandem.com>`
- **References:** `<9Mle4.49434$xC4.657357@newshog.newsread.com> <20000208231415.29145.00000322@ng-ba1.aol.com> <38A17721.DFF61C4F@dignus.com>`

```

>> I _am_ former COBOL programmer who has used Gearing, and it's not that
bad. I
>> wouldn't reccomend it as the primary book to learn C from, but he does a
pretty
>> good job of explaing the parralels between the 2 languages.
>> For example he shows a COBOL
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>> }

Then he did you a disservice; the moreStuff field should have been
   char moreStuff[10];
to allocate 10 bytes.  Perhaps he was confused by the subscript range being
0:9 in C, 1:10 in COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.comp.lang.learn.c-c++,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<a3t5ask4ecregnqp78u8nensiri9abq0so@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9Mle4.49434$xC4.657357@newshog.newsread.com> <20000208231415.29145.00000322@ng-ba1.aol.com>`

```
On 09 Feb 2000 04:14:15 GMT joebonans@aol.com (JoeBonans) wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>I _am_ former COBOL programmer who has used Gearing, and it's not that bad. I
:>wouldn't reccomend it as the primary book to learn C from, but he does a pretty
:>good job of explaing the parralels between the 2 languages.
:>For example he shows a COBOL
:>01 STUFF.
:> 05 MORESTUFF PIC X(10).
:> 05 NUMSTUFF PIC 9(05).

:>next to a C struct
:>struct stuff
:>{
:> char moreStuff[11];
:> int numstuff;
:>}

Huh??

In the Cobol map, MORESTUFF will take exactly 10 character positions. In the C
map, it will be eleven (twelve?) positions.

In the Cobol map, NUMSTUFF is five character positions. In the C map it will
be whatever the installation/compiler definition of "int" is, 1 2 or 4
positions.

The C record definition for MORESTUFF has to be a non-zero terminating string
of exactly 10 positions to be able to process the same records.

   [ snipped ]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.comp.lang.learn.c-c++,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<ab4basccl19a2uv3t2qupm51k3indu2ls2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9Mle4.49434$xC4.657357@newshog.newsread.com> <20000208231415.29145.00000322@ng-ba1.aol.com>`

```
On 09 Feb 2000 04:14:15 GMT joebonans@aol.com (JoeBonans) wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>I _am_ former COBOL programmer who has used Gearing, and it's not that bad. I
:>wouldn't reccomend it as the primary book to learn C from, but he does a pretty
:>good job of explaing the parralels between the 2 languages.
:>For example he shows a COBOL
:>01 STUFF.
:> 05 MORESTUFF PIC X(10).
:> 05 NUMSTUFF PIC 9(05).

:>next to a C struct
:>struct stuff
:>{
:> char moreStuff[11];
:> int numstuff;
:>}

Huh??

In the Cobol map, MORESTUFF will take exactly 10 character positions. In the C
map, it will be eleven (twelve?) positions.

In the Cobol map, NUMSTUFF is five character positions. In the C map it will
be whatever the installation/compiler definition of "int" is, 1 2 or 4
positions.

The C record definition for MORESTUFF has to be a non-zero terminating string
of exactly 10 positions to be able to process the same records.

   [ snipped ]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** rlb@fdhoekstra.nl (Richard Bos)
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.comp.lang.learn.c-c++,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<38a7efd2.878281104@news.worldonline.nl>`
- **References:** `<9Mle4.49434$xC4.657357@newshog.newsread.com> <20000208231415.29145.00000322@ng-ba1.aol.com> <ab4basccl19a2uv3t2qupm51k3indu2ls2@4ax.com>`

```
postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen) wrote:

> On 09 Feb 2000 04:14:15 GMT joebonans@aol.com (JoeBonans) wrote:
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> positions.

No, an int takes <anynumber> of _bytes_, but the standard specifies that
an int can hold at least numbers up to 32767, and an unsigned int up to
65535. This does mean, of course, that you can't (portably) represent
all five-position numbers in an int; you should use a long here.

Richard
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer

I should point out that Steve Summit's user site (mentioned
above) has course notes for beginning and intermediate C, and
also notes to accompany the K&R book.  It has been pointed out
that you, as an experienced programmer, ought to have no problem
reading the plain-vanilla book.  Agreed.  But you may want to
have the convenience of such notes anyway.

have run into a problem.  How do you handle decimals?

decimal?

chris

Note :  If this is an in appropriate message for your group, please accept my
apologies!

Opportunities at growing, friendly ERP consulting firm in Santa Clara!  


Please do not send attachments!
Applicants-Please reference appropriate job code when submitting resumes!  
 

PROGRAMMER / ANALYST / CLIENT SERVER APPLICATIONS

Summary:

Incumbent will  work with clients and Kastle Consulting staff, to create,
maintain, and debug Lawson  Self-Evident Applications™ (SEA)  client server
application programs. Excellent communication skills, strong analytical skills
and a client server application development background, will enable this person
to be productive quickly. Future projects will include web oriented development
using Java Script and HTML.

Responsibilities:

Create and maintain application programs per specifications provided.
Participate in multiple projects (2-3).
Create and maintain the documentation of new and existing
applications. 
Provide technical assistance to other programmer and project
managers. 
Coordinate with our project managers to and direct supervisor (Development
Manager), to manage priorities and establish schedules.

Background:

Excellent communication skills (both verbal and written).
3-5 years work experience developing client/server business applications is
required.
Strong understanding of Business Operations required.
Experience with Lawson SEA applications strongly preferred.
Experience using Lawson, SAP, Peoplesoft, BaaN, JD Edwards, Oracle Financials
or  other applicable client server tools, in a UNIX or NT environment, will
contribute to  timely success. 
Experience with Microfocus Cobol Strongly desired.
RDBMS experience such as Oracle or Informix required.
Java,  Java/Script, and HTML experience beneficial.

Education:

Bachelor’s Degree in Business,  Computer Science or related field. 

Respond to:
Kastle Consulting
PROGRAM/LBM
lbmconsulting@aol.com



PROJECT MANAGER/  BUSINESS APPLICATION CONSULTANT / ERP
JOB CODE:  PROJECT/LBM

Summary:

Incumbent shall work with clients and Kastle Consulting  staff to identify
software project requirements, schedules, and resources.  The ability to
effectively communicate with managers, end-users, and technical personnel is
essential for success in  this position. Experience with Lawson, SAP,
Peoplesoft, BaaN, JD Edwards, Oracle Financials or other applicable ERP
application, will be considered.  Experience in project management/ functional
consulting, in a client server environment, will enable this person to be
productive quickly.

Responsibilities:

Create and maintain documentation such as project plans, functional
requirements, and high level technical specifications.
Participate and/or manage concurrent projects (2-3).
Utilizing your functional business knowledge of Finance/Accounting/Inventory
practices and procedures, work with the client to identify project requirements
such as training, procedures, etc.
Coordinate with our Development Manager to secure the necessary
internal resources for project work.

Background

3-5 years work experience in project management (business applications focus
preferred).

Education:

Bachelor’s Degree in Business,  Computer Science or related field. 

Respond to:
JOB CODE:  PROJECT/LBM
Kastle Consulting
lbmconsulting@aol.com

UPON COMMENCEMENT OF EMPLOYMENT, ALL CANDIDATES MUST PROVIDE DOCUMENTATION
ATTESTING TO THEIR LEGAL RIGHT TO WORK IN THE US.

greatly
> >> >appreciated. thanks

but

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How Can I Display Time In My Program?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jason Bailey" <jayandtonya@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gXXq4.1678$BS.34425@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<87ukcf$cnc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <nLAo4.3988$lK6.84557@iad-read.news.verio.net> <880d1j$mjp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eWRo4.4419$lK6.95663@iad-read.news.verio.net> <8847tr$chn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Place the code that grabs the System time inside of a thread that run every
1 second.  Depending on the runtime and compiler you're using, this would
result in a smooth running clock for your app that wouldn't interfere with
your application's primary function.

Good luck,

Jason

> well, probably the lecturer will give extra credits but usually they
> don't. i just wanna put it in so it looks nicer besides could learn
> something new in the process. thats all.
```

#### ↳ Re: How Can I Display Time In My Program?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<880g8h$isk$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<87ukcf$cnc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
kelvincsc@my-deja.com wrote:
: I would like to display a time which are constantly update so the
: second will refresh like a real clock. any idea and help are greatly
: appreciated. thanks

That is one way of chewing up all of a Pentium III's CPU time so
that the machine runs like a '286.

If you run it on a multiuser machine (Unix, Linux, TSE) then you
will certainly piss off all the other users.

Just get a real OS that puts a clock program on the status line
or desktop.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How Can I Display Time In My Program?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<881cqc$8du$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<87ukcf$cnc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <880g8h$isk$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
> That is one way of chewing up all of a Pentium III's CPU time so
> that the machine runs like a '286.

I wouldn't say that. If the guy could stay with hours:minutes rather than
hours:minutes:seconds, it does not necessarily have to eat resources. It all
depends on how you implement it.

The generic Cobol method would be to use 'ACCEPT ... BEFORE msec' and then
respond appropriately and go back into the ACCEPT statement.

If you were to do this in windows environment, installing a timer and
monitor the messages would not make your computer kneel, because timer
messages has the lowest priority on windows computers, hence the 'real' work
would get done prior to the timer.

Cheesle
```

<a id="t-3ad70a39d2"></a>
## Thread: Microfocus Cobol copy files — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Microfocus Cobol copy files

- **From:** Johan den Boer <jj.den.boer@hccnet.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A2EA82.D683DF16@hccnet.nl>`

```
Hi,

I am using microfocus cobol on a HP-UX platform and
I have the following problem :

I have some problems in using copy files in my cobol
programs. If I have a statement like this

copy /usr/local/cobol/cpy/mycopy.

This statement is translated to uppercase letters.

COPY /USR/LOCAL/COBOL/CPY/MYCOPY.

On unix machines file names are case sensitive so the compiler
cannot find it.
What compiler flag should you I give to use lower case
letters, so it can find the copy files.
I can not find anything about it in the manuals.

I have set COBCPY to the copy paths.

thanks a lot,
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus Cobol copy files

- **From:** "Steven Lalewicz" <strl@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87utt9$f3c$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38A2EA82.D683DF16@hccnet.nl>`

```

Johan den Boer wrote in message <38A2EA82.D683DF16@hccnet.nl>...
>Hi,
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi Johan,

If you place quotes round the file name the compiler will keep
the case of the copy books.

You don't say what version of the compiler you are using, but
there is a directive FOLDCOPYNAME(case) which can fold the copy
name to either lower or upper.
Example: cob demo.cbl -C "FOLDCOPYNAME(LOWER)"
Hopefully now the compiler will find your copy files.

Regards,
Steve.

>thanks a lot,
>
```

<a id="t-ef68cf1f60"></a>
## Thread: Dinosaur Code! — 70 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Dinosaur Code!

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

Some folks just talk about the Oldene Dayse, when (person) could do
(activity) such at ten people cannot do today... other folks work in
places where, as Faulkner said, the past is not only not-dead... it ain't
even laid down yet.

Behold some Production Code... this is something which runs Every Day:

--begin quoted code:

ID DIVISION.                                          
PROGRAM-ID. FRIIID27.                                 
REMARKS. CONVERTED 10-73 TO ANS COBOL BY R HOWELL.    
DATE-WRITTEN. SEPT. 1, 1971.                          
ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.                                 
CONFIGURATION SECTION.                                
SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-370.                             
OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-370.                             
INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.                                 
FILE-CONTROL.                                         
    SELECT ID240I1 ASSIGN TO                          
    DA-2314-S-IN24 RESERVE NO ALTERNATE AREAS.        
    SELECT UNMATCH ASSIGN TO                          
    UT-2400-S-INMTCH RESERVE NO ALTERNATE AREAS.      
    SELECT ID270I2 ASSIGN TO                          
    DA-2314-S-OUT27 RESERVE NO ALTERNATE AREAS.       
    SELECT ID270I1 ASSIGN TO                          
    UT-2400-S-IN27 RESERVE NO ALTERNATE AREAS.        

--end quoted code

Just *sings*, don't it?... granted, in something like Ars Antiqua
chant-style, but it *sings*... differentiating between DA-2314 and
UT-2400!  RESERVE NO ALTERNATE AREAS!  Maybe if I look in the corner over
there I'll find a dusty ol' routine for Daddy Bones or Hank 'n Arch!

(note for young'uns - 'Daddy Bones' and 'Hank 'n Arch' were
stock-characters in Minstrel Shows, a form of entertainment in the Oldene
Dayse, when an actor could tread the boards wearing blackface such as
*ten* actors could not wear today)

DD
```

the last revision. How much better could it get?

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dinosaur Code!

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<882nt1$53c2$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net> <wO0p4.573$tT6.11143@news.swbell.net>`

```

Jerry P <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:wO0p4.573$tT6.11143@news.swbell.net...
> Are you complaining about the code or bragging about the code?
>
> Me, I would be bragging. Running everyday for twenty-seven years since
> the last revision. How much better could it get?

I agree with your assessment, Jerry.  I started programming in 1974.  In
1971, there were legitimate reasons for the RESERVE NO ALTERNATE AREAS
clause (indicating use only 1 I/O buffer for this file since the default was
2) due to disk space cost and sometimes shop restrictions of load module
size.  Back then, it was oftentimes necessary to use unblocked files for the
same reason instead of having the luxury of half-track blocked files whick
are common today.  I also think that the references to the 2314 disk device
and the 2400 tape device were required back then.  I'd hate to hazard a
guess as to the probability of programs written today still running in
production in the year 2029.  Think about it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dinosaur Code!

- **From:** Bill Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<38A51325.7B8C6DF4@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net> <wO0p4.573$tT6.11143@news.swbell.net> <882nt1$53c2$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Terry Heinze wrote:
> 
> Jerry P <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote in message
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> production in the year 2029.  Think about it.

Based on several factors, this was likely late Jurassic COBOL. Do you
have the radiocarbon tests back yet?

This was originally a DOS (that's dee oh ess, BTW) COBOL program,
probably D-COBOL, although I think the F-level compiler was available by
then. OS/360 always (IIRC, I didn't work with it until 1975) supported
device independence for sequential files, so OS COBOL programs did not
specify 2314 or 2401 and buffering was more complex than 1 or 2 I/O
buffers (can't tell you how long it's been since I wrote "I/O buffers").
OS systems typically had substantially more core (that's what it was
called), too. 

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dinosaur Code!

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38bba87c.6250049@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net> <wO0p4.573$tT6.11143@news.swbell.net> <882nt1$53c2$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```
On Fri, 11 Feb 2000 22:34:12 -0600, Terry Heinze wrote:

>I agree with your assessment, Jerry.  I started programming in 1974.  In
>1971, there were legitimate reasons for the RESERVE NO ALTERNATE AREAS
>clause (indicating use only 1 I/O buffer for this file since the default was
>2) due to disk space cost and sometimes shop restrictions of load module
>size.  
[snip]

Another use I have seen for the RESERVE NO ALTERNATE AREAS clause was
with a card reader input file (not spooled).  If there was no
buffering, then a program could read a card, evaluate it, and decide
if it liked it.  If not, it could then pause and display a console
message asking someone to correct the card and then pass it back
through the card reader.  Obviously, the card reader was tied-up
during the process.

Such schemes would be big time wasters today, even if cards were still
the usual input medium.  In fact, they were generally not very
efficient in the "old days" either, but I have seen it done.  I guess
that some folks did not place much value on people's time or on
sharing resources/devices among users.
```

It's been a long time, but I believe in the 70s, RESERVE NO ALTERNATE AREAS
was required for print files.  It had something to do with the possible
overlapping of print lines as I recall.
```

docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
> Behold some Production Code... this is something which runs Every Day:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> there I'll find a dusty ol' routine for Daddy Bones or Hank 'n Arch!

Yeah, back then we ran a whole damn company (a major utility) on a Honeywell 200 with 
24K (that's 24,000 BYTES) of RAM (known as "core" then) with four tape drives, one 
reader-punch, one line printer, no disk, no console typewriter. (Had to "boot" it the 
hard way by keying in machine code with the buttons on the box).
I had a routine that printed 20 rows of * on the line printer to wake up the operator 
and lure him/her out of the little office to look at the message giving instructions for 
tape mounts or whatever.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dinosaur Code!

- **From:** "Bernard Nemeth" <bnemeth@shaw.wave.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<xItp4.54825$up4.913456@news1.rdc1.ab.home.com>`
- **References:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38A50D8D.201A@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <psdp4.4836$a27.145266@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com>`

```
I remember it well.  The Honeywell H200 came with 4K core memory in the low
end entry level model.  Anything beyond that was optional. The only software
that could run in the entry  configuration was the Easycoder-A assembler.
To create an executable program, you ran the source code card deck through
the card reader twice along with the assembler card deck, and if all went
well the card punch would spit out your self-booting executable program and
a listing would appear on the printer.  The basic peripherals were the card
reader,and punch, a line printer.  Seven tracks magnetic tapes appeared on
the higher end models.  Random access devices (drums and disks) evolved
later.

Software development packages those days were classed as A, B, D, F, or H
depending on whether the target machine was 4K, 8K, 16K, 24K or 32K.  Thus
the COBOL compiler for the 8K machines was labelled as COBOL-B.

Bernie Nemeth

"Charles Goodman" <cgoodman@mbnet.mb.ca> wrote in message
news:psdp4.4836$a27.145266@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com...
>
> "Ed Guy" <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> wrote in message >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> characters of datanames were significant.  That was when ALTER statement
was
> valuable.
>
> Charlie Goodman
>
>
```

> That was when ALTER statement was
>valuable.
>

Other than trying to sharpen your flint knife, what has ALTER ever been
"valuable " to do??  I ask because my first assignment was maintenance on a
hopsital claim program that contain no less than 15 ALTER...GO TO statements. 
I hated it then (spent three months rewriting the sucker, finally!!) and
despise it now.

"And if you try to tell that to the young people today, they don't believe
you!"

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dinosaur Code!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Charles Goodman" <cgoodman@mbnet.mb.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fWpq4.7003$a27.178606@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com>`
- **References:** `<psdp4.4836$a27.145266@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com> <20000215002617.01108.00001016@ng-bk1.aol.com>`

```
The object code for a GO TO is very small and very quick.  Remember the
PERFORM statement did not exist.  In place of a perform statement you would
ALTER a GO TO at the end of a bunch of code you wanted to 'perform'.  The
alternative was to set a flag and use an IF with GO TO at the end of the
'performed' code.  The comparison generated large code and was slow.
Remember 8K and 1 microsecond cycle time.  I can remember modifying code to
use ALTERs so that it would execute more quickly and fit into memory.

Charlie Goodman


"Steve Newton" <stevenln@aol.comnospam> wrote in message
news:20000215002617.01108.00001016@ng-bk1.aol.com...
> > That was when ALTER statement was
> >valuable.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> "valuable " to do??
```

Charles Goodman wrote:
>Remember 8K and 1 microsecond cycle time.

Perhaps you mean 8k and 1 millisecond.  That's where I started.  But
the programs actually had to run in 4k.  We only had 8k because we
were a development center, and the compiler wouldn't run in 4k. :-)
```

Dean T. Miller wrote:
> 
> Hey Ed,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> -- Dean -- from (almost) Duh Moines  (CDP, KB0ZDF)

Yeah, I guess it dates me.  I took it when the DPMA were still administering it.

Took it because like most of my generation there was no specific computer degree when I 
was a student.  To me it was an "electrical machine" so I took an EE degree.  My sister 
took a math degree and others took accounting degrees and we all ended up as computer 
professionals.  I wouldn't have bothered, but I took a job where the need arose to give 
expert testimony and I needed a specifically Data Processing designation.

I was surprised a few years ago when I was at an Institute of Internal Auditors course 
in San Francisco they still recognise it along with their  CIA designation (Certified 
Internal Auditor - a friend has a lot of fun with his business card saying Jerry M---, 
CIA) and the CISA (Certified Information Systems Auditor).
```

>>spend hours navigating menus.

>Only times I ever needed to twist a pot was to get my voice into the
>red...

Now why in the heck would you want to do that?

\iii/

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dinosaur Code!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** david <dskirk@usa.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5f9eascdqh0ebm17c5jbnp35jdf2uj9q2q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38A50D8D.201A@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38a6e8c0.364507854@news1.newscene.com> <dpbsmith-E29D49.16115013022000@news5.bellatlantic.net>`

```
 >.... <big snip>....
>> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>hiring managers) seem to have any idea what it is.

two thoughts:
1. i well remember the honeywell... and philco and GE and Univac and
Burroughs and RCA...   a golden era of computing when no one
considered it relevant that we use the same hardware instruction set
or the same operating software... was an exciting time... i began
programming in january, 1965, and the world is far different today...

2. on the CDP, it was a good idea in early 1970's, but lost favor as
companies began to drift from the mainframe. certification is still
important, but many are now looking to the client/server
certifications...  of course, the CDP indicated an awareness and skill
in busines priorities and processes; the client/server certification
is focused solely on knowledge of the technology. we have indeed lost
something in the process.  
david 


__________________________________________________
david                               dskirk@usa.net
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dinosaur Code!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** walterm@rose.hp.com (Walter Murray)
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<88a0qj$p12$1@web1.cup.hp.com>`
- **References:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38A50D8D.201A@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38a6e8c0.364507854@news1.newscene.com> <dpbsmith-E29D49.16115013022000@news5.bellatlantic.net> <38A72970.BB33986@ix.netcom.com>`

```
MCH (milesh1@ix.netcom.com) wrote:
: > I have a CDP but I've stopped mentioning it since nobody (certainly no
: > hiring managers) seem to have any idea what it is.
: > 
: > Daniel P. B. Smith

: Well, what is it?  "Certified Dilbert Programmer"?

Here's my recollection of things.  It may not be completely correct,
but should be pretty close.

Back in the '60s and '70s there was talk of regulating computer
professionals.  It was being suggested in several state legislatures.
Pressure mounted in the wake of the realization that computers could
actually be used to embezzle.  Anybody remember the Equity Funding
scandal?

Several professional organizations, including the Data Processing
Management Assiciation (DPMA) and the Association for Computing
Machinery (ACM) realized it would be better if they took the
lead to develop credentials that would be recognized universally,
rather than have each state develop their own standards.

I think DPMA took the lead with the Registered Business Programmer
(RBP) examination.  Then a consortium of about seven organizations,
originally referred to as "the Foundation" developed the Certificate
in Data Processing (CDP), patterned after the CPA credential.  
Requirements to be awarded a CDP varied over the years, but
typically included several years of related college-level courses,
several years of experience in computing, passing an exam, being
of good moral character, subscribing to a code of ethics, etc.
The exam was a full-day affair in five sections.  (You got experience
for the programming section if you had already passed the RBP exam.)
It was not trivial.  Most people did not pass all five sections on
the first try.  Meeting all the requirements and passing the exam
gave you the right to use the initials CDP after your name.

Somewhere along the line the RBP program was replaced with the
Certified Computer Programmer (CCP) credential, which came in
three flavors:  business (COBOL), scientific (FORTRAN), and systems
(assembler).  

Eventually it was decided to create a new organization to promote
certification and to develop and administer new exams.  Thus was
born the Institute for Certification of Computer Professionals (ICCP).
They discontinued the Certified Computer Programmer credential and
recycled the abbreviation CCP to mean Certified Computer Professional,
which was the successor to the CDP.  The CCP program added  a
requirement for continuing education in order to retain the right
to use the credential.  However, holders of the CDP were grandfathered
in and could apply to receive a CCP.

Walter Murray, RBP, CCP, CDP
```

- **References:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38A50D8D.201A@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38a6e8c0.364507854@news1.newscene.com> <dpbsmith-E29D49.16115013022000@news5.bellatlantic.net>`

```
Hi Dan,

"Daniel P. B. Smith" <dpbsmith@bellatlantic.net> wrote:

>I have a CDP but I've stopped mentioning it since nobody (certainly no 
>hiring managers) seem to have any idea what it is.

They had no idea in the 60's, either.  :)

I (and Kenniston Lord and others) attempted to start the "Society of
Certified Data Processors" around 1970.  It was patterned upon the CPA
association, but never got off the ground.  There was (and continues
to be) too much demand for computer people, so certification didn't
mean much (and doesn't seem to mean very much right now).

-- Dean -- from (almost) Duh Moines  (CDP, KB0ZDF)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dinosaur Code!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<iHWp4.2625$Ez.62409@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38A50D8D.201A@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38a6e8c0.364507854@news1.newscene.com> <dpbsmith-E29D49.16115013022000@news5.bellatlantic.net> <38a7803b.10743298@news1.newscene.com>`

Dean T. Miller wrote:
>
>I (and Kenniston Lord and others) attempted to start the "Society of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>mean much (and doesn't seem to mean very much right now).

It doesn't mean much because raw knowledge about facts, which is what
those kinds of tests are designed to rate, is only a very small part
of what makes for a good programmer.  Smarts, attitute and experience
are, in decreasing order, each far more important in most situations.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dinosaur Code!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<Kwfq4.4796$QK6.56356@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38A50D8D.201A@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38a6e8c0.364507854@news1.newscene.com> <dpbsmith-E29D49.16115013022000@news5.bellatlantic.net> <38a7803b.10743298@news1.newscene.com> <iHWp4.2625$Ez.62409@news2.mia> <dpbsmith-635E43.20451814022000@news5.bellatlantic.net>`

```
Daniel P. B. Smith wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> Dean T. Miller wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>so is the Code of Ethics.

Daniel,

Are we having the same conversation? ;-)  Yes, I agree that "systems
analysis" is much more than "raw knowledge and facts."  However, the
discussion was about the value (or lack) of having a CDP certification,
and those were were issued based on tests.  Those kinds of tests try
to measure raw knowledge of facts, rather than ability, because direct
tests of ability (and grading of same) are far too difficult to arrange
in a completely objective way.  So my comments were about the tests and,
by implication, the actual certifications.  My comments had nothing
whatever to do with 'systems analysis' per se (or the Code of Ethics).
Your comments imply that either you completely missed my point, or I
have completely missed yours. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dinosaur Code!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** dtmiller@midiowa.net (Dean T. Miller)
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<38a9ec3d.547076@news1.newscene.com>`
- **References:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38A50D8D.201A@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38a6e8c0.364507854@news1.newscene.com> <dpbsmith-E29D49.16115013022000@news5.bellatlantic.net> <38a7803b.10743298@news1.newscene.com> <iHWp4.2625$Ez.62409@news2.mia>`

```
Hi Judson,

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>Dean T. Miller wrote:
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>are, in decreasing order, each far more important in most situations.

I see you've never taken the CDP exams.  

Eight hours of tests, half of which covered management areas -- and to
be allowed to take the test, you had to have at least a BA/BS and 3
years of experience in the computer field (at least, those were the
requirements in 1964).  The technical part of the tests covered tab
machine analysis and operation (it was '64, after all), operations
research and other applied math, plus knowledge of computers
(primarily IBM), systems design methodology and programming languages
of the day.

My biggest joy in passing the exam was having the guy sitting next to
me fail it.  He was formerly my "boss" when I was in the army, a Ph.D.
in Physics and a GS something -- a real horn-hair.

-- Dean -- from (almost) Duh Moines  (CDP, KB0ZDF)
```

> Hi Judson,
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> of the day.

And you might have added: no "syllabus" specified in advance.  They took the position 
that they could test on ANYTHING they considered relevant.  I passed the tests in 1972, 
which was the first year they included lots of questions on telephony and 
communications.  That year the failure rate was about 80%.  I was thankful for my EE 
degree!

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dinosaur Code!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<lNfq4.4823$QK6.56775@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38A50D8D.201A@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38a6e8c0.364507854@news1.newscene.com> <dpbsmith-E29D49.16115013022000@news5.bellatlantic.net> <38a7803b.10743298@news1.newscene.com> <iHWp4.2625$Ez.62409@news2.mia> <38a9ec3d.547076@news1.newscene.com>`

```
Dean T. Miller wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>Dean T. Miller wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>I see you've never taken the CDP exams.

No. :-)

>Eight hours of tests, half of which covered management areas -- and to
>be allowed to take the test, you had to have at least a BA/BS and 3
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>in Physics and a GS something -- a real horn-hair.

I fail to see your point.  To pass the test you answered questions,
isn't that true?  And to correctly answer the questions you had to
have specific knowledge, right?  So, the test measured knowledge.
I did not mean to imply that the tests were easy, or worthless, or
that they had no meaning.  What I was trying to say is that raw
knowledge simply does not equate with ability and/or performance.
There is come correlation, yes.  But every one of us has known people
who were very well educated, but could hardly tie their shoes in a
practical situation, or solve a real world problem.  The fact is,
there are factors far more important than raw knowledge.  An extremely
bright and talented person with incredible initiative, will, in the
long run, *always* trounce someone with less intelligence, initiative
and talent.  There are many other important attributes, such as the
willingness to suffer hardship to get the job done, tenacity, good
judgment (common sense), integrity, etc.  But those are much more
difficult to measure objectively than raw knowledge.  And as it turns
out, those bright, intelligent and motivated people will eventually
gain the knowledge, so testing knowledge does loosely correlate to
ability.  My point is that the correlation is much looser than might
be expected, because there are a lot of people out there who have
memorized a lot of facts, but who couldn't use one if it bit them in
the nose.  Perhaps your old Army boss was such a person? :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dinosaur Code!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** dtmiller@midiowa.net (Dean T. Miller)
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<38a9d276.48758080@news1.newscene.com>`
- **References:** `<2g_o4.4570$lK6.100048@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38A50D8D.201A@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <38a6e8c0.364507854@news1.newscene.com> <dpbsmith-E29D49.16115013022000@news5.bellatlantic.net> <38a7803b.10743298@news1.newscene.com> <iHWp4.2625$Ez.62409@news2.mia> <38a9ec3d.547076@news1.newscene.com> <lNfq4.4823$QK6.56775@news4.mia>`

```
Hi Judson,

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>Dean T. Miller wrote:

>>I see you've never taken the CDP exams.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>have specific knowledge, right?  So, the test measured knowledge.

The point of the above is that the knowledge measured was wide-ranging
enough to reject people who were active in the computer field but
didn't have adequate knowledge of the field.

By wide-ranging I mean the tests covered all areas of computer design,
operations and management.  The specific areas covered by the test
questions were a surprise to those being tested.  The only studying
possible was to study the entire field of computers (and tab
machines):  business, scientific and control.  

>I did not mean to imply that the tests were easy, or worthless, or
>that they had no meaning.  What I was trying to say is that raw
>knowledge simply does not equate with ability and/or performance.
>There is come correlation, yes.  

Well, the tests were timed (when I took them), so there is a type of
performance measurement.

But ... I agree that the tests weren't designed to measure conceptual
ability or the ability to come up with innovative solutions.

>But every one of us has known people
>who were very well educated, but could hardly tie their shoes in a
>practical situation, or solve a real world problem.  The fact is,
>there are factors far more important than raw knowledge.  

IMO, these tests were designed to reveal practical abilities, as
evidenced by my "boss's" rejection.  He had lots of theoretical
knowledge, but not much hands-on experience (How many punched cards
are in a stack 1" thick?).

>My point is that the correlation is much looser than might
>be expected, because there are a lot of people out there who have
>memorized a lot of facts, but who couldn't use one if it bit them in
>the nose.  Perhaps your old Army boss was such a person? :-)

Again, I agree.  


-- Dean -- from (almost) Duh Moines  (CDP, KB0ZDF)
```

and assign clauses worked.

been converted from Honeywell to IBM in the early seventies  The
results were the most unreadable code I've ever seen.

to kill himself, is it considered a hostage situation?

## Thread: student - clock in display — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### student - clock in display

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5qPo4.611$w9.5131@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Hi,

Another student in my class is trying to get a "ticking" clock on his
display.  He has a loop ACCEPTing the time from the system when the second
changes (control break).  He did get it going, but, of course, he can never
get outside that loop without shutting down the ACCEPT loop , so the screen
freezes up.

How can this be accomplished?  Since it's not my program, I may have some
details incorrect.

Thanks!
Pat
```

#### ↳ Re: student - clock in display

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nOSo4.73$cH3.2161@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<5qPo4.611$w9.5131@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
You are never going to get this to work in  a procedural language/OS
combination such as MS-DOS or Linux COBOL. The closest you can get is to
update the on-screen clock "often" by updating the clock once per "main"
loop. Pseudo-code might look like...


000-MAIN.
   PERFORM 100-MAINLOOP  UNTIL DONE.

100-MAINLOOP.

         PERFORM TIME-ROUTINE
         PERFORM ONE-UNIT-OF-REAL-WORK.

TIME-ROUTINE.

         ACCEPT WS-TIME FROM TIME
         SUBTRACT WS-LAST-TIME FROM WS-TIME GIVING TIME-DIFFERENCE
         IF TIME-DIFFERENCE > ONE-SECOND-OR-WHATEVER-YOU-WANT
             (Update the screeen)
             MOVE WS-TIME  TO LAST-WS-TIME.


You can perform TIME-ROUTINE whenever you feel like it, when there is a
'break in the action'.

If you keep the user busy enough, they won't notice when it's two seconds
between updates instead of one second. (They also won't care).

If you are writing for Windows, of course, you can use a timer. In 32-bit
Windows the OS will interrupt your other processes for you and do the screen
update. In 16-bit Windows, you'll have to release control to Windows
periodically, just as you need to perform TIME-ROUTINE yourself as above.

MCM


Pat wrote in message <5qPo4.611$w9.5131@newsfeed.slurp.net>...

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8888un$8t2$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:5c2p4.678
> Multi-threading under DOS/Linux? I never heard of that.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> cool.

To my knowledge, the multithreading is handled by the runtime. That is,
anything pure Cobol is likely to be preemptive, but I presume a call to for
instance the Win API would not be preemptive.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: student - clock in display

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A419BD.50933EBE@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<5qPo4.611$w9.5131@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Pat wrote:

> Another student in my class is trying to get a "ticking" clock on his
> display.  He has a loop ACCEPTing the time from the system when the second
> changes (control break).  He did get it going, but, of course, he can never
> get outside that loop without shutting down the ACCEPT loop , so the screen
> freezes up.

Sure.  This is the kind of detail which students often want to put into
programs.  I have to admit though if I were teaching a course in a business
language such as COBOL, I would lower the grade of the program which pulled a
stunt like this.

Goals which I want to teach:
1.  Meet the users requirements.
2.  Make it easy for users to use.
3.  Make maintenance easy.  (KISS)
4.  Don't re-invent the wheel (If there is a clock on their computer's GUI,
don't clutter things up by putting another one).
5.  Look for alternative business solutions (put a clock on the wall)
6.  If you have done all of the above, make your program efficient.

Now where in the above would an on-screen clock be appropriate?  Where would it
be a detraction?  This is business, not playing.
```

#### ↳ Re: student - clock in display

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8988oa$60s$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<5qPo4.611$w9.5131@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Using MF (Merant) for many years I forgot how difficult this is if your
compiler don't support
'Accept My-Field with timeout 1'.

Look in your compiler for a function like that. If it's not there, you
should consider giving up on the subject.

Regards
Ib

<a id="t-ec70b6c38f"></a>
## Thread: Converting MF Cobol pgms for international use — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Converting MF Cobol pgms for international use

- **From:** jrinere@mindspring.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<880v3g$3c7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We are starting down the road of converting 400+ MFCobol pgms to be
used in other languages.  The good news is that these are all batch
programs (reports), nothing interactive.  The bad news is that they
weren't designed/written with this in mind.

My current working idea is to implement a compile time solution by
isolating all headings, labels, literals, etc to working storage and
creating a copybook.  We would then create a copybook for each language
to be supported.  By altering the appropriate path's at compile time
and runtime, the correct binary will be compiled/executed.

The difficulty I'm facing now is how to provide a tool to non
programmers to do the translations to the copybooks.  They can't edit
them directly as they will have no concept of syntax, etc.  I'm
thinking about creating some kind of app that will display the English
language text and allow entry of the foreign text.

Anybody have any similar experience?  I would appreciate any
suggestions or comments.  This is not pretty but has to be done.

Thanx for any and all help.

Runtime environment: Microfocus Cobol/HP-UX.
Development: MF Netexpress/Windows NT.
The folks doing the translation will be Windows NT users.



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting MF Cobol pgms for international use

- **From:** jrinere@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<888r29$chh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<880v3g$3c7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <881d2o$8ip$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Thanx for the reply.....

The copybooks would be pretty typical stuff, but since they've been
developed over a number of years by a number of programmers, they are
not consistent.

A simplified example of a heading layout might be:

01  heading-line-1.
    03  filler                   pic x(08)    value "Customer".
    03  filler                   pic x(18)    value spaces.
    03  filler                   pic x(07)    value "Address".
    03  filler                   pic x(19)    value spaces.
    03  filler                   pic x(05)    value "Phone".

01  heading-line-2.
    03  filler                   pic x(04)    value "Name".
    03  filler                   pic x(48)    value spaces.
    03  filler                   pic x(06)    value "Number".

But it may also be coded:

01  heading-line-1.
    03  filler                   pic x(08)
    value "Customer".
    03  filler                   pic x(18)
    value spaces.
    03  filler                   pic x(07)
    value "Address".
    03  filler                   pic x(19)
    value spaces.
    03  filler                   pic x(05)
    value "Phone".

01  heading-line-2.
    03  filler                   pic x(04)
    value "Name".
    03  filler                   pic x(48)
    value spaces.
    03  filler                   pic x(06)
    value "Number".

And a couple of folks thought this was clever:

01  heading-line-1.
    03  filler                   pic x(10)    value "Customer  ".
    03  filler                   pic x(10)    value spaces.
    03  filler                   pic x(10)    value "     Addre".
    03  filler                   pic x(10)    value "ss        "
    03  filler                   pic x(10)    value spaces.
    03  filler                   pic x(10)    value " Phone    ".

01  heading-line-2.
    03  filler                   pic x(10)    value "Name      ".
    03  filler                   pic x(10)    value spaces.
    03  filler                   pic x(10)    value spaces.
    03  filler                   pic x(10)    value spaces.
    03  filler                   pic x(10)    value spaces.
    03  filler                   pic x(10)    value " Number   ".

(No, I'm not kidding!)

In addition, you might have subtotal lines:

01  subtotal-line-1.
    03  subtotal-lit             pic x(25).
    ...
and the associated literals:

01  literals.
    03  ws-customer-subtot-lit   pic x(25)
    value "Total for customer:".
    03  ws-style-subtot-lit   pic x(25)
    value "Total for style:".
    03  ws-color-subtot-lit   pic x(25)
    value "Total for color:".


In article <881d2o$8ip$1@news.cerf.net>,
  "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:
> <jrinere@mindspring.com> wrote in message

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> It would certainly help if you provided some sample of what your copy
books
> look like.
>
> Cheesle
>
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.

<a id="t-d3563b8924"></a>
## Thread: Code problem - adjustment creation — 5 msgs

### Code problem - adjustment creation

- **From:** aflinsch <aflinsch@imisys.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A42336.9F712118@imisys.com>`

```
I am having a problem in creating adjustment transactions. The code I
am
currently using is attached.

Prior to performing the 2300-WRITEOFF process, the table is loaded
from 
a VSAM file and then sorted in descending order by usage charge. 

I need to create an adjustment that is either for the exact amount or 
as near as possible to the exact amount.

The problem is in the writeoff procedure
examples are for a writeoff of $10

ex 1 - usage charges of 23,10,5,4,3,1
exact amount is reached with the $10 charge.

ex 2 - usage charges of 23,7,5,3,2
exact amount is reached using the $7 and $3 charges

ex 3 - usage charges of 23,9,8,7
partial adjustment is created using the $9 amount. This is OK.

ex 4 - usage charges of 23,7,5,5,2
adjustment is created using the $7 charge and the $2 charge for a
partial
adjustment of $9. If there were a way to pick the 2 $5 amounts, I
could
create a full adjustment for the $10. 


What I have works for examples 1,2,3 but does not work for the last 
example. Any ideas on how to search the table for a combination of 
values that would allow me to find a combination that would result
in an exact match?


 01  WS-USAGE-TABLE.                                             
     03  USG-SUB                    PIC S9(04) COMP-3.           
     03  USAGE-DATA OCCURS 500 TIMES                             
                    INDEXED BY USG-IDX, USG-MAX.                 
     05  USG-FLAG                   PIC X(01).                   
         88  USG-REC-USED      VALUE 'X'.                        
     05  USG-RIPS-KEY                  PIX
X(25).                          
     05  USG-RIPS-DATA.                                          
         10  USG-PE                    PIC X(01).                
         10  USG-CHARGE                PIC S9(9)V99 COMP-3.      
         10  USG-BTN.                                            
             15  USG-BTN-NPA           PIC X(03).                
             15  USG-BTN-NXX           PIC X(03).                
             15  USG-BTN-LINE          PIC X(04).                
                                           
                                           
 2300-WRITEOFF.                                                  
*                                                                
* HOW MUCH DO WE NEED TO WRITEOFF?                               
*                                                                
      COMPUTE WS-WRITEOFF-REMAIN =                               
           WS-CARM-WRITEOFF-AMT-IN * -1                          
      END-COMPUTE                                                
      SET NOTHING-WRITTEN-OFF TO TRUE                                
                                                                 
*                                                                
* IF WE FIND SOMETHING IN THE TABLE THAT CAN BE USED             
* DECREMENT THE REMAINING BALANCE BY THE TABLE AMOUNT            
* AND CONTINUE.                                                  
*                                                                
      PERFORM VARYING USG-IDX FROM 1 BY 1                        
              UNTIL (USG-IDX > USG-MAX) OR                       
                    (WS-WRITEOFF-REMAIN = ZERO)                  
         IF USG-CHARGE(USG-IDX) NOT > WS-WRITEOFF-REMAIN         
            SUBTRACT USG-CHARGE(USG-IDX) FROM WS-WRITEOFF-REMAIN 
            SET USG-REC-USED(USG-IDX) TO TRUE                    
            SET SOMETHING-WRITTEN-OFF TO TRUE                    
         END-IF                                                  
      END-PERFORM                                                
                                                                 
*                                                                
* IF WE ACTUALLY WERE ABLE TO USE THE TABLE DATA                 
* CHECK TO SEE IF THE REMAINING BALANCE IS ZERO,                 
*                                                                
* IF IT IS ZERO, WE HAVE AN EXACT AMOUNT, OTHERWISE              
* IT IS A PARTIAL WRITEOFF, SO SEND TO THE CORRECT               
* REPORT FILES                                                  
*                                                                
* IF NOTHING WAS WRITTEN OFF                                     
* THEN WE HAVE A NO DETAIL CONDITION, SO WRITE IT TO THAT        
* REPORT                                                         
*                                                                
      IF SOMETHING-WRITTEN-OFF                                   
         IF WS-WRITEOFF-REMAIN = ZERO                            
            PERFORM 3200-EXACT-MATCH                             
         ELSE                                                    
            PERFORM 3300-PARTIAL-MATCH                           
         END-IF                                                  
         PERFORM 4000-CREATE-ADJUSTMENTS                         
      ELSE                                                       
         PERFORM 3400-NO-DETAIL                                  
      END-IF.
```

#### ↳ Re: Code problem - adjustment creation

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A43026.9C94A1CC@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38A42336.9F712118@imisys.com>`

```
I remember a utility someone wrote once to copy as many files as possible
onto as few floppy disks as possible.  I usually use your procedure,
moving largest available files which could fit.  What this author did was
to calculate every possible combination.

With your sample choices, this would work - but if you have thousands of
usage charges, it would be too inefficient unless you had no choice.
Obviously, you can eliminate all usage charges and sums too large to
work, building up a table of usage charges and sums.

01   USAGE-TABLE     OCCURS 10000
       05   USAGE-SUM     PIC S9(5)V99.
       05   USAGE-CHARGE PIC S9(5)V99   OCCURS 10.

After you have the table created, you do a search for the largest
available sum, and then plug in the usage-charges associated with that
sum.

Are your quantities such that this solution would work?

aflinsch wrote:

> I am having a problem in creating adjustment transactions. The code I
> am
…[95 more quoted lines elided]…
>       END-IF.
```

## Thread: Using Forms with MF COBOL — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Using Forms with MF COBOL

- **From:** jgill_1@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87vr88$am1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I'm working on an invoicing system using MicroFocus Cobol (Ver 3.2).
I would like to print a completed Invoice on the laser printer.
My goal is to merge the data from the Invoicing system with an
image of the invoice form and then print the result. I do not want
to use a preprinted form.
In other words, I want to buy a forms program where:
  1. I can design my invoice with fonts and logos.
  2. Have my Cobol program create a file that has the invoice data.
  3. Merge the file and the form image and send to laser printer.
  4. I might print 1 to 10 invoices at a time.
  5. The Cobol program would run as a DOS program under Windows 9X.

Any suggestions on a forms program that can do this ????


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Using Forms with MF COBOL

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38a40782.1031497@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<87vr88$am1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
jgill_1@my-deja.com wrote:

>I'm working on an invoicing system using MicroFocus Cobol (Ver 3.2).
>I would like to print a completed Invoice on the laser printer.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any suggestions on a forms program that can do this ????

Jgill:

FormPrint can do everything above, *except* run under DOS.  Micro
Focus COBOL version 3.2 *does* support the ability to run in a Windows
16 bit environment.  Have you considered running the program under
Windows 3.1/3.11?  We have a version of FormPrint which supports the
16 bit Windows environment.

I'm not aware of any DOS oriented forms programs which support Micro
Focus COBOL.

Sorry!


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Using Forms with MF COBOL

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<881djg$qct$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<87vr88$am1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I have Fujitsu code to do this, if you want it.  To tell the truth, though,
I am replacing it with SP2 Formprint, as it was primarily a stopgap measure,
and while it works, it is a bit clumsy.  The code works as follows:

The code is written to produce a plain ASCII text print file, as a line
sequential file complete with formfeeds.  It is then spooled using the
subroutine and a form name.  The form is the output of MS word.  That is,
create the form, and print  the form to a file. The file can be tested by
copying it to the printer, and you should get a blank form.

The spooler scans the text file for formfeeds.  Whenever it finds one, it
ejects the current page (if one is there), and reads the form file, passing
it to the printer.  When it gets to the final formfeed of the form, it
stops, resets the laser cursor to line one, column 1, resets the font to
courier, and overprints the next page of the report on top of the form
(laser printers have the ability to use cursor control, the same as a
screen). On the last page, it give a final formfeed to eject the last page.
Voila!  Report is printed on a form.

The major problem is that it is written for an HP laser IV only. By going to
FORMPRINT, you can print to any printer.

jgill_1@my-deja.com wrote in message <87vr88$am1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I'm working on an invoicing system using MicroFocus Cobol (Ver 3.2).
>I would like to print a completed Invoice on the laser printer.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

Best regards

#### ↳ Re: Print screen under w98

- **From:** mrtonyr@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8817d3$9ie$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<880i0v$enl$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it>`

```
In article <880i0v$enl$1@pegasus.tiscalinet.it>,
  "Massimo Morgia" <areasoftware@tiscali.it> wrote:
> I must print the screen (Microfocus cobol caracter pgm)
> under w98 how to do ?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In Micro-focus you can use the cobol function "CBL_READ_SCR_CHATTRS"
to read the screen into a buffer and then just write the buffer to the
printer one line at a time. Micro Focus manual should tell you how to
call the function and the working storage requirements.

Tony


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: AcuODBC and Web Development — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### AcuODBC and Web Development

- **From:** root@localhost.com (Deland)
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38a4449f.11127961@news.bayou.com>`

```
Hello,

Has anyone had any experience using AcuCorp's AcuODBC for web
development?  If so, what products were required other than an HTTP
server, AcuCobol GT, and AcuODBC?  What I am trying to do
is use WinNT/IIS or Solaris/Apache with Perl/CGI or PHP to access
vision data files residing on a seperate UNIX server.  To do this, I
would like to use an ODBC connection ( provided by AcuODBC or any
other product that can achieve this).

Anyone have any thoughts??

Thanks in advance,
```

#### ↳ Re: AcuODBC and Web Development

- **From:** "Pete Withey" <peterw@acucorp.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8825hi$ocq$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<38a4449f.11127961@news.bayou.com>`

```
Deland:

   You would need to use an NT server since the AcuODBC driver (Acucorp's
ODBC driver to access Vision files) runs only under a Windows environment
(NT or 9x).  In addition, since the data files are one a UNIX system you
would need the product Acuserver which allows the AcuODBC driver to access
Vision files on a UNIX server acting as a file server.  If you have any
additional questions on this feel free to contact Acucorp Systems
Engineering department at 1-888-767-0670.

Peter Withey
Acucorp Systems Engineering

news:38a4449f.11127961@news.bayou.com...

>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

> Hi:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    COBOL using a GUI such as the wonderful FLEXUS?

## COBOL is a 'Turing Machine,' as such, anything doable in any other
Turing Machine is doable in COBOL. Therefore, the answer to your
question is, theoretically, all.
>
> 2. Would Windows have been more or less dependable because
>    a large percentage was written in COBOL?

## We don't know what percentage of Windows is already in COBOL. Maybe
all of it. If so, your question is moot. Even if there is no COBOL
code in Windows, your question presumes Windows could be made more
dependable. Maybe not.

>
> Thanks
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.

#### ↳ Re: Windows in COBOL?

- **From:** joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net (John McKown)
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn8ahsfe.dfl.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net>`
- **References:** `<883mo2$1aa$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

On Sat, 12 Feb 2000 13:21:07 GMT, Foodman <foodman123@aol.com> wrote:
	[snip]
>1. What percentage of Windows could have been written in
>   COBOL using a GUI such as the wonderful FLEXUS?
 You don't say, but common usage has me guessing that you mean Microsoft
Windows(tm). Since the really large, hairy portion of MS Windows(tm) is the
GUI, then this particular question seems a bit strange to me. Or maybe it's
me who is strange <grin>. Anyway, which COBOL? I doubt that any of the low
level hardware control could be written in any ANSI standard dialect. That's
because COBOL isn't designed to do bits & bytes & I/O ports unless compiler
specific extentions are created.
>
>2. Would Windows have been more or less dependable because
>   a large percentage was written in COBOL?
The actual language used for implementation is not as important as having
programmers and analysts who really know what they are doing. They need a
cohesive, integrated plan which has ways to extend the design in a
non-disruptive manner (i.e. add new features without causing "unrelated"
problems. To the best that I can tell, every version of Windows that is or
has ever been is a kludged together piece of -----. There are a lot of
"neat hacks" in the product (from some of the "unauthorized internals"
books that I've read). But "neat hacks" are only programmer ego boosts and
really poor programming practice. It's a sign of poor design when a change
to the video driver can crash the print driver. They should be totally
separate (except perhaps for some generic rasterizing subroutines), however
I have this happen. This indicates, to me, that there are "global" variables
that are passed all over the stupid system. Well, enough of my ranting.
If you want better, but admittedly not perfect, code, then look at Linux.
At least if the GUI crashes, I can get running in character mode. I haven't
found a way to do that with WindowNT 4.0!
>
>Thanks
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Windows in COBOL?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6jiq4.5608$y15.61723@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<883mo2$1aa$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <slrn8ahsfe.dfl.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net>`

```
John McKown wrote:
>
>The actual language used for implementation is not as important as having
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>found a way to do that with WindowNT 4.0!

Over the last several decades I have designed, written and installed
quite a few computer systems; somewhere around 70.  I can say honestly
that I would be ashamed if I ever produced a system as bad as Windows.
I understand that the average age of Microsoft employees is around 20.
My guess is that they hired all these really bright, just-out-of-college,
know-all-there-is kids, who did not have enough collective experience
to recognize good design if it bit them.  The whole structure of Windows
is inefficient, peculiar, fragile and insecure, from the bottom to the
top.  I can understand this, even though it drives me nuts.  What I
really can't understand is why the whole world seems bent on running
every piece of data processed on the planet through some version of
Windows at some point.  If we had any other commonly used item, be it
automobile, video recorder, toaster oven, you name it, that was as
unreliable as Windows, we would throw it out the window, and maybe sue
the manufacturer.  Anybody who works around mainframes knows that
there are computers that run hundreds of programs at the same time,
day after day, week after week, and month after month, often only to
be rebooted to update the system software.  These computers are set
upon by hoards of program bugs made by whole staffs of programmers,
never to be 'crashed' by any of this.  This is not unusual, this is
common.  So why does everybody put up with all this garbage from a PC
operating system?  I put up with it because my clients demand it.  But
why do they put up with it?  Beats me.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows in COBOL?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A9B86B.C6111180@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<883mo2$1aa$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <slrn8ahsfe.dfl.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net> <6jiq4.5608$y15.61723@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

>  What I
> really can't understand is why the whole world seems bent on running
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> why do they put up with it?  Beats me.

You can't run AOL, TOMB RAIDER, or WORD on a cheap MVS PC, not to mention cutting
and pasting between your terminal emulation program and your document processor.

Being "standard" is very attractive.  That's a lot of the reason why more people
use IBM mainframes than Burroughs mainframes.  Certainly I can't see any
advantage that Unix has over Vax VMS other than Unix is ubiquitous and seemingly
non-proprietary.  But that's enough, Unix beat VMS in the marketing game.
```
