life ended in 1981.


Though MS is touting W2000 as a much better and stable system, they are
abanding lots of things they supported in several prior versions.

My answer would be that they had moxy, money, and there was a void.
However, I did not start out using dos on my pc.

BTW, as a high school student, I thought Thomas Edison was a great
inventor.  He may have been. However, reflecting that perception, my
daughter bought me a biography of old Tom.

It was an eye opener.  The only problem with that was I was also a slow
learner as I believe I was close to retirement at the time I read that
book.

Warren Simmons
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:6jiq4.5608$y15.61723@news3.mia...
> John McKown wrote:
> >
> >The actual language used for implementation is not as important as
having

a
> >cohesive, integrated plan which has ways to extend the design in a
> >non-disruptive manner (i.e. add new features without causing "unrelated"
> >problems. To the best that I can tell, every version of Windows that is
or
> >has ever been is a kludged together piece of -----. There are a lot of
> >"neat hacks" in the product (from some of the "unauthorized internals"
> >books that I've read). But "neat hacks" are only programmer ego boosts
and
> >really poor programming practice. It's a sign of poor design when a
change
> >to the video driver can crash the print driver. They should be totally
> >separate (except perhaps for some generic rasterizing subroutines),
however
> >I have this happen. This indicates, to me, that there are "global"
variables
> >that are passed all over the stupid system. Well, enough of my ranting.
> >If you want better, but admittedly not perfect, code, then look at
Linux.
> >At least if the GUI crashes, I can get running in character mode. I
haven't
> >found a way to do that with WindowNT 4.0!
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows in COBOL?

- **From:** "Bubba King" <bubba@king.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<epdjkvQf$GA.276@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<883mo2$1aa$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <slrn8ahsfe.dfl.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net> <6jiq4.5608$y15.61723@news3.mia>`

```
It is to no surprise that the saying goes " bad design = bad code ",
regardless of the OS they run on.

It is just incredible that there are still people that criticize Windows and
do not an iota about the OS. Realistically, are you referring to Windows as
Win9x, or Win NT? Have you ever seen the code inside Linux for a change? How
can one say that Windows is hacked when Linux is ALL a bunch of hacks, that
every single task requires one to recompile a kernel, which may or may not
work? Also, have you seen how MVS is written as to tell if it has or has not
hacks on it?

The fact is that blaming on Windows for programmer's inability to write
decent code is a habit that many a manager have. If the age what MS personal
is a factor, what about the age of your own shops? Since when we are
measuring capabilities with age? Or have we become wines now ????

Frankly, I seldom write to the forum because it seems that instead of
working to solve interoperability issues with the language it became a Mecca
for blaming MS for everything under the sun ....

My 0.02 ....

Andre

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows in COBOL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<N4xs4.4859$Wv2.60596@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<883mo2$1aa$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <slrn8ahsfe.dfl.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net> <6jiq4.5608$y15.61723@news3.mia> <epdjkvQf$GA.276@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```
Hmmm.  Andre, when you write good code, it works.  You may write crummy
code that still works, but code that does not work is crummy, period.
Sorry, but that's just the way it is.  If it doesn't work correctly,
it is not serving its purpose.  The *only* reason for programs to exist,
after all, is to work correctly.  It doesn't matter whether Microsoft
wrote it, or me.  In over 31 years of programming, I have never delivered
a single program or system that failed as often as once a month.  I often
go literally years without a single bug report from any client.  Many of
these systems have hundreds of thousands of lines of code, and are very
complex.  There is not one single user of Windows on this planet that has
seen anything remotely near that kind of reliability.  There is probably
not been one Microsoft application that has had that kind of reliability.
When you do it right, it works.  When it doesn't work, you didn't do it
right.  Microsoft is not doing it right, or Windows would be more stable.
They do things for the wrong reasons; marketing instead of quality.  It
was their choice, I am simply commenting on it. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows in COBOL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SHJs4.122$M65.1320@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<883mo2$1aa$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <slrn8ahsfe.dfl.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net> <6jiq4.5608$y15.61723@news3.mia> <epdjkvQf$GA.276@cpmsnbbsa04> <N4xs4.4859$Wv2.60596@news2.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@bellsouth.net>

> In over 31 years of programming, I have never delivered
> a single program or system that failed as often as once a month.  I
often
> go literally years without a single bug report from any client.
Many of
> these systems have hundreds of thousands of lines of code, and are
very
> complex.

Windows 2000 has over 31,000,000 lines of code and over 8,000 (as of
today) drivers written for it. A staff in excess of 4,000 worked full
time for over 4 years (and even then, they didn't start from scratch,
but had a working NT 4 system to build on). In its final beta, W2K was
in production use on over 100,000 desktops. The goal of W2K
(NT/98/95/3) is to facilitate the interaction of hundreds of thousands
of possible hardware hunks with a like number of software entities,
many of which - both hardware and software - are, frankly, not up to
Microsoft's impeccable standards.

> When you do it right, it works.  When it doesn't work, you didn't do
it
> right.  Microsoft is not doing it right, or Windows would be more
stable.

Windows is perfectly stable. There is not a case on record of Windows
crashing on its own. In every reported instance, there was hardware
hooked up to it and even, sometimes, application software. We all know
the old saw: "It's the hardware, the software, or the data." Note that
"the Operating System" is not one of the usual suspects.



> They do things for the wrong reasons; marketing instead of quality.
It
> was their choice, I am simply commenting on it.

If it is a choice (and I'm not saying it is) between marketing and
quality, pick marketing. History is littered with the dried, bleached,
and scattered bones of quality (Betamax, Macintosh, Hunter autos).

Just my two cents.

P.S.

I bought Microsoft at $36.
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<890upv$9lg$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<883mo2$1aa$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <slrn8ahsfe.dfl.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net> <6jiq4.5608$y15.61723@news3.mia> <epdjkvQf$GA.276@cpmsnbbsa04> <N4xs4.4859$Wv2.60596@news2.mia> <SHJs4.122$M65.1320@news.swbell.net> <38B3EB04.C3E2015A@cusys.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38B3EB04.C3E2015A@cusys.edu...
> Jerry P wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> it, it would never have crashed.

Well, there is comparision to AS400 another place in this thread, and I
would like to emphasize that this comparision is somewhat unfair.

One issue is that AS400 on the one side, is proprietary, and if any third
party vendor comes in, it is after a number of extensive qualifications.
While the Wintel platform on the other side, is open to anyone which has a
piece of HW/SW to supply.

Another issue is that because the AS400 is targeting businesses only, more
money are put in to quality test that HW/HW and HW/SW cooperates well. You
can get Wintel computers too, that are rock solid as long as you don't mess
with them, and in particular if you are willing to pay the price.
Obviously, if you buy that smash offer from company xyz by mail, you have no
idea what you get. If, you on the other hand purchase a dedicated server
from Compaq, Dell you will have to pay the price, but they will also
guarantee you that the parts and software cooperate well.

One should also note the difference in that while the AS400 is primarily a
server computer, what one compares it to on the Wintel side is a
workstation. Wintel servers, while not as reliable as AS400 is still way
more reliable than its workstations.

The basic problem with Wintel and its instances of sudden death is that the
device drivers are too close to the kernel. It could have been moved out,
but given the heavy graphics of the Wintel computers, this is a choice of
stability versus speed. Device drivers are often done by third party, hence,
Wintel stability are then relying on the "good" work of people that are not
blamed in this context. Microsoft could have demanded to do the drivers
themselves, and put them through the same quality criteria as for an AS400.
But I be damned if Mr. John Doe would like to a) pay the extra cost this
would imply and b) accept the limited market of cheap peripheral equipment
this would force.

Finally, let's not forget that the OS may be blamed for many things, but it
can't be blamed for the fact that the Intel CPU's still today has big flaws,
because of the poor design done back then when the first 8080 was
introduced. It is called backward compatibility.

Wintel is a success because of our demand to have as much as possible for as
little as possible. Ever thought about why mainframes are as expensive as
they are?

I am no admirer of Microsoft as such myself, as I have pointed out earlier
in this NG, I used to be a die hard OS/2'er. But for the case of seriousity,
we should differ between facts and fiction.

Someone will certainly point out that Linux is on the Intel hardware as
well, indeed they are, and indeed it is a puzzle, for those that would like
to make their Linux installation cooperate a tape streamer, CD Rom, sound
card, nic, powerful graphics and may be even an USB or two. Particularly if
playing around with some games.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows in COBOL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B2A261.F7D424F0@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<883mo2$1aa$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <slrn8ahsfe.dfl.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net> <6jiq4.5608$y15.61723@news3.mia> <epdjkvQf$GA.276@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```
Bubba King wrote:

> It is to no surprise that the saying goes " bad design = bad code ",
> regardless of the OS they run on.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Win9x, or Win NT? Have you ever seen the code inside Linux for a change?

As a customer, I don't care why my system crashes.  I just want the OS to handle
these mistakes.  On a PC, I can live with the crashes.  If my sound card doesn't
handle NT or my CPU, then I blame HIVAL for ripping me off (and I do).

But an OS which can't abort a misbehaving program without crashing is not
qualified to handle enterprise needs.

But Microsoft is responsible for a bit more than the actual OS.  They set the
de-facto standards for programs as well.  With the rate of change of technology,
it is not inconceivable that we might want to upgrade our hardware.  When we do
so, we will want to move our software (Microsoft Office, for instance), complete
with personal settings and live updates.  (not to mention enhanced NTFS with
NT's service pack 5).  If we work with workgroups, we want workgroup based
spellcheck libraries.

The main thing wrong with Windows is that it isn't yet what Microsoft wants it
to be.
```

I'll agree with you, Howard.

I could work with Win 3.1x and usually get things working.  Not only
does Win 9x do things really better here, but it does things behind your
back that make things worse.  We have a 64 bit chip here!  I've worked
with 16 bit machines that were a whole lot more reliable and much harder
to crash than Win9x, even WinNT 4.0.  Ask some of the AS/400 people how
often they have to reboot their machine.  I've done it as part of
installing a whole-package upgrade.  I've seen one go out, but only
because someone unplugged it!

Charles R

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<8998ag$5dh$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<883mo2$1aa$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <slrn8ahsfe.dfl.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net> <6jiq4.5608$y15.61723@news3.mia> <epdjkvQf$GA.276@cpmsnbbsa04> <38B2A261.F7D424F0@cusys.edu> <38B2C2C5.4C3F4913@sprynet.com> <MPG.132163d2b33b66f79898e2@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote in message
news:MPG.132163d2b33b66f79898e2@news.mersinet.co.uk...
  <much snippage>
>
> If anyone bought a new car and it behaved like Windows, they'd be
> demanding a refund and a new car.
>

For many years, I have been meaning to write a new book (acknowledgement to
Nader) called:

  "Unsafe at any release"

This would be based on my experience(s) working for both mainframe and
"PC-ish" software vendors.  I came up with this idea LONG before Windows 3.0
(remember THAT fiasco!).  It really does astonish me (still) what
"expletive-deleted" most software users are willing to put up with - as long
as they can find SOME circumvention to get their work done. Particularly
surprising (to me) is how often paying customers get "individual fixes" that
go away when the next GA version of the software comes out - when the same
old problem reappears.
```

>Hi:
>

>   COBOL using a GUI such as the wonderful FLEXUS?

Tony:

Because COBOL sp2 displays its Windows GUI screens by invoking the
Windows API functions directly from the sp2.dll, it sounds like this
could be a "chicken and egg" discussion.  In other words, which came
first?  We are indeed dependent upon the Windows API functions for
displaying our GUI screens, therefore, I'm not sure thsi could be
done.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

## Thread: Everyone Sing - "Give Me That Old-Time Dinosaur Code, It's Good Enough For Me!" — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### Everyone Sing - "Give Me That Old-Time Dinosaur Code, It's Good Enough For Me!"

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 2000-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000212002523.00248.00002059@ng-fo1.aol.com>`

```
Here's progress for you...

In COBOL, we used IF AGE IS NUMERIC...

Per the author's request for acknowledgement, I've clipped the following
Javascript equivalent from:

http://sunflower.singnet.com.sg/~kongeuta/prgmming/jsinnov.htm

<head>
	<script language="javascript">
		function validate(){
			var digits="0123456789"
			var temp

			if (document.testform.age.value==""){
				alert("Invalid Age !")
				return false
			}
			for (var i=0;i<document.testform.age.value.length;i++){
				temp=document.testform.age.value.substring(i,i+1)
				if (digits.indexOf(temp)==-1){
					alert("Invalid Age !")
					return false
				}
			}
			return true
		}
	</script>
</head>

<body>
	<form name="testform" onSubmit="return validate()">
		Name:<input type="text" size=30 name="Name">
		Age:<input type="text" size=3 name="age">
		<input type="submit" value="Submit">
	</form>
</body>
```

<a id="t-101c78fff2"></a>
## Thread: Microsoft Cobol 5.0 — 2 msgs

### Microsoft Cobol 5.0

- **From:** kelvincsc@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8848p9$d82$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
is microsoft cobol 100% compatible with microfocus cobol under unix ??


<kelvincsc>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Microsoft Cobol 5.0

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<884aqe$6ka$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8848p9$d82$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Assuming Microsoft 5 - as in your subject line when - then, yes it was
compatible with the "Micro Focus COBOL" of the same age (which is about a
decade old now).  There certainly is NO claim that current Micro Focus COBOL
is compatible with "ancient" (and long unsupported) Microsoft COBOL.
(Although there are directives and run-time switches to make it "medium
compatible".)

If you *REALLY* meant to ask if the current Micro Focus COBOL under Wintel is
compatible with the current Micro Focus COBOL under Unix, then the answer is
"basically" - but not 100% (some subroutines are operating system specific -
as are some facilities/operations) - but they are close enough be considered
"highly" compatible.
```

## Thread: Am I wasting my time? — 18 this month / 22 total

### Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** "" <graham@kinmelbay.fsbusiness.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
I'm currently studying COBOL as a first language to enter the world of
progamming.  My view is that it's a good first language, but my first
programming job is more likely to be in the  VB arena.
Do you get my drift?  Learn an easy language and then move on to more
contemporary languages once the principles have been learnt.
Am I making sense or have I lost it?  Either way, it will only be a few
months now before I start the job hunting.
Please give me some sort of reassurance, it must be out there somewhere!
However, am I completely wrong?


Graham Williams
```

#### ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<887aki$vrj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
In comp.lang.cobol "" <graham@kinmelbay.fsbusiness.co.uk> wrote:
: I'm currently studying COBOL as a first language to enter the world of
: progamming.  My view is that it's a good first language, but my first
: programming job is more likely to be in the  VB arena.
: Do you get my drift?  Learn an easy language and then move on to more
: contemporary languages once the principles have been learnt.

In what way is a Basic derivitive 'more contemporary' ?
Basic is from the 60s.  VB is a proprietry language that is 
rwritten with each new version and is stuck on one platform
(unless you consider all the fragmented versions of MS Windows
as several platforms).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<888s64$lam$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <887aki$vrj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
I also find it amusing that someone thinks it an advantage to program in an
difficult language.  I always thought the purpose of a compiler was to make
it easier, not to make it harder.  If I want hard, I can just go back to
coding in hex, the way we bootstrapped up to this point.

Mind you, expecting intelligence from a school kid is a bit much,
considering the quality of the teaching.

Richard Plinston wrote in message <887aki$vrj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
>In comp.lang.cobol "" <graham@kinmelbay.fsbusiness.co.uk> wrote:
>: I'm currently studying COBOL as a first language to enter the world of
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>-------------------------------------------------------------- */
```

#### ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ERHp4.1858$0g.34837@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
If it is your goal to study the language-de-jour, that is the only job
you will ever have.

We had a thread recently where a programmer found a COBOL program in
his shop that was used every day and that had not been revised in
twenty-three years!

Further, COBOL is not an 'easy' language - none of them are. But COBOL
is a good tool to teach programming in that it more closely resembles
English. Those folk not comfortable with symbol manipulation can grasp
the concepts of programming more easily than if the school used, say,
(God forbid!) Java.

It's OK to learn lots of languages; your programming language skills
will make you a coder - someone who translates a flow-chart into
machine-sensible source code. If you want to be a professional
programmer or system analyst or programming manager or vice-president
or entrepreneur, take all the mathematics (at least through
differential equations) and business courses you can find.

In sum, don't strain on the nuances of COBOL in your course;
concentrate on the concepts. In the case at hand, COBOL is only a
means, not an end.

Best wishes, and let us know how the course turned out.



 <graham@kinmelbay.fsbusiness.co.uk> wrote in message
news:8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk...
> I'm currently studying COBOL as a first language to enter the world
of
> progamming.  My view is that it's a good first language, but my
first
> programming job is more likely to be in the  VB arena.
> Do you get my drift?  Learn an easy language and then move on to
more
> contemporary languages once the principles have been learnt.
> Am I making sense or have I lost it?  Either way, it will only be a
few

>If you want to be a professional
>programmer or system analyst or programming manager or vice-president
>or entrepreneur, take all the mathematics (at least through
>differential equations) and business courses you can find.

While I agree with the general thrust of your post, and my own formal
educational background is in physics and math, and I am for learning
as much as possible; other than algebra and statistics, I have never
had the need for higher math in business programming.  I have used
calculus on some personal projects, but never for business.  In the
sciences, yes.  But business?  I would be interested in hearing what
needs others may have encountered in business programming for math
beyond algebra and statistics?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A835B2.31432F97@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <ERHp4.1858$0g.34837@news.swbell.net> <oFVp4.2503$Ez.59564@news2.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> While I agree with the general thrust of your post, and my own formal
> educational background is in physics and math, and I am for learning
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> beyond algebra and statistics?

My undergraduate degree is in physics, and I have never had any reason to
use any math which I didn't learn in the couple of weeks spent in high
school learning the slide rule.  Unfortunately, when the slide rule died,
so did many of the lessons which it taught students:

1.  With a slide rule, you learn proportion and inverse proportion.  Not
only are these useful for math, this whole method of thinking is useful in
non-math topics (which is why it is use in English portions of SATs (big
is to little as giant is to X).
2.  With a slide rule, you learn the value of significant digits.  You
know that a circle which is about a yard in diameter isn't
3.1415926589793... yards in circumference.  PI=3 to one significant
digit.  This is even a voting issue when people talk about dangers of
cyclamates, etc.
3.  With a slide rule, you learn to estimate your results before you make
them.  You have to know what answers make sense and recognize when the
numbers you get are silly.

How many people know these lessons?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bWWp4.2651$Ez.62465@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <ERHp4.1858$0g.34837@news.swbell.net> <oFVp4.2503$Ez.59564@news2.mia> <38A835B2.31432F97@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>How many people know these lessons?

That's a good point.  Exposure to mathematical reasoning and process
might be more important than the math itself, in many cases.  I also
took a slide rule  course, but the single math course that most expanded
my mental horizons was high school geometry, which introduced me to
formal mathematical proof and reasoning, followed by calculus where I
learned where all those formulas for area, volume, acceleration, etc.
came from that I had been memorizing all those years. :-)  I found my
physics courses tougher than the math, because in physics they take
knowledge of the math as a given, merely a tool to solve the *real*
problem.  It's probably Newton's fault; he invented calculus simply
as a tool, an incidental side issue, to study gravity, which was the
*real* problem.  A fact that Leibniz no doubt pondered upon from time
to time. ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A87DD1.FBD9E5D6@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <ERHp4.1858$0g.34837@news.swbell.net> <oFVp4.2503$Ez.59564@news2.mia> <38A835B2.31432F97@cusys.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote:
<snip>
> 3.  With a slide rule, you learn to estimate your results before you make
> them.  You have to know what answers make sense and recognize when the
> numbers you get are silly.
> 
> How many people know these lessons?

Pilots who have to use a flight computer (circular slide rule)
learn this lesson visually.  I hadn't used a slide rule for so
long that when I took up flying, it took me a week to remember
how to use one (I have several slip sticks in my old Electronics
class stuff).  Since then, I prefer the circular slide rule
(Flight Computer) to the calculator.

Lastly, my instructor is an ME.  And one of the things he keeps
harping about is the reasonability tests.  I can't tell you how
many errors I've caught while planning a cross-country just
because of recognizing when a number is just plain silly (or, too
good to be true, etc.).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88a835$cj9o$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <ERHp4.1858$0g.34837@news.swbell.net> <oFVp4.2503$Ez.59564@news2.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:oFVp4.2503$Ez.59564@news2.mia...
> While I agree with the general thrust of your post, and my own formal
> educational background is in physics and math, and I am for learning
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."

    I started out studying electrical engineering and had one year of
calculus and physics but never finished the original plan.  Was assigned to
data processing while in the service and acquired a liking for programming,
so when I started out in the business world, I started as an operator then
programmer.  I've not had much of a need for the math in programming since I
feel that it's more important to understand logic than math when dealing
with business problems and solutions.  The value of determining
"reasonableness" or "approximating" as one poster mentioned can't be
over-emphasized.  Something as simple as knowing the approximate total of
accumulated numbers or an average.  Using a slide rule, as one mentioned, is
invaluable in teaching the "approximating"  and "reasonableness" lesson.
Basically, I think it's more important to be able to think logically than to
be able to do the math.  After all, COBOL does that pretty good!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O1dq4.749$BS.8452@news.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <ERHp4.1858$0g.34837@news.swbell.net> <oFVp4.2503$Ez.59564@news2.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:oFVp4.2503$Ez.59564@news2.mia...
> Jerry P wrote:
> >
> >If you want to be a professional
> >programmer or system analyst or programming manager or
vice-president
> >or entrepreneur, take all the mathematics (at least through
> >differential equations) and business courses you can find.
>
> While I agree with the general thrust of your post, and my own
formal
> educational background is in physics and math, and I am for learning
> as much as possible; other than algebra and statistics, I have never
> had the need for higher math in business programming.....

Misunderstanding. I didn't mean to imply you would actually USE
differential equations or hyperbolic arctangents or gaussian functions
or point-set topology or calculus of manifolds. These college courses
are, like the poster's COBOL, a means to an end. And the end, in my
judgment, is logical thinking, deductive thought processes, and symbol
manipulation.

On a more prosaic level, consider Plane Geometry. How much of a demand
for surveyors is there anyway? No, I believe high schools offer
geometry to teach 'thinking' not 'triangles.'

When I went to law school, I was stunned. I ranked in the top 10% of
my freshman class. I went to an advisor. "What's going on?" said I. "I
study very little compared to some of my classmates who, I'm sure,
even DREAM in Latin."

"What was your undergraduate major?" he inquired.

"I have a master's in math," I replied.

"Oh, you'll have no trouble in law school," he said. "Our job is not
to teach the law (because it changes every hour), but to teach
thinking like a lawyer, that is, deductively. You already know how to
do that. People who come to us from math, the physical sciences,
engineering, they usually have no trouble. Business majors do average.
And those who come from liberal arts, music, history, English, and so
on, have it really tough."

P.S.,

<asbestos suit zipped up>

Ever seen a program written by an English Literature major?
```

#### ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A80EF4.F5B08AB9@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
 wrote:

> I'm currently studying COBOL as a first language to enter the world of
> progamming.  My view is that it's a good first language, but my first
> programming job is more likely to be in the  VB arena.
> Do you get my drift?  Learn an easy language and then move on to more
> contemporary languages once the principles have been learnt.

Which is the easy language?  I know VB is harder to maintain, but for the
functions it is designed to be good in, it is pretty easy.  If you have a
good instructor who can teach you why various programming techniques were
developed for COBOL, and how file formats have changed, you will find COBOL
useful.

Or if you want to work with a widely used language which doesn't have lots
of new programmers competing with you, it could be useful.  It might be good
to look at what kind of jobs are offered in your area and decide which kinds
you would enjoy working in.  And a job is more than the tool (language), it
is the business environment.

Of course, what you really need is a variety of skills with experience.  A
career plan (subject to change) is essential.  Keep relatively current with
different tools and be ready for change.


>
> Am I making sense or have I lost it?  Either way, it will only be a few
> months now before I start the job hunting.

Good time to do this research.
```

#### ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38a859b0.10197202@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
The better profesionals in this field don't consider one language to
be better than another.  The correct answer should always be "it
depends upon what needs to be done".  Your ability to "go with the
flow" of the current business need is a better measure of your
profesionalism.

You will often have un-pleasurable languages thrown at you.  Proving
you can do well in COBOL even though you hold a negative attitude
towards it would look good on your resume.

You might find it interesting what my 'real world' is like;
We don't use VB here because it has limitations we don't like.  We use
PowerBuilder and Oracle Forms for our big desktop applications.  Our
primary database for PowerBuilder is DB2 running on a mainframe, and
we found that if our PowerBuilder programs call COBOL programs on the
mainframe, the desktop applications run faster.  We still hire COBOL
programmers here.

Pete

In article <8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>,
  "" <graham@kinmelbay.fsbusiness.co.uk> wrote:

The first language I learned was ADA,  I found it to be as straight
forward as COBOL.  I took a VB class and found it was easier in certain
respects. Each language has its own strengths and weaknesses. I now
maintain COBOL programs and find it more than maddening to follow
someones else's spaggetti code and uncertain logic. I guess what I
saying is that programming logic is really more important than learning
a certain lanaguage. If you can get the logic down, you will be a decent
programmer no matter what language is used.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** whoisthis@worldnet.att.net (Whos This)
- **Date:** 2000-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38bae5ab.20079204@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk> <88ambd$nid$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Thank you CrazedHope.  I was reading this thread and no one else
brought up the logic issue.  It does not matter what language and/or
how well you know it, if you don't think in a direct logical manner,
you can never program well.  I had one programmer working for me that
could quote ever rule of Cobol.  It knew it frontward and backward,
but he didn't have the logic to put it together.  He was a great
mathematician and teacher but a terrible programmer.  (He may have
written some of the spaghetti code you are trying to maintain.)  

You can teach programming languages, but logic is something you have.


BTW, I am not great in math.  I don't have to be, I have a computer to
do my math.  ;-)

#### ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** Tim Hopper <hoppertr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38AAA4D7.4E4F5AA8@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
I just finishe a master's program in CIS.  They teach advanced Cobol at the
university and I took it.  I graduated undergraduate in 1983 and had the
standard two courses is Cobol 74, but have worked as  a machinist since.
After being layed off and fired several times over the years, I decided to
go back to graduate school and get a masters.  I fini8shed Dec. 1999.
Beside the numerrous graduate research papers required in the curriculum,
the Cobol course required a term paper for completetion.  The subject I
chose was the future of Cobol and OOCobol.  It seems as if you asked an
"expert" five years ago if Cobol would be dead today, they would have said
yes.  But not today (judging from the articles I read).  Cobol seems to have
a future, and it did seem that not many universitites (I in typing
withdrawal) are teaching it anymore.  This worried me as I approached
graduation that I was learning legacies again, but most of the articles
confirmed that Cobol is indeed alive and well, and on tap for object
orientation.  I sure hope so.  Since I graduated I have been teaching myself
C++, Visual Basic and Java,  But I still love Cobol.  I'm still in shock at
being able to use my home computer to program these languages at home, but
it sure beats having to wait for a terminal at the university
lab(1981-1982)!!!!!!!

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

```
Hi Guys,

I'm trying to read some data out of a visual cobol ISAM file, but I can't
get
my hands on the sourcecode anymore. Is there some free- or shareware tool
that can read this format, or can anyone tell me what the file structure
looks like ?

#### ↳ Re: ISAM

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<887gmk$49p$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<887gb3$c2a$1@enterprise.cistron.net>`

```
When you say "visual cobol" - do you happen to know the vendor?  The only
vendor that "officially" has a product by that name is Mbp - but that is
"medium uncommon" (but as your ID shows you are in Europe, it is more common
there).  Micro Focus had a product called "Visual Object COBOL" (VisOC for
short).  There are a NUMBER of other compilers that have "visual programming
environments" - so it could be something totally different.

All of this relates to the fact that many (most? all?) COBOL ISAM file
systems are proprietary (some belonging to the compiler vendor - some to
other vendors) - so we would need to know which you mean to give you "useful"
information.
```

#### ↳ Re: ISAM

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88i5n7$hon$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<887gb3$c2a$1@enterprise.cistron.net>`

```
I don't have a direct answer for this - but have found the web-pages that
MIGHT get you started on mbp (Visual COBOL) contacts.  See,

There is a web page with the contact information for the product at
    http://www.microfocus.com/visualcobol/addr.htm
- to get to the Visual COBOL information, you need to go to
    http://www.microfocus.com/visualcobol/
```

SAS/ACCESS with DB2 : on line documentation.
Lista UM DB2 : Discussion list in Portuguese about DB2 (Brazil).
A few OS/390 tools and toys : programs and tools written by Doug Nadel.
Teradata review : database magazine about Teradata RDBMS and Data
Warehouse: archives

- Technical papers:
http://www.multimania.com/db2usa/earticle.htm
DB2 Update archives (Xephon) includes now year 1997: 43 new articles.

- DB2 Redbooks:
http://www.multimania.com/db2usa/eredbook.htm
Redpiece "Cool ways to develop DB2 Stored procedures:" has been renamed
and became a redbook:
SG24-5485-00 1999-12-16 Developing Cross-Platform Stored Procedures and
Stored Procedure Builder.

Best regards,

  db2usa@hotmail.com



.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

>I'm a PC DOS Cobol programmer wondering if anyone knows
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

I write such applications.  You won't get rich, but there is a market

to even give it a chance.  To learn one way to get to a GUI without
having to learn the Windows API, check out http://www.flexus.com

<a id="t-0135808e37"></a>
## Thread: Am I wasting my time? — 4 msgs

### Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** "l " <graham@kinmelbay.fsbusiness.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8878io$r0u$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
I'm currently studying COBOL as a first language to enter the world of
progamming.  My view is that it's a good first language, but my first
programming job is more likely to be in the  VB arena.
Do you get my drift?  Learn an easy language and then move on to more
contemporary languages once the principles have been learnt.
Am I making sense or have I lost it?  Either way, it will only be a few
months now before I start the job hunting.
Please give me some sort of reassurance, it must be out there somewhere!
However, am I completely wrong?
```

#### ↳ Re: Am I wasting my time?

- **From:** "twcheezer" <cheezer@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ODHp4.16$Hm.2251@typhoon.mw.mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<8878io$r0u$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
I too studied COBOL as a first language to enter the job market.  While it
did seem like it was a relatively 'easy' language in school, I have found
that the programs I encounter in the work world are far different from the
ones I worked on in school.   'Real' programs are far more complex and
involved at work,  they routinely incorporate principles that we only
touched on in school, and it takes more than a few months to learn the
nuances of any programming language (imho), let alone to  master that
language.

If you apply at and get hired by a company that uses Cobol for the majority
of it's programming needs,  then you are likely to do your first 'official'
programming in Cobol.  On the other hand,  If the place you work uses little
Cobol and instead uses VB, VC++, C, or some other language then it would
seem that you would be more likely to program in a language besides Cobol.

I believe that the decision as to what you want to do as far as programming
is mostly up to you at this point.  There are places that use Cobol and many
of them will continue to use Cobol for quite some time.  Also, there are
places that don't use it and have no plans to use it.  From what I have
seen, Cobol is still a vital language, and you may find it more challenging
to program in than you think.  If you are perhaps worried that Cobol will
not be stimulating enough to hold your interest, I would urge you to
reconsider your opinion.

Sincerely .... Cheezer

 wrote in message <8878e3$qt1$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>...
>I'm currently studying COBOL as a first language to enter the world of
>progamming.  My view is that it's a good first language, but my first
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

>I'm currently studying COBOL as a first language to enter the world of
>progamming.  My view is that it's a good first language, but my first
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

> I am an Analyst/Programmer supporting a CICS COBOL / VSAM application and
> our client is looking at setting up an Internet font end which passes data
> back to our application for processing.  I am not that familiar with
TCP/IP
> and using sockets on a mainframe.  Does anyone know where I might find any
> additional information.
>
Look at the IBM manuals. The CICS TCP/IP interface is explained.

The CICS Listener is the TCP/IP communications entry point.

Anything that is reliant on a terminal device, (Queue Names, TCTUA) will
need to be modified.
```

Emulov <e@mulov.com> wrote in message
news:889m9f$6gh$1@newnews1.news.nl.uu.net...
> Hi.
>
> I was wondering if there's an easy way to check if one date is earlier
than
> another date.
> I know I could seperate the days, months and years and do some
calculations,
> but maybe there's a function for doing this?
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: comparing dates

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38bba8d4.6338575@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<889m9f$6gh$1@newnews1.news.nl.uu.net>`

```
On Mon, 14 Feb 2000 20:48:10 +0100, Emulov wrote:

>I was wondering if there's an easy way to check if one date is earlier than
>another date.
>I know I could seperate the days, months and years and do some calculations,
>but maybe there's a function for doing this?

Arrange the dates in CCYYMMDD format.  You are right in that you may
have to separate the days, months, etc., depending upon the original
format of the dates.

However, you should not have to do any calculations.  Once you have
put both of the dates in CCYYMMDD format, it becomes a simple matter
to use the LESS THAN comparison to determine if one date is earlier
than the other.
```

- **References:** `<88a3ek011sh@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
My shop uses PowerBuilder for some large desktop applications that
talk to DB2 on the mainframe on the back side.  We discovered writing
COBOL/CICS/DB2 programs that are called by PowerBuilder as "Remote
Stored Procedures" improves performance of these applications, because
there are fewer hops thru the gateway compared to dynamic SQL.

Pete


On Mon, 14 Feb 2000 18:34:44 -0500, "Jeff Baynard"
<union27@macconnect.com> wrote:

>I am taking a class on PowerBuilder 7.0 and I think it's really slick.  
>There are a total of 4 COBOL programmers in my class of 12.  Anybody else
>use this product?
>
>Jeff
```

removed from all internal objects.

Enjoy - you'll love it.

## Thread: How many files to open at once? — 3 msgs

### Re: How many files to open at once?

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1q1q4.3327$QK6.42366@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<889qd6$fkg$1@watserv3.uwaterloo.ca>`

```
What Platform?

MS/PC-Dos is about 3 UNLESS the config.sys  is edited:
FILES=nn
BUFFERS=nn

Windows is much higher
MVS/OS-390
Unlimited except by available system memory, user / partition memory, Job
memory

Ranjithkumar Nadarajah <rnadaraj@ecexh.uwaterloo.ca> wrote in message
news:889qd6$fkg$1@watserv3.uwaterloo.ca...
> Does anyone knowknow how many files that we can open concurrently in a
COBOL program?  How many at once?
>
> ANy help will be appreciated.
>
```

#### ↳ Re: How many files to open at once?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88aog1$q4g$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<889qd6$fkg$1@watserv3.uwaterloo.ca> <1q1q4.3327$QK6.42366@news4.mia>`

```
James King <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:1q1q4.3327$QK6.42366@news4.mia...
> What Platform?
>
 <snip>
> MVS/OS-390
> Unlimited except by available system memory, user / partition memory, Job
> memory
>

James,
  Are you certain of that?  I know that there used to be a 255 DDNames per
step limit (or maybe it was 1024 - I know that it was some power-of-2 type
number).  I remember it getting "expanded," but I don't remember it being
eliminated completely.

OTOH, I don't know of any COBOL program that actually ever hit the limit.

FYI - the current IBM LRM shows a limit of 65,535 SELECT statements, so I
think that is the current *theoretical* limit (at least outside CICS) that a
program could address (have open) at one time.  Again, I don't know of any
program running into this limit, but it isn't QUITE "unlimited".
```

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> > MVS/OS-390
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

still be a limit but it is much higher.

I would hate to maintain the JCL though.

Thanks

## Thread: How many files to open at once? — 1 msgs

### Re: How many files to open at once?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<889sns$1er$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<889qd6$fkg$1@watserv3.uwaterloo.ca>`

```
The answer is

   As many as your compiler support under your specific operating system,
i.e.

There is no "universal" answer - we would need to know

   - your compiler (with release and/or version)
   - your operating system
   - any external "switches" or settings that impact this behavior
```

notepad and ftp the source to the server.
Cobol ReSource has a separate compiler utility within the Wang VS shell.

from within the /usr/wang/ReSource path...

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC drivers for Microfocus/Microsoft ISAM files ?

- **From:** "Greg Stevenson" <greg@primesoft.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88s4ll$egl$1@news.ihug.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<88a78r$b8c$1@news.ihug.co.nz> <88h6oc$9gp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
No problems. Just thought I better research if I had the most cost effective
solution. I intend to run without the server side accessing isam files on a
nt server. Read only to start with. A maximum of four uses accessing the
same data over a 100Mbit network. Do you feel speed and data integrity (if
we decide to do write access) may be an issue without the server side.?


<Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-international.de> wrote in message
news:88h6oc$9gp$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> In article <88a78r$b8c$1@news.ihug.co.nz>,
>   "Greg Stevenson" <greg@primesoft.co.nz> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC drivers for Microfocus/Microsoft ISAM files ?

- **From:** Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-international.de
- **Date:** 2000-02-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88u1ib$rus$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<88a78r$b8c$1@news.ihug.co.nz> <88h6oc$9gp$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <88s4ll$egl$1@news.ihug.co.nz>`

```
Accessing the data using 'normal' odbc will surely increase network-
traffic because all comparings will be done on client site AFTER
retrieving all datas (hopefully retrieved by index-optimization).

But I can't see any data integrity problems if your updates are using
the right strategy. Will say: Your update/delete clause has to be set to
'key and updateable columns ' or (depends on your requirements) 'key
and modified columns'. But never use 'key columns only'.
Since we're using the ParkWay Server (avaible for Unix only as far as I
know...) our PB-Applications are running at least 10-15 times faster
than accessing the files using direct network access. In fact, users
can't see any difference accessing cobol ISAM-files or accessing tables
on a Sybase Server.
Now it makes a lot of fun to replace existing (very old) cobol-progs
thru PB-applications....

One more problem: Be sure that your ODBC-Driver and your Cobol programs
are both using the SAME file-handlers. If not, index corruption will be
a big problem.

HTH - Stefan Meyer

In article <88s4ll$egl$1@news.ihug.co.nz>,
  "Greg Stevenson" <greg@primesoft.co.nz> wrote:
> No problems. Just thought I better research if I had the most cost
effective
> solution. I intend to run without the server side accessing isam
files on a
> nt server. Read only to start with. A maximum of four uses accessing
the
> same data over a 100Mbit network. Do you feel speed and data
integrity (if
> we decide to do write access) may be an issue without the server
side.?
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

Actually, as I recall, it depends on the relative humidity they were
stored at and how much they swell!

I was trained to adjust and calibrate the 'picker knives' of card
readers for the military.  We were taught to always use a local-storage
stock card for the calibration... never bring one from outside!  Some
of those cards stored in the tropics got split by the new, freshly
adjusted machines shipped in from stateside!

Terry Heinze wrote:
>
>My CDC metal ruler indicates 140 per inch, not 150.  :-)

So does my Burroughs ruler.  Not only does it have printer pinfeed holes,
paper punch (e.g. 2, 3 hole punch), check sizes, MICR registration hole,
MICR symbol table, lines @ 6lpi scale, handwriting sizing scale, line
weight scale, printer spacing and ruler with 1/16 marks, but it also has a
table equating paper weight to thickness for bond, ledger, index and carbon
forms.  There is also a set of holes 3/8", 5/8", etc., and a square hole,
a triangular hole, a round hole and a card shaped hole for flowcharting.
It's stainless steel and will probably be around for Y3K. :-)

<a id="t-2f24fc9e05"></a>
## Thread: RM ISAM Format — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### RM ISAM Format

- **From:** Al Christians <achrist@easystreet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A92A1D.15A165E7@easystreet.com>`

```
I'm looking for info on what's inside an RM-Cobol 85 keyed/Isam file.
I've been trying to reverse engineer it by guess/gosh, and I'm ready
for some help.  Anyone know if this is documented in public anywhere?  

Looks like there are about 1k of header information, then the data
starts. 

In particular, I'd like to find out:

1. What's/where in the header, 

2. If the 1-byte flag at the end of the record tells what's deleted, 
and if so how

3. How the key compression works

4. How to traverse all the data pages in a file 

Obviously, I don't have an RM Cobol compiler and would like to 
write some programs to decode some files without buying one (pretty
expensive), so I will be using some other language (e.g. C/Delphi/YNI).

Alternatively, can anyone inform me if the current RM-Cobol compiler 
will compile programs that can read files created with a version for DOS
about 12 years old and produce runtimes runnable with the older runtime 
system?  

Very TIA for any clues.


Al
```

- **From:** isaac kattan <i.kattan@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<saivos832pd119@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<38A92A1D.15A165E7@easystreet.com>`

```

Al Christians wrote:
> 
> I'm looking for info on what's inside an RM-Cobol 85 keyed/Isam file.
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Al
Al,
RM/Cobol is backwards compatiable as long as its RM/85 standards.  If you 
need more info please call me at 800-762-6265.
Thanks
Isaac
```

Hi.

api-calls
to WIN32 ??

Thanks,

Tommy Nilsen
tommynilsen@yahoo.ocm

#### ↳ Re: Win32 and timers...

- **From:** JohndeV <johndevNOjoSPAM@yahoo.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2c2e3ab8.a5d2ec29@usw-ex0101-006.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<tifq4.355$Po1.6596@news1.online.no>`

```
Tommy

Try this in your program;

       working-storage section.

       01 sleep-time    pic 9(8) comp-5.
       01 sleep-ok      pic 9(8) comp-5.

       procedure division.
           move 5000 to sleep-time

           call WINAPI "SleepEx" using
               by value sleep-time
               by value isFALSE size 4
               returning sleep-ok
           end-call

<a id="t-f03a90a65e"></a>
## Thread: MicroFocus Cobol - mfcobolrc files — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus Cobol - mfcobolrc files

- **From:** RobToftness <rob.l.toftness@uwrf.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A98339.1EA31698@uwrf.edu>`

```
We are using MicroFocus Object Cobol 4.1.10 on a Solaris 2.6 system.
The global "mfcobolrc" file has the LANG argument for the [EDIT-USER]
tag set to XOPEN, OSVS, ANS85, VSC2(1), VSC2(2), VSC2(3).  When we use
"tbox", we must toggle through these six languages before it is set to
blank each time we log on. Is there a way to set the LANG argument so it
will default to blank in our local ".mfcobolrc" files?

Thanks,
Rob Toftness
University of Wisconsin - River Falls
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus Cobol - mfcobolrc files

- **From:** "Alan Staves" <as1@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88eah1$9b5$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38A98339.1EA31698@uwrf.edu>`

```
Unfortunately I don't believe that it is possible to set the default to be
blank if you want to keep the alternative options. If you only want to use
the blank option then simply delete (or comment out) all of the LANG lines.
Then you'll just be left with the blank default.

If you do want to keep the other options then my best suggestion is to add a
new LANG entry before the XOPEN one and call it something like DEFAULT (you
also have to specify at least one directive). So you'd be adding a line
something like:

LANG DEFAULT   MF

Regards.
Alan.

"RobToftness" <rob.l.toftness@uwrf.edu> wrote in message
news:38A98339.1EA31698@uwrf.edu...
> We are using MicroFocus Object Cobol 4.1.10 on a Solaris 2.6 system.
> The global "mfcobolrc" file has the LANG argument for the [EDIT-USER]
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** "RUSSELL STYLES" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88g98k$36e$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<38A98339.1EA31698@uwrf.edu> <88eah1$9b5$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
    Use "[]"  (square brackets) to represent blank.  At least this is how it
works in the
CLI.MNU file used by workbench 3.2 (DOS/win95).

## Thread: OLD circa 68 COBOL compiler question... — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### OLD circa 68 COBOL compiler question...

- **From:** Thomas David Rivers <rivers@dignus.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A98ED4.9240A239@dignus.com>`

```
I'm playing around with the MVT COBOL compiler
for OS/360 (talk about dinosaur...)  and, I don't
seem to be able to get it to run....

I'm hoping someone might remember what should
be done when you get this message (the compiler
appears to have actually generated code, etc...) but
the driver ABENDS with:

IKF0030I-D FRAGMENTED CORE. RUN IN LARGER SIZE REGION.  COMPILATION
ABANDONED.

I've tried adjusting the SIZE parm, to no avail.  Of course, I've
specified a large region - 32M - which would have been
absolutely outrageous in those days.

If any happens to recall this - your help would be
appreciated!

    - Dave Rivers -
```

#### ↳ Re: OLD circa 68 COBOL compiler question...

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38A9B576.1A8@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<38A98ED4.9240A239@dignus.com>`

```
Thomas David Rivers wrote:
> 
> I'm playing around with the MVT COBOL compiler
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> absolutely outrageous in those days.

A possible clue.  Remember that IBM machines of that time used three byte addressing, so 
16 meg would be the maximum they could address.  You could handle programs that were 
HUGE in the context of those days in a 750K partition.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OLD circa 68 COBOL compiler question...

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Rskq4.11807$UP1.271572@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<38A98ED4.9240A239@dignus.com> <38A9B576.1A8@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`

```
Hi Thomas,

Ed told you like it is, but perhaps to the point of your question, it may
be that the program was
written using Sections, and the compiler supported
overlays.  Not saying it was or did, but that we used IBM 1460's to run
some very large programs. Also a variety of other vendors.  Today Sections
are only used, it seems, to support something approaching
structured programming.

Warren Simmons

### MF Cobol 4.1, Error in MOVE

- **From:** alexander_burton@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88am9e$ni9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We are experiencing data corruption when
performing the following command :

MOVE DB-VEH-REC   TO   ST-VEH-REC.

DB-VEH-REC is read from an Oracle Database using
a SQL call, the field is correctly returning what
is in the database.

ST-VEH-REC is an exact copy of DB-VEH-REC, except
that it is a working storage copy. It is
initialised with Low-values. Once the move
statement has been performed the data appears to
repeat from the 385 char to the end of the record
(509 chars).

Is there a limit we are running into for the
maximum size of record ? Any suggestions would be
greatfully recieved.

If you require any further information, please
feel free to contact me directly.

Regards,

Alex.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

group where OCCURS/REDEFINES happen.

Steve.

```

Oscar, 
  When executing programs in Acucobol, do you ever get the error message:
WRUN32 caused an Invalid Page fault in WIN32.DLL? I was just wondering 
because I get that message and don't know how to fix it.

Oscar Jofre wrote:
> 
> 
> Can I change the name of the Wrun32.exe ( in the Process 
Administration ) in
> Windows Nt with the name of the program ??
> So when I run \:> wrun32 prog1, I'd like to see running prog1.exe not
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: AcuCobol ( change the name of wrun32.exe )

- **From:** "Pete Withey" <peterw@acucorp.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88cneq$lm3$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7sglvm$99k$1@talia.mad.ttd.net> <sahi3ine2pd31@corp.supernews.com>`

```
JC:

  First to answer Oscars question yes you can rename wrun32.exe anything you
want.  Starting with Acucobol-GT version 4.3 there are two files: wrun32.dll
and wrun32.exe.  When you remake the runtime you now remake the .dll.  You
cannot change the name of the .dll since the .exe calls it.  However you can
still rename the .exe anything you like.

  Now as far as your invaid page fault is concerned I would suggest that you
forward your question to support@acucorp.com.  They'll need to know the
release you're running under, what the program is doing at the time is
occurs, whether it reproduces consistantly or is random, etc.  If you are
doing gui programming and the version is less that 4.3 we had some memory
leaks which could cause this error.  If it's one of them it would appear to
occur randomly over time.  Upgrading to 4.3 would resolve it assuming that's
the problem.  However Tech Support can tell you better.

Peter Withey
Acucorp Systems Engineering


"J.C." <jeans96@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:sahi3ine2pd31@corp.supernews.com...
>
> Oscar,
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.help.com/
```

- **References:** `<889qd6$fkg$1@watserv3.uwaterloo.ca>`

```
Bill, I think you are correct there, it seems for some reason, you are
allowed an ULIMITED (and) UNCHECKED EXTRA 'ASSIGN TO' list of names.
(I misread the chart initially)

ALthough, I can't see any purpose in coding

SELECT myfile
    ASSIGN TO UT-1, UT-2,...UTnnn.
myself, but it is allowed and unlimied (since its treated as a comment!)

But Programmers, BEAR IN MIND, you will ALWAYS be limited by:
Job/Step Memory limit, Region / Machine Memory, BUFFER Memory/Space.


Here pasted directly from the manual:

APPENDIX1.1 Appendix A.  VS COBOL II Compiler Limits

The following table lists the compiler limits for VS COBOL II programs
running under MVS/ESA(*). () Other operating systems may impose further
limits.  The numbers are guidelines to the limits.

+------------------------------------------------------------------------+
� Table 43. VS COBOL II Compiler Limits                       �
+--------------------------
+------------------------------------------------+-----------------------�
� FILE-CONTROL paragraph              �                             �
+------------------------------------------------+-----------------------�
� SELECT file-name ...                         � 65,535                �
� ASSIGN system-name ...                   � N/A(2)                 �
� ALTERNATE RECORD KEY            �                             �
�   data-name ...                                      � 253
�
� RECORD KEY length                           � N/A(3)               �
� RESERVE integer (buffers)                � 255(4)                �

+------------------------------------------------+-----------------------�
� Data Division                                     �
�
+------------------------------------------------+-----------------------�
� File Section                                       �
�
+------------------------------------------------+-----------------------�
� FD file-name ...                                  � 65,535
�
�     LABEL data-name ...                    � 255                      �
�       (if no optional clauses)                �
�
�     Label record length                        � 80 bytes              �
�     DATA RECORD data-name ...    � N/A(2)                  �
�     BLOCK CONTAINS integer         � 1,048,575(5)        �
�     RECORD CONTAINS integer      � 1,048,575(5).       �
�     Item length                                      � 1,048,575 bytes(5)
�

+---------------------------------------------------------------------------
--------------------------�
� Note:
�
�
�
� (1) Items included in 4,194,303 byte limit for Procedure Division plus
�
�    constant area.
�
� (2) Treated as comment; there is no limit.
�
� (3) No compiler limit, but VSAM limits it to 255 bytes.
�
� (4) The QSAM limit is 255; the SAM limit is 2.
�
+---------------------------------------------------------------------------
--------------------------+

----- Original Message -----
From: William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
To: <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
Sent: Tuesday, February 15, 2000 5:34 AM
Subject: Re: How many files to open at once?


> James King <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
> news:1q1q4.3327$QK6.42366@news4.mia...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Unlimited except by available system memory, user / partition memory,
Job
> > memory
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> think that is the current *theoretical* limit (at least outside CICS) that
a
> program could address (have open) at one time.  Again, I don't know of any
> program running into this limit, but it isn't QUITE "unlimited".
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ranjithkumar Nadarajah <rnadaraj@ecexh.uwaterloo.ca> wrote in message

<a id="t-98de89ad17"></a>
## Thread: MicroFocus print problem from Win95 workstations/Netware server — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus print problem from Win95 workstations/Netware server

- **From:** cdr21@yahoo.com (C.D. Rounds)
- **Date:** 2000-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<salv2lli2pd70@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I have an accounting application written, using MicroFocus' Cobol
Toolset version 3.2.37.   When my runtimes load, a MicroFocus 
Dos Extender also loads, version 1.4.6.
My print problem occurs when i try to redirect print jobs to a network 
printer attached to the server using the netware (3.12) CAPTURE utility. 
From 2 of my 5 workstations the capture works fine, but from the other 
3, the CAPTURE refuses to work, and the print job is sent to the local 
printer.   The workstations are running the same netware client and have
identical setups. THIS PROBLEM IS BEGINNING TO KEEP ME UP AT NIGHTS...:(
any ideas?

Best Regards

David
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus print problem from Win95 workstations/Netware server

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MiDq4.7549$QK6.86089@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<salv2lli2pd70@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I would be interested in an answer to that question.  I only recently
upgraded from MF COBOL 3.2 to NetExpress 3.0.  I made the assumption
that a full 32 bit compiler would allow me to use the Windows printing
subsystem so I could specify printers, etc.  Duh!  As far as I can
tell (please correct me if I am wrong), it prints directly to the LPT
device, just like a DOS program.  If you can redirect a DOS program,
you can probably redirect the output from MF COBOL Windows programs.
But it can be a bear to do, especially if there is no printer connected
to the local PC port.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus print problem from Win95 workstations/Netware server

- **From:** "RUSSELL STYLES" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88g98i$36e$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<salv2lli2pd70@corp.supernews.com> <MiDq4.7549$QK6.86089@news4.mia>`

```
    I have a lot of experience in this area, and I have not as yet found a
solution that works 100%
of the time.  Be certain to use the +P switch, which changes the way it
access the printer (DOS instead
of BIOS).  Note: when using +P, you MUST use the +L switch.  Without the +L
switch, if the printer
is not available (ie under DOS, or if spooler is turned off), an abort by
God aborts, without closing ANY file
at all.

    It SHOULD allow you to use a long timneout, or disable timeouts
altogether when using
standard file I-O, and file names LPT1, LPT2, etc.

    Be certain to check the windows setup.  Buried in the printer propertys
is a check box that
asks "Do you spool dos jobs".  If unchecked, it will go to local printer.
Try setting the network
printer up in windows rather than using capture (Ie, "Q1.SERVER" instead of
"LPT1:".



Judson McClendon <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:MiDq4.7549$QK6.86089@news4.mia...
> I would be interested in an answer to that question.  I only recently
> upgraded from MF COBOL 3.2 to NetExpress 3.0.  I made the assumption
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus print problem from Win95 workstations/Netware server

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88gjc5$hi9$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<salv2lli2pd70@corp.supernews.com> <MiDq4.7549$QK6.86089@news4.mia>`

```
Judson,


Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>I would be interested in an answer to that question.  I only recently
>upgraded from MF COBOL 3.2 to NetExpress 3.0.  I made the assumption
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>device, just like a DOS program.

The NetExpress product DOES allow you to use the Windows printing subsystem.
As you quite correctly stated, by default the product does send output for
files declared as ASSIGN TO PRINTER directly to LPT1. However, you can get
the product to send the output via the Windows print spooler without
requiring any source code changes by setting the 'printer_redirection'
tuneable (full details in the on-line help). In addition, if you want to
control the printer at a slightly lower level, there is a set of
PC_PRINTER_... calls.

If you can redirect a DOS program,
>you can probably redirect the output from MF COBOL Windows programs.
>But it can be a bear to do, especially if there is no printer connected
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus print problem from Win95 workstations/Netware server

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nJUq4.9741$Ez.192588@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<salv2lli2pd70@corp.supernews.com> <MiDq4.7549$QK6.86089@news4.mia> <88gjc5$hi9$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Gael Wilson wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>I would be interested in an answer to that question.  I only recently
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>PC_PRINTER_... calls.

Gael,

Thank you very much for that information!  One of my criticisms of the
Micro Focus documentation is that it is so fragmented.  One should be
able to look up "printing" in the index and locate a section that tells
you all there is to know about the compiler's printing capabilities or
at least direct you to all the places where this information can be
found.  And there are so many 'undocumented' features it drive me nuts.
I know that when you use certain Microsoft COBOL compatability switches
that it causes certain options and behaviors to change, but it should
clearly explain exactly what and how.  And the documentation on ADIS
is some of the worst I have ever seen.  Once I spent two days trying
to figure out how I could assign a particular function to a particular
key, but I finally gave up without finding it.  Any time spend reading
documentation is time well spent.  Time spent searching for information
is a total waste.  Other than the documentation, I like MF COBOL, and
have been using it since Microsoft COBOL 3.0, which was based on Micro
Focus COBOL (2.x I believe).
```

## Thread: translate from EBCDIC to ASCII — 6 msgs

### translate from EBCDIC to ASCII

- **From:** "Joe" <jleary@vr.state.ut.us>
- **Date:** 2000-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88f1mb$hp0$1@coward.ks.cc.utah.edu>`

```
Hi all,

Anyone have a routine to translate standard text from mainframe (EBCDIC) to
PC (ASCII)?  I FTP a file to my PC and need to translate to ASCII,
preferably with Fujitsu COBOL.

TIA

Joe
```

#### ↳ Re: translate from EBCDIC to ASCII

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88f6c2$rui$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<88f1mb$hp0$1@coward.ks.cc.utah.edu>`

```
If all of the fields are either PIC X or PIC 9 (with no signs - or sign is
separate) then you should just be able to specify "EBCDIC" to "ASCII"
conversion when doing the download.

If, HOWEVER, you have any COMP, Binary, Packed-Decimal, or "included signs",
then you will need to write a simple program. This can be done by "moving"
you input to an output field converting from "internal" format to "external
format".  To do this, you should probably look at the CODE-SET clause.  The
Standard says that you can't use this with files that have computational
usages, but many implementors don't enforce this.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: translate from EBCDIC to ASCII

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000217081316.08112.00000562@nso-ce.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<88f6c2$rui$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <88f6c2$rui$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> writes:

>
>If all of the fields are either PIC X or PIC 9 (with no signs - or sign is
>separate) then you should just be able to specify "EBCDIC" to "ASCII"
>conversion when doing the download.
>

I don't know about your setup, but when I FTP the default is to translate
from EBCDIC to ASCII.   If there are COMP, COMP-3 fields in the file
you MUST specify BINARY mode before issuing the GET.
```

#### ↳ Re: translate from EBCDIC to ASCII

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000217081317.08112.00000563@nso-ce.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<88f1mb$hp0$1@coward.ks.cc.utah.edu>`

```
In article <88f1mb$hp0$1@coward.ks.cc.utah.edu>, "Joe" <jleary@vr.state.ut.us>
writes:

>Anyone have a routine to translate standard text from mainframe (EBCDIC) to
>PC (ASCII)?  I FTP a file to my PC and need to translate to ASCII,
>preferably with Fujitsu COBOL.
>
>

If you have the Data Tools package that came with the Fujitsu COBOL CD,
you can supply a file layout/copylib to the Data Converter tool and it will
translate
the EBCDIC file to ASCII including all COMP and COMP-3 fields.
I have used this extensively to test my mainframe programs on the PC before
compiling them on the mainframe for production.
```

## Thread: Unable to compile DB2/MFCobol program - sqlgstart gives 173 error — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Unable to compile DB2/MFCobol program - sqlgstart gives 173 error

- **From:** iboyes@ibcs.co.nz (Ian Boyes)
- **Date:** 2000-02-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.databases.ibm-db2
- **Message-ID:** `<38ab0e8c.64271622@news.clear.net.nz>`

```
Hi

I am running DB2 connect V6.1 and MFCobol V4.0 on a HPUX 10.20 system.
I have a small test cobol program with some embedded sql and when I
try to compile it  I get a 173 error load error on 'sqlgstrt'

I have tried a number of different ways to compile. I first tried ..

db2 connect to database
db2 prep program.sqb bindfile
db2 bind program.bnd
cob -cx program.cbl

and it failed on the cob statement. I then tried to put $set SQL(DB2
DB=database) in the source and just compile the program but it gives
the same error. 
It appears that MFcobol is trying to run this routine when it is
creating the object file so this is not a link problem. To check this
I removed all the calls to sqlgstrt that the prep put into the .cbl
file and the cob still gives the 173 error

Any help would be much appreciated
Thanks
Ian Boyes
ian.boyes@clear.co.nz or iboyes@ibcs.co.nz
```

be wrong as I don't know much about languages.  

For english there are a set number of words that are needed.  For
example "One", "Two", "Twenty", "Thirty", "Thousand", "Hundred", etc
etc.

If this is the same no matter what language you use, you could setup
tables with the word for each language, then your logic would just use
a subscript based on the language needed.  Thus you could just use
some existing english version, and change it to use these tables.

Just an idea.

Paul
