#### ↳ Re: Amount printed as words

- **From:** sbricklin@aol.com (SBRICKLIN)
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000229182015.01609.00000969@ng-bk1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<88gp6n$vln$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
THIS PROGRAM PROBABLY NEEDS SOME CHANGES, BUT THIS IS GOOD START. GOOD LUCK.
---------------------------------------------------------      

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. MONEY2.
      *==MONEY CONVERSION==*
      * THIS PROGRAM WILL REPLACE NUMERIC CHARACTERS WITH ALPHABETIC
      * SUCH AS 123 WILL BE REPLACED WITH ONE HUNDERD TWENTY THREE
      **************************************************************
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  HARD-UNITS          PIC X(45) VALUE
            'ONE  TWO  THREEFOUR FIVE SIX  SEVENEIGHTNINE '.
       01  TABLE-UNITS REDEFINES HARD-UNITS.
          05  UNITS    OCCURS 9 TIMES.
             10  UNITS-VALUE       PIC X(5).
       01  HARD-TENNERS  PIC X(63)  VALUE
                      'TEN    TWENTY THIRTY FORTY  FIFTY  SIXTY  SEVENTY
      -               'EIGHTY NINETY '.
       01 TABLE-TENNERS REDEFINES HARD-TENNERS.
          05  TENNERS OCCURS 9 TIMES.
              10  TENS-VALUE     PIC X(7).
       01  HARD-COMPOUNDS      PIC X(81)  VALUE
                 'ELEVEN   TWELVE   THIRTEEN FOURTEEN FIFTEEN  SIXTEEN
      -          'SEVENTEENEIGHTEEN NINETEEN '.
       01  TABLE-COMPOUND REDEFINES HARD-COMPOUNDS.
          05  COMPOUNDS   OCCURS 9 TIMES.
              10  COMP-VALUE      PIC X(9).
       01 INPUT-TABLE VALUE ZEROES.
          05  ENTRIES OCCURS 4 TIMES.
              10  H       PIC 9.
              10  T       PIC 9.
              10  U       PIC 9.
       01  CHAR-CNTR.
           05 WORD-CNTR            PIC S9(2) COMP VALUE +0.
           05 LAST-CHAR            PIC S9(2) COMP VALUE +1.
           05 SPACE-REMAINING       PIC S9(2) COMP VALUE +0.
       01  SUB1                    PIC S9(2) COMP VALUE +0.
       01  SUB                     PIC S9(2) COMP VALUE +0.
       01  SUB-OUT                 PIC S9(2) COMP VALUE +1.
       01  MORE-INQUIRES           PIC X     VALUE 'Y'.
       01  SLOT                    PIC X(15)   VALUE SPACES.
       01  DISPLAY-TABLE.
           05  DISPLAY-LINE OCCURS 3 TIMES PIC X(80) VALUE SPACES.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       000-MAIN-LINE.
           DISPLAY                'ENTER TWELVE NUMERIC CHARACTERS, CAN NOT BE
      -           ' NOT BE ALL ZEROES: TO EXIT - PRESS (N)'.
      *    IF INPUT-TABLE IS NOT NUMERIC
      *     DISPLAY 'NOT NUMERIC VALUE! CONTINUE OR PRESS (N) TO EXIT'.
      *     PERFORM 000-MAIN-LINE.
           PERFORM 1000-PROCESS UNTIL MORE-INQUIRES = 'N'.
           STOP RUN.
       1000-PROCESS.
           ACCEPT INPUT-TABLE
           IF INPUT-TABLE = 'N'
             MOVE 'N' TO MORE-INQUIRES.
           IF INPUT-TABLE IS NOT NUMERIC
      *     DISPLAY 'NOT NUMERIC VALUE!'.
            PERFORM 000-MAIN-LINE.
           PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB1 > 4
                 PERFORM EVALUATE-ENTRIES
           END-PERFORM.
           PERFORM CHECK-TABLE
           DISPLAY 'GET YOUR RESULT:' DISPLAY DISPLAY-TABLE
           DISPLAY 'ARE THERE MORE INQUIRES (Y/N)?'.
           ACCEPT MORE-INQUIRES.
           DISPLAY 'ENTER TWELVE NUMERIC CHARACTERS:'
           INITIALIZE DISPLAY-TABLE SLOT WORD-CNTR.
           MOVE +1 TO LAST-CHAR.
       EVALUATE-ENTRIES.
           EVALUATE TRUE ALSO TRUE ALSO TRUE
               WHEN H(SUB1) = 0 ALSO T(SUB1) = 0 ALSO U(SUB1) > 0
                   PERFORM TRIPLET-1
               WHEN H(SUB1) = 0 ALSO T(SUB1) > 0 ALSO U(SUB1) = 0
                   PERFORM TRIPLET-2
               WHEN H(SUB1) > 0 ALSO T(SUB1) = 0 ALSO U(SUB1) = 0
                   PERFORM TRIPLET-3
               WHEN H(SUB1) = 0 ALSO T(SUB1) > 0 ALSO U(SUB1) > 0
                   PERFORM TRIPLET-4
               WHEN H(SUB1) > 0 ALSO T(SUB1) > 0 ALSO U(SUB1) = 0
                   PERFORM TRIPLET-5
               WHEN H(SUB1) > 0 ALSO T(SUB1) = 0 ALSO U(SUB) > 0
                   PERFORM TRIPLET-6
               WHEN H(SUB1) > 0 ALSO T(SUB1) > 0 ALSO U(SUB1) > 0
                   PERFORM TRIPLET-7
               WHEN OTHER CONTINUE
           END-EVALUATE.
       TRIPLET-1.
           PERFORM REGULAR-UNITS
           PERFORM MOVE-CNT-TXT
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE.
       TRIPLET-2.
           PERFORM REGULAR-TENS
           PERFORM MOVE-CNT-TXT
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE.
       TRIPLET-3.
           PERFORM REGULAR-HUNDS
           MOVE 'HUNDRED' TO SLOT
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE
           PERFORM MOVE-CNT-TXT
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE.
       TRIPLET-4.
           IF T (SUB1) = 1
               PERFORM TENS-ONE
           ELSE
               PERFORM REGULAR-TENS
               PERFORM REGULAR-UNITS
           END-IF
           PERFORM MOVE-CNT-TXT
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE.
       TRIPLET-5.
           PERFORM REGULAR-HUNDS
           MOVE 'HUNDRED' TO SLOT
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE
           PERFORM REGULAR-TENS
           PERFORM MOVE-CNT-TXT
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE.
       TRIPLET-6.
           PERFORM REGULAR-HUNDS
           MOVE 'HUNDRED' TO SLOT
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE
           PERFORM REGULAR-UNITS.
       TRIPLET-7.
           PERFORM REGULAR-HUNDS
           MOVE 'HUNDRED' TO SLOT
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE
           IF T (SUB1) = 1
               PERFORM TENS-ONE
           ELSE
               PERFORM REGULAR-TENS
               PERFORM REGULAR-UNITS
           END-IF
           PERFORM MOVE-CNT-TXT
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE.
       REGULAR-TENS.
           MOVE T (SUB1) TO SUB
           MOVE TENS-VALUE (SUB) TO SLOT
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE.
       REGULAR-UNITS.
           MOVE U (SUB1) TO SUB
           MOVE UNITS-VALUE (SUB) TO SLOT
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE.
       REGULAR-HUNDS.
           MOVE H (SUB1) TO SUB
           MOVE UNITS-VALUE (SUB) TO SLOT.
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE.
       TENS-ONE.
           MOVE U (SUB1) TO SUB
           MOVE COMP-VALUE (SUB) TO SLOT.
           PERFORM COUNT-LINE.
       MOVE-CNT-TXT.
           IF SUB1 = 1 MOVE 'BILLION' TO SLOT.
           IF SUB1 = 2 MOVE 'MILLION' TO SLOT.
           IF SUB1 = 3 MOVE 'THOUSAND' TO SLOT.
           IF SUB1 = 4 PERFORM LAST-IF.
       CHECK-TABLE.
          IF INPUT-TABLE = 000000000000
              PERFORM 000-MAIN-LINE.
       LAST-IF.
          IF INPUT-TABLE = 000000000001
              MOVE 'DOLLAR' TO SLOT
          ELSE
              MOVE 'DOLLARS' TO SLOT
          END-IF.
       COUNT-LINE.
           INSPECT SLOT TALLYING WORD-CNTR FOR CHARACTERS
             BEFORE INITIAL SPACES
           SUBTRACT LAST-CHAR FROM 80 GIVING SPACE-REMAINING
           IF SPACE-REMAINING < WORD-CNTR
              ADD 1 TO SUB-OUT
              MOVE SLOT TO DISPLAY-LINE(SUB-OUT)(1: )
              MOVE ZEROES TO LAST-CHAR
              ADD 2 WORD-CNTR TO LAST-CHAR
           ELSE
              IF SPACE-REMAINING = WORD-CNTR
                 MOVE SLOT TO DISPLAY-LINE(SUB-OUT)(LAST-CHAR: )
              ELSE
                 MOVE SLOT TO DISPLAY-LINE(SUB-OUT)(LAST-CHAR: )
                 ADD 1 WORD-CNTR TO LAST-CHAR
              END-IF
           END-IF.
           MOVE ZEROES TO WORD-CNTR.
```

---

<a id="t-c0cf8fe675"></a>
## Thread: 32 Bit ASM from 32 Bit Windows COBOL — 6 msgs

### 32 Bit ASM from 32 Bit Windows COBOL

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88hrh7$pl9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I need an assist.  I'm trying to convert a system from 16 to 32 bit
windows.  I have MASM 6.11, and a handful of called assembler
routines.  In need to convert these to 32 bit flat memory model for
Windows.

I have the following directives:

.386
.MODEL flat, c

for calling from Fujitsu COBOL. However if ANYONE with ANY 32 bit COBOL
is calling 32 bit ASM routines, I would like to hear from you.  My
problem is that while I get a clean assemble and link, I get a
protection exception when running.  I think I am missing something in
the addressability of the call parms.

I'm looking for example ASM code and the corresponding COBOL code that
calls the ASM.

One reason I think that I am missing something about parameter
addressability is that when I assemble these with STDCALL, I get
_MODULE@0 for an entry point, but the calling program is trying to call
_MODULE@8


Thanks in advance.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: 32 Bit ASM from 32 Bit Windows COBOL

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38acda8f.13194991@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<88hrh7$pl9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Here is a lot more info and some actual code.

Info:

     - The calling program sets the structure of passed parameters.
       In Realia, this was always ES:DI for 1st parm, DS:SI for 2nd,
       and the remaining passed on the stack.  Since I did not want
       to use the stack (since this has its own compatibility
problems)
       and since I wanted to make all my routines standard, all of
them
       are written with exactly two parameters.

     - Realia supplied the information as to the structure.  If
Fujitsu
       does not, you would have to see the assembler source listing
for
       a compiled program to see how it passes data parms during a
call.
       I would imagine, though, that even if it was not documented,
you
       could ask one of their programmers.  This is important to know
as
       it would relate what the new 'PROC USING' should contain.

     - As to LINK errors, I never really had to worry about them
because
       after the assembly, it created an .OBJ file, which was what was
used
       when I linked the COBOL program (e.g. LINK CBLPGM+ASMSUB).  If
there
       are any link errors during that, then it is probably because
the
       conventions (segment names or addresses) of the subroutine does
not
       match what the calling program is expecting.

     - I read through the manual and it seems as though what you did
was
       correct (the '.model flat, stdcall') in order to make it 32
bit.
       I don't know what else it could be unless it is automatically
       assuming 16 bit because of something in the program.

     - In reading through the manual, it says that referring to the
old
       register names (ES,DI,DS,SI,CS,SS,AX,BX,CX,DX) just causes
reference
       to the lower 16 bits of the 32 bit register.  Although for
address
       registers (ES,DI,DS,SI,CS,SS), it would probably want to
reference
       the entire 32 bits (I am guessing that EDI = expanded 32bit DI
reg).

     - The only other thing I noticed in the manual was OPTION EXPR32
to
       force 32 bit registers.	Maybe if you tried this, it would give
an
       error as to what is causing the (maybe) 16 bit assumption.

     - I am no expert assembler programmer.  I basically got one
program to
       work (with Realia's sample and help) and used it as a template
to
       write all the others.

     - I did have a problem with the routines (in 1990) when I started
writing
       DLL code instead of DOS EXE code (I think it was I had to name
all of
       the segments CODE instead of the program names I was using or
something
       stupid like that) in order for it to be called correctly from
Realia.
       In my experience in dealing with ASM calls, it was always one
little
       thing like that which changed it from non-working to working.

     - I hope some of this is helpful.

-------------
The orig code.  I know how to rewrite these two in COBOL, but these
are just simple test cases, there are some that are much more
complicated.

; * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
*
; * Compress a bit pattern into a byte.
*
; *     CALL "KBIT2C"                  USING W-BYTE K-KBIT-WORK.
*
; *		  PARAMETER 1	 1 byte
*
; *		  PARAMETER 2	 8 bytes
*
; * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
*
; *    Bit Pattern field:
*
; *        Spaces, low-values, or 'N' is considered off.
*
; *	   Everything else is considered on.
*
; * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
*

CODE	  SEGMENT PUBLIC
	  ASSUME CS:CODE
	  PUBLIC KBIT2C

KBIT2C	  PROC	 FAR
          MOV    BX,' N'               ;set constants  BH=N
BL=spaces
	  XOR	 DX,DX		       ;clear DL, set  DH=00

KB1:	  LODSW 		       ;get first two bytes of pattern
	  CMP	 AL,BL		       ;test for spaces or null
	  JE	 KB2
	  CMP	 AL,BH
	  JE	 KB2
	  CMP	 AL,DH
	  JE	 KB2
	  OR	 DL,10000000B
KB2:	  CMP	 AH,BL		       ;test for spaces or null
	  JE	 KB3
	  CMP	 AH,BH
	  JE	 KB3
	  CMP	 AH,DH
	  JE	 KB3
	  OR	 DL,01000000B
KB3:	  LODSW 		       ;get next two bytes
	  CMP	 AL,BL
	  JE	 KB4
	  CMP	 AL,BH
	  JE	 KB4
	  CMP	 AL,DH
	  JE	 KB4
	  OR	 DL,00100000B
KB4:	  CMP	 AH,BL
	  JE	 KB5
	  CMP	 AH,BH
	  JE	 KB5
	  CMP	 AH,DH
	  JE	 KB5
	  OR	 DL,00010000B
KB5:	  LODSW 		       ;get next two bytes
	  CMP	 AL,BL
	  JE	 KB6
	  CMP	 AL,BH
	  JE	 KB6
	  CMP	 AL,DH
	  JE	 KB6
	  OR	 DL,00001000B
KB6:	  CMP	 AH,BL
	  JE	 KB7
	  CMP	 AH,BH
	  JE	 KB7
	  CMP	 AH,DH
	  JE	 KB7
	  OR	 DL,00000100B
KB7:	  LODSW 		       ;get next two bytes
	  CMP	 AL,BL
	  JE	 KB8
	  CMP	 AL,BH
	  JE	 KB8
	  CMP	 AL,DH
	  JE	 KB8
	  OR	 DL,00000010B
KB8:	  CMP	 AH,BL
	  JE	 KRET
	  CMP	 AH,BH
	  JE	 KRET
	  CMP	 AH,DH
	  JE	 KRET
	  OR	 DL,00000001B

KRET:	  MOV	 ES:[DI],DL	       ;return formatted byte
	  RET
KBIT2C	  ENDP

CODE	  ENDS
	  END

------------
; * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
*
; * Return the bit Pattern of a byte.
*
; *     CALL "KBIT2E"                  USING K-KBIT-WORK W-BYTE.
*
; *		  PARAMETER 1	 Eight bytes that will have either
*
; *                              a 'Y' or 'N' in them depending
*
; *				 on the value of Parameter 2.
*
; *		  PARAMETER 2	 Any one byte field.
*
; * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
*
; *    01  K-KBIT-WORK		       PIC X(8).
*
; * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
*

CODE	  SEGMENT PUBLIC
	  ASSUME CS:CODE
	  PUBLIC KBIT2E

KBIT2E	  PROC	 FAR
          MOV    AX,'NN'               ;clear pattern to all 'N'
	  MOV	 CX,4
	  REP	 STOSW		       ;DI now at end of pattern
          MOV    DH,'Y'                ;set DH to 'Y'
	  MOV	 AL,DS:[SI]	       ;move bit to test to al
	  CMP	 AL,0		       ;end if no values are on
	  JE	 KRET

KB8:	  TEST	 AL,001H
	  JZ	 KB7
	  MOV	 ES:[DI-1],DH
KB7:	  TEST	 AL,002H
	  JZ	 KB6
	  MOV	 ES:[DI-2],DH
KB6:	  TEST	 AL,004H
	  JZ	 KB5
	  MOV	 ES:[DI-3],DH
KB5:	  TEST	 AL,008H
	  JZ	 KB4
	  MOV	 ES:[DI-4],DH
KB4:	  TEST	 AL,010H
	  JZ	 KB3
	  MOV	 ES:[DI-5],DH
KB3:	  TEST	 AL,020H
	  JZ	 KB2
	  MOV	 ES:[DI-6],DH
KB2:	  TEST	 AL,040H
	  JZ	 KB1
	  MOV	 ES:[DI-7],DH
KB1:	  TEST	 AL,080H
	  JZ	 KRET
	  MOV	 ES:[DI-8],DH

KRET:	  RET
KBIT2E	  ENDP

CODE	  ENDS
	  END
------------

Now, here is my attempt.  Note, this DOES link and the entry point
shows the right number of bytes of call parms.  But I think I am doing
something fundamentally wrong.

; * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
*
; * Return the bit Pattern of a byte.
*
; *     CALL "KBIT2E"                  USING K-KBIT-WORK W-BYTE.
*
; *		  PARAMETER 1	 Eight bytes that will have either
*
; *                              a 'Y' or 'N' in them depending
*
; *				 on the value of Parameter 2.
*
; *		  PARAMETER 2	 Any one byte field.
*
; * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
*
; *    01  K-KBIT-WORK		       PIC X(8).
*
; * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
*
.386                          ;tah
.MODEL flat, stdcall          ;tah

CODE	  SEGMENT PUBLIC
	  ASSUME CS:CODE
	  PUBLIC KBIT2E

PBYTE     TYPEDEF PTR BYTE      ;tah

KBIT2E    PROC   USES esi edi,  ;tah
          KBITWRK:PBYTE,        ;tah
          WBYTE:PBYTE           ;tah
          MOV    eSI,KBITWRK    ;tah
          MOV    edi,WBYTE      ;tah
          MOV    AX,'NN'               ;clear pattern to all 'N'
	  MOV	 CX,4
	  REP	 STOSW		       ;DI now at end of pattern
          MOV    DH,'Y'                ;set DH to 'Y'
          MOV    AL,DS:[eSI]            ;move bit to test to al
	  CMP	 AL,0		       ;end if no values are on
	  JE	 KRET

KB8:	  TEST	 AL,001H
	  JZ	 KB7
          MOV    ES:[eDI-1],DH
KB7:	  TEST	 AL,002H
	  JZ	 KB6
          MOV    ES:[eDI-2],DH
KB6:	  TEST	 AL,004H
	  JZ	 KB5
          MOV    ES:[eDI-3],DH
KB5:	  TEST	 AL,008H
	  JZ	 KB4
          MOV    ES:[eDI-4],DH
KB4:	  TEST	 AL,010H
	  JZ	 KB3
          MOV    ES:[eDI-5],DH
KB3:	  TEST	 AL,020H
	  JZ	 KB2
          MOV    ES:[eDI-6],DH
KB2:	  TEST	 AL,040H
	  JZ	 KB1
          MOV    ES:[eDI-7],DH
KB1:	  TEST	 AL,080H
	  JZ	 KRET
          MOV    ES:[eDI-8],DH

KRET:	  RET
KBIT2E	  ENDP

CODE	  ENDS
	  END

-----------------

I am calling using

    CALL "KBIT2E" with stdcall  USING W-OUTPUT W-FIELD1.

000150 01  W-MISC.
000160	   05  W-FIELD1 		   PIC X(2).
000170	   05  W-OUTPUT 		   PIC X(16).

When I run I get an invalid page fault.  Help!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 32 Bit ASM from 32 Bit Windows COBOL

- **References:** `<88hrh7$pl9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38acda8f.13194991@news.cox-internet.com>`

```
anyone know a quick html link to an explanation of proc using?

thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
>Here is a lot more info and some actual code.
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>       it would relate what the new 'PROC USING' should contain.
```

#### ↳ Re: 32 Bit ASM from 32 Bit Windows COBOL

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38b611f6.184711302@news.cox-internet.com>`
- **References:** `<88hrh7$pl9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
This was kind of a tedious journey - but I made it.

My thanks to Kevin Hansen, and Bob Wolfe, both of whom lent me
valuable insight into how to solve this - and my thanks too, to Tim
Robbins of the microsoft.public.masm news group (I thanked him there
already) for his help.

Here is a completed version of the example I posted earlier.

If anyone wants to call 32 bit ASM code from Fujitsu COBOL (This
should work with Realia too), please let me know.  I know the secret
code now.  Please please don't tell anyone I can do some PC assembler.
Not that I don't like it mind you - it's interesting, but not
something I want to do full time.  It would be like admitting that I
can code in RPG <G>.

BTW - the thing that finally pushed me over the hump was when Tim
explained what my attempt looked like in C - I had one too many levels
of indirection.  

; * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
*
; * Return the bit Pattern of a byte.
*
; *     CALL "KBIT2E"                  USING K-KBIT-WORK W-BYTE.
*
; *		  PARAMETER 1	 Eight bytes that will have either
*
; *                              a 'Y' or 'N' in them depending
*
; *				 on the value of Parameter 2.
*
; *		  PARAMETER 2	 Any one byte field.
*
; * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
*
; *    01  K-KBIT-WORK		       PIC X(8).
*
; * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
*

.386

_TEXT     SEGMENT PUBLIC PARA USE32 'CODE'
PUBLIC    _KBIT2E
;
;  Stack Frame for _KBIT2E
;
PARMDATA  STRUCT
 EBP_SAVE DD	 ?		       ;Saved EBP
 EDI_SAVE DD	 ?		       ;Saved EDI
 ESI_SAVE DD	 ?		       ;Saved ESI
 EBX_SAVE DD	 ?		       ;Saved EBX
 RA	  DD	 ?		       ;Return Address
 PARM1	  DD	 ?		       ;Address of PARAMETER-1
 PARM2	  DD	 ?		       ;Address of PARAMETER-2
PARMDATA  ENDS

_KBIT2E   PROC NEAR
	  PUSH	 EBX		       ;Preserve EBX register
	  PUSH	 ESI		       ;Preserve ESI register
	  PUSH	 EDI		       ;Preserve EDI register
	  PUSH	 EBP		       ;Preserve EBP
	  MOV	 EBP,ESP	       ;Point ESP to new stack frame

	  ASSUME EBP:PTR PARMDATA
;
;    Load parameter 1 (address) into EDI
;
           MOV    EDI,[EBP].PARM1

;
;    Load Parameter 2 (address) into ESI
;
           MOV    ESI,[EBP].PARM2

          MOV    EAX,'NN'              ;clear pattern to all 'N'
          MOV    ECX,4
	  REP	 STOSW		       ;DI now at end of pattern
          MOV    DH,'Y'                ;set DH to 'Y'
          MOV    AL,[ESI]              ;move bit to test to al
	  CMP	 AL,0		       ;end if no values are on
	  JE	 KRET

KB8:	  TEST	 AL,001H
	  JZ	 KB7
          MOV    [EDI-1],DH
KB7:	  TEST	 AL,002H
	  JZ	 KB6
          MOV    [EDI-2],DH
KB6:	  TEST	 AL,004H
	  JZ	 KB5
          MOV    [EDI-3],DH
KB5:	  TEST	 AL,008H
	  JZ	 KB4
          MOV    [EDI-4],DH
KB4:	  TEST	 AL,010H
	  JZ	 KB3
          MOV    [EDI-5],DH
KB3:	  TEST	 AL,020H
	  JZ	 KB2
          MOV    [EDI-6],DH
KB2:	  TEST	 AL,040H
	  JZ	 KB1
          MOV    [EDI-7],DH
KB1:	  TEST	 AL,080H
	  JZ	 KRET
          MOV    [EDI-8],DH

KRET:     
;
;    Return to COBOL, restoring preserved registers
;
	  MOV	 ESP,EBP	       ;Remove stack frame for
previous call

	  POP	 EBP		       ;Restore registers
	  POP	 EDI		       ;
	  POP	 ESI		       ;
	  POP	 EBX		       ;
	  RET			       ;And return

_KBIT2E   ENDP
_TEXT	  ENDS
	  END
```

<a id="t-5cc1a5cba0"></a>
## Thread: IBM COBOL to support 31-digits — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### IBM COBOL to support 31-digits

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88h8m8$65$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI, the following was just put out in the IBM-MAIN list.  (I am positive
that I understand IBM-speak well enough to say that this isn't a
"guarantee" - but sure sounds like something in the "highly likely
category".)

" ...Depending on your timing (how soon you need it) we have relief coming
soon.  In order
to take advantage of it, you will have to run under Language Environment and
use the
newest COBOL compiler, COBOL for OS/390 & VM (5648-A25).  We are adding
support for up to 31 digit decimal numbers via APAR, probably in the first
half of 2000.  If this
will satisfy your requirement, then you need to start your run-time migration
from the
VS COBOL II run-time library (COB2LIB) to the LE run-time library (SCEERUN).
Once that is completed, you can compile any one (or all) of your programs
with COBOL for OS/390 & VM and use up to 31 digit numeric data items."
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM COBOL to support 31-digits

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uGsOBpWe$GA.274@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<88h8m8$65$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`


##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM COBOL to support 31-digits

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88hb12$n64$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"required" in the next COBOL Standard.  As I recall, not only does DB2
already support this, but I think PL/I has (for years?)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM COBOL to support 31-digits

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000217234340.14751.00000173@ng-fh1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<88hb12$n64$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill Klein writes ...



[snip]
>(Possibly?) more important is the fact that this is the number that is being
>"required" in the next COBOL Standard.  As I recall, not only does DB2
>already support this, but I think PL/I has (for years?)

Nah. The IBM PL/I compilers only support 15 digits, one of the areas where
COBOL has outshone PL/I for a long time. 

However, the new Visual Age PL/I for OS/390 compiler supports 31 digits if you
specify a compiler option.

Regards,





Steve Comstock

256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
worse run class you are allowed to run in.  Would you rather be in a run
```

#### ↳ Re: How many files to open at once?

- **From:** SimonLElliott <"Simon.L.Elliott[Delete.Me]"@CapGemini.Co.UK>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ABBD9A.A51FC2C6@CapGemini.Co.UK>`
- **References:** `<889qd6$fkg$1@watserv3.uwaterloo.ca> <fyoq4.5846$QK6.70448@news4.mia> <88d82c$ivv$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <x%Kq4.16049$NP6.194779@pm02news>`

```

We had a problem a while back with a CICS region that tried to allocate too many
files at one time and we stumbled across this. The following cutting comes from
the OS/390 Initialisation and Tuning Reference and refers to the
SYS1.PARMLIB(ALLOCxx) member.


TIOT
    Specifies the installation defaults for the task I/O table (TIOT)

    SIZE(nn)
        Specifies the size of the TIOT. The TIOT contains an entry for each DD
statement.

        The size of the TIOT controls how many DDs are allowed per jobstep. By
specifying any integer from 16 to 64 as the value of this parameter,
        the installation controls the default DD allowance.

        The following table shows the relationship between the size of the TIOT
and the maximum number of DDs allowed:

            SIZE Value                    Maximum number
            Dec (Hex)    Size of TIOT     of DDs allowed
            16   10      16384 (16K)         816
            24   18      24576 (24K)        1225
            32   20      32768 (32K)        1635
            40   28      40960 (40K)        2045
            48   30      49152 (48K)        2454
            56   38      57344 (56K)        2864
            64   40      65536 (64K)        3273
```

#### ↳ Re: How many files to open at once?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38AC04CD.C86E3263@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<889qd6$fkg$1@watserv3.uwaterloo.ca> <fyoq4.5846$QK6.70448@news4.mia> <88d82c$ivv$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <x%Kq4.16049$NP6.194779@pm02news>`

```
I suppose the OS has to be able to handle the numbers of files which databases
and on line shell programs require (lots), but I am curious:

What kinds of batch applications want to open large quantities of files at once?

I suppose it could be a ledger program with one file per ledger, but there are
better ways to do this.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How many files to open at once?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88h9vm$4gc$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Stipulating that there are DEFINITELY better ways of doing this, I could
imagine a program with 365 (or 366) files for each day of the year. This
would have exceeded the old IBM MVS "255" limit.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How many files to open at once?

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O5QA4xWe$GA.117@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<889qd6$fkg$1@watserv3.uwaterloo.ca> <fyoq4.5846$QK6.70448@news4.mia> <88d82c$ivv$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <x%Kq4.16049$NP6.194779@pm02news> <38AC04CD.C86E3263@cusys.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38AC04CD.C86E3263@cusys.edu...
>
> What kinds of batch applications want to open large quantities of files at
once?
>

From what I have seen:

1) There are programs designed to pass a LARGE master file once, and pass
several (usually 15-25) transaction files to other processes. I have seen
this with 24-7 systems which cannot afford to have files off-line for
extended periods of time. They do all end-of-day/week/month processing in a
single pass.
I have used this method to create over 20 extracts from a master for a data
warehouse application.

2) In some shops I have seen files split for parallel processing. Split the
transaction files, and then start n JOBs which run the same programs against
the split files.

I have never seen more that 30 files produced.
```

<a id="t-6c4bfd0797"></a>
## Thread: space=(trk,10,RLSE) — 6 msgs

### space=(trk,10,RLSE)

- **From:** Mark Framness <framnesso@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88hnug$n0e$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Greetings

Question.

Say I code the space parameter in a DD statement as is given in the
title. 10 tracks of primary space with a release.

Let us say that the job only creates 6 tracks worth of data so now the
remaining four tracks are released back to the system.  Programmer X now
runs a job that grabs those four tracks.

Picture it as:  SSSSSSXXXX, (where S is my dataset i.e. Speed
Programmer's and X is for Programmer X's dataset).

Now let us say I need that dataset again with a disp of mod and now I
add three tracks of data to it.

What happens?

Does Programmer's X's dataset get moved?  Does my new data get put
somewhere else and a link created?  If so then what purpose does
dataset pre-allocation *REALLY* server (an experienced colleague says
certain storage units might be used for specific purposes and my data
might be fit into one of those categories).

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: space=(trk,10,RLSE)

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88hppl$bdnk$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<88hnug$n0e$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Mark Framness <framnesso@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:88hnug$n0e$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Greetings
>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.

Once the unused allocation has been released to the system, you are stuck
with a total of 10 tracks.  Appending to your data set via the MOD parameter
will add records only as long as you don't exceed your primary allocation of
10 tracks.  Attempting to add beyond that point will result in an abend.  If
you expect to add onto an existing data set once it's been written to, I'd
strongly suggest using a secondary allocation initially or NOT using the
RLSE subparameter.  I'd recommend the former.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: space=(trk,10,RLSE)

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88i2ge$5ru6$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<88hnug$n0e$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <88hppl$bdnk$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```

Terry Heinze <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote in message
news:88hppl$bdnk$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com...
>
> Mark Framness <framnesso@my-deja.com> wrote in message
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> with a total of 10 tracks.  Appending to your data set via the MOD
parameter
> will add records only as long as you don't exceed your primary allocation
of
> 10 tracks.  Attempting to add beyond that point will result in an abend.
If
> you expect to add onto an existing data set once it's been written to, I'd
> strongly suggest using a secondary allocation initially or NOT using the
> RLSE subparameter.  I'd recommend the former.
>
>
Correction to my previous reply:  Originally, you allocated 10 tracks, you
used 6 and released 4.  Appending new records will be limited to whatever
fits in your now 6 tracks.  Attempting to add beyond that will cause an
abend.
```

#### ↳ Re: space=(trk,10,RLSE)

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TRCQiHAKaHr4EwzM@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<88hnug$n0e$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <88hnug$n0e$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Mark Framness <framnesso@my-
deja.com> writes
>
>Say I code the space parameter in a DD statement as is given in the
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Does Programmer's X's dataset get moved?
No

>  Does my new data get put
>somewhere else and a link created? 
No

>From:  Mark Framness

What happens (I think) is that your primary allocation of ten tracks has
already been made and the fact that you RLSE 3 tracks does not mean that
you can subsequently add 3 more tracks whenever you want (you have no
secondary allocation made so the system will attempt to mod onto the end
of the existing dataset within the existing allocation). If you do wish
to add the three tracks back for MOD then either remove the RLSE or code
SPACE=(TRKS,(10,10)). Remember that you have only got one primary
allocation and upto 15 secondary allocations (theoretical maximum of 160
tracks). If you use SMS then don't be surprised if fragmentation of the
disk packs and the movement of datasets by SMS results in the use of all
extents to store volumes of data less than the theoretical maximum (e.g.
SMS could result in only 16 tracks being allocated). 
```

#### ↳ Re: space=(trk,10,RLSE)

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ueor4.13084$QK6.141513@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<88hnug$n0e$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
First, lets clarify RLSE:  it ONLY releases unused space if the File was
opened OUTPUT AND
the last Operation was WRITE, AND the step terminates normally.

If all is true, then when the File is Closed AND the STEP ends, THEN the
Space is
ReLeaSEd to the system.  The space is IMMEDIATELY (upon release) AVAILABLE
to ANY other qualified Job.
RLSE does NOT affect Secondary allocations.

WHEN you NEXT open the file DISP=MOD, use the SAME SIZE parameters.

The system will allocate space where it can fnd it.

ONCE YOU RELEASE SPACE IT IS GONE TO YOU.
The next time the file needs more space, you will get ANOTHER
EXTENT allocated.


Mark Framness <framnesso@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:88hnug$n0e$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Greetings
>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

## Thread: CICS Session Hangs — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS Session Hangs

- **From:** "James" <jamsan@uq.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-02-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88imn9$nvb$1@bunyip.cc.uq.edu.au>`

```
I am using VA Cobol 2.2, along with VA CICS EAD V3.1 on NT 4 SP5, and I have
encountered the following problem.

I have written 2 CICS Cobol programs. Pgm A calls Pgm B (using CICS LINK),
and passes data to Pgm B for its use.

Pgm B massages the data, and writes the information out to a DB2 database.
So far so good.

Pgm B then issues a CICS SEND command to print a report
         EXEC CICS SEND TEXT
               FROM (WS-SEND-TEXT)
               LENGTH (1000)
               PRINT
               ERASE
               WAIT
             END-EXEC.

Control is then returned to Pgm A with a CICS RETURN.

Pgm A can then immediately call Pgm B again, passing new data to it.

If this occurs, Pgm B will hang in the print of the first report.

My understanding of the WAIT in the CICS SEND, was that it will delay until
the printing is complete, but this is not the case (unless I have coded it
wrong).

I do not wish to place a delay in the program, any ideas on how to get
around the problem ?

Thanks,
    James
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS Session Hangs

- **From:** "James" <jamsan@uq.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88qlc1$h2u$1@bunyip.cc.uq.edu.au>`
- **References:** `<88imn9$nvb$1@bunyip.cc.uq.edu.au>`

```
Thanks for the help.

I feel a complete idiot. I have solved the problem, and CICS was not it. My
DB2 queries caused the problem.

Sorry for the error.

James
```

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)

```
Charles Hankel writes:

>COBOL is not C, it is not PASCAL, it is not FORTRAN, it is not ALGOL, 
>etc., etc.  It is a language in its own right and there's been too much 
>messing around with it for no good reason over the past decade or so.  
>Some new features have been useful, but I won't run out of fingers when 
>counting them.

I agree.  For example, I have yet to find need for even one of the Intrinsic
Functions, and they've been around for what?  8-10 years now? 

I think I might need to use a POINTER type to call a rasty 'C' interface soon,
but maybe not.

And as for people who go on about Objet D'art programming, they've completely
missed the point of the MOVE and PERFORM verbs.  If any of these other
languages had MOVE and PERFORM verbs, you could throw out 80% of their code
statements. 

  



Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38b04f48_2@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<38b025f5_4@news3.prserv.net> <20000220135104.18058.00000634@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```

FRLSFLY <frlsfly@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000220135104.18058.00000634@ng-cf1.aol.com...
> >RANDOM is useful for generation of test data.
>
> Why would you want random test data
> values?

I have in the past used random data for several things.
Such as:
1) testing of internal sort routines
2) producing transaction data for performance tests
3) Monte Carlo simulation

I grant you that for many COBOL programmers
in the "trenches" of legacy program maintenance,
these topic border on the esoteric.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2000-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000220174519.18894.00000898@ng-ca1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<38b04f48_2@news3.prserv.net>`

```
>> >RANDOM is useful for generation of test data.
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>these topic border on the esoteric.


Using RAND functions for any sort of testing has not been a profitable
excercise in my experience.  Real world data distribution and workloads are not
even pseudo-random; and using randomly generated values and/or volumes can lead
to invalid test conclusions.

For one example, consider a randomly generated account number for use in volume
testing a credit card or ATM switch.  As a rule, these account numbers need to
pass check-digit routines and range edits.  Should you simply spew randomly
generated account numbers at the switch, then the reject rate will be
artificially high. This could lead to spending a great deal of time tweaking
error routines that will only be called every few thousand transactions in
actual duty.  If you start by pre-filtering your account numbers, then you may
as well simply build an iteration loop to generate the account numbers.

The same problem holds for Sort routines.  Algorithims that beautifully sort
keys of even distribution can be deadly pigs when dealing with clustering keys.
 Random generation can lead you  astray in your performance (and sometimes your
functional) testing.

As for Monte Carlo simulations, I must admit that my only brush with them was
in a  'C'  class taken roughly in the mid Pleistocene.  Still, were I
mistakenly selected to do any serious statiscal probability analysis, (and I
would be the first to call out that I would be in way over my head trying to do
that) I would not depend on the standard library function to deliver
sufficiently sophisticated pseudo-random numbers.   



Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38b08f88_3@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<38b04f48_2@news3.prserv.net> <20000220174519.18894.00000898@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```

FRLSFLY <frlsfly@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000220174519.18894.00000898@ng-ca1.aol.com...
> >> >RANDOM is useful for generation of test data.
> >>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> excercise in my experience.

I do agree that random numbers should not be used at random.
Also, different people may have different experience. Random
numbers have been useful to me; that does not mean that they
are useful in your situation.
The transaction data I was referring to were actually not the
random bit, rather it was the inter-arrival time between
transactions that was supposed to be random. Also
take into account that if you add several ransom numbers the
result is not evenly distributed over an interval but approaches
a normal (i.e. clustered around a mean value) distribution.
In short, when you deal with random numbers you've got to be
careful, but if you are, then what's the problem?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88q23h$lrk$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<38b025f5_4@news3.prserv.net> <20000220135104.18058.00000634@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```

FRLSFLY <frlsfly@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000220135104.18058.00000634@ng-cf1.aol.com...
> >RANDOM is useful for generation of test data.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Big Endian" is what I like.

I recently needed random numbers for use as the primary key of some DB2
table inserts.  There was a subprogram available to return such a number.
I'm sure a COBOL intrinsic function would've been faster than dynamic calls
to a subprogram.
```

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>

```
COBOL for MVS and VM on the IBM S390.
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<eqFcPNCg$GA.249@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<38b025f5_4@news3.prserv.net> <20000220135104.18058.00000634@ng-cf1.aol.com> <88q23h$lrk$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <38B2FA84.ADA96307@istar.ca> <MPG.132156d5c635c2ab9898da@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```

Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote in message
news:MPG.132156d5c635c2ab9898da@news.mersinet.co.uk...
> I noticed that Clark F. Morris, Jr. as cfmtech@istar.ca said...
> > Remember that the code generated by a COBOL function in many cases will
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Don't know for sure, but the COBOL Optimizer now optimizes for speed not
size.

Paragraphs, Sections and nested programs may be in-line in the generated
machine code.
That is, the PERFORM is replaced with the code in the paragraph. If a
routine is PERFORMed in 4 places, the PERFORMed logic could be repeated 4
times.

The last time I checked, Optimized programs could reach 4 times the size of
un-optimized code.

I don't know if this was done for other languages.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Function not necessarily faster than dynamic call was Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89cghe$ql4$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38b025f5_4@news3.prserv.net> <20000220135104.18058.00000634@ng-cf1.aol.com> <88q23h$lrk$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <38B2FA84.ADA96307@istar.ca> <MPG.132156d5c635c2ab9898da@news.mersinet.co.uk> <eqFcPNCg$GA.249@cpmsnbbsa05> <6nihbssvrpkqacp9k0hm6009ih8ijbavb8@4ax.com>`

```
Assuming we are talking about the IBM COBOL for this-and-that compiler(s),
then the answer is NO, there is no way to get the compiler to "optimize for
size" (and given OS/390 architecture - I can't really imagine why you would
want to).

FYI, here is what the COBOL Programming Guide says about the OPTIMIZE
compiler option,

"The COBOL optimizer is activated when you use the OPTIMIZE compiler option.
The purpose of the OPTIMIZE compiler option is to do the following:

Eliminate unnecessary transfers of control or simplify inefficient branches,
including those generated by the compiler that are not evident from looking
at the source program.

Simplify the compiled code for both a PERFORM statement and a CALL statement
to a contained (nested) program. Where possible, the optimizer places the
statements inline, eliminating the need for linkage code. This optimization,
known as procedure integration, is further discussed in "PERFORM Procedure
Integration" in topic 4.6.2.1.1 and "Contained Program Procedure Integration"
in topic 4.6.2.1.2. If procedure integration cannot be done, the optimizer
uses the simplest linkage possible (perhaps as few as two instructions) to
get to and from the called program.

Eliminate duplicate computations (such as subscript computations and repeated
statements) that have no effect on the results of the program.

Eliminate constant computations by performing them when the program is
compiled.

Eliminate constant conditional expressions.

Aggregate moves of contiguous items (such as those that often occur with the
use of MOVE CORRESPONDING) into a single move. Both the source and target
must be contiguous for the moves to be aggregated.

Delete from the program, and identify with a warning message, code that can
never be performed (unreachable code elimination).

The FULL suboption requests that the compiler discard any unreferenced data
items from the DATA DIVISION, and suppress generation of code to initialize
these data items to their VALUE clauses. "

   ***

If you are interesting in IBM's recommendations (which stress "GOOD
algorithms" over compiler options), I suggest that you look at:

 http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYPG204/4%2e6

which is the topic
  "4.6 Chapter 24. Program Tuning"

in
  Title: COBOL for OS/390 & VM Programming Guide
  Document Number: SC26-9049-04
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Function not necessarily faster than dynamic call was Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Oe7ufcYg$GA.259@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<38b025f5_4@news3.prserv.net> <20000220135104.18058.00000634@ng-cf1.aol.com> <88q23h$lrk$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com> <38B2FA84.ADA96307@istar.ca> <MPG.132156d5c635c2ab9898da@news.mersinet.co.uk> <eqFcPNCg$GA.249@cpmsnbbsa05> <6nihbssvrpkqacp9k0hm6009ih8ijbavb8@4ax.com>`

```

G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:6nihbssvrpkqacp9k0hm6009ih8ijbavb8@4ax.com...
> "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com> wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This is the IBM Mainframe compiler COBOL for MVS built-in optimizer (COMPILE
OPTION).
If you don't optimize you get a smaller, and slower load module.

If you have the CA-optimizer product it will optimize for size.
The last time I used it (over a year ago) it produced a smaller load module.
I don't know if you can combine them.
That is, Optimize for speed using the IBM compiler option, and then run it
through the CA-optimizer.

With the storage available, I don't know why you would want to optimize for
size.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<evas4.249$d26.3332@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<38b025f5_4@news3.prserv.net> <20000220135104.18058.00000634@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
You've gotten some good answers on this, but one of the best reasons to use
a random number generator is because the data are repeatable. This means you
can generate 10,000 test cases, and if one fails, you can fix the routine
and run it again with the same data. Very handy!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B14C71.913829FF@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38b025f5_4@news3.prserv.net>`

```
> FRLSFLY <frlsfly@aol.com> wrote

>  I agree.  For example, I have yet to find need for even one of the
> Intrinsic
>  Functions, and they've been around for what?  8-10 years now?

Define "need".  I hard coded my versions of NUMVAL, before it was
available, so obviously I didn't NEED this function.  It does make it
easier for someone to be confident I didn't have a bug.

I also sometimes check the length of a record to make sure it matches
the copybook length.  Never did that before (unless it was hard coded).

There were other ways of doing MIN, MAX, RANDOM, MEAN, CURRENT-DATE,
MOD, and even FACTORIAL.  (I imagine there was a way of doing all of the
intrinsic functions, I am just thinking of ones which I have done).
Would I write these the old way?   No.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.13216008e672d1469898de@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <ufo4pwGf$GA.267@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
I noticed that DennisHarley as LegacyBlue@email.msn.com said...
> 
> FRLSFLY <frlsfly@aol.com> wrote in message
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> I might possibly use SUM.

OK - want a laugh?  In the third quarter of last year, I was on a 
contract supporting a suite of "old" COBOL/CICS/DB2 stuff and was told to 
use the COBOL for OS/390 & VM compiler.  I merrily tried to use an 
intrinsic function to obtain the system date for timestamping and had my 
neat compile, link and go job fall over.  After lots of investigation, I 
decided to resort to contacting a systems programmer who happened to be 
located elsewhere.  I described what had happened and he asked for the 
usual details.

A couple of days later he called me back to break the news that it was 
all my fault for trying an intrinsic function because the runtimes were 
from a the OS/VS compiler.

If this explanation seems bass ackwards to anyone, the truth of the 
matter is that they had installed COBOL for OS/390 & VM COBOL compiler 
back in 1997 but had retained the previous runtimes - they had never 
installed the COBOL for OS/390 & VM runtime libraries.  And this was a 
place that had completed all of its y2k projects in late 1998 - 
allegedly.

So the one time that I tried to use an intrinsic function, the concept 
failed because of some really dumb decisions about the compiler.  Even 
now, I still find it hard to believe that an installation would go 
through all the grief of converting to LE and COBOL for OS/390 & VM 
without using the runtimes for that product.
```

```
this is the best book for the beginner and you can get a free C++
compiler from Borland. COBOL is a language that you should be *paid* to
write, not something you would write on your own time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol for free ??

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B3FF62.785D9F58@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
shine98@my-deja.com wrote:

> You simply must put a smiley when you are being funny. What limitations
> would C or C++ have that COBOL doesn't? Keep in mind that I really like
> COBOL, and write it every workday, but I think C++ is much more
> interesting and teaches you more about computing and has become one of
> the most used languages because it is very powerful.

The line I quoted almost demanded my reply, which was basically my attempt
at being funny.  But...

C++'s limitations are mainly in the area of costly maintenance.  Most any
characteristic of a language has both advantages and disadvantages (as most
any choices in life).  Spreading code around, hiding methods, etc. are
supposed to eliminate bugs, but sometimes they just make them hard to find,
especially when you change some code which is widely used (C++ programmers
preach the virtues of reuse, I also see advantages in reuse).  As with
COBOL, C++ requires standards, in this case to make sure programmers aren't
too terse, and put appropriate code where we can find them.

We can do more with C++, but we can do even more with machine language.  So
maybe "limitations" is a poor word to use.  It isn't that the language is
limited, it is that effective business use of the language is not always
optimal.  It should be primarily a business decision which tools a company
should choose.


> > > Learn both. There is a tremendous demand for converting legacy C++
> > > programs to COBOL.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

```
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

This concept you preach is true, however the reasons you cite are not
the issues.

C++ is complex and getting the design right is crucial.  How do you
get the design right if you know only what the university or a book
teaches you.

For a foray into OO I would recommend smalltalk.  It has some really
trick code management tools that allow you to correct mistakes with
your design almost automatically.  Once you understand the OO concepts
then you can work with C++.

Or even OO cobol. Oops I am back on topic...

Ken Foskey
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ limitations

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vklt4.76240$45.4230268@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B3FF62.785D9F58@cusys.edu> <38B52BDD.EC20FB85@zip.com.au> <161bbsovtnot5hafqp7rhm88qndpl4pto8@4ax.com>`

```
Business requirements are always changing, due to changes in legislation,
market forces, product directions, growth, new hardware, etc. The systems
have to adapt with the business. A perfect design will still require
changes.

Most systems take advantage of components provided by other vendors
including the operating system, graphical interface, database, etc. As those
vendors supply new versions with new features your 'perfect design' may have
to adapt to a new environment.

I want to meet the moron who decided that the software development cycle
consisted of planning, analysis, design, construction and "maintenance" (IBM
AD/Cycle et al). Maintenance is just another word for doing the first four
steps all over again.

Karl Wagner

"G Moore" <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:161bbsovtnot5hafqp7rhm88qndpl4pto8@4ax.com...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol for free ??

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<shine98@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> You simply must put a smiley when you are being funny. What limitations
> would C or C++ have that COBOL doesn't? Keep in mind that I really like
> COBOL, and write it every workday, but I think C++ is much more
> interesting and teaches you more about computing and has become one of
> the most used languages because it is very powerful.

I agree with you that I can't see what the limitations should be.
I can agree with you that C++ is interesting
I disagree that C++ learns one about computing??? More than any other
language???
I would say that you are mixing up, computing is language independent, I
guess you are refering to learning more about the C++ language and its
opportunities. In all this OOP hype, we should not forget that deep under
all is sequencial step by step anyway. The OOP is a wrapper. CPU's still
perform instructions one by one.

As regards to the statement "most used languages", I suggest you better
recheck your records, C++ is far from the being one of the most popular
languages, it is at best on 4th place, that is, after Cobol, C, VB. I
wouldn't be surprised to find it further down on the list too.

Of course, you may say that it is one of the most used languages in a
certain context, but then you have to say so. I understood your opinion to
be generic.

Finally, "powerful". Well, definitely not in terms of execution speed.
Certainly not in readability. As far as development costs, I doubt it is the
cheapest one. So, what is left? :-)

C++ has its benefits, no doubt. As any language has. My philosophy is to
used the right language to the right task. The clue is to know which is
right where.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol for free ??

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qTXs4.72622$45.4016130@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
A language isn't fast, the machine code produced by compiler is what
determines execution speed. A program written in COBOL may produce identical
machine code to that written in C. It all depends on the people who wrote
the compiler and the way they chose to implement a function.

When we benchmarked Realia COBOL versus Microsoft C v5.0 some years back,
Realia was almost a full order of magnitude faster doing a typical business
function involving file I/O. IIRC,  Microsoft's own benchmarks placed
compiled VB 5.0 ahead of Microsoft C++ for a while.

Karl Wagner

<shine98@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Aside from machine code and Forth, what would be faster than C?
> Certainly not COBOL. Certainly not VB. Whatever was faster would be used
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<<SNIP>>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol for free ??

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B5874F.7106@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
shine98@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Aside from machine code and Forth, what would be faster than C?

For many classes of problem Fortran is often faster than C.
There are various technical reasons for this, such as C always promoting
float to double on a function call.

> Certainly not COBOL. 

I have small sample of code where Cobol is faster than the C
equivalent.  Overall C may be 'faster', but often with the types of
systems that are written in Cobol the actual CPU execution speed is
almost irrelevant - File processing speed may be the constraint on
performance.  Of course with interactive systems the programs spend 99%
of the time waiting for keystrokes.

> Certainly not VB. 

VB may well be fast enough for what it is used for.

>  Whatever was faster would be used to develop games. 

Actually the portability of C was attractive to games developers.  They
often put assembler code where this mattered.
In many cases the 'game' code is only a very small part of the CPU time
as the rendering engine is using up all the CPU available.  As the
rendering engines can be called from any language then a Cobol game
could be unoticably slower.

> I see an awful lot of games development in C. 

Fine, use C to develop games, but don't think that makes it appropriate
for other purposes.

> C++ is a superset of C.

And don't think that means that C++ is as fast as C and has some extra
bits.  While C++ is getting much better it had the reputation of being
dog-slow because of the virtual class accessing and other issues.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:

> The performance of the C++ compilers have come a long way.  Virtual
> classes will not make a substantial difference in an application
> today.

As have COBOL compilers.  But any run time choice will take a performance
hit over a compile time choice.  We usually don't care anymore if there
are significant advantages (virtual classes) - which is why most COBOL
shops have standards to have run time calls.

But if someone claims C++ is fast enough because of compiler
improvements, it is hard to claim that COBOL is too slow because it is
further away from machine language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu>`

```
One reason C is faster than COBOL is that COBOL doesn't have usable
pointers. Processing tables is much slower than processing linked lists.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<eX#LjVuf$GA.250@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

<shine98@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> One reason C is faster than COBOL is that COBOL doesn't have usable
> pointers. Processing tables is much slower than processing linked lists.
>
COBOL does have POINTERs.
When it first came to my attention I was tempted to set up a Binary Search
Tree routine. But because it could need to be modified, I abandoned the
solution. Why have people scratching their heads wondering what had been
done?

There are a variety of methods which can speed up table searching depending
on the attributes of the key.

I can set up a table of indexes which are directly addressed using an
integer.
For example if I have a 4 character Key, I can assign an unsigned integer
value by treating the key as numeric (ABCD = 1234) I will have duplicates,
but I will find ABCD within 4 elements of my starting 1234 key.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<evDq1tuf$GA.266@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eX#LjVuf$GA.250@cpmsnbbsa05>`

```
Excuse me I have to correct myself:
DennisHarley <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com> wrote in message
news:eX#LjVuf$GA.250@cpmsnbbsa05...
>
> I will have duplicates,
> but I will find ABCD within 4 elements of my starting 1234 key.
>
If my 4 character key is XXXX, I will be within 256 elements. This assumes
that the Keys are densely packed.
If my 4 character Key is XXX9, I will be within 64 Elements.
If my 4 character key is XX99, I will be within 16 elements.
If my 4 Character Key is X999, I will be within 4 elements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<893sc0$kap$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <eX#LjVuf$GA.250@cpmsnbbsa05>`

```
DennisHarley <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com> wrote in message
news:eX#LjVuf$GA.250@cpmsnbbsa05...
>
> <shine98@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL does have POINTERs.

*STANDARD* COBOL does NOT have pointers (yet).  However many (most?)
implementors have already included them as an extension.  Unfortunately, the
current source code rules are HIGHLY implementor-specific.  For example,
Micro Focus (currently) allows you to set a POINTER  data-item to the address
of a Working-Storage item - but IBM does not.  There are also a variety of
differences on "pointer manipulation" statements.
```

```
  <snip>

> > *STANDARD* COBOL does NOT have pointers (yet).  However many (most?)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > of a Working-Storage item - but IBM does not.  There are also a variety
of
> > differences on "pointer manipulation" statements.

> Does the new standard have anything such as "SET POINTER UP BY 1"?

Yes it does.  It also allows you to SET pointer-item UP BY
arithmetic-expresssion which makes it "easier" to move thru a piece of
storage divided into "implicit" elements (similar to a table).
```

```
The key word here is *usuable*. In COBOL one may not take the address of
just any old variable, it must be of a linkage section item. This
effectively hamstrings using pointers in COBOL. C allows you to take a
pointer any time you want to.

 I wouldn't shoot off my big mouth unless I knew most of what I was
talking about.

In article <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net>,
  "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:
> <shine98@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

> > pointers.
>
> Oh really? How about doing some research before stating the truth? I
have

Cobol.
> The most important limitation to Cobol is newbies like you walking
around
> and believing you know the truth, which you obviously don't. How about
> taking a more humble attitude and rather ask if this is so and so,
than
> claiming truth for stuff you don't know?
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ POINTERS (wasRe: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<896n4r$5p6$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net>`

```
I am really "fascinated" by this entire thread on adding POINTERS to COBOL.
I really would love to take almost any of the anti-pointer-in-COBOL notes and
replace "pointer" with "REDEFINES".

REDEFINES are highly error-prone (if not used correctly).

They can lead to memory problems, hard-to-debug-problems, and "proper design"
can always avoid them.

Beginning programmers tend to misunderstand them, and advanced programmers
sometimes "throw them in" just to be "tricky" (i.e. do more "advanced"
things).

The way they work is NOT necessarily portable across implementations (i.e.
redefines of a BINARY field as PIC X - or vice versa).

  ***

Ok folks - how many of you think that it was a mistake to add REDEFINES to
the COBOL language????

As indicated elsewhere in this thread, there is no requirement that ANY new
or existing program be "enhanced" to use POINTERS, but adding them to the
language provides a capability that canNOT be handled (easily) in other ways.
```

- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <896n4r$5p6$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I'll be on the side of Redefines.

In those days, and I suspect now, too, many record
layouts were identical except for a few fields that
indicated a different use for some of the fields.
It was a technique that came in with card systems anyway, and the way to go
to get the card systems onto computers.

It saved a lot of writing and key punching as that was before copy and
paste, etc.

However, I would love to see the interface change to allow the record to be
"drawn" for documentation, and the entry program audit the layout for
consistancy and other things, then provide the drawing to be used in the
documention.

Warren Simmons
w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:896n4r$5p6$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net...
> I am really "fascinated" by this entire thread on adding POINTERS to
COBOL.
> I really would love to take almost any of the anti-pointer-in-COBOL notes
and
> replace "pointer" with "REDEFINES".
>
> REDEFINES are highly error-prone (if not used correctly).
>
> They can lead to memory problems, hard-to-debug-problems, and "proper
design"
> can always avoid them.
>
> Beginning programmers tend to misunderstand them, and advanced
programmers
> sometimes "throw them in" just to be "tricky" (i.e. do more "advanced"
> things).
>
> The way they work is NOT necessarily portable across implementations
(i.e.
> redefines of a BINARY field as PIC X - or vice versa).
>
>   ***
>
> Ok folks - how many of you think that it was a mistake to add REDEFINES
to
> the COBOL language????
>
> As indicated elsewhere in this thread, there is no requirement that ANY
new
> or existing program be "enhanced" to use POINTERS, but adding them to the
> language provides a capability that canNOT be handled (easily) in other
ways.
>
> --
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **Message-ID:** `<8997oo$50n$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <896n4r$5p6$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <DkEt4.5756$YG.401358@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <ED2667C7CC4BE92F.A3547F9B5B8688FA.8B4E24510960E00C@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Just so it is clear, in my earlier note  about REDEFINES, I was NOT
suggesting that anyone would really like to remove them from COBOL.  I was,
however, trying to point out that (almost?) all the "problems" with adding
explicit POINTERS to the language, also apply to the (long existing)
REDEFINES.

P.S.  Can anyone tell me if there is ANY existing major (or minor?) '85
Standard COBOL compiler that doesn't support (as extensions!):

 - USAGE POINTER
 - SET ADDRESS OF and/or SET pointer-item to ADDRESS OF

???

All of the "major players" that I know of already support this (and I think
that many of the less major ones do as well).  Given this fact, I am even
more "confused" by the strong anti-POINTER sentiment that I hear from *some*
C.L.C. people.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B8309D.5C7E@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> well, if you want to get really dweeby, all COBOL programmers use pointers,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> CALL-ee expects pointers for X Y and Z)

No, those are not 'pointers', the CALL is 'by reference'.  While what is
passed is the memory address, this does not make the call use a pointer.
A pointer is a _variable_ that is used to hold an address (or other
direct memory reference.  The implication of a pointer is that it can be
set to any arbitrary value and then used to access that memory.

A CAALL by reference does not create any variable that can be called a
pointer.  There is a difference between a container and its content.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89977q$4he$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B8309D.5C7E@ibm.net>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:38B8309D.5C7E@ibm.net...
> Michael Mattias wrote:
> >
  <snip>
> >
> > All that's 'passed' are pointers. (CALL-er passes pointers to A, B,C;
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Actually - as with so much of both POINTERS and CALL statements, this is
implementor specific.  As early as the 1970's (possibly 60's), IBM mainframe
COBOLs would allow CICS to explicitly access these "unnamed" things, via
BLL-cells.  Today, almost every implementation that I know, allows you to do
"get at" that unnamed item via a SET pointer-item to ADDRESS OF
LINKAGE-SECTION-ITEM.

Having said that, there is nothing in the COBOL Standard that even requires
you to "pass an address" rather than something else in a CALL BY REFERENCE.
If the implementation (and please don't ask me WHY) wanted to "copy" the data
to a temporary storage area in the called subprogram when "going in" and to
copy it back to the storage of the calling program when exiting, this would
be TOTALLY ANSI/ISO COBOL conforming.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B8F10A.2A70@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B8309D.5C7E@ibm.net> <89977q$4he$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> >
> > No, those are not 'pointers', the CALL is 'by reference'.  While what is
> > passed is the memory address, this does not make the call use a pointer.
 
> BLL-cells.  Today, almost every implementation that I know, allows you to do
> "get at" that unnamed item via a SET pointer-item to ADDRESS OF
> LINKAGE-SECTION-ITEM.

Exactly.  'Pointer-Item' is a variable (in W-S usually) that is a
pointer and may have an address set into it. When the pointer variable
is the target of a SET the address being set into it need not be of a
Linkage item, though the only way to de-reference the pointer is through
a linkage item.
```

```
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
>
> well, if you want to get really dweeby, all COBOL programmers use
pointers,
> because in....
>
>   CALL TheProg  USING A B C
> ...

Your point is on target for most of us.

But *ALL* COBOL programmers do not use pointers.

I was at a meeting where a fellow programmer was literally foaming at the
mouth when I suggested using CALLED modules. The old argument of run time
degradation keeps popping up.

I worked in a shop where a bug was found in a called module. The
programmer(?) assigned to fix it wanted to move the logic to the calling
module. Even when he was informed that the module was used extensively, he
did not want to touch the called module. Want to guess why?

There are quite a few programmers who have avoided using called programs.
```

```
be converted to nested programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UaTt4.37747$po2.388497@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <#NboHaCg$GA.252@cpmsnbbsa05> <qrQt4.2659$Xr4.38527@news.swbell.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
>
>Oops. You mean there are those who think a COBOL CALL is measurably
>less efficient than a PERFORM?

Only those of us who understand that the CALL may be of an external
executable, which in that case, requires the OS to do a directory
search, open, and load the module containing the called routine, at
least the first time it is called.  Or those who understand that, on
some computer architectures a CALL of a routine in another module
takes more machine overhead than a CALL of a routine in the same
module. :-)
```
