_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uU9FMFPg$GA.239@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <#NboHaCg$GA.252@cpmsnbbsa05> <qrQt4.2659$Xr4.38527@news.swbell.net> <UaTt4.37747$po2.388497@news1.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:UaTt4.37747$po2.388497@news1.mia...
> Jerry P wrote:
> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> module. :-)

This is true. The machine does more trivial work with called modules.

There are costs associated with machine usage. I sometimes see a JOBs cost
broken down by CPU utilization, DISK I/O, and TAPE I/O.
Costs are also associated with Human Resources.

If my Human Resource costs cannot be recovered through reduced Machine
Utilization costs, why should I choose multiple instances of code over
single instances of code?
In other words, the choice of using called modules is situational.
The addition overhead may not be significant.
```

- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <#NboHaCg$GA.252@cpmsnbbsa05>`

```
DennisHarley wrote in message <#NboHaCg$GA.252@cpmsnbbsa05>...
>
>I was at a meeting where a fellow programmer was literally foaming at the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Now me, I LOVE using called programs.  There is nothing I hate more than
writing the same code multiple times. Nothing.

Recently, I needed to change such a routine. It was in fourteen programs.
Turns out the external routine needed *no* call-time parameters changed;
that is, the logic of the routine was such that I could replace :

   CALL 'PROGRAMNAME' USING ....

with
  CALL The-Program-Name USING...

and the programs all worked just fine.

Since I had to recompile/link all the programs anyway (well, technically all
I had to do was re-link, but that's another tale) , I took the extra ten
minutes per program to change the calls to dynamic.

JUST ONE ONE WEEK LATER....

The client changed the business rule again. My one hundred forty extra
minutes (less the recompilation/relink time it would have taken the 'old'
way ) looked like a really good investment.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BA9334.250F358A@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <#NboHaCg$GA.252@cpmsnbbsa05> <s_Qt4.104$oO6.3002@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I will guess why someone didn't want to fix a called program.


He will have to TEST each and every program which accesses the called program.
Even though the enterprise would be better off with such a fix, he doesn't want
to go through the channels to get the enterprise to pay for this expanded fix
which was not part of his original mandate.  His estimates for how long the
original task would work are now way too low, and he believes he won't be
rewarded for being late - even for such a good reason.

What happens in OO shops when a bug is found in a basic component?  Does
everything which uses that object get a thorough test?  How expensive is that?
```

What happens in OO shops when a bug is found in a basic component?  Does
everything which uses that object get a thorough test?  How expensive is that?
Your mention of a regression test indicates that you can do one test.  Certainly
I can't do a single test when I change a Procedure Division copy member, instead
I have a major undertaking to test.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#XLLPRlg$GA.261@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <#NboHaCg$GA.252@cpmsnbbsa05> <s_Qt4.104$oO6.3002@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38BA9334.250F358A@cusys.edu> <OOqxeMhg$GA.219@cpmsnbbsa03> <38BAC74C.7EF0AEA0@cusys.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38BAC74C.7EF0AEA0@cusys.edu...
>
> What happens in OO shops when a bug is found in a basic component?  Does
> everything which uses that object get a thorough test?  How expensive is
that?
> Your mention of a regression test indicates that you can do one test.
Certainly
> I can't do a single test when I change a Procedure Division copy member,
instead
> I have a major undertaking to test.
>
I haven't worked in a COBOL OO environment yet, but I think the testing
requirements would be similar.

Testing is pretty much situational.
The type of change will dictate the testing requirements.

The case I mentioned involved a file I/O module (VSAM Indexed "KSDS"). The
module saved the last 64 records read in a table, and tried to match the
table to new record requests. Someone had modified the program, and the
table search wasn't working because they were trying to match an 8 character
search key to a 10 character table key (the search key should have been 10
characters). The output format and content would not have changed, but the
file I/O count should have gone down. A simple regression type test would
suffice, since there was no change to the output and therefore no impact on
existing logic outside the called module.

Another system I worked on had a unit of measure routine which produced 4 +
0 = 3.98.
The algebra worked but the arithmetic didn't. An exhaustive system test was
not required.
A driver with input designed to fully exercise the module could establish
that the results were correct.
The format remained the same, the output value was corrected. The system had
been operational for over a year, the inventory counts were known to be
incorrect.

When you get to format changes, you have modification of both calling, and
called modules. You have to run a well planned system test.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BBAB3B.95331C62@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <#NboHaCg$GA.252@cpmsnbbsa05> <s_Qt4.104$oO6.3002@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38BA9334.250F358A@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I will guess why someone didn't want to fix a called program.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> expensive is that?

This is a problem with modules more than OO.  OO tend not to have as
open an interface and therefore can be modified in a safer manner. As
a close example would you test all programs that reference a copybook
that you just added an 88 level to or would you rely on a compile?

The second part is extensive scaffold tests (coded unit tests).  This
means you can modify the subroutine and reasonably expect that all
existing programs will continue to function since the test scaffold
tests all boundaries cases and strange results.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

- **Message-ID:** `<C7Tt4.37740$po2.388398@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net> <mtut4.138$395.2288@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <38B78F7B.6051@ibm.net> <UXGt4.2191$395.24337@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

Michael Mattias wrote:
>
>well, if you want to get really dweeby, all COBOL programmers use pointers,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>CALL-ee expects pointers for X Y and Z)

Yes, but the COBOL programmer who uses that need not know a pointer
from a hat pin.  It is the explicit definition and manipulation of
pointers that causes the debugging nightmares, not the implicit use
of pointers by compiler generated code. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-25T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<38b67da5_1@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B6DA93.180E@ibm.net>`

```
> >> have
> > > myself created a three dimentional spreadsheet using linked lists in
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > claiming truth for stuff you don't know?

you don't need pointer to do linked lists:

2. Doubly-linked Lists
You will see from the above examples that simple linked lists suffer from
some problems.  Most importantly, they use a variety of different pointers,
so that you need specific code to add and remove nodes in different
circumstances.  The various end-conditions make this code quite complex.
Further, you cannot scan the nodes backwards directly; there is only a
pointer to the next node.  To find the preceding node, you must scan from
the start of the list.  This makes simple operations such as removing a node
from the middle of the list very expensive.
Doubly-linked lists solve these problems, at the cost of slightly more
storage space.  A node in a doubly-linked list has two pointers; one
forwards and one backwards:
                       ??????????????
   to previous node <---- ? data ? ----> to next node
                       ??????????????

The other difference is that the free pool and list head are not simple
pointers, but also nodes (there is no list tail).  This has the effect of
simplifying the end-conditions and the code that adds or removes nodes.
a. Defining a Doubly-linked List
Define a doubly-linked list as follows:
01  LIST-POINTERS.
    02  EMPTY-PTR               PIC S9(3)  COMP  VALUE ZERO.
    02  FREE-POOL               PIC S9(3)  COMP  VALUE +1.
    02  LIST-HEAD               PIC S9(3)  COMP  VALUE +2.
    02  FIRST-NODE              PIC S9(3)  COMP  VALUE +3.
    02  LAST-NODE               PIC S9(3)  COMP  VALUE +100.
    02  NODE-NBR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02  LIST-PTR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02  PREV-PTR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02  NEXT-PTR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.

01  LIST-NODES.
    02  LIST-NODE                          OCCURS 100 TIMES.
        03  NODE-PREV           PIC S9(3)  COMP.
        03  NODE-NEXT           PIC S9(3)  COMP.
        03  NODE-ITEM           PIC X(30).

Note that FREE-POOL and LIST-HEAD point to the first and second nodes.
These two nodes are not part of the available free pool, but are reserved as
the starting points for the two lists that we are keeping.  If we wanted to
keep ten lists, we could reserve ten nodes in this way.  The end-cases for a
doubly-linked list are as follows:
 ? empty list: NODE-NEXT (LIST-HEAD) = LIST-HEAD       NODE-PREV (LIST-HEAD)
= LIST-HEAD.
 ? no more room: NODE-NEXT (FREE-POOL) = FREE-POOL       NODE-PREV
(FREE-POOL) = FREE-POOL.    Note, that this is precisely the EMPTY-LIST
condition for FREE-POOL.
 ? overflow condition: no more room before adding an item.
A useful way of thinking about a doubly-linked lists is to realize that it
is circular:
                       ???????    ???????
                .------?     ?----?     ?------.
                |      ???????    ???????      |
             ???????                        ???????
             ?Head ?                        ?     ?
             ???????                        ???????
                |      ???????    ???????      |
                `------?     ?----?     ?------'
                       ???????    ???????

The head is not different in any way from any of the other nodes.  This
explains why adding a node after an arbitrary node in the list is exactly
the same code as adding a node to the list.  Simply use the previous node as
the head.
The following code initializes the doubly-linked list:
INIT-LINKED-LIST.
    MOVE LIST-HEAD TO NODE-NEXT (LIST-HEAD)
                      NODE-PREV (LIST-HEAD)
    MOVE FREE-POOL TO NODE-NEXT (FREE-POOL)
                      NODE-PREV (FREE-POOL)

    MOVE FREE-POOL TO LIST-PTR
    PERFORM ATTACH-NODE-TO-LIST
      VARYING NODE-NBR FROM FIRST-NODE BY 1
        UNTIL NODE-NBR > LAST-NODE
    .

ATTACH-NODE-TO-LIST.
    MOVE NODE-PREV (LIST-PTR) TO PREV-PTR
    MOVE LIST-PTR TO NODE-NEXT (NODE-NBR)
    MOVE NODE-NBR TO NODE-NEXT (PREV-PTR)
    MOVE PREV-PTR TO NODE-PREV (NODE-NBR)
    MOVE NODE-NBR TO NODE-PREV (LIST-PTR)
    .

Note that the paragraph ATTACH-NODE-TO-LIST is quite general, and will work
with either list, whether it is empty or not.  This generality is one of the
best reasons for using doubly-linked lists.
b. Adding data to a Doubly-linked List
An empty doubly-linked list looks like this:
    prev      next
    ??????????????
.----- ? main ?  ?<--.
`-->?  ? list ? -----'
    ??????????????

When one node is added, the doubly-linked list looks like this:
 .---------------------------------------.
 :  ??????????????      ??????????????   :
 `---- ? main ?  ?<------- ? node ?  ?<--'
.-->?  ? list ? ------->?  ?  A   ? ----.
:   ??????????????      ??????????????  :
`---------------------------------------'

When more nodes are added to the doubly-linked list, the list looks like
this:
 .----------------------------------------- - - -----------------.
 :  ??????????????      ??????????????          ??????????????   :
 `---- ? main ?  ?<------- ? node ?  ?<-- - - ---- ? node ?  ?<--'
.-->?  ? list ? ------->?  ?  A   ? ---- - - -->?  ?  Z   ? ----.
:   ??????????????      ??????????????          ??????????????  :
`------------------------------------- - - ---------------------'

Before you can attach a node to the list head, you must get a node from the
free pool.  To do this, take the node at NODE-NEXT (FREE-POOL) and remove it
from the free pool.  If there were no nodes left in the free pool, this will
have no effect (try it with pencil and paper).
GET-NODE-FROM-FREE-POOL.
    MOVE NODE-NEXT (FREE-POOL) TO NODE-NBR
    PERFORM REMOVE-NODE-FROM-LIST
    .

REMOVE-NODE-FROM-LIST.
    MOVE NODE-PREV (NODE-NBR) TO PREV-PTR
    MOVE NODE-NEXT (NODE-NBR) TO NEXT-PTR
    MOVE NEXT-PTR TO NODE-NEXT (PREV-PTR)
    MOVE PREV-PTR TO NODE-PREV (NEXT-PTR)
    .

After asking for a node from the free-pool, you should test that NODE-NBR is
not equal to FREE-POOL:
STORE-LINKED-LIST-RECORD.
    PERFORM GET-NODE-FROM-FREE-POOL
    IF NODE-NBR NOT = FREE-POOL
        MOVE LIST-HEAD TO LIST-PTR
        PERFORM ATTACH-NODE-TO-LIST
        MOVE THE-ITEM TO NODE-ITEM (NODE-NBR)
    .

It is also easy to add nodes into the middle of a doubly-linked list - if
you study the code for ATTACH-NODE-TO-LIST above, you will see that the
following code adds a node after any NODE-NBR:
    MOVE NODE-NEXT (NODE-NB) TO LIST-PTR
    PERFORM ATTACH-NODE-TO-LIST

c. Removing data from a Doubly-linked List
The general nature of doubly-linked lists makes it easy to remove any node
in any list.  If the node is at NODE-NBR, the following code removes it from
the list and returns it to the free pool:
    PERFORM REMOVE-NODE-FROM-LIST
    MOVE FREE-POOL TO LIST-PTR
    PERFORM ATTACH-NODE-TO-LIST

As well as adding or removing individual nodes, you can perform much more
complex operations on doubly-linked lists.  For example:
 ? To move a node from one place in the list to another, remove it and then
re-attach it where desired.
 ? To exchange two nodes, remove, then re-attach them both.
 ? To remove a sub-list of nodes (i.e. an entire chunk of the list), cut the
pointers at either end, and re-attach where desired.
 ? To exchange two sub-lists, cut both sets of pointers, and re-attach.
Clearly, you will need more than NEXT-PTR and PREV-PTR to perform such
operations.  Note however that at all times, we move and change pointers
rather than data.  This can be very efficient compared to a data structure
where large amounts of data are moved around.
d. Processing a Doubly-linked List
To process all nodes in a doubly-linked list, start with NODE-NEXT
(LIST-HEAD) and process each node in turn.  Note, that this loop will work
correctly even if the list is empty:
PROCESS-LINKED-LIST.
    MOVE NODE-NEXT (LIST-HEAD) TO NODE-NBR
    PERFORM DISPLAY-LINKED-LIST-ITEM
      UNTIL NODE-NBR = LIST-HEAD
    .

PROCESS-LINKED-LIST-NODE.
    MOVE NODE-ITEM (NODE-NBR) TO THE-ITEM
    ....
    MOVE NODE-NEXT (NODE-NBR) TO NODE-NBR
    .

e. Doubly-linked Lists Revisited
Picture the middle of a list:
        K          L          M
     ???????    ???????    ???????              ???????
     ?  L  ?    ?  M  ?    ?  N  ?              ?Next ?
---->???????--->???????--->???????-------->     ???????
     ?  J  ?    ?  K  ?    ?  L  ?              ?Prev.?
     ???????    ???????    ???????              ???????

The node L has a left node K and a right node M as neighbors.  The direction
of the list is determined by saying that M is the next node and K is the
previous node with respect to L.
Remove L by exchanging L,M and K,L as shown:
        K                  L                 M
     ???????    Swap    ???????           ???????
     ?  M  ?<---------->?  L  ?           ?  N  ?
     ???????            ???????    Swap   ???????
     ?  J  ?            ?  L  ?<--------->?  K  ?
     ???????            ???????           ???????

Now K and M are linked and L is left out in the cold.  Since the swap is
symmetric, we can insert L between K and M by performing exactly the same
swaps:
        K                  L                 M
     ???????    Swap    ???????           ???????
     ?  L  ?<---------->?  M  ?           ?  N  ?
     ???????            ???????    Swap   ???????
     ?  J  ?            ?  K  ?<--------->?  L  ?
     ???????            ???????           ???????

We are now back to where we started.  Thus removing and inserting a node are
actually the same operation:
 swap(next[left] , next[node])
 swap(prev[right], prev[node])

For this to work it is only required that nodes be initialized to point to
themselves.
How do we determine what is left and what is right?  For removal that is
straight forward:
 Remove: left = prev[node]
    right= next[node]

For insertion, it is even simpler because left and right are two adjacent
nodes between which we want the new node to go.  The only complication is
that we usually think of attaching the new node to a given one of the nodes
in the list.  There are now two possibilities for the other node: the
previous one or the next one.  If the other node is the next one, the new
node is inserted after the given node.  If the other node is the previous
node, the new one is insert before the given one.  Thus:
 Insert after: left = given node
    right= next[given node]

 Insert before: left = prev[given node]
    right= given node

With the following data definitions:
01  LIST-POINTERS.
    02 NODE-PTR                 PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02 GIVEN-PTR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02 LIST-PTR                 PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02 LEFT-PTR                 PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02 RIGHT-PTR                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02 SWAP-PTR1                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
    02 SWAP-PTR2                PIC S9(3)  COMP.

01  LIST-NODES.
    02  LIST-NODE                          OCCURS 100 TIMES.
        03  NODE-PREV           PIC S9(3)  COMP.
        03  NODE-NEXT           PIC S9(3)  COMP .
        03  NODE-ITEM           PIC X(30).


the code now becomes absolutely obvious:
SWAP-PTRS.
    PERFORM SWAP-NEXT-PTRS
    PERFORM SWAP-PREV-PTRS
    .

SWAP-NEXT-PTRS.
    MOVE NODE-NEXT (LEFT-PTR) TO SWAP-PTR1
    MOVE NODE-NEXT (NODE-PTR) TO SWAP-PTR2
    MOVE SWAP-PTR1 TO NODE-NEXT (NODE-PTR)
    MOVE SWAP-PTR2 TO NODE-NEXT (LEFT-PTR)
    .

SWAP-PREV-PTRS.
    MOVE NODE-PREV (RIGHT-PTR) TO SWAP-PTR1
    MOVE NODE-PREV (NODE-PTR)  TO SWAP-PTR2
    MOVE SWAP-PTR1 TO NODE-PREV (NODE-PTR)
    MOVE SWAP-PTR2 TO NODE-PREV (RIGHT-PTR)
    .

REMOVE-NODE.
    MOVE NODE-PREV (NODE-PTR) TO LEFT-PTR
    MOVE NODE-NEXT (NODE-PTR) TO RIGHT-PTR
    PERFORM SWAP-PTRS
    .

INSERT-AFTER-GIVEN.
    MOVE GIVEN-PTR TO LEFT-PTR
    MOVE NODE-NEXT (GIVEN-PTR) TO RIGHT-PTR
    PERFORM SWAP-PTRS
    .

INSERT-BEFORE-GIVEN.
    MOVE GIVEN-PTR TO RIGHT-PTR
    MOVE NODE-PREV (GIVEN-PTR) TO LEFT-PTR
    PERFORM SWAP-PTRS
    .

ATTACH-TO-LIST-AT-END.
    MOVE LIST-PTR TO GIVEN-PTR
    PERFORM INSERT-BEFORE-GIVEN
    .

ATTACH-TO-LIST-IN-FRONT.
    MOVE LIST-PTR TO GIVEN-PTR
    PERFORM INSERT-AFTER-GIVEN
    .

INIT-NODES.
    PERFORM INIT-NODE
      VARYING NODE-PTR FROM 1 BY 1
        UNTIL NODE-PTR > MAX-NODE
    .

INIT-NODE.
    MOVE NODE-PTR TO NODE-NEXT (NODE-PTR)
                     NODE-PREV (NODE-PTR)
    .

We have special procedures for INSERT and ATTACH because it is easy to get
it wrong: to attach at the end, you must insert before!
The list-head is just one of the nodes in the circular list.  It is only us
that attach special significance to it.
The advantage of initializing nodes to point to themselves is not only that
the remove/insert code becomes identical, but also that there is no garbage
floating around.  You can f. ex. tell if a node is currently in a list by
checking if NODE-PTR = NODE-NEXT (NODE-PTR).
The swap code can be made one line shorter by having only one temporary
pointer.  On most machines you do not gain anything because moving from one
subscripted variable to another subscripted variable involves storing the
value internally anyway as the registers are needed for the subscripting.
The above code is, of course, slightly less efficient than one that does
remove/insert as separate codes because it cannot take advantage of removal
of redundant code.  The remove/insert code is never time critical anyway.
For really die-hard speed freaks it is a purely mechanical procedure to move
the SWAP-PTRS paragraphs in-line and strike out redundant code.  This
process maintains the programmer's understanding of what is happening.
```

> You may notice that he never mentioned that he used _pointers_ in
> createing linked lists.

I would think that his implication is that he *did* use pointers. I said
that COBOL doesn't have *usable* pointers, and IBM's COBOL II certainly
doesn't, although one can construct linked lists with what is
implemented. I think that Cheesle was pointing this out and saying to
the effect not only does it have pointers but I have used them to
program a 3D spreadsheet. Only Cheesle could clarify this and I hope he
does, but it would seem to me that you have jumped to a conclusion that
may or may not be true. But you have jumped.

> Clueless newbies may not know that pointers are not the only way to
> implement structures, nor necessarily the best.

I realize that I am bantering with the big boys here so let me pose this
as a question, such as I am obliged to do: If you have a variable number
of objects and wish to provide service up to the limits of available
memory and spped is of the essence, is their another, better way? Please
instruct.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8968t8$m79$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <893nun$dtq$1@news.cerf.net> <8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<shine98@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:8941jg$70j$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> The key word here is *usuable*. In COBOL one may not take the address of
> just any old variable, it must be of a linkage section item. This
> effectively hamstrings using pointers in COBOL. C allows you to take a
> pointer any time you want to.

You are perfectly right of that issue, I would however not consider that a
big issue. In all those years I have been programming in C, Cobol, Pascal, I
have yet to find a situation that has put me up to a corner because of this
limitation.

As far as I am concerned, the only need I have had for manipulating memory
addresses in C, that is *not* related to a function call (which Cobol
supports) is for particular string operations. Where it is indeed useful.
However, Cobol has other primitives to deal with this kind of tasks, like
modifying by reference and redefining as a table. That is, my need for
memory address manipulation in C has been limited to the implicit limitation
there is in having NULL terminated strings. Although this is a benefit in
that sense you may have infinite string sizes, the disadvantage is as
obvious in that parsing a string is somewhat more tedious than the PIC X(nn)
and string[nn] counterparts in respectively Cobol and Delphi.

This limitation does however not affect what you shot at in the first place,
the ability to have a linked list. Which in Acucobol is fully possible. Look
at my posting to Sff5ky and you will see somewhat generic implementation of
a linked list in Acucobol.

I will admit though, that it is correct as William Klein pointed out,
pointers are not yet a part of the Cobol standard. So in one sense, you are
correct.

>  I wouldn't shoot off my big mouth unless I knew most of what I was
> talking about.

Seriously, it was not my intention to offend you, but you got me triggered
with your high-sounding statement. No bad feelings here, hope it is the same
with you.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com

```
This whole discussion is very interesting, and brings back memories of the 4
semesters of C/C++ in college, but many shops still use old technology that
doesn't support much of what has been discussed. My current gig is at a Unisys
shop using ACOB (ANSI 74) no debugging tools, IDE's, etc, nothing but putting a
monitor statement in the code.  Hell, I'd do backflips down the halls if I even
got to use an ISPF like editor let alone something like Xpediter, InterTest, or
FileAid.   

Move LT-NO-WHINE to WS-WHINE-MODE.

There, I feel better now.  ;-)

James Johnson
Ex nuke, now just a coder.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.13261830426e4ace98991b@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I noticed that shine98@my-deja.com as shine98@my-deja.com said...
> One reason C is faster than COBOL is that COBOL doesn't have usable
> pointers. Processing tables is much slower than processing linked lists.

Hardly relevant to most mainstream applications, even if it were true.  
Why would you want to use a linked list when you have files with 
capacities limited only by available disk space, access methods that will 
do the insertion job for you and utilities that will track these changes 
for audit purposes?  Put another way, why waste time building up a linked 
list when most of the time it is neither necessary nor efficient?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: C++ criticisms

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89jdjr$k92$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <ZqJs4.107$M65.2940@news.swbell.net> <38B3EA54.D5F69253@cusys.edu> <890t95$tfn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <890vlj$9tt$1@news.cerf.net> <891ao6$838$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38B5874F.7106@ibm.net> <38B52DA8.291EAC93@zip.com.au> <38B53D6B.34ED19F8@cusys.edu> <893ilt$r1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <MPG.13261830426e4ace98991b@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
"Charles F Hankel" <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote in message
news:MPG.13261830426e4ace98991b@news.mersinet.co.uk...
> Why would you want to use a linked list when you have files with
> capacities limited only by available disk space, access methods that will
> do the insertion job for you and utilities that will track these changes
> for audit purposes?  Put another way, why waste time building up a linked
> list when most of the time it is neither necessary nor efficient?

For Windows, which I usually work with, the limitation is virtual memory,
that is, both disk space AND physical memory. Hence, the best out of two
worlds.

As regards to insertion job. With the proper copy book that is a write once
use many scenario. Easy, easy.

The linked list construction is *not* any more wasted time than doing your
disk aksess per definition. Depending on the context each way is the most
efficient one. Anything that isn't going to permanent storage and used more
than one cycle is more efficient with a linked list.

I really can't understand how you can say something like this.

Cheesle
```

Basic, PL/I, ADA, RPG, Pascal, and others. No, C++ is a niche
language.

As a little digression I would like to point out that the inventor of C++,
Bjarne Stroustrup is Danish, while the inventors of Simula, that is
considered the old grand lady of OOP, is invented by my fellow countrymen;
Ole-Johan Dal and Kristen Nygaard. The first version of Simula was born in
1965. With other words, only 5 years younger than Cobol! A mature language
with other words. In contradiction to C++, Delphi, VB which all are hybrids,
Simula is natively OOP and does not allow for any procedural programming at
all.

Cheesle

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol for free ??

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B322FB.6A9DD37E@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<38afb480@grissom> <i4Wr4.8261$nu4.685335@news.flash.net> <88u5fg$uqo$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
If you are considering new languages to learn there are many
interesting and useful ones:

- Python (scripting for neat and organised people - see what VB
could be)
- Perl (scripting for messy pragmatic people)
- ML or variants CAML or OCAML, or Haskell, or even LISP
(functional languages that 'make it very easy to write powerful
but often slow programs'). Microsoft seems to be putting some
money into these.
- C or C++ - machine independent assembler (with obfuscation, in
the case of C++). Most commercial apps are written in C/++.

Enjoy!

Tim Josling

<a id="t-fd31978957"></a>
## Thread: gnt files — 7 this month / 8 total

### gnt files

- **From:** borko@cigojne.com
- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88q80e$8cj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Can somebody explain to me what is the purpose of .GNT files in Micro
Focus Cobol version 3? Is there an editor that reads .GNT files?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: gnt files

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38b0c26f_4@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<88q80e$8cj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
GNT modules are modules created by the Micro Focus run-time system
(RTS), and can only be used by the MF RTS.  From what I understand they
are also platform portable, meaning that you could take a GNT module
created on a windows system and run it successfully under an OS/2 system
(though I have never done so).  It is an acronym for GeNeraTed code.
Again, it will not run without the MF RTS.

Three types of modules created by MF Cobol v3:

INT - Intermediate Code Module (used in animation)
GNT - Generated Code Module (faster that an INT)
EXE or DLL - Executable or Dynamic Link Library (fastest format)

Also, there is no editor that reads GNT modules that I know of.  With MF
v4, I think there is a way to animate a GNT module, but I am not for
sure on that.

Hope this helps.

TL

<borko@cigojne.com> wrote in message news:88q80e$8cj$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Can somebody explain to me what is the purpose of .GNT files in Micro
> Focus Cobol version 3? Is there an editor that reads .GNT files?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: gnt files

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38b434d5.2504749929@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<88q80e$8cj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38b0c26f_4@news1.prserv.net>`

```
"Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net> wrote:

>GNT modules are modules created by the Micro Focus run-time system
>(RTS), and can only be used by the MF RTS.  From what I understand they
>are also platform portable, meaning that you could take a GNT module
>created on a windows system and run it successfully under an OS/2 system

Change "GNT" to "INT" and your statement is correct. INT-Files are
intermediate (say precompiled) code that is machine independent.

GNTs are machine dependent and therefore faster than INTs but no
longer portable. With one exception: The GNTs for InTel-Unixes (SCO,
UnixWare, Linux) seem to be portable.

Best regards
```

#### ↳ Re: gnt files

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88vcn4$8vc$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<88q80e$8cj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I use gnt's as they allow dynamic calls to be made. The External referencing
is done
on the first call. The big benefit is that gnt's are much smaller than exe's
when using
your own standard routines as these don't need to be link to each individual
program.
I use 170(approx) routines with every app written witch on average will
contain 250
programs. This results in an 8K exe & a 3.5 - 4MB LBR containing the gnt's.

<borko@cigojne.com> wrote in message news:88q80e$8cj$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Can somebody explain to me what is the purpose of .GNT files in Micro
> Focus Cobol version 3? Is there an editor that reads .GNT files?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: gnt files

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tcIs4.71207$45.3910545@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<88q80e$8cj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <88vcn4$8vc$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
That's not entirely true. It depends on the number of users and the
platform, the size of the run-time, and whether or not you're using shared
libraries or DLLs for the common functions.. IIRC, gnt's are loaded into
user memory so you get an instance per user. If you have a few users then
there may be some advantage (There's certainly an advantage when compared to
statically linking all the run-time bits into each application). However,
once you have a lot of users, then you quickly exhaust system memory with
all those duplicate instances of both data and code. For more info you can
check out

http://www.microfocus.com/cgi-bin/WhitePapers2?file=1175.unix&title=Packagin
g+Multiuser+COBOL+Applications+for+Performance+and+Supportability+

Karl Wagner

"Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:88vcn4$8vc$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk...

referencing
> is done

individual
> program.

gnt's.

```
I noticed that Tim Josling as tej@melbpc.org.au said...
> 
> You do have a point though; the new cobol standard is bascially
> 'C++ dressed as COBOL'.

That's been my objection to it all along.

Cynically, one could claim that the main danger in the standards process 
now is that many of the people involved are mainly compiler writers 
looking for their next functional specification, so changes need to made 
in order to come up with a substantial sellable revision.  One could 
then, equally cynically, add that compiler writers tend to CS graduates 
and that a lot of them are indoctrinated into the "C/C++ is best" 
brigade.

Not that I would dream of being so cynical, of course.
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
  <rant on>
As regular readers of this newsgroup know, I have LOTS of problems with the
current Standards development process, but I must say the thing that REALLY
bugs me is "real users" who claim that the Standards process is limited to
vendors.  I have tried EVERY method that I know of to get programmers and
others interested in COBOL to express their opinions, desires, interests to
the COBOL committees (either J4 or WG4) - but it seems like everyone is so
interested in "solving today's business needs" - that they just don't have
the time, effort, or energy to actually read and comment on (much less
contribute to) the Standards process.

Interestingly enough, about 1/3 of the current J4 membership is from
non-vendors - and significantly MORE of that for WG4.  On the other hand,
when it comes to actually submitting Public Review comments or other input to
these committees, it seems like most COBOL interested people just ASSUME that
it isn't  worth their time - with absolutely nothing to back this up.

 <end rant>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88s0p1$80l$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Gods4.25750$po2.257991@news1.mia>`

```
Judson,
  I don't remember the exact details of your problems - but I do remember
that there was one time that I *erroneously* forwarded on private email
(which may have been from you) to the committee list.  If this was the case
that you are thinking of, then, I apologize again - as I did at the time.

There is, however, an important distinction that I should make clear.  There
is a "world of difference" between email notes that are distributed and
discussed via the J4 (or WG4) distribution lists - versus email (or
snail-mail) "submissions" to the committee for consideration.  I can't think
of any of the latter type of "input" that hasn't received reasonable J4
consideration.  I will admit, that some of the "outside" input comes from
people who only know one dialect of COBOL (which certainly does NOT apply to
you).  Often these documents are hard to process because they are really
complaints about problems or changes with existing implementations and not
with the Standard itself.

Similarly, this current revision is so WILDLY over-due, that it is hard for
J4 (or WG4) to seriously think about ADDING anything new to this revision at
this time.  HOWEVER, they are gathering input (even as we speak) for ideas to
be added in the NEXT revision.

As I said in another note in this thread, I know that I would be happy to
help "format" any comment or submission that ANY COBOL interested person
might have - so that it will receive the best possible reception at J4.
(Similarly, the J4 chair would most likely be willing to help you if you
asked for input assistance.) However, even for totally "un-edited" input, I
just don't think that J4 looks at input as anything other than useful and
productive.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l1is4.26403$po2.265329@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Gods4.25750$po2.257991@news1.mia> <88s0p1$80l$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>
>I don't remember the exact details of your problems

I was only referring to how my e-mail was received by committee members.

>There is, however, an important distinction that I should make clear.  There
>is a "world of difference" between email notes that are distributed and
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>with the Standard itself.

One of the points I made at the time was that it is a pretty stupid policy
to make submission a very formal and little known (among most programmers)
procedure.  If the committee were truly interested in input from users,
they would, at least individually, consider any suggestion on its merits
alone, regardless of how it was submitted.  If someone came up to you on the
street and casually suggested an idea that was good, would you say "I can't
receive this until you submit it in three copies, etc."  After all, is the
point to make the language better, or make sure all submission requirements
are checked off like some game?  If the latter, a typical bureaucratic
mindset, then I don't care to waste my time.  It is good ideas and concepts
which are most important, not procedure.

>Similarly, this current revision is so WILDLY over-due, that it is hard for
>J4 (or WG4) to seriously think about ADDING anything new to this revision at
>this time.  HOWEVER, they are gathering input (even as we speak) for ideas to
>be added in the NEXT revision.

I can understand this point.  If they had not been so busy putting in
'features du jour' that will probably be little used, and discarded after
a decade or two, they would have had time for more stuff that matters
in the trenches.  Even after many years of OO experience, there is still
great division among programmers as to its merits, and the best methods
of implementation.  COBOL has been a success because it is COBOL, not
C/C++ or Smalltalk.  When COBOL is changed into a poor copy of those
languages it will inherit the trouble and problems those languages have,
and will lose its primary benefit: *simplicity*.  Simple things are
easier to do correctly than complex things.  This has been, and will
always be, true.  Adding READ PREVIOUS to indexed files is logical, and
should have been in there since indexed files were introduced.  Object
Orientation completely changes the nature of what COBOL is.  But enormous
effort has been spent on adding OO, while I understand READ PREVIOUS, a
small but useful improvement, may not wind up in the final standard.
Those who made this decision were not thinking of making COBOL a better
tool for programmers, they were thinking of making it 'cool'.  COBOL has
*never* been 'cool'.  COBOL is a truck, not a sports car.  Trucks are
used for work, sports cars are for fun, not work.  That's why COBOL is
the most successful computer language of all time, because doing work
well is most important.  It doesn't need a complete change of direction,
it needs updating.  But that's not what it's getting, and I am convinced
this will do more to promote the downfall of COBOL than any other factor.
COBOL is already thought of as a dinosaur in many circles, but is
'tolerated' because it is a simple, efficient, practical workhorse.  If
COBOL inherits the problems of OO and C/C++, which it will, it will no
longer be the simple, efficient, practical workhorse, and become bloated,
inefficient, fragile and error prone, just like other such languages.
We will have then removed the main reason why COBOL has been successful
for so long.  I am convinced of this, though it won't happen overnight.
Time will tell.

>As I said in another note in this thread, I know that I would be happy to
>help "format" any comment or submission that ANY COBOL interested person
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>productive.

Bill, by the time the new standard is finalized, and more changes are
considered, I will probably be retired, or even dead.  It would be a
complete waste of time, from my personal perspective. <sigh>
```

- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Gods4.25750$po2.257991@news1.mia> <88s0p1$80l$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <l1is4.26403$po2.265329@news1.mia>`

```
Just to clarify something in this note (and to expand a LITTLE on another of
my notes related to this):

If you have input that you would like J4 to consider (and you are not a
current member) you ABSOLUTELY may just send an email note to the J4 chair at

   doncobol <at> mediaone.net

and REQUEST that this be submitted to J4 as an "official" input document.

If it doesn't say this, the chair may well think that you just have an
"informal" discussion topic that you would like input from the J4 mailing
list about.

Non-members are NOT required to "format" or do anything special with their
comments.  It does help (but again isn't required) that if your
comment/question relates to the ANSI'85 Standard that you give a page
reference.  If it relates to the current draft revision, then (again) it is
nice - but not required - if you can give a page/paragraph reference AND tell
which version (i.e. CD 1.7) you are referring to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88sf73$56p$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Gods4.25750$po2.257991@news1.mia> <88s0p1$80l$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <38B1A23E.2112064C@melbpc.org.au>`

```
OK,  let me try and summarize:

As far as WG4 goes (the ISO-ish group), individuals are NEVER welcome to
submit comments or input documents.  Instead what you need to do is to send
something to your "national body" (or its WG4 membership) for THEM to submit
to the group.  I have frequently put out a web-page where you can find your
national body, but if you have trouble finding information on this, send me
private email and I will help you. As far as I know most (almost all?) of the
active WG4 member countries are QUITE willing to pass on individual comments
for the entire committee to consider - but this is a "country by country"
decision and there is no guarantee of this. (Only your specific national body
can tell you how to provide input via this channel.)

This restriction on WG4 input applies at all time but is particularly
important during "official" public review periods.  You need to submit your
comments to your country and they will submit a "single" country ballot on
the revision.

As far as J4 goes (the ANSI-ish) group, there are two very distinct types of
"input."  Questions on the current Standard, comments on the current DRAFT
revision, ideas for the next revision are *always* "in order" and may be
submitted to the J4 chair at ANY TIME.  The J4 chair can be reached via email
at:
      doncobol <at> mediaone.net

Although they are under no "official" obligation to do so, the committee will
usually reply to you (informally) on their processing of input of this type.
Such "submissions" are *always* processed by J4 at a meeting.  (Right now,
the committee is placing ideas for the NEXT revision - after this one -
fairly low on the agenda, so they may not be processed quickly - but they
WILL be processed.)

"Official" ANSI Public review comment periods (usually 2 to 4 months) are a
time for providing input to ANSI on a publicly available "official" draft
document.  Comments during this time *must* be submitted thru ANSI - actually
NCITS, I think - (not J4).  An official public review press release is issued
and it includes instructions on how to submit comments during this period.
J4 is REQUIRED to reply to all such comments (in writing) and there is an
appeal process if you are not happy with what happens with these comments.
The J4-TAG (Technical Advisory Group?) takes all such US/ANSI input and
determines which of these comments are passed on to the ISO process as the US
ballot on an outstanding ISO COBOL Ballot.  (Timing of these two processes
can get complicated and even lead to some comments being delayed by a cycle,
but they are ALWAYS processed.)

If you are interested in being notified of an OFFICIAL public review period,
then you can contact the J4 chair - or follow the J4 external web site at:
  http://merant.microfocus.com/Standards/

One thing that MAY be causing some concern from non-committee members is the
fact that we (J4/WG4) are fairly far along in (and severely overdue for)
revision work.  Therefore, if you submit a "new" comment or suggestion at
this stage of the revision which requires J4 to take committee time on
something that has already been resolved (often multiple times), J4 does tend
to get "annoyed" by this.  However, even with such input, I know of no case
where the input wasn't processed fairly and in a manner appropriate to the
current stage of revision work.

    ****

Does this help?
```

- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Gods4.25750$po2.257991@news1.mia> <88s0p1$80l$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <38B1A23E.2112064C@melbpc.org.au> <88sf73$56p$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
A little clarification from one of my "usually reliable sources" about input
to WG4 via your national body,

"I think what you say about "countries" being "QUITE willing to pass on
individual comments for the entire committee to consider" does happen,
particularly in early ballot stages in cases where the group forming the
national body position does not have consensus.

But I do not think it is true at FCD stage.  I know that the J4-TAG was
willing to pass on anything in the first (informal) public review.  For the
CD ballot, they were NOT willing to pass on just anything, but they did
pass on SOME things where there was no consensus one way or the other.  I
do not expect the tag to submit comments on the FCD ballot just because
they cannot reach consensus.

I think other countries have similar views, and that the requirement for
supporting your own comments gets stronger as the document proceeds to
higher stages.

It think it would serve people better if you told them that their comments
would probably be considered in forming a national position, along with any
other comments received.  (I say "probably" because I do not know for sure
that every country has an open process where comments are considered from
people not participating in their national standards organizations.)"
```

on our own)
   3 contractors/consultants: Karlins' Korner, Victor Consulting, Wizard
Systems
   6 "implementors: Compaq (was Tandem), Fujitsu, HP, IBM, MERANT, UNISYS
   2 "misc":  EDS, ITSCJ (Japanese Standard group),
   1 "user group": Interex (HP user group,  - IBM and Dec user groups USED to
be on, but have dropped off)

Any way you look at it, that seems a pretty BRAOD spectrum of the COBOL world
to me.

FYI,
  The current J4 membership list can always be found from the J4 web page at:
    http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/index.html

  The most current one (from the last meeting) is at:
    http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/00-0232.doc

If you look at that document, you will see that there are a NUMBER of other
vendors and non-vendors who are also on the various "observers" lists.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.131aae05f27e77fe9898d0@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I noticed that William M. Klein as wmklein@nospam.netcom.com said...
>   <rant on>
> As regular readers of this newsgroup know, I have LOTS of problems with the
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>  <end rant>

That's why I wouldn't dream of being so cynical, Bill.  :-)

You're right to state that most of us are so busy using COBOL that we 
probably don't have time to discuss it.  From what I can make out at 
sites I work at, most COBOL programmers are happy to leave programming in 
the office and may possibly have a PC at home for web surfing and CV 
(resum�, to you American chaps) production.  They don't want to be 
involved, the worst thing that happens in their programming lives is the 
introduction of a new compiler and the extra work that it brings.  In 
general, most of them would still be happy with COBOL-74.

I work for myself.  I like to keep up with developments but taking more 
time than posting to a few newsgroups would have a severe effect on my 
welfare and committee submissions are always wrapped up in so much 
formality IME and red tape, that the idea of formally putting anything 
forward and spending the time to follow through is not appealing.

It's all very well these committees existing but they don't seem too 
approachable from where I sit (near Liverpool in the UK, in case you're 
interested), and simple straightforward "why?" questions don't seem to be 
particularly well received IMO.  I appreciate that you have been a 
regular here for some years but how many other of the committee people 
take enough interest in what the guys on the ground think to come here 
and find out?  Why should we need to make formal submissions?

Fortunately for me, I'm getting on in years now and probably won't need 
to use COBOL for much more than another decade, not as a programmer 
anyway.  I still feel, however (as I have posted many times in the past 
here), that the move to making COBOL look like just another CS language 
is doing the language and its users a disservice, and is departing 
dramatically from its original purpose.

A large number of COBOL programmers have little or no interest in 
"algorithms" per se, they work on a different paradigm (please note use 
of trendy management word) in order to solve the problem of, say, making 
the sales ledger balance.  Quite frankly, most of them have more interest 
in the beer at the local pub than they do in COBOL per se.  The apathy is 
there because they feel that the standards process is exclusive, and they 
feel excluded.

I'd happily come to all the meetings and contribute as selflessly as 
possible but when I don't work, I don't get paid; and when I don't work 
enough, I lose the contract.
```

I noticed that William M. Klein as wmklein@nospam.netcom.com said...
> CV in England is most common - Resume is most common in the US
> 
> They "mean" the same thing.

Ooh, semantics, huh?  Actually, resum� means "summary" while curriculum 
vitae (or CVRRICVLVM VITAE to really nitpick) means the course of life 
or life history which is naturally more encompassing than a tiny little 
summary, but who said size matters?  CV is more or less universal whereas 
"resum�" tends to be restricted to the USA.
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.132549da24da80a5989904@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.131aae05f27e77fe9898d0@news.mersinet.co.uk> <38B14FA2.CB25ECBC@cusys.edu> <88rnp6$si8$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <srds4.25758$po2.254642@news1.mia> <88rvp7$g7$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.13214daf2b64fa749898d7@news.mersinet.co.uk> <38BA8C4A.BA7E27DA@cusys.edu>`

```
I noticed that Howard Brazee as howard.brazee@cusys.edu said...
> 
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> receives?  (besides looking at the address of the applicant?)

If he looks that far.  Companies used to conduct recruitment but now it's 
more often than not a matter of selection.  Even for the most skilled of 
jobs, the CVs (or resum�s) are checked through by junior clerks with a 
keyword checklist.  Too few marks on the checklist and out it goes, no 
matter what the candidate may be bringing to the party.  So, even the 
likes of Dr Watson and Admiral Hopper could find themselves unemployed if 
their CVs didn't have the right keywords.
```

Rick Price wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>us to have taken it from you. <G>

Well, you probably picked it up from those pesky Normans just after
Y1K, and later we colonists brought it over here. ;-)

I think it is interesting how language has been passed down.  After
the Norman conquest, they were the consumers (bosses) and the Anglos
the producers (serfs).  For bovine on the hoof, we say 'cow' or
'cattle', Anglo words.  But when the meat is prepared for eating, we
call it 'beef', a French word. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B19911.CEF74B7@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.131aae05f27e77fe9898d0@news.mersinet.co.uk> <38B14FA2.CB25ECBC@cusys.edu> <88rnp6$si8$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <38B166B5.F78A55A4@cusys.edu> <FWds4.25828$po2.258876@news1.mia> <88rth2$tv$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <zRfs4.26162$po2.261884@news1.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> I think it is interesting how language has been passed down.  After
> the Norman conquest, they were the consumers (bosses) and the Anglos
> the producers (serfs).  For bovine on the hoof, we say 'cow' or
> 'cattle', Anglo words.  But when the meat is prepared for eating, we
> call it 'beef', a French word. :-)

Calf     Veal
Pig        pork
deer    venison
sheep    mutton  (lamb appears to be both)

I don't know of any other language which does this.  I guess you have to be
conquered at the right time.

Then the English had their foreign language fads, in which a foreign language
was popular among the educated.  Besides French, at various times Greek and
Latin have squeezed their way in, especially in science.  Why do scientists have
one word for a species and civilians another?

Wasn't it a Latin scholar who decided that a preposition was not to end a
sentence with?  If it didn't make sense in Latin, obviously it was wrong in
English as well!!!!

Of course the BBC accent is relatively new, and was not used by most of the
British aristocracy throughout history.  But when Sparticus was filmed, the
slaves were played by Americans and the Romans by Brits using an accent less
than 200 years old.

Most Americans have ancestors who were literate when they learned English.
Therefore, we are much more phonetic than the British whose ancestors knew how
the words were pronounced before they learned to read and write.  On the other
hand, we often miss the class struggle a Michael Caine feels in his bones,
observing that he is a Brit, maybe that he's English, but only marginally that
he's a Cockney - or even caring about that detail.
```

In article <38B19911.CEF74B7@cusys.edu>, Howard Brazee
<howard.brazee@cusys.edu> writes
> Why do scientists have
>one word for a species and civilians another?
Try finding out the very real differences between blue sharks (in UK a
relatively harmless shark and in S. Africa another name for the Great
White) and you will know why scientists behave in such unreasonable a
manner as to use a single name for a single species.

>
>Most Americans have ancestors who were literate when they learned English.
>Therefore, we are much more phonetic than the British whose ancestors knew how
>the words were pronounced before they learned to read and write.
Perhaps your more correct phonetic roots spring from the pilgrim
fathers, and those who came after, not having their language adulterated
by words picked up from foreign conquests. I understand (from others)
that American English is closer to the language spoken by the pilgrim
fathers than UK English is.
 

>  On the other
>hand, we often miss the class struggle a Michael Caine feels in his bones,
>observing that he is a Brit, maybe that he's English, but only marginally that
>he's a Cockney - or even caring about that detail.
Your society is at least as stratified as my own. Please don't get hung
up on class struggle, it isn't worth it.
>
```

- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88rvnm$l2g$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <38B14F20.FDEB5AE2@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38B14F20.FDEB5AE2@cusys.edu...
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
>
> > Interestingly enough, about 1/3 of the current J4 membership is from
> > non-vendors - and significantly MORE of that for WG4.  On the other hand,
> > when it comes to actually submitting Public Review comments or other
input to
> > these committees, it seems like most COBOL interested people just ASSUME
that
> > it isn't  worth their time - with absolutely nothing to back this up.
>
> I think it is more that we're allergic to politics and committees and
fights, and
> are more willing to lose our language and jobs than to become politicians
> ourselves.
>

I still don't understand this.  There is NO requirement that any
non-committee member needs to get into "politics" to suggest enhancements or
comment on problems or omissions.  There isn't even a requirement that you
"snail-mail" a comment to the committee (much less attend any meeting).
*ALL* you have to do is to send (via normal email) your comments,
suggestions, whatever to the J4 chair and they *WILL* be considered.  Yes,
there are things that the committee has already dealt with and will probably
spend less time on.  Yes, it probably helps if you can show "real business
needs" for what you are suggesting, but you do NOT need to get into the
committee politics (which there actually is fairly little of) or bureaucracy
(which there is LOTS of) - to provide input.

P.S.  If you are interested in "formatting" your input to get the best
possible reception, I know that I would (as I suspect would the J4 chair) be
more than happy to help you with a "draft" of your comments.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-02-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B539DB.D7D693F5@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As someone who has commented with variying results, I think that there
would be a lot more participation if the process were different and a
change had a prayer of being implemented within 2 years after first
proposal.

I believe that I am on record with X3J4 as proposing the following:

1.  Only those changes to the standard that have been tested on two
separate architectures will be approved.

2. A specific development process should be set up where customers and
vendors can be in an implementatin program where they have a voice in
the development of the new function and a major say in any changes to
the proposed function.  This would be especially important regarding
changes that impact the "beta" code.

3. I propose that X3 require that a conversion process be provided for
changes that would adversely impact existing code.

4.  No more than 2 years should go by (and preferably only one year)
between the adoption of additions to the standard that add new function.
The fact that the first time they tried it, it took four years showed
that the process needed improvement, not that it should be scrapped.

If a letter re-iterating these points would be helpful, I will send it.

The best way to handle these ideas would be for X3J4 to get a separate
group that just looks at process and how to handle standards development
to look at the process and make recommendations for change.  The
Internet task force seems to be able to move at a faster pace and there
may be other alternatives.  These are issues that require a set of
expertise that neither I nor X3J4 have.  If COBOL can not evolve with
smaller increments provided on a more timely basis, it will die.  We
already have a number of proprietary languages that address areas that
should be within the scope of COBOL because of the perceptions about the
standards process (primarily that function will not be delivered when it
is needed).  

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
cfmtech@istar.ca    

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
>   <rant on>
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> > It's only net news but I like it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8956nu$gnu$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk> <88puhm$g9g$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <38B539DB.D7D693F5@istar.ca>`

```
Clark,  (begin semi-ramble)
  I don't know how you sent these opinions in, but I don't remember ever
seeing them as J4 documents.  A couple of facts that I will give you (to HELP
you send in comments - that would stand the best chance of being processed).

1) X3J4 hasn't existed for years, the correct name (for the COBOL committee)
is J4.  What *used* to be X3 is now NCITS.

2) Both ANSI and ISO have current rules that say that a Standard *must* be
revised (or at a minimum re-affirmed) every 5 years (and theoretically, there
can never be more than 2 reaffirmations).  HOWEVER, there is no "rules" for
what happens when this process doesn't proceed at this speed. (The closest
option that I have ever heard, is that the existing Standard simply gets
withdrawn with no replacement.)  If you have a suggestion regarding process,
I would think it should be sent to NCITS, not J4 - because they "own" the
Standards process that J4 works under.  See
  http://www.ncits.org/
for NCITS information.

3) For various reasons a MAJOR change occurred between the last Standard
('85) and this one.  The '85 Standard was an ANSI Standard that was
(subsequently) adopted as an ISO Standard.  This Standard is being developed
as an ISO Standard (with the expectation that it will eventually be adopted
by ANSI).  What this means is that the REAL rules for how the revision is
processed is set by ISO (and WG4's parent body SC22). However, there is a
problem that WG4 (the COBOL ISO group) doesn't have the "resources" to
actually do the development, so they have "contracted" that out to J4 who
*must* follow whatever WG4 (or more accurately ISO) tells them to. (See the
Fortran development for the problems that this can lead to.) See
    http://anubis.dkuug.dk/JTC1/SC22/
for SC22's home page. See
   http://www.iso.ch/
for the ISO home page.

4) The "quick" amendment approach was attempted TWICE not just once.  The
first was the "Intrinsic Function Amendment" - which showed how even a minor
(basically single)  enhancements took 5 years.  The *SECOND* was the
Correction Amendment which showed that EVEN (basically minor) fixes couldn't
be processed in less than 4 years.

5) I believe that you are aware of what Canada said on ITS last report to
SC22 about lack of individuals supporting it.  However, the same is really
true EVERYWHERE.

   ***

I do NOT mean to stop (or even discourage) you from submitting whatever you
think would be useful to J4, NCITS, or via the Canadian Standards body to
either WG4 or ISO.  HOWEVER,  I think that "monopolies" like Microsoft
(today) - or IBM for COBOL (in the '70s) can *set* quick DE FACTO standards.
Unfortunately, any "official method" (especially one that is designed to
handle not only programming languages, but screw sizes, electronic pulses,
etc, etc) simply cannot work in a "quick" and responsive manner.

For COBOL, X/Open (for UNIX - COBOL) and MIA (for the Japanese COBOL market)
*attempted* to set "Standards" via a (relatively small) group of companies.
Both of these attempts failed quite badly.

The fact that I have tried REPEATEDLY to get many C.L.C. readers to submit
comments to J4 - and very, VERY few have, gives me a pretty good idea of the
fact that there must isn't  the "will" among today's COBOL users to "shape
their language's future".  There certainly is an INCREDIBLY limited number of
individuals (with or without company sponsorship) who are willing to put time
into developing Standards.

I know of multiple people who have suggested (at various times) the idea that
the ONLY things that can/should be added to a "new" Standard are things that
are already in existing implementations.  I also know, however, that a
SIGNIFICANT number of COBOL vendors have (basically) stopped doing
enhancements/extensions UNTIL they are already in an approved Standard.

The bottom-line is that I totally agree that the current Standards process is
broken - at least as far as COBOL goes.  However, I (again personally) don't
see a solution to this - without PAYING customers putting their own time and
money into this - and DEMANDING that their vendors do too.  (Note the fact
that Dec, AcuCOBOL, Mbp, CA-Reallia, RM, WANG have *all* been on and DROPPED
off of the J4 committee. Along with the fact that both GUIDE, the IBM user
group, and DECUS the DEC, user group, used to be on, but are no longer,)
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38B14E79.4C9E3411@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<MPG.1317b0b7f966cd92989866@news.mersinet.co.uk> <20000219024740.13434.00000368@ng-fx1.aol.com> <38AF3123.4DDF0365@melbpc.org.au> <MPG.1319f48add50fcdd9898bd@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
Charles F Hankel wrote:

> I noticed that Tim Josling as tej@melbpc.org.au said...
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's been my objection to it all along.

If they really looked at COBOL and COBOL applications first, then high on
the list would be such things as English language checks to expand AT END,
such as RECORD-NOT-FOUND.  Checking to see if RETURN-VAL = '00' or
RETURN-VAL = '93' after an OPEN is not COBOL, even if it's coded in a COBOL
program.

Or allow for meta-data, add on most commands which pre-compilers convert
into calls, and in general, increase COBOL's ability to handle today's data
directly.

But instead, the standards committee seems to be more concerned in telling
the C++ programmers that COBOL can be modern too.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol programming

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-21T00:00:00

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:38B14E79.4C9E3411@cusys.edu...
> Charles F Hankel wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Have you looked at the new EXCEPTION HANDLING capability in the draft?  I
think it does what you are asking for here.

As far as a general view of the draft Standard, although the OO capabilities
have much of the publicity, if you look at the SUBSTANTIVE CHANGE annex, you
will see that BY FAR the majority of changes are for things that have nothing
to do with OO - or C-ification.

Examples of some (and this is only a VERY small portion) of the NON-OO and
non C-like enhancements include:

    GoBack
    Hex Literals
    Validate facility
    Enhanced VALUE capabilities for OCCURS
    File-Sharing/Record-Locking
    EXIT PERFORM/PARAGRAPH/SECTION
     Call proto-types (for parameter checking at compile-time)

There may be OTHER things that you might have wanted added to this revision,
but I go back to my original question:

   DID you send your thoughts to the committee when they were gathering input
for what would be included in this revision?  HAVE you already sent in your
suggestions for what should be added in the next one?
```
