## Thread: Can anybody answer this question? — 1 this month / 3 total

### Can anybody answer this question?

- **From:** Tim Hopper <hoppertr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BB0978.EB6C0DD4@bellsouth.net>`

```
My name is Tim Hopper.  I just graduated in December 1999 with a
master's in Accounting/Information Systems.  I did my bachelor's in
Management concentrating in Quantitative methods and business systems
(1983), e prelude to Management Information systems.  Instead of
pursuing an information systems job in 1983-4, I joined the Navy for 6
years and have worked in the mechanical field every since.  After being
let go from several jobs over the past years I decided to go back into
the computer field, my first love.  I had the standard intro to Cobol
and the advanced in my undergraduate studies, as well as analysis and
design and other standard IS subjects.  My final courses at the graduate
level were database management and I took the advanced Cobol for
graduate students.  I am trying to learn C++, Java, and Visual Basic on
my own, but this will take a little while.   My question to anyone out
there, especially anyone who has entered the field as entry level, can
these degrees work for me?  Research into the field tends to support the
fact that the demand for information systems people will be great for
years to come.  Can anyone verify this?  I would really appreciate
anyone giving some insight into what I might do to succeed and/or what I
should do.  There is a career couselor based here in Nashville that has
a workbook that states that the successful job candidates focus more on
the job search, than super-skills.  He says that you should find 30-40
companies you would like to work for, send and introductory letter, a
week later send a cover letter and resume, then a week later follow up
with the phone call directly to the hiring manager.  Since 87% of all
jobs are not advertised, this seems logical.  If anyone has had success
at this I would love to here from you.  I would give my left arm to work
as programmer/analyst, Cobol especially.  Thanks to anyone out there
that would like to respond.


                                   Tim Hopper        e-mail:
Hoppertr@bellsouth.net
```

```
i am looking for a way to connect to the peekmessage function in
fujitsu cobol 3.0

thing?

made an approximate port (below) but i don't know how to call it. in
c, you just include toolhelp.h


      ***********************
      *peekmessage

      *BOOL return value is 16bit

      *msg structure
      *since peekmessage with no remove, just a place to dump
      *temp info

      *filterlast and first are two message boundaries.

      *msg is far pointer

       02 no-remove    pic xx  value   h"0".
       02 remove-pos   pic xx  value   h"1".

       02 msg-structure.
           05 hwd  pic xx  comp-x.
      *    HWD 16bit value

           05 message  pic xx  comp-x.
           05 wparam   pic xx  comp-x.
           05 lparam   pic x(4)    comp-x.
           05 time     pic x(4)    comp-x.
           05 point.
      *        x and y are type int
               10 x    pic xx  comp-x.
               10 y    pic xx  comp-x.


       set msg to address of msg-structure
      *verify msg is far pointer size
       move no-remove to removeflags
```

<a id="t-6469b22b6e"></a>
## Thread: fwd to j4? set pointer up by — 2 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### fwd to j4? set pointer up by

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7ambsgperi1nt08al4bfm0osen026rnlu@4ax.com>`

```
someone said that there was going to be an addition to the standard to

02 pointer-val usage pointer.
02 boundary-size 	pic 9	usage packed-decimal.
02 boundary-size2 	pic 9	usage display.

i would hope

set pointer-val up by boundary-size

would give the same pointer-val as

set pointer-val up by boundary-size2

in fact,

set pointer-val up by 1

should probably not even be allowed, because to set a pointer up
usually requires one to understand the memory architecture, the
boundary size, if it is segmented, ect.

furthermore, a value of 1 *should* always match a 1 byte boundary. if
you set the pointer up, and you are on a 64 bit machine, then you
should do

move 8 to boundary-size
set pointer-val up by boundary-size

*always* to increment the pointer value. some architectures are
segmented, some are not, but if you have

linkage section.
02 char-string pic x(10) usage BINARY.

working-storage section.
02 pointer-val usage pointer.

set pointer to address of char-string
perform do-something-with-pointer
	varying char-index from 1 by 1
	until char index is 9
.

do-something-with-pointer.
	perform do-something-with-pointer	
	set pointer up by literal-1

*in the above line, if you have the wrong architecture, it might
*increment to the 9th character(64 bit environment) instead of the
*2nd, if literal-1 is not the correct boundary-size, because of usage
*binary
.



reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

#### ↳ Re: fwd to j4? set pointer up by

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89fkue$ul1$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a7ambsgperi1nt08al4bfm0osen026rnlu@4ax.com>`

```
The following are the rules that relate to the

    SET data-pointer UP/DOWN BY arithmetic-expression-1

statement in CD 1.8

"17) If arithmetic-expression-1 does not evaluate to an integer, the
EC-SIZE-ADDRESS exception condition is set to exist, the execution of the SET
statement is unsuccessful, and the content of identifier-10 is unchanged.

18) The address contained in identifier-10 is incremented, if UP is
specified, or decremented, if DOWN is specified, by the number of bytes
specified by arithmetic-expression-1. If this new address is outside the
range of values allowed by the implementor for a data-pointer data item, the
EC-RANGE-PTR exception condition is set to exist and the value of the data
item referenced by identifier-10 is unchanged."

   ***

I think that this "resolves" your concerns.

(FYI, an arithmetic expression can be a single numeric item - so your
Packed-Decimal, Display, or even COMP values would all be treated as the same
number - as would a numeric literal.)

FYI-2,
  The size of a "byte" is totally "implementor defined," so if you are
talking about a 7-bit, 8-bit, or 16-bit architecture, the result of the SET
statement is STILL to increment/decrement to the next "byte" - as defined for
that architecture.
```

<a id="t-a50ac1d0c1"></a>
## Thread: newbie seeking help: Fujitsu Cobol: WINEXEC-Console doesn't recognize 'Return' key — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### newbie seeking help: Fujitsu Cobol: WINEXEC-Console doesn't recognize 'Return' key

- **From:** "Claude Kaber" <ckaber@pt.lu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38bc1373@news.vo.lu>`

```
Hello,
I'm new to Cobol and i'm currently trying Fujitsu Cobol 3.0.
When i am executing programs (with the WINEXEC program from fujitsu), the
Console Window reacts strangely to the 'Return' key:
When i  hit the 'Return'-key for terminating an input (demanded by an ACCEPT
statment in within the program), the cursor simply moves to the next line of
the console window (like if i were typing text in a text editor), but the
input is not accepted.
The 'Return' key is not accepted until i type in as many Characters as there
are in the Variable filled by the ACCEPT statment.  EG: if i have a variable
of PIC X(100), i would need typing 100 characters !!! before the
WINEXEC-console window will accept the input. If i understand well the COBOL
standard, it should be possible to terminate the input by hitting 'Return' ,
it should not be necessary to type all the characters .
Is this a bug in the Fujitsu-WINEXEC program or am i missing something ?

Any help is much appreciated,
Claude Kaber
```

#### ↳ Re: newbie seeking help: Fujitsu Cobol: WINEXEC-Console doesn't recognize 'Return' key

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89h6dj$efq$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38bc1373@news.vo.lu>`

```
I believe that this is a "documented feature" (i.e. not considered a bug) by
Fujitsu.  Most other compilers do NOT work this way, but there is nothing in
the Standard to say that is wrong.  I know it has been discussed before in
comp.lang.cobol - so you may want to check out www.deja.com

I will check this out with Fujitsu to see if there is any option to "overcome
it".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: newbie seeking help: Fujitsu Cobol: WINEXEC-Console doesn't recognize 'Return' key

- **From:** "Claude Kaber" <ckaber@pt.lu>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38bc414c$1@news.vo.lu>`
- **References:** `<38bc1373@news.vo.lu> <89h6dj$efq$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Hello,
Thanks for the 'deja.com' tip. The topic was indeed discussed earlier.
One (the) solution consists in adding 'FROM Console' to the ACCEPT statment:

eg: 'ACCEPT myvariable FROM Console'   (instead of simply 'ACCEPT
myvariable').
You may then hit 'Return' for terminating the input within the
WINEXEC-console window.

many thanks for the prompt reply,
Claude Kaber

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message

"overcome
```

```
US-IL-Wood Dale-Intern Programmer Analyst

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
----

  Household International, a Fortune 200 Company, is one of the leading
providers of loans and credit cards to consumers. We have an employee
population of nearly 25,000 people. Our team of women and men is comprised

team atmosphere, the ability to do real work (not just make copies), social
events and outings, and the chance to earn valuable work experience. We are
looking for high potential summer interns for our Wood Dale and Prospect
Heights offices. These are PAID internships. Responsibilities will include
working with Sr. Programmer Analsysts in either: Mainframe, Client Server,
or Web Design environment - area will depend on applicants course work. The
internship program runs for 10 to 12 weeks starting in May or June. Reliable
transportation is required.

Juniors in Computer Science with a 3.0 GPA or better, Excellent
communication skills both verbal and written, and the ability to work with
others. Classroom training in one or more of the following needed: COBOL,
CICS, UNIX, Assembler, Powerbuilder, VB, Web design and development.
```

<a id="t-04f01f6eda"></a>
## Thread: SAS sucks — 2 this month / 18 total

### Re: SAS sucks

- **From:** hancock4@bbs.cpcn.com (Lisa nor Jeff)
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89fcen$q3f@netaxs.com>`

```
SAS gets a bad rap for performance primarily because
managers think it can be written by anybody, not necessarily a
professional SAS programmer..


Therein lies a big problem with SAS right there.  Everybody knows
COBOL, and most programmers have lots of experience in it.  The
advantage of COBOL is that it's a universal language.

Like it or not, SAS is a specialized language.  Not everyone
knows it, certainly not to the level you speak of to use it
properly.  Unless an organization standardizes on SAS and trains
everyone in it _thoroughly_, the organization won't develop the
expertise.



Regarding your comment of coding complex routines, my point is
that if coding is required, you might as well code in COBOL
rather than SAS.
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>

```

non professional programmers?  If SAS is marketed that way, then is
it not fair to assess SAS in that environment? :-)
```

## Thread: Windows in COBOL? — 2 msgs

### Re: Windows in COBOL?

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.1325477f7b09c4bc989901@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<883mo2$1aa$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <slrn8ahsfe.dfl.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net> <6jiq4.5608$y15.61723@news3.mia> <epdjkvQf$GA.276@cpmsnbbsa04> <N4xs4.4859$Wv2.60596@news2.mia> <SHJs4.122$M65.1320@news.swbell.net> <38B3EB04.C3E2015A@cusys.edu> <890upv$9lg$1@news.cerf.net> <MPG.132161c415c0d2849898e0@news.mersinet.co.uk> <89bqkk$fis$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
I noticed that Cheesle as cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no said...
> "Charles F Hankel" <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote in message
> news:MPG.132161c415c0d2849898e0@news.mersinet.co.uk...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> fiction.

OK but the implication was that AS/400 is a closed system whereas it is 
not and slots very nicely into various mixed environments without any 
real need for user interference.  The whole concept of the system was to 
be one that could be started up and left to run in a quiet corner and 
get an occasional flick with a feather duster just to keep the box clean, 
and it achieves this.  I don't know of a Windows-based server that hasn't 
required attention at unexpected moments and I just don't see them in the 
same class.

Of course, I do have a theory that there's a hell of a lot of Windows 
"users" out there who only use their system for installing, uninstalling 
and occasional forays onto the Internet.  Oh and they may polish up their 
CV occasionally as well.  Users of most other systems spend very little 
time on these tasks, if any.
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows in COBOL?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89gn00$2c$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<883mo2$1aa$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <slrn8ahsfe.dfl.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net> <6jiq4.5608$y15.61723@news3.mia> <epdjkvQf$GA.276@cpmsnbbsa04> <N4xs4.4859$Wv2.60596@news2.mia> <SHJs4.122$M65.1320@news.swbell.net> <38B3EB04.C3E2015A@cusys.edu> <890upv$9lg$1@news.cerf.net> <MPG.132161c415c0d2849898e0@news.mersinet.co.uk> <89bqkk$fis$1@news.cerf.net> <MPG.1325477f7b09c4bc989901@news.mersinet.co.uk>`

```
"Charles F Hankel" <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk> wrote in message
news:MPG.1325477f7b09c4bc989901@news.mersinet.co.uk...
> OK but the implication was that AS/400 is a closed system whereas it is
> not and slots very nicely into various mixed environments without any

It has been my understanding that the AS/400 is a more restricted
environment than the Wintel platform, my apologies for being wrong. But, I
believe we can agree that the market of independent vendors on the Wintel
platform is substantially larger. Hence, the potential of error prone
situations are more likely. This is not said as a negative argument towards
the AS/400, rather the contrary. As I pointed out in my original message, it
is the users that are forcing the poor reliability on the Wintel platform,
because of the demand for lowest price possible and backward compatibility.

> and it achieves this.  I don't know of a Windows-based server that hasn't
> required attention at unexpected moments and I just don't see them in the
> same class.

I do not disagree that the Wintel alternative is not of the same class as
AS/400 when it comes to up time and reliability, what I was targeting was
that Microsoft alone was not the sole reason for Windows to be unstable, but
the Wintel platform (Ms + Intel + 3rd party) as a whole.

> Of course, I do have a theory that there's a hell of a lot of Windows
> "users" out there who only use their system for installing, uninstalling
> and occasional forays onto the Internet.

Very descriptive indeed.

Cheesle
```

<a id="t-1e3356a600"></a>
## Thread: PIC S9(6)V99 help please — 3 this month / 6 total

### PIC S9(6)V99 help please

- **From:** "Polaire" <p.bureau@dial.oleane.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89glte$vsn$1@minus.oleane.net>`

```
I have to transfert a cobol database.

One field have this picture : S9(6)V99

I think that S is for 'signed' but in my file one of the record is
'p0015115'

What 'p' mean. Plus ? or is it an error in the record ?

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: PIC S9(6)V99 help please

- **From:** Warren Porter <wb999port@bellsouth12.net>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38BBE2E8.23B147D3@bellsouth12.net>`
- **References:** `<89glte$vsn$1@minus.oleane.net>`

```
Polaire wrote:

> I have to transfer a cobol database.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> What 'p' mean. Plus ? or is it an error in the record ?

The "S" in that picture means one byte in the field will contain both
the value of that digit as well as the sign of the field.  In this case
the last four bits of the letter "p" indicate the value of that digit, a
zero so the value of your field is 151.15.  Not knowing your system, it
is impossible to know if that value is positive or negative.  If you
have a "p" in every occurance of that field you can probably ignore it.
If you find something else there do your best to track that value
down--that would help answer a lot of your questions.  HTH
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PIC S9(6)V99 help please

- **From:** "Polaire" <p.bureau@dial.oleane.com>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89gqaa$3um$1@minus.oleane.net>`
- **References:** `<89glte$vsn$1@minus.oleane.net> <38BBE2E8.23B147D3@bellsouth12.net>`

```

Picture : S9(6)V99
Value : 0166NN
```

<a id="t-d127504ba0"></a>
## Thread: Can anybody answer this question? — 1 msgs

### Re: Can anybody answer this question?

- **From:** Charles F Hankel <charles@hankel.mersinet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-02-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.13260956d9578e3d989918@news.mersinet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38BB0978.EB6C0DD4@bellsouth.net> <sq8mbsgdelqo6aftqqcp8kq85vtcqbfbnp@4ax.com>`

```
I noticed that Bearhair as anonymous@enteract.com said...
> Tim Hopper <hoppertr@bellsouth.net> wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >years to come.  Can anyone verify this?

Yes, the demand looks like it will always be there as newer systems come 
onto the marketplace and the number of installed systems increases year 
on year.  It sounds as though you might have the sort of non-CS degree 
that will appeal to some employers who find that they have to retrain the 
average CS graduate for real-world COBOL and suffer the "in C you can..." 
whinges.

I've been getting more and more involved in web technologies in recent 
times and the marketplace is wide open to all-comers but there is still a 
significant demand for COBOL skills as well.  Possibly all the "sexy" web 
work will soak up a lot of newcomers leaving plenty more COBOL work as we 
older ones decide that fly fishing is more enjoyable than sitting in an 
office, cracking code.

> >  I would really appreciate
> >anyone giving some insight into what I might do to succeed and/or what I
> >should do.  There is a career couselor based here in Nashville that has
> >a workbook that states that the successful job candidates focus more on
> >the job search, than super-skills.

Not bad advice.  Any half-decent employer will train the right person in 
the required skills, and they will be relevant to the job.

> >  He says that you should find 30-40
> >companies you would like to work for, send and introductory letter, a
> >week later send a cover letter and resume, then a week later follow up
> >with the phone call directly to the hiring manager.  Since 87% of all
> >jobs are not advertised, this seems logical.

I always wonder where these statistics come from.  The main problem with 
advertised jobs is that any applicant is immediately in a large pool and 
selection may be based on petty criteria rather than the whole person, 
and conducted by someone who may not be qualified to make that selection.

> >  If anyone has had success
> >at this I would love to here from you.  I would give my left arm to work
> >as programmer/analyst, Cobol especially.  Thanks to anyone out there
> >that would like to respond.

Giving your left arm might cramp your style.  After all, how are you 
going lean against a bar and drink your beer at the same time?  It 
probably takes a good deal of practice.
 
>    Any chance you're related to Admiral Grace Hopper? That'd be a way to get
> your foot in the door!

Now that might open a few doors.

Here in the UK most of the jobs are put through agencies, and most of 
them and us use a website (http://www.jobserve.com) called Jobserve which 
is the biggest one of its kind over here.  I assume that there must 
surely be an American equivalent.  The joy of this is that a job can 
become available on a Monday and filled before the end of the week, which 
is a lot quicker than can be accomplished by other forms of advertising.  
Using this route, you virtually guarantee that you are in the first wave.
```
